title: Efficient Federated RLHF via Zeroth-Order Policy Optimization
authors: Deyi Wang, Qining Zhang, Lei Ying
abstract: This paper considers reinforcement learning from human feedback in a federated learning setting with resource-constrained agents, such as edge devices. We propose an efficient federated RLHF algorithm, named Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization (Par-S$^2$ZPO). The algorithm is built on zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation, resulting in low communication, computation, and memory complexity by design. Our theoretical analysis establishes an upper bound on the convergence rate of Par-S$^2$ZPO, revealing that it is as efficient as its centralized counterpart in terms of sample complexity but converges faster in terms of policy update iterations. Our experimental results show that it outperforms a FedAvg-based RLHF on four MuJoCo RL tasks.

title: SafeAnchor: Preventing Cumulative Safety Erosion in Continual Domain Adaptation of Large Language Models
authors: Dongxin Guo, Jikun Wu, Siu Ming Yiu
abstract: Safety alignment in large language models is remarkably shallow: it is concentrated in the first few output tokens and reversible by fine-tuning on as few as 100 adversarial examples. This fragility becomes critical in real-world deployment, where models undergo sequential adaptation across domains such as medicine, law, and code, causing safety guardrails to erode cumulatively. Yet all existing safety-preserving methods target only single-task fine-tuning, leaving the multi-domain sequential setting entirely unaddressed. We introduce SafeAnchor, a framework that anchors safety in place throughout continual adaptation. SafeAnchor first identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space via Fisher Information eigendecomposition, then constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these subspaces, and finally monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay. Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across a three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks, SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

title: Detectability of minority communities in networks
authors: Jiaze Li, Leto Peel
abstract: Community structure is prevalent in real-world networks, with empirical studies revealing heterogeneous distributions where a few dominant majority communities coexist with many smaller groups. These small-scale groups, which we term minority communities, are critical for understanding network organization but pose significant challenges for detection. Here, we investigate the detectability of minority communities from a theoretical perspective using the Stochastic Block Model. We identify three distinct phases of community detection: the detectable phase, where overall community structure is recoverable but minority communities are merged into majority groups; the distinguishable phase, where minority communities form a coherent group separate from the majority but remain unresolved internally; and the resolvable phase, where each minority community is fully distinguishable. These phases correspond to phase transitions at the Kesten-Stigum threshold and two additional thresholds determined by the eigenvalue structure of the signal matrix, which we derive explicitly. Furthermore, we demonstrate that spectral clustering with the Bethe Hessian exhibits significantly weaker detection performance for minority communities compared to belief propagation, revealing a specific limitation of spectral methods in identifying fine-grained community structure despite their capability to detect macroscopic structures down to the theoretical limit.

title: Random-State Generation and Preparation Complexity in Rydberg Atom Arrays
authors: Edison S. Carrera, Grégoire Misguich
abstract: Rydberg atom arrays are powerful platforms for studying quantum many-body systems. We consider the Rydberg-Ising Hamiltonian on periodic chains and numerically study ensembles of states generated by random global pulse sequences subject to hardware constraints and fixed evolution times. We compare the statistical properties of such states with those of Haar-random states within the relevant lattice symmetry sector. In the strong-interaction regime (short interatomic distance), the dynamics is governed by an effective blockade that restricts Hilbert-space exploration and limits entanglement growth. In this regime, level-spacing statistics of reduced density matrices are close to random-matrix predictions, while the distribution of measurement probabilities deviates from Porter-Thomas behavior. For weaker interactions (larger interatomic distance), the system approaches Haar-like statistics at long times, as reflected in entanglement entropy, entanglement spectrum statistics, and the distribution of measurement probabilities. At intermediate interactions, this behavior is observed on experimentally relevant timescales. Motivated by this observation, we investigate whether generic symmetric quantum states can be efficiently prepared using quantum optimal control in this regime. Employing target states drawn from an ensemble with a broad entropy distribution, we observe high fidelities (infidelities between $10^{-5}$ and $3\times 10^{-2}$ for 9 spins). The fidelity, however, decreases with the entanglement entropy of the target state, demonstrating that highly entangled states are intrinsically harder to prepare under realistic constraints.

title: Families of symplectic Galois representations over small parabolic eigenvarieties for Siegel cuspforms of genus $2$
authors: Muhammad Manji, Frederick E. Thøgersen, Ju-Feng Wu
abstract: We construct small parabolic eigenvarieties for holomorphic Siegel cuspforms of genus $2$ and study families of Galois representations attached to them in the spirit of Bellaïche--Chenevier. In the course, we introduce the notion of $(\varphi, Γ)$-modules with $G$-structures and the notion of refined families of symplectic Galois representations by implementing the theory of symplectic Galois determinant d'après Moakher--Quast. We then prove an infinitesimal $R=\mathbb{T}$ theorem under mild hypotheses. As an application, we study the relationship between the geometry of the small parabolic eigenvarieties at the Saito--Kurokawa lifts for cuspidal eigenforms (both finite-slope and infinite-slope) and the Bloch--Kato Selmer groups of those eigenforms.

title: Effect of antibiotic spectrum on the abundance of resistant bacteria in multispecies communities
authors: Magnus Aspenberg, Erik Andreas Martens, Kristofer Wollein Waldetoft
abstract: Antibiotic resistance is a major threat to global health. It emerges in multispecies microbial communities under antibiotic exposure. This makes antibiotic spectrum -- a drug's distribution of effects across species -- a potential key parameter in resistance management. However, we currently lack evolutionary theory for resistance dynamics in a multispecies setting. Analysing established community ecology theory, we develop a simple mathematical measure for how one taxon (strain or species) affects another taxon through all direct and indirect interactions in a complex interaction network. Using this, we derive the expected effects of different antibiotic spectra on the abundance of resistant taxa in microbial communities. This furthers our understanding of microbial evolutionary ecology in multispecies communities, and provides a formal theoretical basis for empirical work on optimal antibiotic choice.

title: EmbodiedLGR: Integrating Lightweight Graph Representation and Retrieval for Semantic-Spatial Memory in Robotic Agents
authors: Paolo Riva, Leonardo Gargani, Matteo Frosi, Matteo Matteucci
abstract: As the world of agentic artificial intelligence applied to robotics evolves, the need for agents capable of building and retrieving memories and observations efficiently is increasing. Robots operating in complex environments must build memory structures to enable useful human-robot interactions by leveraging the mnemonic representation of the current operating context. People interacting with robots may expect the embodied agent to provide information about locations, events, or objects, which requires the agent to provide precise answers within human-like inference times to be perceived as responsive. We propose the Embodied Light Graph Retrieval Agent (EmbodiedLGR-Agent), a visual-language model (VLM)-driven agent architecture that constructs dense and efficient representations of robot operating environments. EmbodiedLGR-Agent directly addresses the need for an efficient memory representation of the environment by providing a hybrid building-retrieval approach built on parameter-efficient VLMs that store low-level information about objects and their positions in a semantic graph, while retaining high-level descriptions of the observed scenes with a traditional retrieval-augmented architecture. EmbodiedLGR-Agent is evaluated on the popular NaVQA dataset, achieving state-of-the-art performance in inference and querying times for embodied agents, while retaining competitive accuracy on the global task relative to the current state-of-the-art approaches. Moreover, EmbodiedLGR-Agent was successfully deployed on a physical robot, showing practical utility in real-world contexts through human-robot interaction, while running the visual-language model and the building-retrieval pipeline locally.

title: Eikonal, nonlocality and regular black holes
authors: Mariano Cadoni, Lorenzo Herres, Leonardo Modesto, Lorenzo Orlando, Mirko Pitzalis
abstract: We investigate the leading gravitational eikonal in nonlocal $D$ dimensional theories of gravity. We analyze the simplest cases of $2\rightarrow2$ massless and massive scalar scattering at tree level, studying the effects of nonlocal form factors in the gravitational sector. We give an interpretation of our results in terms of geodesic motion in effective generalized Aichelburg-Sexl geometries for the massless case, and in smeared linearized Schwarzschild metrics for the massive case in the probe limit. Combining our results for the geometries at linearized level with general requirements about the behaviour of the solutions in the core, we propose a nonlinear completion of the geometries. The resulting spacetimes describe singularity-free, asymptotically flat deformations of the Schwarzschild solution with a de Sitter core. We also analyze the main geometric and thermodynamic features of these solutions.

title: The effect of interstellar scattering on coherent radio emission from stars: the case of CU Vir
authors: J. S. Morgan, B. Das, H. E. Bignall
abstract: A subset of magnetic stars exhibit periodic radio pulses produced by the coherent electron cyclotron maser mechanism. These pulses are known to exhibit both temporal and spectral variations, which have been attributed to phenomena intrinsic to the stellar magnetosphere. However, in order to fully characterise the radio pulses and use them as magnetospheric probes (as suggested by past studies), it is also important to consider the effects of phenomena extrinsic to the magnetosphere. In this paper, we investigate whether interstellar scintillation could be a relevant mechanism for explaining spectral and temporal variations observed for coherent stellar radio emission. For that, we consider the case of the well-characterised magnetic hot star CU Vir. At 400 MHz, coherent radio emission from the star was reported to exhibit a peculiar spectral evolution that remains unexplained. We show that a plausible level of turbulence along the line of sight can produce the observed phenomenon of spectral features. Our analysis shows that diffractive interstellar scintillation can have a strong effect on the observed dynamic spectrum of radio emission from stars, for an assumed size of the emitting region of $0.01r_\odot$, and that caution should therefore be taken in separating intrinsic and extrinsic features, particularly at low frequencies. These results are preliminary and further work is required to fully model the scintillation of ECME from stars (in particular the change in source location with frequency), and to explore the full range of plausible scintillation parameters. We suggest how further observations may be used to test the interstellar scintillation hypothesis.

title: Classical counterparts of shortcuts to adiabaticity in nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems
authors: Jincheng Shi, Yicheng Pan, Yue Ban, Xi Chen
abstract: Shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) were first developed in quantum dynamics to realize rapid transformations with suppressed residual excitations. Here we show how the same idea can be implemented in classical nonlinear dissipative Lagrangian systems. Using a coupled $r$-$θ$ manipulator as an illustrative model, we perform inverse engineering on the Euler-Lagrange equations with Rayleigh dissipation by prescribing endpoint-stationary trajectories, obtaining the corresponding force and torque profiles and quantifying how geometric coupling amplifies errors and residual energy. We further compare smooth STA protocols with actuator-bounded time-optimal solutions and with proportional-integral-derivative tracking, which highlights a trade-off among smoothness, speed, and robustness. Finally, we introduce a single-shot correction based on one mid-course measurement to reduce the effect of early deviations while keeping the inputs nearly smooth. These results provide a practical bridge between quantum STA concepts and their classical counterparts.

title: Bosonization, vertex operators and maximal violation of the Bell-CHSH inequality in wedge regions
authors: J. G. A. Caribé, M. S. Guimaraes, I. Roditi, S. P. Sorella
abstract: It is pointed out that the vertex operators of a chiral boson in 1+1 dimensions provide an explicit realization of dichotomic, bounded, Hermitian operators that saturate the Tsirelson bound of the Bell-CHSH inequality in the vacuum state.

title: Seed Layer Engineering for Effective Charge Transfer Doping of MoS$_2$ Transistors
authors: Sahej Sharma, Shao-Heng Yang, Himani Jawa, Rana Yuvraj, Bach Nguyen, Chang Niu, Shiva Radhakrishnan, Shalini Tripathi, Dennis Lin, Cesar Javier Lockhart de la Rosa, Pierre Morin, Dmitry Zemlyanov, Francesca Iacopi, Zhihong Chen, Joerg Appenzeller, Thomas E. Beechem
abstract: Integrating two-dimensional semiconductors such as MoS$_2$ with dielectric materials remains a central challenge for their use in future logic technologies. While seed layers are typically introduced to promote dielectric nucleation and adhesion, we show that they also critically govern charge-transfer doping and, in turn, transistor performance. Back-gated monolayer MoS$_2$ transistors passivated on their top-surface with a Ta-seed/HfO$_x$ dielectric stack were fabricated and characterized electrically and physically using Raman, photoluminescence, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies. Threshold voltage and on-current varied strongly with Ta-seed thickness and deposition conditions, and these changes correlated with signatures observed across all spectroscopic probes. The results reveal that the seed layer both introduces disorder into the MoS$_2$ channel and modifies the interfacial charge environment controlling charge transfer between HfO$_x$ and MoS$_2$. Optical spectroscopy shows that on-current tracks seed-induced disorder, whereas X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy indicates that threshold voltage correlates with shifts in the local electrostatic environment associated with interfacial charge transfer. Better performance was obtained with ultrathin 0.2 nm Ta seed layers deposited under oxygen-poor conditions, which limit deposition-induced damage while facilitating charge transfer. These findings identify seed-layer engineering as a key strategy for controlling disorder and interfacial doping in MoS$_2$ devices and establish multimodal spectroscopy as a practical during-fabrication approach for process development and monitoring.

title: Dirac's theorem and the switch geometry of perfect matchings
authors: Ross J. Kang, Clément Legrand-Duchesne
abstract: Let $G$ be a graph on an even number $n$ of vertices and let ${\cal M}_G$ be the collection of perfect matchings in $G$. Dirac's theorem says that if the minimum degree $δ(G)$ of $G$ is at least $n/2$, then ${\cal M}_G$ is guaranteed to be non-empty, while this is not necessarily the case if $δ(G) \le n/2-1$. Given an integer $k\ge 2$, let $\mathcal H_k(G)$ be the reconfiguration graph formed on ${\cal M}_G$ by connecting two distinct $M_1,M_2\in {\cal M}_G$ by an edge in $\mathcal H_k(G)$ if $M_1$ can be obtained from $M_2$ by switching at most $k$ edges. Besides non-emptiness, as per Dirac's theorem, what other natural properties of $\mathcal H_k(G)$ are guaranteed based on the minimum degree $δ(G)$ of $G$? We show that if $δ(G) \ge \lfloor2n/3\rfloor+1$, then $\mathcal H_2(G)$ must be connected and an expander, while for each $δ\le \lfloor(2n-2)/3\rfloor$ there are $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with minimum degree $δ$ such that $\mathcal H_2(G)$ is disconnected. We also show that, if $δ(G) \ge n/2+2$, then $\mathcal H_3(G)$ must be connected and an expander, while for each $δ\le n/2-C_k$ there are $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with minimum degree $δ$ such that $\mathcal H_k(G)$ is disconnected, for some $C_k$ depending on $k\ge 3$. Furthermore, for every $\varepsilon >0$, there exists a $c>1$ such that for every $k\ge 2$ and every large enough $n$, there are $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with $δ(G) \ge \frac{n}2-\varepsilon kn$ such that $\mathcal H_k(G)$ has at least $c^n$ components. With respect to guaranteeing that $\mathcal H_k(G)$ has positive minimum degree (or, equivalently, no isolated vertices) we show that if $δ(G) \ge n/2+1$, then $\mathcal H_2(G)$ must have positive minimum degree. For $k\ge 3$, we show how this threshold for $δ(G)$ is related to the notorious Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture.

title: Conformal Robust Set Estimation
authors: Alejandro Cholaquidis, Emilien Joly, Leonardo Moreno
abstract: Conformal prediction provides finite-sample, distribution-free coverage under exchangeability, but standard constructions may lack robustness in the presence of outliers or heavy tails. We propose a robust conformal method based on a non-conformity score defined as the half-mass radius around a point, equivalently the distance to its $(\lfloor n/2\rfloor+1)$-nearest neighbour. We show that the resulting conformal regions are marginally valid for any sample size and converge in probability to a robust population central set defined through a distance-to-a-measure functional. Under mild regularity conditions, we establish exponential concentration and tail bounds that quantify the deviation between the empirical conformal region and its population counterpart. These results provide a probabilistic justification for using robust geometric scores in conformal prediction, even for heavy-tailed or multi-modal distributions.

title: Non-linear Lie Conformal Algebras and One-Loop Corrections of self-dual Yang-Mills amplitudes
authors: Charles Igel, Jeremy Mancinas, Juan Villarreal
abstract: This work is motivated by recent developments in celestial holography. In \cite{CP}, the authors interpreted QCD collinear singularities in terms of operator product expansions in a two-dimensional CFT. We reformulate the algebraic structures arising in their work using the formalism of non-linear Lie conformal algebras developed in \cite{SK}.

title: A Study on the Correlation between Cognitive Load Theory and APP Interface Icon Design: A Case Study of XX App

authors: Bushra Hammad amon Amon, Ahmed N Rashid

title: Collaborative Neural Networks significantly Improve Global Aerosol Retrievals From Multi-Angle Polarimeters
authors: Guangliang Fu, Sha Lu, Meng Gao, Jeroen Rietjens, Otto Hasekamp
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: We present the RemoTAP-Hybrid algorithm for global aerosol products retrieved from Multi-Angle Polarimeters (MAP), and apply it to one-year aerosol retrievals from the SPEXone MAP instrument. For the first time, Multiple Collaborated Neural Networks (MCNN) are used to perform global aerosol MAP retrievals over land and ocean. The combined use of MCNN with a full physics inversion in RemoTAP-Hybrid, significantly improves accuracy and computational speed of SPEXone aerosol retrievals. We validate the retrieved Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD), Angstrom Exponent (AE), Single Scattering Albedo (SSA), Absorption AOD (AAOD), Fine mode AOD (FAOD), Coarse mode AOD (CAOD), and fine mode effective radius (reff_fine) with AERONET data. The validation suggests that aerosol retrievals can be further improved when extending the aerosol description to include a fine non-spherical mode. For AOD, the Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE) is 0.051 (0.037) over land (ocean), while 78.3% (80.8%) of the retrievals are within the requirement formulated by the Global Climate Observing System (GCOS). The RMSE is 0.186 (0.207) for AE, 0.038 (0.044) for SSA, 0.023 (0.014) for AAOD, 0.047 (0.031) for FAOD, 0.043 (0.027) for CAOD, and 0.021 (0.026) for reff_fine over land (ocean). Compared to the current RemoTAP algorithm, RemoTAP-Hybrid shows improved agreement with AERONET for AOD, AE, and FAOD over land and ocean while the number of valid retrievals is increased by 4% (3%) over land (ocean) and the computational time is reduced by 43% (32%). Finally, we present the first global seasonal maps of aerosol optical and microphysical properties and chemical composition from SPEXone.

title: On the Systematic Design Framework for Intuitive Interfaces in Telerobotic Operations: A Case Study in Surgical Robotics_supp1-3684095.docx
authors: Seok Chang Ryu
abstract: This paper proposes a design framework for intuitive interfaces in telerobotic operation, moving beyond outcome-based evaluation toward systematic interface design. A human-centered framework is introduced to guide interface strategy selection based on learning dynamics and to support implementation-level design decisions through analysis of human sensorimotor interaction. As a case study, the framework is instantiated through an embodiment-oriented index finger interface for controlling a distally bendable surgical instrument with unconventional kinematics. A telerobotic system was implemented, and the finger interface was compared with a familiarity-driven stylus interface in a dual-session human-subject study consisting of a training session and a retention session two weeks later. Results reveal a distinction between initial and later-stage interaction characteristics. While the stylus interface exhibited higher initial performance, the finger interface showed faster learning progression and achieved higher performance after learning stabilized. These findings suggest that embodiment-oriented interface design can support intuitive control by facilitating sensorimotor adaptation beyond immediate familiarity. Although evaluated in a surgical context, the proposed framework is applicable to the design of teleoperation interfaces for robotic systems with unconventional kinematics in broader domains.

title: Calculation of the Impact of Overvoltage Transients in Hydroelectric Plants and the Electric Power Industry
authors: Álvaro Soto A., Vladimir Esparza H., José Mahomar J., Daniel Quezada M., Jaime Rohten C.
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article analyzes the impact of transient overvoltages on electrical power systems based on field measurements in Chile. Three representative cases were studied: a hydroelectric power plant, an electric vehicle charging station, and an industrial plant. The campaigns were carried out in accordance with IEEE 1159-2019 using a Fluke 434 with a valid calibration certificate, recording brief high-magnitude events with cumulative effects on insulation. At the Cipreses Power Plant, pulses of up to 60.91 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{kV}(441 \%$</tex> of Un) were detected, concurrent with 2.37 million partial discharges per second, evidence of advanced stator degradation. In DC chargers, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5-20 \text{kHz}$</tex> disturbances with 600 V and 200 A were identified during energization, causing erratic tripping due to low relay immunity. In the industrial plant, impulsive/oscillatory transients were observed which, when interacting with inductance and capacitance, amplified the surges and compromised the stability of the equipment. A synergistic mitigation scheme based on coordinated SPDs (Types 1-2-3), Point-on-Wave (PoW) switching, and detuned reactive compensation is proposed, aimed at reducing the magnitude of impulses, avoiding resonances, and preserving operational reliability. The results support the incorporation of transient analysis into predictive maintenance and critical asset planning, and provide guidelines for prioritizing mitigation investments with high technical and economic impact.

title: Techno-Economic Analysis of a Diesel-Windphotovoltaic Hybrid System With Battery Storage for an Off-Grid Household on Amantani Island, Peru
authors: Ubaldo Yancachajlla Tito, Celso Antonio Sanga Quiroz, Edilberto Velarde Coaquira, Nelson Esteban Chambi Quiroz
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This study presents a techno - economic analysis of a hybrid photovoltaic - wind - diesel - battery system designed to supply an off - grid household on Amantaní Island (Lake Titicaca), Peru. Modeling in HOMER Pro incorporated the electrical load profile, solar irradiation, wind resource, and fuel prices. Three configurations were evaluated: Scenario 1 (Load Following), Scenario 2 (Cycle Charging), and Scenario 3 (PV + wind + diesel). Scenario 1 proved optimal in terms of Net Present Cost (22274 USD) and levelized cost of energy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C O E} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 2 6 4}$</tex> USD/kWh), achieving a 96.7 % renewable fraction and annual diesel consumption of 96.7 L. Although Scenario 2 reaches 100 % renewables and greater autonomy (43.7 h), its additional cost justifies the moderate diesel backup of Scenario 1 as the best techno - economic compromise. These results confirm the viability and replicability of hybrid systems in remote high Andean communities.

title: Editor Preface
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This IEEE Chilecon Conference is the continuation of the Chilean Congress of Electrical Engineering, founded in 1975 by Dr. Guillermo González Rees, and was the first conference held at the University of Chile. For 37 years, it was held every two years in odd-numbered years, in agreement with the Chilean Automatic Control Association, which holds its conferences in even-numbered years. In 1987, a cooperative alliance was signed between ACCA and the IEEE Chilean Control Chapter to co-organize these conferences. In 2012, the IEEE Chilean Section assumed co-organization, changing the name to IEEE Chilecon.

title: Early Nutrient Deficiency Detection in Hydroponic Lettuce Via CNN-Based Image Analysis
authors: Yessica Recalde, Alfredo Renault, Diego F. Palacios, Alba Chaparro, Alejandro Carissimo, Juan S. Gonzalez, Maira Santacruz, Mario Arzamendia, Derlis O. Gregor
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a methodological approach for the automated diagnosis of plant health in hydroponic lettuce using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). In this first stage, a semantic segmentation system was developed based on the UNet architecture with a ResNet34 encoder, trained on labeled RGB images from a dataset created with the Roboflow platform. Training and validation were carried out on Google Colab using a T4 GPU, achieving promising performance metrics: a mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 0.7569, an F1-Score of 0.8593, and a pixel accuracy of 87.13 % on a test set of 61 unseen images. Model performance was assessed through training and validation curves, confusion matrix analysis, and comparative benchmarking against international standards such as Pascal VOC and COCO. The results demonstrate the model's ability to distinguish between healthy and diseased tissue with high reliability, validating its applicability as a foundational tool for future advanced systems that integrate multispectral imaging and IoT sensors in controlled greenhouse environments. This research is part of the PINV01-26 project funded by CONACYT and represents a key step toward the integration of computer vision in precision agriculture, with potential impact on the efficiency a nd s ustainability of h ydroponic production systems.

title: Translating Community-Specific Factors into Engineering Solutions: An HCD Framework for Rural Technology Adoption
authors: Aroun Clément Baudouin-van Os, S. Sabarinath, Udaya Bhaskar Reddy Ragula, Sasangan Ramanathan, Renjith Mohan, Maneesha Vinodini Ramesh
abstract: Achieving sustainable livelihoods in marginalized communities remains a complex global challenge. Too often, technical interventions falter due to a disconnect between design and local context. This study examines a solar thermal steam distillation initiative in the tribal community of Valaramkunnu, Kerala, India, introduced to replace a traditional wood-fired livelihood lost to deforestation policies. While environmentally sound, the initial solar prototype suffered from low adoption, highlighting the gap between technological potential and community acceptance. Addressing this gap, this research defines and applies a Socio-Technical Translation Framework to structure a dual-phase methodology: first, an inductive thematic analysis of project documents and stakeholder interviews identified 76 community-specific adoption factors across eight dimensions, revealing that economic viability and system reliability were paramount over environmental purity. Second, by employing Human-Centered Design (HCD) principles within the framework's translation phase, the study converted these insights into 15 targeted system enhancements. The most significant outcome is the development of a pragmatic hybrid energy system integrating solar, electric, and gas sources that prioritizes the year-round operational reliability required for livelihood security. The successful technical validation of the hybrid architecture and safety systems through controlled endurance testing supports this HCD-integrated framework as a replicable model for integrating deep contextual analysis into the iterative design of rural technologies. It argues that for sustainable solutions to be resilient, they must balance environmental goals with the specific socio-economic capabilities of the user community.

title: A Framework for Measuring Trust as a Software Architecture Quality Attribute
authors: Ricardo Gacitua, Carla Taramasco Toro
abstract: Trust is a critical yet underspecified concern in modern software-intensive systems, especially in regulated domains such as healthcare and finance. While widely discussed, trust remains weakly formalized at the software architecture level, leaving architects without systematic means to reason about or measure it. Existing trust-assessment approaches lack architectural focus, often addressing trust in an ad hoc manner that fails to capture its socio-technical, multidimensional, and lifecycle-dependent nature. This paper proposes a novel, comprehensive framework for conceptualising, measuring, and integrating trust as a first-class attribute of software architecture quality. The framework structures trust into six socio-technical dimensions and operationalises it using the Goal–Question-Metric (GQM) approach, yielding traceable, context-sensitive trust metrics linked to architectural decisions and lifecycle stages. The framework is empirically evaluated through in-depth case studies in healthcare information systems and AI-enabled credit scoring platforms, two trust-critical domains with stringent regulatory constraints. The results show that the framework supports systematic trust operationalisation, improves transparency, explainability, and regulatory alignment, and facilitates stakeholder-driven architectural reasoning across domains. This work positions trust as an explicit, measurable quality attribute and provides a structured, adaptable foundation for designing and evaluating trustworthy software architectures under evolving socio-technical conditions.

title: Graph Neural Network-Enhanced Multi-Objective Scheduling and Offloading for Hybrid Applications in Edge-Cloud Networks
authors: Yang Sun, Jitao Yi, Yuwei Bian, Meng Li, Pengbo Si, Fei Richard Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: The rapid proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) has driven the emergence of intelligent applications characterized by complex task dependencies and heterogeneous quality of service (QoS) requirements. Efficient coordination of edge-cloud resources to satisfy diverse QoS demands of hybrid applications remains a challenging problem. In this paper, we formulate the task scheduling and offloading problem in edge-cloud networks as a multi-constraint, multi-objective combinatorial optimization problem through a two-dimensional decision model that jointly determines task prioritization and server selection. To support adaptive decision-making in dynamic environments, the problem is further modeled as a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP). Based on this formulation, we propose a graph neural network-enhanced multi-objective reinforcement learning (GEMORL) framework that integrates a graph neural network (GNN) with multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL). The GNN hierarchically extracts task-level, application-level, and global-level representations from task-related and latency-related attributes of applications, providing expressive state embeddings for policy learning. A multi-objective policy gradient (MOPG) algorithm is employed to train an independent policy network for each objective, and a deterministic policy is derived via a scalarization method. Extensive simulations demonstrate that GEMORL achieves superior trade-offs among conflicting objectives and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline schemes across diverse network scenarios.

title: Towards AI-Native Ultra-Dense Networks: Cell Association via Multi-View Sensing
authors: Khoa Anh Ngo, Seungnyun Kim, Jihoon Moon, Byungju Lee, Jiao Wu, Byonghyo Shim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Millimeter wave (mmWave)/terahertz (THz) ultra-dense networks (UDNs) are anticipated to meet the technical performance requirements (TPRs) of immersive 6G applications. By deploying a large number of small base stations (SBSs), UDN can mitigate the severe attenuation in mmWave and THz frequency bands. To fully enjoy the benefits of mmWave/THz UDNs, ensuring line-of-sight (LoS) communication between SBSs and user equipments (UEs) is essential. However, identifying LoS conditions and determining proper cell associations require a contextual understanding of the surrounding environments, which conventional wireless measurement-based approaches may not readily provide. In this paper, we propose a novel cell association scheme for 6G mmWave/THz UDN based on vision sensing and computer vision (CV) technologies. The key idea of the proposed technique is to directly extract the positions and LoS conditions of UEs using multi-view RGB images. As the proposed technique operates without relying on wireless measurements, it eliminates the need for pilot transmissions and channel feedback, thereby reducing signaling overhead and latency significantly. Furthermore, by capturing UEs from multiple viewpoints, we can effectively detect them even in the presence of occlusions. From extensive numerical evaluations on realistic UDN scenarios, we show that the proposed scheme achieves 30% throughput gain over conventional cell association methods.

title: Neural Demodulation for Anti-Eavesdropping Embedded Waveforms in IoT Networks
authors: Chenpeng Shi, Wen-Bin Sun, Zhaolin Zhang, Haochen Liu, Jia Su, Ling Wang, Wei-Xiao Meng
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices often deliver sensitive sensing and control data over wireless links that are inherently broadcast, making passive over-the-air eavesdropping a persistent threat even for simple point-to-point transmissions. Many physical-layer security (PLS) techniques, however, rely on accurate channel knowledge, additional spatial degrees of freedom, or strong secrecy assumptions that are often hard to guarantee in practical IoT links. In this paper, we propose an anti-eavesdropping secure transmission scheme by deliberately embedding controllable artificial interference into a conventional BPSK waveform on the same carrier, thereby forming an interference-embedded waveform transmitted over an AWGN channel. To exploit the common architectural asymmetry in IoT systems, the legitimate receiver located at an IoT gateway/edge node employs an Anti-Eavesdropping Waveform Demodulator (AEWD), a ResNet–self-attention–BiLSTM neural demodulator trained end-to-end to recover bits directly from raw I/Q samples without explicit interference parameter estimation. Under identical channel conditions, conventional model-based receivers that perform deterministic interference suppression followed by demodulation incur substantial BER degradation and frequently exhibit interference-limited error floors. Extensive simulations across a wide range of SNR and SIR demonstrate that AEWD consistently outperforms classical baselines under both single-tone and multi-tone nonstationary interference. The results suggest that waveform-level interference embedding combined with a dedicated neural demodulator can create a practical receiver performance gap to strengthen confidentiality for IoT communications.

title: Accelerated Transfer-Entropy-Weighted ADMM for Learning Causal Directed Acyclic Graphs
authors: Jinren Zhang, Jinde Cao, Leszek Rutkowski, Xinli Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Learning directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational time series is a fundamental task in causal inference, yet commonly used objectives based on instantaneous scoring rules can fail to capture lagged or nonlinear dependencies. To address this issue, we propose a transfer entropy-weighted (TE-weighted) optimization problem with an accelerated ADMM solver, called ATEW–ADMM. The framework embeds empirical TE as prior edge weights in an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\ell \_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-regularized objective under a NOTEARS-style smooth acyclicity constraint. ATEW–ADMM combines a lazy static TE–ordered projection, over-relaxation, and adaptive penalty scheduling to reduce per-iteration cost, damp oscillations, and balance primal-dual progress. Under a Kurdyka-Łojasiewicz (KL) property and bounded penalty scheduling, we prove global convergence of ATEW–ADMM to a KKT point and establish an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(1/\sqrt{K})$</tex-math></inline-formula> best-iterate bound on the combined primal–dual residual. We further show that the lazy projection step satisfies a one-step energy decrease inequality for the augmented Lagrangian. In addition, we derive sufficient conditions for exact support recovery under a transfer-entropy separation margin. Experiments on synthetic data across multiple network sizes demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves higher recall, lower false discovery rates, and smaller structural Hamming distances than continuous optimization baselines based on instantaneous scores. Ablation studies show that each of the three accelerations contributes markedly to the overall performance. Overall, ATEW–ADMM provides a scalable and theoretically grounded approach for causal DAG recovery in complex dynamic systems.

title: Buffer Management for Timely Reconstruction: Lower Bounds and Near-Optimal Policies_supp1-3684353.pdf
authors: Sunjung Kang
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT sensing, monitoring, surveillance, and healthcare, where updates must be sufficiently fresh for real-time decisions while also informative enough to reconstruct past trajectories. We study this dual objective for a single-source system whose state follows a Wiener process and whose updates are generated at rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> and transmitted over a non-preemptive <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M/M/1/2$</tex-math></inline-formula> communication link, i.e., a single-server link with exponential service times of rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula> and a buffer that can store at most one waiting packet. Freshness is measured using the average peak Age of Information (AoI) and the time-average AoI, while historical fidelity is captured by a long-run reconstruction error (RE) derived from LMMSE interpolation. We first analyze a stationary randomized replacement (SRP) rule that, upon an arrival to a full system, keeps the fresh packet with probability <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and derive closed-form expressions for AoI and RE showing that AoI decreases monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> while RE varies non-monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>. In the heavy-traffic limit (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda \to \infty$</tex-math></inline-formula>), RE converges to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/(3\mu)$</tex-math></inline-formula> regardless of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, revealing a fundamental lower bound on reconstruction accuracy imposed by the service rate of the communication link. To further navigate the timeliness-fidelity trade-off, we introduce a threshold-based dropping policy that switches from keeping old to fresh packets after a tunable threshold <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Closed-form analysis and numerical experiments reveal regimes where thresholding reduces RE relative to keep-fresh with only a modest age penalty, providing practical guidelines for selecting <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula> across different load conditions.

title: Monolithic 3-D Integration of IAZO/Si-based 2T0C DRAM with RRAM for Monte Carlo Dropconnect Neural Networks
authors: Lin Bao, Bowen Jiang, Jiaqi Zhao, Haisu Zhang, Yingtong Ji, Yingchen Ji, Jingwei Sun, Yujie Chen, Fan Zhang, Cuimei Wang, Ling Liang, Zheng Zhou, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai, Shanguo Huang
abstract: Edge intelligent systems demand high energy efficiency and robustness, driving interest in Bayesian method-based AI algorithms such as Monte Carlo dropconnect neural networks (MCDNNs) for uncertainty quantification. However, in-memory computing (IMC) for MCDNN faces a critical endurance-retention trade-off. In this paper, we propose a 2T0C-1R IMC unit leveraging heterogeneous volatile/non-volatile memory to resolve this. Fabricated via monolithic 3-D (M3D) integration, it combines an oxide semiconductor write transistor for DRAM retention and a silicon read transistor for RRAM operation. The 2T0C-1R unit achieves >1000 s volatile retention and >4-bit non-volatile storage while meeting endurance requirements of random dropconnect. Experimental MCDNN validation shows a 33% calibration performance improvement over traditional neural networks, highlighting the 2T0C-1R array’s potential for reliable, energy-efficient edge AI applications.

title: Multi-Cycle Optimal Trajectory Control (OTC) with ZVS for LCC Converters Under Wide Range Load Transition
authors: Deshang Sha, Chao Gao, Xiangshuai Jia
abstract: Fast dynamic response is a critical attribute for LCC resonant converters. This paper proposes a multi-cycle optimal trajectory control (OTC) for LCC resonant converters under wide range load transition. The steady-state trajectories and characteristic equations are established. Then the muti-cycle control trajectories are designed to enable the resonant tank to reach the new steady state quickly. Meanwhile, zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) on for all switches are ensured during all OTC cycles. With real-time load detection, the target power can be quickly determined, and the required OTC cycles can be found in lookup tables. The effectiveness of the proposed control is verified on a 1-kW prototype.

title: Nightlights for Older Adults: Enhancing Bedroom-to-Bathroom Transitions With Community Resident Feedback
authors: Yue Luo, Aubrey Contreras, Susana Valdivia-Rivas, Yuhao Chen, Boyi Hu
abstract: This study aimed to inform nightlight design by analyzing older adults’ subjective feedback and kinematic responses during evening activities. Twenty-seven older adults (mean age: 81 ± 6 years; 14 females) participated in the study. Their preferences for nightlight illuminance and hue were surveyed, and gait and posture adaptations under two illuminance levels were analyzed using spatiotemporal comparisons, principal component analysis (PCA) based analysis, and waveform reconstruction. The results revealed that older adults preferred low illuminance over high illuminance, with warm white and cool colors as the favored choices for evening lighting. Significant differences were observed in spatiotemporal gait variables, particularly stride length, and walking speed, between the two illuminance levels. Differences in illuminance conditions also led to overall and localized posture changes, as demonstrated by the reconstruction of joint angles through PCA-based posture analysis. Additionally, correlations between spatiotemporal gait variables and principal component vectors revealed that PCA provides complementary spatial and temporal information, emphasizing its value alongside traditional gait analysis. These findings emphasize the importance of integrating older adults’ subjective preferences and objective kinematic observations into nightlight design. Nightlight systems that consider illuminance levels, color tones, and biomechanical efficiency can enhance confidence, comfort, and mobility during nighttime activities, contributing to the residential well-being of older adults.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)

title: Data Science in Education: Analyzing and Mitigating Bias from an Intersectional Perspective
authors: Carmen Beatriz Salinas De la Vega, Maria Victoria Tournier Nieto, Noelia Beltramelli, Lorena Etcheverry, Aiala Rosá
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The use of data science in education offers opportunities to improve educational trajectories, but it also entails the risk of reproducing social inequalities. In this work, we analyze a predictive system for academic dropout from a critical perspective, grounded in data feminism and intersectionality. We combine algorithmic fairness metrics with a case study, assessing the impact of sensitive attributes such as gender, age, parental occupation, and enrollment status. In addition, we explore bias mitigation techniques at different stages of the model. The results reveal significant disparities associated with certain attributes and show that some mitigation strategies improve fairness with a slight decrease in accuracy. This work proposes guidelines for designing fairer systems, emphasizing the need to incorporate intersectional approaches from the early stages of development.

title: Bayesian Learning Aided Doubly-Selective Simultaneous Sparse CSI Estimation in Multi-User MIMO Systems Relying on Orthogonal Time Frequency Space Modulation
authors: Meesam Jafri, Rahul Singh, Suraj Srivastava, Aditya K. Jagannatham, A. Chockalingam, Lajos Hanzo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: New delay and Doppler (DD)-domain channel state information (CSI) estimation schemes are conceived for multi-user (MU)-orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems, for both uplink (UL) and downlink (DL)-scenarios. An important feature of the proposed schemes is that the pilots are modulated and transmitted directly in the time-domain, leading to a low pilot-overhead, flexible training duration, and reduced complexity. These techniques, therefore, achieve a high bandwidth efficiency, since they eliminate the need for DD-domain guard bands corresponding to all the transmit antennas. Moreover, the DD-domain CSI of the MU-UL system is shown to be row-sparse in nature, while that of the MU-DL scenario exhibits block-sparsity. The multiple measurement vector (MMV)-based Bayesian learning (BL) technique for UL MIMO OTFS (MB-ULMO) is then proposed for exploiting the row-sparsity and for enhancing the UL channel estimation performance. Similarly, for the downlink, the block BL (BB) for DL MIMO OTFS (BB-DLMO) algorithm is proposed in order to exploit the block-sparsity for CSI recovery. Furthermore, the BCRLBs are derived for characterizing the MSEs of the proposed DD-domain channel estimation schemes. A low-complexity linear MMSE detector (LCLMD) is derived for data detection that is ideally suited for practical implementation since it does not require any prior information about the number of active multipath components at the receiver. Our simulation results demonstrate the improved performance of the proposed BL-based schemes both in comparison to extensions of other sparse schemes and to conventional estimation techniques.

title: Einstein from Noise: Statistical Analysis
authors: Amnon Balanov, Wasim Huleihel, Tamir Bendory
abstract:
“Einstein from noise” (EfN) is a prominent example of the model bias phenomenon, where systematic errors in the statistical model lead to spurious but consistent estimates. In the EfN experiment, one falsely believes that a set of observations contains noisy, shifted copies of a template signal (e.g., an Einstein image), whereas in reality, it contains only pure noise observations. To estimate the signal, the observations are first aligned with the template using cross-correlation and then averaged. Although the observations contain nothing but noise, it was recognized early on that this process produces a signal that resembles the template signal! This model bias pitfall was at the heart of a central scientific controversy about validation techniques in structural biology.

This paper provides a comprehensive statistical analysis of the EfN phenomenon above. We show that the Fourier phases of the EfN estimator (namely, the average of the aligned noise observations) converge to the Fourier phases of the template signal, thereby explaining the observed structural similarity. Additionally, we prove that the convergence rate of Fourier phases is inversely proportional to the number of noise observations and, in the high-dimensional regime, to the Fourier magnitudes of the template signal. Moreover, in the high-dimensional regime, the EfN estimator converges to a scaled version of the template signal. This work not only deepens the theoretical understanding of the EfN phenomenon but also highlights potential pitfalls in template matching techniques and emphasizes the need for careful interpretation of noisy observations across disciplines in engineering, statistics, physics, and biology.

title: Map-Based Indoor Localization Framework Under Constraint Radio Channel Resource
authors: Junhao Zheng, Zhiqiang Yuan, Wei Fan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Indoor localization has gained significant interest from industry and academia in recent years, due to a wide range of location-aware applications. However, most state-of-the-art radio positioning techniques depend heavily on abundant radio channel resources—such as large bandwidth and extensive antenna array aperture, to achieve high spatial and delay resolution in multipath extraction—which are often impractical in real-world deployments. This letter introduces a novel map-based approach that overcomes this limitation by actively integrating indoor environment geometry into signal processing. Low-resolution multipath components (MPCs) obtained under constrained resources are actively validated and refined using map-derived virtual target and specular reflections, filtering out spurious paths that conventional methods cannot resolve. The refined, high-confidence MPCs are then fused to achieve centimeter-level localization accuracy, even with minimal radio channel resources. Experiments using a bandwidth of 200 MHz and 20 antenna elements achieve localization errors of 5.1 cm in line-of-sight and 16.8 cm in obstructed-line-of-sight scenarios, respectively. Compared with state-of-the-art benchmark schemes used a bandwidth of 2 GHz and 720 antenna elements, the proposed framework achieves smaller errors while requiring significantly fewer resources, demonstrating a resource-efficient, geometry-aware pathway for indoor localization.

title: A Single-Layer Wideband CP Filtering Reflectarray With Wide Out-of-Band Rejection via Intrinsic Orthogonality of CMs
authors: Ming Fu, Xinhuai Wang, Zhenlin Li, Ying Mei, Yin Li, Yin Xu, Xiaowei Shi, Lei Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article proposes a novel design method for a circularly polarized filtering reflectarray (CPFRA). The method first exploits the inherent orthogonality of characteristic modes (CMs) to convert circularly polarized (CP) reflection responses into the modal characteristic angle difference (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>CA) of the reflecting cell. The <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>CA is then correlated with the modal resonance frequency difference (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta f_{\mathrm {res}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), which can be effectively tuned via characteristic mode analysis (CMA). With the aid of CMA, multiple appropriate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta f_{\mathrm {res}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> are obtained to synthesize the desired CP filtering reflection response. For next-generation low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite applications (e.g., Starlink), three design strategies for the wideband CPFRA with wide out-of-band rejection are further formulated using the proposed method and applied to a single-layer right-triangular patch reflectarray (RA) cell. By adjusting the locations of six grounded vias loaded near the three edges of the cell, multiple modal current path lengths are modified to obtain the target <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta f_{\mathrm {res}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Ultimately, the fabricated RA exhibits a 3-dB gain bandwidth of 28.86% and a 3-dB axial ratio (AR) bandwidth of 32% in the passband, along with 15-dB rejection bandwidths of 37.92% and 33.84% in the lower and upper stopbands, respectively. These results validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed design method and strategies.

title: 70 Years of the IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management: Trends and Patterns in Scholarly Publishing
authors: Mohammad Sadegh Khorshidi, José M. Merigó, Dilek Cetindamar, T.M. Choi
abstract: This study presents a comprehensive bibliometric and science mapping analysis of the IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management (IEEE TEM), one of the longest-standing journals in the field of technology, innovation and engineering management. Renamed in 1963, IEEE TEM has evolved from its earlier forms as the Transactions of the IRE Professional Group on Engineering Management (1954) and the IRE Transactions on Engineering Management (1955-1962). Drawing on data from Web of Science and Scopus (1954-2024), the study examines publication and citation trends, leading authors, institutions, and countries, as well as the journal's knowledge structure and thematic evolution. Standard bibliometric techniques-including co-citation, bibliographic coupling, and keyword co-occurrence (VOSviewer; Bibliometrix)-were employed to uncover intellectual foundations, collaborative networks, and emerging research frontiers. Findings highlight the journal's strong international orientation, increasing interdisciplinarity across business management, industrial engineering, computer science, and information systems, and a marked surge in activity since 2020. Analyses of influential documents, institutions, and topic clusters reveal IEEE TEM's central role in advancing research on innovation, project management, digital transformation, sustainability, and supply chain systems. The paper summarizes IEEE TEM's historical development and current standing and distills evidence-based implications from the reported indicators and maps.

title: Deterministic Kinematic Synchronization for High-Speed Manipulation of Non-Rigid Substrates
authors: Santosh R. Yadav
abstract: The automated manipulation of non-rigid substrates—such as textiles and flexible films—presents a fundamental challenge in industrial robotics due to the stochastic nature of pneumatic actuation and the geometric paradox of mapping circular seams to linear processing heads. This research proposes an integrated mechatronic solution utilizing a synthesized reciprocating linkage and a Jacobian-based coordinate mapping strategy. By replacing stochastic fluid-power with deterministic kinematic synchronization, we enable sub-millisecond control cycles (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<1 ~\text{ms}$</tex>). Experimental validation in a high-speed sewing application at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3,000 \text{RPM}$</tex> demonstrates an 85% reduction in path-tracking error compared to traditional PB systems. This architecture provides a scalable framework for high-throughput, autonomous manufacturing in sectors previously limited by material variability.

title: Human-Centered Explainable AI for Security Enhancement: A Deep Intrusion Detection Framework
authors: Md Muntasir Jahid Ayan, Md. Shahriar Rashid, Tazzina Afroze Hassan, Hossain Md. Mubashshir Jamil, Mahbubul Islam, Lisan Al Amin, Rupak Kumar Das, Farzana Akter, Faisal Quader
abstract: The increasing complexity and frequency of cyberthreats demand intrusion detection systems (IDS) that are not only accurate but also interpretable. This paper presented a novel IDS framework that integrated Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to enhance transparency in deep learning models. The framework was evaluated experimentally using benchmark dataset NSL-KDD demonstrating superior performance compared to traditional IDS and black-box deep learning (DL) models. The proposed approach combined Convolutional Neural Network (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for capturing temporal dependencies in traffic sequences. Our DL results showed that both CNN and LSTM reached 0.99 for accuracy whereas LSTM outperformed CNN at macro average precision, recall and F-1 score. For weighted average precision, recall and F-1 score, both model scored almost similar. To ensure interpretability, XAI model SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) was incorporated, enabling security analysts to understand and validate model decisions. Some notable influential features were srv_serror_rate, dst_host_srv_serror_rate, and serror_rate for both models as pointed out by SHAP. We also conducted a trust-focused expert survey based on IPIP6 and Big Five personality traits via an interactive user interface (UI) to evaluate the system's reliability and usability. This work highlighted the potential of combining performance and transparency in cybersecurity solutions and recommends future enhancements through adaptive learning for real-time threat detection.

title: Data-Driven Insights & Predictive Modeling in Fitness Tracking
authors: Nabeel M Sheikh, Amit Kumar, Vansh Agarwal
abstract: Heart rate variability (HRV) is increasingly used in wearable-based fitness tracking due to its sensitivity to stress, recovery, and cardiovascular function. This study examined whether body composition metrics (BMI and body fat percentage) collected manually and via wearables are consistent, and whether HRV features offer additional insight into individual health trends. A review of prior literature highlighted both the promise and challenges of integrating HRV into predictive modeling, especially regarding individual variability and external influences. The purpose of this study was to assess the reliability of wearable data and evaluate whether HRV varies systematically with body composition. Using datasets from Kaggle and Harvard Dataverse, we analyzed biometric, activity, and HRV metrics from 35 participants. Results showed that wearable-reported body composition data closely matched manual entries, supporting their use in health tracking. However, no strong associations were found between body composition and HRV features. In contrast, HRV varied significantly over time, while BMI remained stable, highlighting HRV's utility for personalized monitoring. These findings support the reliability of wearable sensors and reinforce the value of HRV as a dynamic health indicator for individualized wellness insights. These findings suggest that HRV may serve as a more responsive and individualized metric for real-time health feedback, offering practical applications for adaptive fitness programs and stress management interventions.

title: Semantic Segmentation for Pest Detection on Organic Banana Leaves
authors: Pierina García, Javier Machacuay, José Manrique, William Ipanaqué
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Pest recognition in agriculture currently relies on manual inspections. This approach is subjective, time-consuming, and difficult to scale. In precision agriculture, research studies address this issue for different types of plants by leveraging machine learning models to identify pests in classification and segmentation settings. However, to the best of our knowledge, there exists little or no research on banana leaf pest disease segmentation. To address this gap, this research work develops two segmentation models: one based on the U-Net architecture and the other on the SegFormer architecture, both of which are well known in the literature. The best model, U-Net, achieved an average Dice coefficient of 0.9532. It outperformed SegFormer in accuracy but required higher computational costs. These results and the visual outputs confirm the viability of deep learning for accurate and automated banana pest segmentation.

title: Design of a Solar-Based Energy-Efficient IoT-PWM-Peltier Operated Advanced HVAC-AHU Cold Storage Without VFD Technique
authors: Sourish Mitra, Sandip Roy, Rajesh Bose
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Proper preservation of daily consumables like fruits and vegetables is essential for human health, as it helps extend their shelf life while maintaining their nutritional value without compromising taste. On this occasion, cold storage plays a vital role for preserving these consumables and making them available during the off-season. Conventional HVAC system of cold storage suffers from high energy consumption due to Compressor-Condenser-Evaporator based AHU mechanism, which provides non-adjustable cooling at a constant speed, even demand becomes low, manual controls that lack real-time adaptability, high operational costs, inconsistent accuracy, high carbon emissions and overcooling issues. Modern VFD equipped AHU can solve above problems for some extent but still suffers from solar un-compatibility with grid dependency, high harmonic distortion, more power consumption due to inclusion of external inverters for converting 3 phase-grid AC to DC that added energy loss and EMI filtering required for reducing high EMI, high maintenance cost, moderated carbon emission with heat production, non remote accessibility etc. In this proposal, solar powered IoT based PWM-peltier operated advanced AHU cooling unit is designed for achieving better environment sustainability with less carbon emission, implementing CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> -VOC-excess humidity removal strategy and energy efficient dynamic PWM cooling intensity adjustment without measuring airflow rate.

title: Design and Simulation of Terahertz Antennas for Biomedical Sensing: A Comprehensive Performance Analysis
authors: Arpita Santra, Maitreyi Ray Kanjilal, Moumita Mukherjee, Puja Paul
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Terahertz (THz) antennas are increasingly recognized for their transformative potential in biomedical applications, owing to their ability to interact with biological tissues without inducing ionizing effects. This paper presents a comprehensive analytical study of THz antenna systems, emphasizing design architectures, modelling methodologies, simulation protocols, and key performance indicators such as gain, radiation efficiency, and specific absorption rate (SAR). Optimization strategies are explored through advanced material engineering and the integration of machine learning techniques to enhance antenna performance and adaptability. The relevance of these antennas is demonstrated across diverse biomedical domains, including high-resolution medical imaging, non-invasive diagnostic sensing, and wearable health monitoring systems, highlighting their pivotal role in nextgeneration healthcare technologies.

title: From Prototype to Production: A Checklist for Production-Ready AI Focused on Data Quality, Monitoring, and Human Oversight
authors: Femi Oke, Anjum Razzaque, Abdullahi Ibiyeye
abstract: Many AI projects face failure since the data is messy, since AI is not monitored properly, or possibly end-users are not involved enough. Building such a prototype appears straightforward, whereas a production-ready system is more challenging. For instance, Machine Learning (ML) projects fail to transition from promising prototypes to reliable products due to issues with data quality, lack of continuous monitoring, or insufficient human oversight. We present a clear checklist of the current research gap for developing “production-ready” AI systems across three critical areas: data quality, continuous monitoring, and human oversight. Our work offers best practices for each area, using clean, unbiased, and representative data, monitoring models for performance and data changes, and incorporating human-in-the-loop for safety and accountability. Such a checklist helps identify early risks, mitigate technical problems, and promote trust in AI. Case studies and references help us establish best practices to bridge the gap from prototype to production research.

title: Cough Detection for Smart-Home Health Monitoring: Room-Scale Edge Acoustic Sensing with Mel-Spectrogram Classification
authors: Jelan Womack, Fariha Alam, Muhammad Hassan Tanveer, Yusun Chang, Razvan Cristian Voicu
abstract: Smart homes are increasingly studied as sensing platforms that can deliver context-aware services and health monitoring without requiring constant user attention. Cough is a clinically informative symptom spanning respiratory infection, asthma exacerbation, allergies, and exposure to environmental irritants; changes in cough activity can support earlier screening, improve symptom timeline fidelity, and enable longitudinal studies outside the clinic. This paper presents a room-scale cough detection sensor for smart-home health monitoring that emphasizes low-latency, privacy-aware edge processing. The system continuously estimates sound level from a far-field four-channel directional microphone array, triggers short-window recording only after a threshold crossing (74 dB), converts captured audio into a mel spectrogram, and classifies events using an ESC-50-based label space with cough as the target class. All feature extraction and inference were performed locally on an embedded edge compute module, enabling data minimization and avoiding cloud upload by default. We evaluated the feasibility at 1 m, 3 m, and 5 m from the sensor with uncovered and covered-mouth coughs. Across 18 staged trials, the prototype achieved 77.8 % cough classification accuracy, with stable performance at 1-3 m and degradation at 5 m. Misclassifications were driven primarily by transient-event similarity and dataset/domain mismatch between curated training clips and in-room acoustics. Beyond feasibility, we provide a deployment-oriented analysis covering privacy threats, event semantics for smart-home integration, and a road map toward research-grade validation, including binary cough-vs-non-cough modeling, in-situ fine-tuning, adaptive triggering, and multi-participant evaluation.

title: A Virtual Braille Keyboard Prototype for Accessible Text Input on Smartphones
authors: Larissa Braga De Carvalho, Afonso Ueslei Da Fonseca, Thamer Horbylon Nascimento, Eduardo Amorim, Juliana Felix, Fabrizzio Soares
abstract: Text input on smartphones remains a significant challenge for users with visual impairments, particularly regarding privacy. Despite advances in accessibility, private and efficient text entry is a critical challenge for blind users. While tactile interfaces are promising, hey require robust software infrastructure. This paper presents the architectural redesign and implementation of Braille Écran, transforming into a native Android Input Method Service (IME). Unlike previous version restricted to messaging, this update enables systemwide Braille input. We introduce a novel Connect interaction mode to complement standard touch, leveraging a heuristicbased confirmation algorithm. By establishing an extensible, open-source framework, this work overcomes legacy limitations and provides the necessary foundation for future research into hybrid tactile-digital interfaces. Main contributions include the IME migration, a dual-mode interaction model, and a modular extensible architecture.

title: Control-Aware Fault Propagation and Latency Analysis in Distributed Automotive Electrification Architectures for ADAS Applications
authors: Abdul Salam Abdul Karim
abstract: Distributed automotive electrification architectures increasingly support ADAS functions through tightly coupled sensor-actuator loops that depend on deterministic timing. In practice, faults can propagate across power and communication domains, and latency can accumulate across zonal ECUs, gateways, and electrification controllers, affecting closed-loop stability and safe actuation. This paper presents a control-aware architectural analysis of fault propagation and latency behavior in distributed automotive electrification architectures for ADAS applications. The study evaluates how timing margin erosion, synchronization drift, and cascading faults influence control robustness across representative centralized, modular, and hybrid topologies. A system-level framework is used to relate architectural decisions (partitioning, fault containment boundaries, and communication determinism) to control-relevant outcomes such as actuation delay and stability margin degradation. The paper then provides practical design recommendations—including hierarchical control partitioning, latency-aware fault management, and hybrid communication strategies—to improve resilience under realistic timing and fault-propagation constraints. The results support the need for control-centric architectural evaluation as electrified platforms scale toward higher automation ADAS use cases

title: MIRA: Mutual Information-Based Reconstruction Attacks on Split Learning
authors: Linbo Zhou
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Split learning (SL) has been proposed as a lightweight alternative to federated learning, as it retains raw data on the client side and exchanges only intermediate activations with the server. Nevertheless, recent studies have shown that such smashed features may still reveal sensitive information about the underlying inputs. In this work, we introduce a new reconstruction attack against SL from an informationtheoretic perspective, termed the Mutual-Information Guided Reconstruction Attack (MIRA). MIRA first trains a substitute client on an auxiliary dataset and aligns its representation space with that of the victim client by maximizing a contrastive lower bound on the conditional mutual information between their activations. After establishing this cross-domain feature coupling, a reconstruction network is trained offline to invert substitute features to the input space and is subsequently applied directly to the recorded smashed data. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR10 using VGG16 demonstrate that MIRA achieves high-quality reconstructions across different cut points and remains effective under limited auxiliary data, underscoring substantial privacy risks in SL under an honest-but-curious server.

title: Improving the Small Object Detection Algorithm for UAV Aerial Images Based on Yolov12n
authors: Ling Wen, Caihong Ma, Shengzhou Ma
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the low detection accuracy of small objects in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery caused by the large number of small targets, complex background interference, and significant scale variation, this paper proposes MCAP-YOLO, an optimized detection algorithm based on the YOLOv12n model. An improved MCAC2f module is designed based on the principle of Monte Carlo random sampling. During training, randomly selected pooling scales are introduced to break the constraints of fixed region partitioning and alleviate cross-scale feature discontinuities of small objects. Meanwhile, Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) channel attention is employed to adaptively reweight informative channels, effectively enhancing discriminative small object features while suppressing background noise. Furthermore, inspired by the ASF-YOLO framework, the neck network is reconstructed by integrating key modules including Zoom_cat, SSFF, and CPAM, enabling efficient multi-scale feature fusion and joint channel-spatial attention enhancement. To further improve the detection of extremely small targets, a dedicated P2 detection head is introduced. In addition, the Wise Intersection over Union version 3 (WIoU-v3) loss is adopted to mitigate the influence of low-quality samples through dynamic gradient gain allocation. Experimental results demonstrate that on the VisDrone2019 dataset, mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95 are improved by 5.7% and 4.4%, respectively. On the DOTA dataset, mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95 improve by 2.1% and 2.3%, respectively, validating the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed algorithm

title: Research on an Improved SimpleNet-Based Algorithm for Detecting Surface Defects in High-Voltage Equipment on Train Roofs
authors: Chunguang Zhang, Qing Zhang, Lifang Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: High-voltage equipment on high-speed train roofs (including pantographs, disconnectors, main circuit breakers, etc.) is continuously exposed to complex outdoor environments. Factors such as wind, sand, rain, snow, and vibration can cause exterior defects like casing damage and component deformation. As core components for power transmission and safety control, the operational status of this equipment directly impacts train safety, necessitating efficient and precise defect detection technology.To address the limitations of existing detection algorithms in identifying subtle defects, this study proposes a roof-mounted high-voltage equipment appearance defect detection algorithm based on an improved SimpleNet. The algorithm integrates the Inception-NeXt module into SimpleNet, optimizing the feature extraction structure to enhance the capture of multi-scale defect features.
