It also introduces the SimAM (Simple Attention Module) attention mechanism to strengthen the model's focus on defective areas and enhance feature response.Experimental results using a proprietary high-speed rail roof-mounted high-voltage equipment fault dataset (CRH-Comp-Fault) demonstrate that this algorithm achieves a component fault detection accuracy (Acc) of 95.8%, a false positive rate (FPR) of 2.0%, and an inference speed (FPS) of 53. Compared to the standalone SimpleNet, the Acc improves by 16.9% and the FPR decreases by 11.0%. This provides an efficient and reliable technical solution for intelligent maintenance of high-speed rail components.

title: Remote Monitoring, Control, and Update Triggering Via LoRaWAN in an Edge-Based Vehicle Counting System
authors: Fabian Palacios-Pereira, Marcelo Palma, Mario Arzamendia, Derlis O. Gregor, Diego Palacios, Ariel Fleitas, Hernán Lezcano
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents an autonomous, low-power, and scalable vehicle counting and monitoring system, both in terms of network and functionality, that does not depend on a continuous internet connection for its operation. The proposal integrates LoRa communication for long-distance transmission of counting data with high energy efficiency, edge processing using an NVIDIA Jetson Nano running a YOLOv8n-based detection model, and remote feature update capability through the decentralized InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) using hash addressing. The system implements bidirectional data flow over LoRa technology, allowing both the sending of information (counts, measurements) and the receiving of control commands (remote reboots, hash updates). Field evaluations demonstrate the system's ability to detect and classify vehicles in real-time with low latency, maintain low power consumption, and update operational scripts with 100 % success in test scenarios. On average, 8.92 FPS were achieved during inference and a full update deployment time of less than 1.2 minutes, validating the effectiveness, resilience, and functional scalability of this architecture for vehicle monitoring in diverse environments.

title: Unsupervised Telemetry Anomaly Detection for CubeSats Across OPSS-SAT and SatNOGS Data
authors: Abeer Ahmed, Anandi Dutta, Blagoy Rangelov
abstract: This paper investigates the use of a dense autoencoder for unsupervised anomaly detection in CubeSat telemetry, motivated by the growing need for autonomous health monitoring in low-cost space missions. The approach is evaluated on two datasets: the OPSSAT-AD benchmark, which provides labeled telemetry for quantitative comparison, and real onorbit telemetry from the StratoSat TK-1 CubeSat collected through the SatNOGS ground-station network. On OPSSATAD, the dense autoencoder consistently outperforms classical unsupervised baselines, including Isolation Forest and Principal Component Analysis, achieving higher precision, recall, F1-score and area under the ROC curve (AUC). The same model is then applied without retraining SatNOGS-collected data to examine how anomaly detection methods trained on onboard spacecraft data behave under different data acquisition conditions. When applied to real telemetry, the model highlights periods of anomalous behavior and deviations in power-system signals, while feature-level reconstruction error analysis indicates which subsystems contribute most strongly to each detected event.

title: Analysis of the Cycling Performance of rSOC System Based on a Multi-Scale Model: Considering Electrochemical Performance Degradation
authors: Guoqiang Liu, Jingxuan Peng, Zhen Wang, Shiwei Zeng, Haoyu Zeng, Xi Li
publish: 2025 14th International Conference on Power Science and Engineering (ICPSE)
abstract: Frequent electricity-hydrogen conversion is the primary application scenario for reversible solid oxide cell (rSOC) systems integrated into microgrids. Revealing the cycling degradation characteristics of rSOC is beneficial for implementing targeted control strategies. This paper establishes a multi-time-scale model for rSOC, which takes into account nickel particle coarsening at the microscopic level, the evolution of three-phase interfaces at the mesoscopic scale, and macroscopic electrochemical performance. Based on this model, the degradation characteristics of nickel particles in both electrolyzer and fuel cell modes are analyzed. Finally, the evolution of the electrochemical performance of the rSOC system during long-time-scale continuous switching is examined in combination with typical photovoltaic duration. The research findings provide valuable references for formulating health management strategies for rSOC systems that consider electrochemical performance degradation.

title: Efficient CPU Burst Forecasting with Low-Latency Machine Learning Models
authors: Md Nazmus Shakib, Md Ashraf Hossain Ifty, Md. Shihab Uddin, Rahul Bhadani
abstract: Efficient CPU scheduling in operating systems depends on the accurate prediction of process CPU burst times. Traditional methods such as exponential averaging (EA) provide simple estimates but often perform poorly under dynamic and heterogeneous workloads. While prior studies have explored machine learning (ML) techniques for CPU burst prediction, their computational overhead and inference latency, critical factors for real-time scheduling, are rarely evaluated. This study investigates the use of machine learning (ML) models, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex>-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision tree (DT), random forest (RF), and multilayer perceptron (MLP), to predict CPU burst times using only submission-time attributes from the GWA-T-4 AuverGrid dataset. These ML models are evaluated against the conventional EA method. In addition to predictive performance, we assess the models' inference latency to examine their suitability for realtime scheduling. While KNN achieves high predictive accuracy, its relatively high inference time may constrain its use in latency-sensitive environments. Our experimental results show that all ML models outperform EA across key metrics, with the decision tree offering the most favorable balance between accuracy (mean absolute error (MAE) of 3514.09 and correlation coefficient (CC) of 0.84) and efficiency (0.0033 ms per sample). Confidence interval analysis and hyperparameter tuning are also conducted to assess model robustness. These findings demonstrate that latency-aware ML-based burst prediction is feasible and essential for practical scheduling systems.

title: Enhancing SNN Efficiency: Time-Step Compression with Plastic Threshold and Weighted Spike Optimization
authors: Xiao Yang, Fan Zhang, Yanzhao Gao, Xiaofeng Qi, Zhengyu Liu, Kai Han, Shuaikang Hou, Yu Shao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have attracted considerable attention due to their event-driven computation characteristics and high energy-efficiency potential on neuromorphic hardware. However, model training presents considerable challenges, frequently necessitating long time-steps for optimal performance, consequently inducing significant inference latency that thereby constrains its real-time capability. Among existing direct training methods, a fixed firing threshold tends to cause neuron “death” or “saturation,” especially under short time-steps, significantly degrading information representation capability. To address this problem, we proposes a neuron model with threshold plasticity (TPLIF) and constructs a high-performance short-time-step Spiking Transformer network combined with an exponentially weighted spike firing rate estimation. The TPLIF neuron dynamically adjusts its threshold based on the actual firing rate of the neuron, maintaining network activation stability. The weighted spike mechanism assigns higher weights to recent spikes, improving information representation efficiency under time-steps compression while preserving spike sparsity and event-driven properties. Experiments are conducted on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets. The proposed method achieves accuracies of 95.41 % and 78.86 %, respectively, using only 2-4 time-steps, outperforming existing mainstream approaches. Particularly when the compressing the time-steps from 4 to 2, the accuracy drop slope on CIFAR-100 is only -1.22, markedly superior to Spikformer (-2.57) and TET (-1.60), demonstrating enhanced temporal. Ablation studies further validate the critical contributions of the plastic threshold and weighted spike mechanism to performance improvement. This study provides an effective solution framework for the practical deployment of low-latency, high-efficiency SNNs.

title: Classification of COVID-19 From Chest X-Rays Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Juan Pablo Vásconez, Diana Parra Meléndez, Álvaro Javier Prado-Romo, Pablo Torres, Ingrid Nicole Vásconez, Oscar Gonzales-Zurita
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Artificial intelligence, machine learning, and deep learning have opened potential solutions for medical diagnostics in recent years. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are particularly useful and efficient in interpreting medical images. These models excel at extracting patterns in X-rays and CT scans, facilitating rapid and reliable diagnosis of respiratory conditions such as tuberculosis, cancers, and otorhinolaryngological disorders. This work focuses on training and evaluating 16 high-performance CNNs in chest X-rays for COVID-19 classification. The proposed CNNs are AlexNet, DarkNet-19, DarkNet-53, DenseNet-201, GoogLeNet, Inception-ResNet-V2, InceptionV3, MobileNet-V2, NasNet-Mobile, NasNet-Large, ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, ShuffleNet, VGG-19, and Xception. The dataset comprised 809 images for training (95%) and 42 for testing 5%. ResNet-50 emerged as the top-performing model, achieving the highest accuracy (83%), precision (94%), and specificity (95%), alongside robust recall (71%) and F1-score (81%). This demonstrates that the ResNet-50 CNN model is the best fit for the proposed dataset's distribution. These findings contribute to creating a path for developing accurate and reliable diagnostic technologies in medical applications.

title: A Unified ML/RL Framework for Intraday Trading with Risk-Aware Evaluation and Explainability
authors: Lucy Jomo, Sanchita Ghose, Abraham Zepeda, Shivangi Narayan
abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes increasingly integrated into financial markets, robust and risk-aware evaluation of learning-based trading strategies is essential. This paper presents a unified intraday trading framework for comparing regression, classification, and reinforcement learning models based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) using 5-minute QQQ price data. All models operate within a shared end-to-end pipeline with identical data preprocessing, featureengineered technical indicators, trading rules, and evaluation metrics, enabling fair comparison across learning paradigms. Trading signals are generated as BUY, SELL, and HOLD decisions and evaluated using risk-adjusted backtesting metrics, including Sharpe ratio and maximum drawdown. Monte Carlo simulation-based robustness analysis is further employed to characterize portfolio value and drawdown distributions, with emphasis on tail-risk behavior. The results reveal trade-offs between profitability, stability, and robustness across supervised and reinforcement learning approaches, offering practical insights for risk-aware short-horizon AI trading systems.

title: Technical Evaluation and Challenges of LoRa Technology Applied to Smart Agriculture 5.0
authors: Héctor Kaschel, Sergio Cordero, Carlos Navarro, Pablo Adasme
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This research paper presents a specific technical overview of the application of LoRa (Long Range) technology, one of the most prominent and widely used technologies in wireless sensor networks (WSN), classified as low-power wide-area networks (LPWAN) due to its versatility and robustness, as well as its wide coverage, low energy consumption, and low cost. The different applications of LoRa technology in the IoT are presented, specifically in location tracking, whether in urban, semi-urban, or rural areas. The focus of this work is to compare the common parameters of LoRa technology, which allows for timely and correct decisions to be made in the selection of technology, according to requirements and in the context of smart agriculture 5.0. The experimental work carried out consisted of using five TTGO T-BEAM ESP32 boards for the implementation of the network and the Thing Network (TTN) platform, which allowed the devices to be configured and location and geographical coordinate information to be obtained. This was then confirmed and verified with Google Earth Pro, highlighting the accuracy of the GW location and that of the LoRa node. Finally, the results obtained in terms of location, coverage, location map, propagation and transmission parameters are presented, thus obtaining the power balance diagrams that demonstrate the technical feasibility of the radio link in terms of power levels, which meant validation when making comparisons with other research.

title: IoT-Based Monitoring of Radio Base Stations: Design, Implementation, and Future Enhancements
authors: Jorge Luis Cruz Vergara, Brayan Hernan Ligña Estévez, Román Alcides Lara Cueva
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Efficient monitoring and management of radio base stations are critical for ensuring continuous communication in remote and urban areas. In such sense, the aim of this paper is to design and implement an intelligent monitoring system for radio base stations, leveraging advanced Internet of Things technologies, databases, and visualization tools. The proposed system integrates sensors connected to a microcontroller, enabling continuous measurement of critical parameters such as AC/DC voltages, temperature, humidity, and door status. The collected data is transmitted to a server configured with Node- RED, InfluxDB, and Telegram, ensuring secure storage, efficient processing, and immediate notifications of anomalous conditions. Near Real-time data visualization is facilitated by interactive dashboards in Grafana, providing comprehensive monitoring and proactive decision-making. A six-day monitoring period revealed significant fluctuations in environmental and electrical parameters, underscoring the absence of appropriate electronic regulation mechanisms within the equipment room. These findings highlight the need for infrastructural enhancements to safeguard critical telecommunications equipment.

title: Data Science and Privacy in Secure Predictive Analysis with Homomorphic Encryption and Partial Anonymization
authors: Alan Rodrigo Corini Guarachi, Juan Pablo Vásconez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Homomorphic encryption (HE) is an advanced cryptographic technique that allows algebraic operations to be performed directly on encrypted data without needing to decrypt it first. This property ensures data confidentiality throughout the entire computation process, making it a valuable tool in environments where privacy is critical. Partial anonymization of sensitive attributes that could facilitate re-identification of individuals mitigates risks associated with personal data analysis. By selectively removing or masking certain identifiers, data utility for analytical tasks is preserved without compromising subject identity. Combining homomorphic encryption with partial anonymization techniques enables a new paradigm of secure predictive analytics. It becomes possible to train machine learning models or perform inference directly on protected data without ever exposing it at any stage of the workflow.

title: An Engineered System Concept for Early Detection of Dry-Type Transformer Failures
authors: Bhushan Lohar, John T. Wade, James Lott, Reid Onderdonk, Angeline Welsh, Bryan Nguyen, Eden Fillingim, Asia Frey
abstract: Dry-type transformers serve as critical nodes in modern electrical distribution networks and utilize solid dielectric insulation to maintain operational integrity. Throughout their operational lifecycle, these insulation systems experience thermal and electrical stressors that precipitate significant dielectric degradation. A primary precursor to catastrophic failure involves the onset of corona discharge. This localized partial discharge initiates impact ionization in the surrounding air. The process creates a self-sustaining plasma field that chemically and thermally erodes the insulation and accelerates the progression toward a total dielectric strike-over. This paper proposes a conceptual engineered system for the early detection of impending transformer failure. The system monitors the concentration of gaseous by-products, specifically Ozone (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>) and Nitrogen Oxides (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$</tex>), which the corona ionization process generates. By utilizing active air-aspirating technology, the proposed system offers a non-invasive and EMI-immune diagnostic alternative to traditional Ultra-High Frequency and thermal monitoring techniques. The study evaluates two primary integration strategies. The first strategy interfaces with Fire Alarm Control Panels for life-safety supervision. The second strategy utilizes Building Automation Systems via protocols such as BACnet or Modbus for real-time trend analysis. This framework shifts the maintenance paradigm from reactive to condition-based monitoring. It provides facility managers with a strategic response window to mitigate business interruption and prevent cascading infrastructure failures.

title: A $10-\text{Gb} / \mathrm{s}, 1.2-\text{pJ} /$ Bit Receiver for Optical Wireless Communication
authors: Zhijie Wu, Wenjin Dong, Yubin Lao, Shaolin Liao, Xianbo Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper presents an optical receiver to support <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10-\text{Gb} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> optical wireless communication (OWC). The receiver employs a shunt-feedback transimpedance amplifier (SF-TIA) based on a three-stage inverter topology to achieve low noise and high bandwidth. In addition, a three-stage post-amplifier (PA) with active feedback is employed to perform single-ended-to-differential conversion and to enhance the signal swing. An output buffer (OB) incorporating inductive peaking is designed to drive a 50Ω load. The entire receiver is implemented in a standard 65 nm CMOS process. Simulation results show that the designed receiver achieves a transimpedance gain of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$72.9 ~\text{dB} \Omega$</tex> and a −3 dB bandwidth of 6.02 GHz, while operating at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 ~\text{Gb} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> with an energy efficiency of 1.2 pJ/bit.

title: Linear Quadratic Integrator &Sliding-Mode Control Comparison for a Transfemoral Prosthetic Knee
authors: Antonio Gallardo Montti
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Achieving safe and energy-efficient gait in transfemoral prostheses demands precise joint-angle tracking under uncertain dynamic conditions. This study systematically compares three discrete time control strategies for a planar double-pendulum model of a prosthetic knee subjected to a 1-radian step input at the hip and knee: (i) a baseline Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller, (ii) a Linear Quadratic Integrator (LQI) that augments the linearized statespace with integral action, and (iii) a first-order Sliding Mode Controller (SMC) that considers an exponential reaching law. All controllers are tuned on identical plant dynamics and a sampling time of 5 ms, in MATLAB. Using a 2 % settling band, the PID controller exhibits <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \mathbf{2 0 \%}$</tex> overshoot with persistent oscillations; LQI eliminates steady-state error and settles between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 3 3 - 1. 5 0 ~ s}$</tex> across joints; and the SMC attains <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.92 ~\mathrm{s}$</tex> settling on both joints with zero overshoot and the gentlest torque profiles. These findings demonstrate that SMC offers a high-performance, lowcomplexity alternative to classical and optimal control techniques, paving the way for more responsive and resilient transfemoral prostheses.

title: A High-Performance 5G/6G Framework for Deep-Space Missions with Multi-Level AI
authors: Jeffrey Wallace, Miroslav Vukovic, Toni Karimovic, William Edwards
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)
abstract: This paper presents a high-performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G} / \mathbf{6 G}$</tex> communication framework for deep-space missions, integrating multi-level artificial intelligence (AI) to support autonomous and resilient operations beyond Earth. Unlike conceptual proposals, this framework builds upon a series of award-winning prototypes and extensive field deployments, including U.S. Army and Indo-PACOM demonstrations, with proven results in low-latency edge processing, network slicing, and distributed AI. The paper details how these technologies, validated in terrestrial and military environments, can be adapted for space applications to address challenges such as extreme latency, bandwidth constraints, environmental resilience, and mission autonomy. We present operational case studies and quantitative metrics demonstrating significant improvements in mission responsiveness, data prioritization, and autonomous reliability supported by Technology Readiness Level (TRL) assessments and experimental measurements. Critical challenges including radiation resilience, cybersecurity, and power efficiency are analyzed, alongside a practical roadmap for space deployment and future evolution toward 6G integration and federated AI learning across distributed space assets.

title: End-to-End Virtual Try-on Via Latent Diffusion Model with Multi-Condition Controlnet
authors: Dongchuang Zhao, Lili Ma, Jinguang Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Image-based virtual try-on is widely used in the e-commerce and fashion fields, aiming to synthesize images of people wearing designated clothing. The rise of diffusion models has promoted relevant research, but existing methods rely on specialized clothing warping modules, suffering from issues such as high system complexity, poor generality, and insufficient high-resolution texture fidelity. To address these problems, this paper proposes an end-to-end latent diffusion model framework that eliminates the need for explicit warping processes. The framework embeds clothing deformation in the latent space and leverages enhanced cross-attention blocks to learn semantic correspondences between clothing and the human body for patchlevel deformation. It also introduces ControlNet, fusing clothing images and DensePose features as multi-condition signals to improve the accuracy of structural alignment under complex poses and backgrounds. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances the generation quality of high-resolution virtual try-on and achieves both qualitative and quantitative breakthroughs on standard benchmarks.

title: T-GAT: Time-Aware Graph Attention Networks for Dynamic Shilling Attack Detection
authors: Yunlong Ji, Yanxiao Li, Bin Zhao, Yilong Gao
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)
abstract: The application of recommendation systems is becoming increasingly widespread, but it is also facing growing threats from malicious users' attacks. Traditional attack detection methods mostly rely on static behavioral features, making it difficult to identify gang attacks with temporal coordination and structural concealment. This paper proposes a novel Time-Aware Graph Attention Network (T-GAT) for dynamic collaborative attack detection. Our method models user interactions as a dynamic graph, integrating multiple strategies (co-reviewing, co-bursting, and content similarity) to capture complex attack patterns. At its core, a temporal attention mechanism is introduced to effectively learn the time-dependent characteristics of user interactions. Furthermore, to address label scarcity, a semi-supervised strategy is adopted, and a community detection algorithm is utilized to analyze malicious gangs. Experiments on the real-world Amazon Prime Pantry dataset show that T-GAT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an AUC of 90.61 % and a Recall of 90.14 % even with limited labeled data, providing strong support for the security of recommendation systems.

title: An Attention-Enhanced Modified CNN for Robust Sentiment Analysis in E-Commerce Recommendation
authors: Sagedur Rahman, Yanchun Pan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In the fast-changing world of e-commerce, sentiment analysis is very important for turning immense amounts of unstructured customer feedback into useful information. Deep learning models like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks have improved the field, but they often have to choose between being fast and understanding the context. Standard CNNs are context blind, meaning they can't pick up on long-range dependencies. On the other hand, LSTM-based models can be too expensive to use in real time. To overcome these constraints, we introduce the Attention-Enhanced Modified CNN (AMCNN), an innovative design that incorporates a dynamic self-attention mechanism within a modified CNN framework. Previous models used static dimensionality reduction methods like Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), whereas AMCNN dynamically recalibrates the relevance of features, which helps filter out noise while keeping context that is rich in sentiment. The Amazon Product Reviews dataset shows that AMCNN has an accuracy of 98.20 % and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{F}$</tex>-measure of 97.85 %. This is far better than the baseline MCNN and LSTM models. Also, a paired t-test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<0.001$</tex> shows that these improvements are statistically significant, making AMCNN a strong and scalable way to do sentiment analysis in recommendation systems.

title: DGLA-Net: Robust MRI-PET Fusion Method Based on Deep Global-Local Attention and Multi-Scale Supervision
authors: Ruichen Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: MRI-PET image fusion aims to integrate anatomical and functional information, which is crucial for precise diagnosis. However, existing deep learning methods struggle to balance texture details with functional fidelity, often facing bottlenecks such as difficulty in bridging the semantic gap, interference from background noise, and the blurring of critical lesions. This paper proposes an improved model, DGLA-Net, based on the UNet++ architecture and a dual robust information fusion attention mechanism (DGLA), aimed at optimizing fusion performance through deep global-local attention. The model utilizes a Nested UNet (UNet++) as the backbone architecture, leveraging its dense nested skip connections to effectively bridge the semantic gap between the encoder and decoder, thereby preserving multi-scale anatomical structural features to the maximum extent. Building on this, the core innovations include three aspects: First, a Deep Global-Local Attention (DGLA) module is designed to be highly compatible with UNet++ multi-level feature paths. By coupling global pooling with local convolution operations, it realizes dynamic screening and interaction of cross-modal features, balancing global context dependency with local texture details. Second, a Residual Denoising Spatial Attention (RDSA) module is introduced, utilizing residual connection mechanisms to strengthen deep feature extraction capabilities and effectively suppress background noise in functional images. Finally, an Adaptive Hybrid Loss function is constructed, synergistically integrating gradient loss, SSIM loss, and PET saliency-weighted intensity loss. Extensive experiments on MRI-PET multi-modal datasets demonstrate that DGLA-Net significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art fusion algorithms. Compared to other models, DGLA-Net not only retains brain anatomical contours more clearly but also locates tumor metabolic regions more precisely through attention heatmaps, effectively achieving an optimal balance between structural fidelity and functional saliency, demonstrating excellent potential for clinical application.

title: Flexible Graphene Tactile Sensors with Spider Web Designs for Human-Computer Interaction
authors: Brandon Singleton, Teja V. S. N. Vuddagiri, Juan A. Zepeda Maldonado, Mose Williams, Sandip Das
abstract: Laser Induced graphene (LIG) is an emerging technology that facilitates the production of multilayer graphene sheets and has found applications in various types of electronic sensors. Here, we present a set of three innovative spider web-shaped tactile sensors fabricated using LIG electrodes on flexible polyimide (PI) substrates. A 450 nm near-UV diode laser was used to directly form conductive spider web graphene electrodes and their connection traces on PI films. Raman spectroscopy and optical microscopy confirmed the formation of multilayer graphene under optimized laser irradiation conditions. By interfacing the graphene electrodes with an ATmega328P microcontroller and front-end electronics, functional capacitive touch sensors were realized. The spider web electrode geometry provided wide-area coverage while maintaining electrical conductivity and mechanical compliance under bending, making these flexible tactile sensors suitable for human-machine interaction (HMI) applications.

title: Domain-Adaptive Named Entity Recognition for Engineering Topology Texts
authors: Longqiang Zuo, Xi Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the challenges of diverse entity types, ambiguous boundaries, and dense domain-specific terminology in topology-oriented technical texts, this paper proposes a deep learning - based information extraction model that integrates domain-adaptive pretraining with a vocabulary enhancement mechanism, termed NetNER. Specifically, the BERT model is further pretrained on topology-related corpora to strengthen domain-aware semantic representations, while a vocabulary boundary - aware mechanism is introduced to improve the recognition of long and composite entities. A CRF decoding layer is then employed to ensure the consistency of label sequences. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach serves as a critical enabling technology for the automated configuration and intelligent management of complex engineering systems.

title: MTSeg: Cascaded Mamba–Transformer Enhancement for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
authors: Mingyi Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Given an image, open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) aims to assign a semantic label to each pixel from an unconstrained set of categories. Most existing approaches build upon vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP, leveraging their strong zero-shot recognition capability. However, a persistent performance gap remains when transferring image-level pretrained VLMs to pixel-level semantic segmentation. In this work, we propose MTSeg, an end-to-end OVSS framework that improves pixel-text alignment while retaining robust generalization to unseen categories. Specifically, we introduce a cascaded Mamba-Transformer enhancement module for visual-domain structural consolidation and text-conditioned semantic recalibration. A fusion decoder further incorporates the initial CLIP-based matching embedding as auxiliary cues and progressively upsamples features to the target resolution for prediction. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that MTSeg consistently improves segmentation performance over prior methods.

title: Automated Testing in a Multi-Agent Systems Simulation Platform
authors: Connor H. Wells, Roberto A. Flores
abstract: We present a novel testing framework to support automated testing of individual agents and teams of agents situated in the Multi-Agent Systems Simulation Platform (MASSim). The framework is written in Java and uses JUnit test cases to run multiple simulations, allowing for repeatable and automated testing of multi-agent systems. This framework addresses difficulties expressed by participants when testing and debugging agents in the Multi-Agent Programming Contest.

title: Mesdasd: A Medical Expert System for Diagnosing Autoimmune System Diseases
authors: Mohammadreza Hajiarbabi
abstract: Expert systems are valuable tools in various fields, including control, diagnosis, and medical applications. In recent decades, expert systems have been increasingly used in the medical field. Immune system diseases encompass a wide range of conditions, affecting approximately 8% of the U.S. population. These diseases target different parts of the body, including neurons, tissues, and skin. Some immune disorders share similar symptoms, while others resemble unrelated conditions, making diagnosis challenging even for specialized physicians. An expert system can assist not only the patients but also the physicians in the diagnostic process by leveraging artificial intelligence. For designing MESDASD (Medical Expert System for Diagnosing Autoimmune System Diseases), forward chaining method was used. For inference, certainty theory was applied. By using certainty theory the rules which are the symptoms of the diseases will have different certainty factors, that shows those with higher certainty factor are more important in the process of decision making. Fourteen tests, two for each diseases in moderate and severe case were used to evaluate the system. The system managed to pass all tests correctly with high margin. The results has demonstrated that MESDASD can accurately diagnose these diseases with a high level of certainty. MESDASD is the first expert system designed to diagnose various immune system diseases. In its initial version, the system primarily focuses on immune disorders affecting the skin and other closely related conditions. The system passed all the tests with high certainty.

title: Inflections Points in Quantum Computing as an Information Technology: A Thematic Analysis
authors: Nathan Jeffrey Elrod
abstract: Information Technology (IT) as a research discipline observes phenomena at the intersection of users, technology and information, and examines the needs and solutions that connect them. Quantum computing is a rapidly advancing field, and its state as an Information Technology at present is not well-understood. This study seeks to enhance the understanding of quantum computing as an Information Technology by identifying inflection points in its history and development through a series of expert interviews. Experts from two commercial quantum hardware/software developers, two academic institutions, and a United States National Lab were interviewed, and ten major historical themes relevant to quantum IT were identified in all. Ten inflection points were developed from these themes and specific historical trends were identified and discussed.

title: Generative Adversarial Network Based Anomaly Detection for Software Defect Prediction
authors: Aradhana Soni, James Ostrowski, Anahita Khojandi
abstract: Predicting software defects effectively is crucial for high-quality software development. Traditionally, defect prediction has relied on supervised techniques, limited by data availability. This paper applies a new Generative Adversarial Network-based technique for detecting anomalies (GAN-AN), tailored to early defect identification in software development. Our technique, which detects defects as anomalies, is compared against three established methods: autoencoder, isolation forest, and one-class support vector machine (OCSVM). Our experimentation, utilizing five datasets from the NASA Promise repository, shows GAN-AN to be superior, with improvements of 7 % in precision-recall area under the curve (PR-AUC) and a significant 20 % in F1 score over previous techniques. Further evaluation using validation datasets reveals that while GAN-AN's overall performance was not uniformly superior, it notably outperformed isolation forest and OCSVM in recall on several datasets. This underscores GAN-AN's potential in specific contexts where recall is critical and highlights the importance of context-specific model selection. Our investigation also reveals that performance metrics vary among models, suggesting that the most effective model may vary depending on the dataset and specific detection goals.

title: Accelerating Enterprise Application Development Through AI-Enhanced Low-Code Platforms: An Empirical Analysis of Oracle APEX with Generative AI Integration
authors: Mohankrishna Kothapalli, Dharanidhar Vuppu
abstract: Low-code platforms promise faster application delivery, but rigorous empirical evidence remains scarce. We conducted exploratory multi-study experiments with 12 professional developers and 20 business analysts to evaluate Oracle APEX with Select AI integration. Our findings reveal substantial productivity gains: developers completed equivalent functionality significantly faster than with ReactJS (mean speedup <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$37 \times$</tex>, though this ratio reflects both platform abstraction and AI benefits), writing 95 % less code. When we tested Select AI's natural language query capabilities across 1,250 queries, GPT4 achieved 71 % accuracy while Cohere and Oracle's models reached 64 % and 61 % respectively. Security testing with 140 adversarial prompts showed Row-Level Security blocked 96% of attacks. Business analysts with no Structured Query Language (SQL) training completed 78 % of data access tasks successfully using natural language alone. These exploratory results suggest AI-enhanced low-code platforms offer significant potential for enterprise development, warranting larger-scale replication studies.

title: Self-Supervised Pretraining and Calibrated Fine-Tuning for Common and Rare Thoracic Findings on Chest Radiographs
authors: Debasmita Biswas, Mohammadreza Hajiarbabi
abstract: This work investigates a self-supervised representation learning (SSL) pipeline for chest radiography and its adaptation to both common and rare thoracic findings. In Phase 1, a ResNet-18 encoder is trained on frontal DICOM chest radiographs using a dual objective, i.e., contrastive learning on augmented image pairs and a lightweight masked-autoencoder-style reconstruction loss on randomly masked patches, without labels. On a balanced probe of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{3, 0 0 0}$</tex> frontal radiographs (pneumonia vs. normal), frozen SSL features support a linear classifier with AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.86$</tex>, indicating meaningful class separability. In Phase 2, the SSL backbone is fine-tuned on RSNA pneumonia vs. normal, reaching validation AUC 0.8758, sensitivity 0.535 at 0.90 specificity, and ECE 0.1788. In Phase 3, the model is adapted to a rare-label task on a CheXpert-v1.0-small pneumothorax subset (1,800 images; 300 positive) using a two-logit head, imbalance-aware training, and a neutral expert-prior layer, yielding AUC 0.6762 with poor initial calibration (ECE 0.6556) that improves to 0.3839 after temperature scaling <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(T=7.8609)$</tex> without affecting AUC. The operating threshold for 0.90 specificity shifts from 0.894 to 0.567, illustrating the practical impact of calibration on deployment. Overall, the three-phase pipeline provides a strong baseline, calibrated operating points, and a plug-in interface for future expert rules and rare-label improvements.

title: Fragmented Infrastructures, Fragmented Learners: The Case for Integrated, Longitudinal Learning Ecosystems in Computing Education
authors: Hasan M Jamil
abstract: None

title: Multi-Objective Optimization and Finite Element-Based Verification of High-Frequency Solid-State Transformer for Multi-Port Smart Grid Applications
authors: Garrett Armstrong, Indranil Bhattacharya
abstract: This article presents an optimization and verification strategy for high-frequency transformers (HFTs) involving the injection of designs found in literature into the search space. Using a genetic algorithm (GA) created in Python, designs are tested through a physics-based evaluation function for several generations. This strategy employs a non-dominated, multiobjective selection process that sorts designs based on maximizing power density and efficiency, while minimizing cost and power losses. Once top designs are selected, they will be verified using Finite Element Method Magnetics (FEMM) and compared side by side. The top design from both processes will be selected, and a more in-depth design will be completed utilizing multiple ports, and the correct Dual Active Bridge (DAB) setup.

title: Emergency Department Forecasting: COVID-19 Impact on Machine Learning Models
authors: Adán Somoza López, Hugo Álvarez-Chaves, Ana Carrero Fernández, Maria D. R-Moreno
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: COVID-19 disrupted emergency department (ED) arrival patterns, creating challenges for traditional forecasting models used in hospital resource planning. This study analyzes ED arrival data from two Spanish hospitals to assess how the pandemic period affects Machine Learning (ML) model performance and identify optimal forecasting strategies. We applied multiple ML techniques (XGBoost, Random Forest, Elastic Net) and timeseries algorithms (ARIMA, Prophet) using both daily and work shift temporal aggregations, incorporating exogenous calendar variables, including holidays and academic schedules. Models were trained on pandemic-inclusive and post-pandemic periods to evaluate the impact of including disrupted historical data. Results demonstrate that models trained exclusively on postpandemic data consistently outperform those including pandemic data, achieving R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> improvements of up to 0.13 for 4-month forecasts. Work shift aggregation significantly enhanced performance compared to daily aggregation (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0. 9 2 vs 0. 6 4 for best models). SHAP analysis revealed that recent patient arrivals and calendarrelated features serve as the most important predictors across all scenarios. These findings indicate that excluding pandemic data from training datasets and employing finer temporal aggregation substantially improve ED arrival forecasting accuracy, providing practical benefits for hospital operations and resource allocation.

title: Towards Reliable Generation of Executable Workflows by Foundation Models
authors: Sogol Masoumzadeh, Keheliya Gallaba, Dayi Lin, Ahmed E. Hassan
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology

title: StepOverflow: PMC Overflows on AMD SEV
authors: Anja Rabich, Jonah Heller, Florian Sieck, Thomas Eisenbarth
publish: Proceedings of the 9th Workshop on System Software for Trusted Execution
abstract: None

title: Research on Collaborative Interference Resource Allocation Based on Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Pengjiao Li, Haoran Gong, Yinshuai Meng, Yumeng Liang, Keding Yan
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: In the process of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) formation penetration, when multiple interference aircraft jointly suppress multiple radars, there are problems such as limited resources, high decision-making dimensions, and low optimization efficiency. This paper constructs a collaborative interference resource allocation model with the goal of maximizing interference effectiveness, comprehensively considering the power and time coordination mechanism as well as the constraints of interference targets and styles. On this basis, an improved genetic algorithm is proposed. By introducing sorting selection, small habitat elimination, and elite retention strategies, the algorithm's global search ability is enhanced and the convergence speed is accelerated. The results show that the proposed algorithm can converge to a better solution within approximately 60% of the iterations, significantly reducing the probability of enemy radar detection, increasing the positioning and tracking errors by 2 to 3 times, effectively compressing the radar exposure area, and providing a relatively safe flight corridor for UAV penetration.

title: Fabrication of Artificial Intelligence enabled Systematic Waste Segregation Model using Logical Sensors with Smart Controller
authors: Haritha V, C Nithiya, Chandrakala R, C Sadhana, P Vijayaraghavan, S Yuvaraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Automation and effective waste separation are important to a sustainable environmental management particularly in urban and industrial aspects. The current paper is a description of the design and implementation of an Artificial Intelligence (AI) based systematic waste segregation model, which is a combination of logical sensors and a smart Raspberry Pi controller. The system utilizes a blend of moisture sensors, metal detectors, IR sensors, gas sensors and weight sensors to categorize the waste into five different types namely, dry, wet, plastic, metal and hazardous. The intelligent classification process is made possible by a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on sensor data and images that are collected in the real world, and the actuation mechanisms are controlled by servo motors that are provided by the use of GPIOs. Experimental outcomes indicate that the classification can be made with a result of 95.2 % when dealing with wet waste, 94.2 % when dealing with dry waste, 95.3 % when dealing with metal waste, 92.7% when dealing with plastic waste, and 91.7 % when dealing with hazardous waste and the overall classification accuracy of the system has been achieved to be 93.82. The system saves more than 70 % of processing time, which is in comparison with manual sorting and makes the categorization of images more consistent. This model includes an OLED based real-time display and bin-level monitoring, as well. The proposed smart solution is a scalable, inexpensive, and real-time waste management structure that can be used in smart cities and industrial campuses. The extensions in the future can be IoT-enabled cloud connectivity and federated AI learning to track and report waste in a decentralized way.

title: Toward Embodied Intelligence: An Architecture for Natural Dialogue and Action Execution in Assistive Robots
authors: Karameldeen Omer, Andrea Monteriù
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This work presents a new architecture for an assistive mobile robot designed to support the elderly and individuals with disabilities in performing daily indoor tasks. The proposed framework integrates multimodal perception, language-based reasoning, and safety-aware action planning to enable natural and effective two-way communication between humans and robots. At its core, the system utilizes large language models (LLMs) for dialogue management, contextual understanding, and reasoning over fused sensory inputs, including vision, speech, and proprioceptive data. By combining speech recognition, object detection, and local memory modules, the robot not only interprets explicit user commands but also infers implicit intentions, predicts missing information, and requests clarifications when necessary. A dedicated safety layer filters and validates action sequences before execution, ensuring reliability and user safety. The architecture further incorporates short- and long-term memory structures, enabling the robot to maintain a dialogue history and semantic knowledge of the environment. This bidirectional interaction model allows the robot to generate both natural conversational responses and executable action plans in a context-aware manner. Preliminary implementation and testing demonstrate promising performance, bridging the gap between conversational AI and embodied robotic action in real-life assistive scenarios.

title: The Risk Operations Center (RoC): A Unified Framework for Proactive, Business-Aligned Cyber Risk Management
authors: Jatinder Pal Singh, Sukriti Das, Nikhil Verma, Sockey Katheria, Shristi Joshi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: As cybersecurity threats continue to grow in complexity, dynamism and business impact, the demand for advanced intelligence systems that move beyond reactive detection methods grows. Traditional Security Operations Centers (SOCs) are typically not predictive, aligned with the business and agile enough to adapt to changing threats with delayed responses, operational inefficiencies and residual enterprise risk. This study plans to design and assess an AI-driven Risk Operations Center (RoC) based on multi-source data aggregation, cognitive threat modeling, predictive and prescriptive analytics, business context alignment, and explainable reinforcement learning to improve proactive cyber risk management. The framework is based on Deep Representation Learning, Graph Neural Network; Transformer based models, Bayesian Inference and DRL Agents, coupled with Enterprise Knowledge Graphs for Risk Contextual Alignment. Policies and models are continually and federatedly refined over time. Comparative analysis shows the superior performance of the proposed RoC with 96.8 % threat detection accuracy, 2.4 % false positive rate, 94.6 % risk forecasting accuracy, 7.8 s incident response time, and 0.93 business impact alignment and 95 adaptability index outperforming the existing frameworks. The RoC is a business-oriented, self-optimizing and adaptive cybersecurity ecosystem to improve decision transparency, operation efficiency and strategic risk mitigation, which is a huge advancement in the field of AI-powered cyber defense.

title: Experimental Evaluation of Synthetic GAN based Data Augmentation Scheme to Predict Improved Rare Diseases from Medical Imaging
authors: L Pattathurani, Shanthashalini, Yashashwini S, S Priya, Soumya T R, M Arun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: Medical imaging can pose a vital challenge to rare disease detection because of the scarcity of medical imaging data and imbalance in the classes. This paper presents a synthetic augmentation framework, Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN), which can be used to solve these problems and enhance the work of diagnostic systems in various categories of rare diseases. Open datasets of MRI, CT, and X-ray modalities that were obtained and published in Kaggle were curated, and annotated classes of muscular dystrophies, rare brain lesions, and pediatric skeletal anomalies. It implemented a piping system that consists of preprocessing, synthetic image generation via GANs and the downstream CNN classifier (ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNet). The outcomes prove that GAN-enhanced datasets outperform in classification and recall significantly. In particular, the ResNet50 has an accuracy of 91.4 that is higher compared to real-only (84.1) and classical augmented (86.9) models. Optimal performance was also observed at a synthetic to real ratio of 5: 1 as found out in ablation studies. The FID (36.3), Inception Score (3.78), and SSIM (0.76) quantitative quality measurements were used to determine the realism and diversity of synthetic samples. The article demonstrates the potential of synthetic augmentation to enhance the ability to detect underrepresented pathologies and offers a repeatable design of improving rare disease classifiers. The approach can be applied in the medical fields with limited labeled data but early diagnosis is a critical factor.

title: DT-Empowered, Social-Aware Service Provisioning in Edge Computing
authors: Jing Li, Jianping Wang, Weifa Liang, Jie Wu, Quan Chen, Zichuan Xu
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is gathering paces in the new era of Industry 4.0, and the Digital Twin (DT) technology bridges the gap between the bursting amounts of data generated by IoT devices and the user requirements for real-time data processing. DT services maintain living digital models of physical objects, and a DT network enables comprehensive service provisioning with the global knowledge of a group of DTs. On the other hand, exposing serverless computing at network edges, the recent advances in Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) introduce new inspirations to the DT landscape that ensure fine-grained resource management and low network-wide delay of DT services. However, social relationships among IoT devices and DT data privacy impact DT orchestrations. In this paper, we first design a differential privacy-based federated learning framework to build a DT network for DT services in response to user requests in an MEC, thereby enhancing the Quality of Services (QoS). Built upon the proposed framework, we then formulate two novel social-aware DT placement problems: the static social-aware S_DT placement problem, and the dynamic social-aware S_DT placement problem, respectively. We also show the NP-hardness of the defined problems. Then, we formulate an Integer Linear Program (ILP) solution to the static social-aware S_DT placement problem when the problem size is small; otherwise we develop an approximation algorithm with a provable approximation ratio for it. Third, we study the dynamic social-aware S_DT placement problem when requests arrive one by one without the knowledge of future request arrivals over the time horizon, for which we devise an online algorithm with a provable competitive ratio. Finally, we conduct simulations to evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithms. Simulation results show that the proposed algorithms outperform their counterparts, improving the performance compared with their baselines by no less than 14.9%.

title: Digital Herd Twin as a Tool to Increase the Efficiency of Cattle Accounting in the Agricultural Sector
authors: Sergei Kurashkin, Yesenia Khrapunova, Kirill Belous, Zakhar Pechenkin, Muhammad Khamraev
abstract: The article focuses on the digital transformation of livestock management through the implementation of a herd digital twin. The main objective of the study is to evaluate the effectiveness of the CIS-Cattle information system, designed to unify accounting processes, improve animal health monitoring, and optimize resource use. The research tasks included analyzing existing paper-based workflows, developing a digital architecture integrating IoT sensors, RFID identification, and machine learning algorithms, and validating the system in real farm conditions. A nine-month field experiment was conducted on a dairy farm with 236 cows. Methods involved real-time data collection, predictive analytics, and comparative analysis of key performance indicators. The results demonstrated an 80 % reduction in manual data entry, a 30 % decrease in disease incidence, 25 % savings in feed costs, and a 17 % increase in calf weight gain, confirming the system's practical efficiency.

title: Discrete-Time Periodic State-Feedback Control via a Variant of Extended LMI with Static Multiplier
authors: Masayuki Sato, Noboru Sebe
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: This paper addresses the design problem of periodic state-feedback controllers for Discrete-Time (DT) linear uncertain systems with static multiplier incorporated. For this problem, we use a variant of Extended LMI formulation, which has been derived through very simple algebraic manipulations with the aid of Elimination lemma, for time-lifted DT linear systems. Although the variant of Extended LMI formulation introduces additional auxiliary matrices and consequently increases the sizes of LMIs in exchange for its simple derivation, their roles have not yet been clarified. We clarify their roles through the design problem of periodic state-feedback controllers for DT linear uncertain systems with static multiplier incorporated, and then examine the conservatism of the proposed method compared with previously proposed methods.

title: Unsupervised Landslide Detection from InSAR-Derived Time Series Using Change Point Analysis
authors: Ioana-Cristina Igret, Gabriel Iuhasz, Delia Teleagă, Valentin Poncos
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: Landslides pose dangerous risks to human safety and to infrastructure, especially in densely populated or geologically unpredictable regions. Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) technology, along with time series analysis has significantly improved the ability to monitor ground deformations associated with such hazards. This study explores the detection of change points in InSAR-derived ground motion time series from the Praid region in Romania, based on Sentinel-1 data collected over a period of five years. The methodology starts with clustering Persistent Scatterers (PS) with similar deformation patterns using the HDBSCAN algorithm, followed by the application of several detrending methods to enhance change point detection accuracy. Four algorithms, Pruned Exact Linear Time (PELT), Binary Segmentation (BS), Window Sliding and Bottom-Up Segmentation, are implemented to identify abrupt fluctuations in ground motion. The absence of ground truth data motivated the selection of a consensus-based on majority voting as evaluation system. More specifically, the validation of a detected change point was correlated with the number of algorithms that identified it independently, with a minimum of two algorithms out of the four implemented. The findings offer insights into deformation activity and highlight the value of change point analysis in landslide detection. Future work will include a more comprehensive analysis of data particularities, the integration of contextual environmental factors and finally, the application of advanced time series techniques, to further enhance detection accuracy and improve early warning and risk management. Our code is available at https://github.com/ioanaigret/unsupervised-change-point-analysis-based-on-InSAR-derived-time-series.git

title: Design and Evaluation of an Immersive VR-Based Basketball Teaching System: Enhancing Student Engagement in College Physical Education Courses
authors: Jinkui Ren
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: The problem of improving student interest in college physical education classes is still a dilemma especially in skill related aspect like basketball. In this paper, the design and analysis of a Virtual Reality (VR)-based teaching system of basketball is provided and combines interactive VR simulations with realtime performance feedback to enhance the learning process and student motivation. The system provides a VR environment that runs on Unity3D with motion tracking sensors to record the movement of the students and give them real time biomechanical and tactical feedback. The instructional model involves adaptive difficulty modification, instructive tutorials, and gamification activities to sustain the interest and promote development of the skills. Mixed-method assessment was undertaken that involved college learners taking basketball classes, and the assessment was done in terms of engagement levels, learning performance, and perceived usability. The evidence of the experiment suggests that the students, who worked with the VR-based system, were more engaged, their shooting accuracy was better, and their knowledge of the tactical movements was better than the students who studied the same matter with the help of regular teaching techniques. The paper also emphasizes the opportunity presented by the system to deliver individualized learning experiences, lower the workload of an instructor and create a safe and immersive atmosphere to acquire certain skills. These results imply that physical education classes at colleges can be improved by immersive VR technologies along with real-time feedback systems that can greatly improve student engagement and learning outcomes.

title: A Novel Self-Aligning Socket Tool with Torque Feedback for Robotic Maintenance: Design, Calibration, and Experimental Validation
authors: Hamidreza Fahham, Gareth Tucker, Reza Saboori, Soran Parsa
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: Robotic maintenance tasks frequently require reliable engagement with fasteners such as caps, bolts, and locks. However, small angular misalignments between the robot and the fastener often led to unreliable engagement, creating potential safety risks, while also causing increased wear on components and excessive contact forces during operation. This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a flexible self-aligning socket tool capable of compensating for rotational misalignments through a passive mechanical mechanism while providing realtime torque feedback. The tool incorporates a slope-based selfalignment interface, a mechanically compliant socket assembly, and a current-sensor-based torque estimation model calibrated using a custom test rig. The complete system is integrated with both collaborative and industrial robot platforms. Experimental evaluations demonstrate reliable cap engagement, accurate torque estimation, and a significant reduction in normal forces compared with conventional engagement methods. The findings indicate that the proposed tool enhances robustness, safety, and adaptability in robotic maintenance applications.

title: KANSeg-BT: Adaptive Kolmogorov–Arnold Segmentation Network for Brain Tumor MRI
authors: Ankit Kumar, Shreya Awasthi, Manish Kumar, Mukul S. Sutaone
abstract: The segmentation of brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has been proven to be one of the most difficult and challenging tasks. The difficulties arise from irregular tumor boundaries, inconsistent intensity levels, and low contrast with nearby tissues. Currently, traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) perform well on standard segmentation tasks but struggle to capture subtle structural and intensity variations present in tumor boundaries. To identify these limitations, we proposed a novel, adaptive model for segmentation, built on the Kolmogorov Arnold network (KAN), called KANSegBT. Unlike traditional CNNs, KANSeg-BT learns flexible feature representations through B-spline-based activations. KANSeg-BT introduces two custom modules: the Kolmogorov-Arnold Enhanced Convolution Block (KAN-ECB) and the KAN Bottleneck Block (KAN-BB), which help capture both local and global structural patterns in MRI data. Finally, we evaluated the model on two datasets. On the BraTS 2020 dataset, the model recorded a Dice score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9327 \pm 0.014$</tex>, along with a loss value of 0.0087. It also achieved a sensitivity of 0.9282, a specificity of 0.9984, and an overall accuracy of 0.9966, indicating highly consistent segmentation results. When tested on the BraTS 2022 dataset, the model showed a slight improvement, obtaining a Dice score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9396 \pm 0.012$</tex> with a loss of 0.0085, a sensitivity of 0.9224, a specificity of 0.9989, and an accuracy of 0.9968. KANSeg-BT outcome surpasses U-Net and attention-based models without extra computation.

title: A Practical Framework for Simulating the Integration of QKD in TLS Protocol Towards Quantum Secure Communication
authors: Radhika Jamwal, Sarada Prasad Gochhayat, Anshuman Kalla
abstract: With the advent of quantum computing, classical cryptographic techniques—such as those underpinning Transport Layer Security (TLS)—are becoming increasingly vulnerable. Although post-quantum cryptographic algorithms are being developed, their long-term security remains uncertain. Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) offers a fundamentally different approach to key exchange and secure communication, as it bases security on the physics of quantum information rather than computational difficulty. This paper presents a practical simulation framework that integrates QKD-derived keys into the TLS protocol using the Pre-Shared Key (PSK) mode. The system employs a layered QKD architecture, implemented using QuNetSim, to generate and manage quantum keys, which are then distributed through a centralized Key Management Server (KMS). The proposed approach demonstrates how quantum-generated keys can be seamlessly integrated into the TLS protocol to enhance resilience against quantum attacks, providing a viable pathway toward post-quantum-secure communication.

title: Current-Tracking Technique Improves RF Amplifier Efficiency in Lower HF Frequency Band
authors: Aleksandar Kiričenko, Vedran Kos
abstract: Over the past decade, a prominent trend in mobile radio communications has been the increasing adoption of envelope-tracking techniques to enhance the efficiency of radio frequency (RF) power amplifiers. An envelope-tracking amplifier is a hybrid linear/non-linear circuit where the supply voltage to the linear amplifier is dynamically adjusted by a nonlinear (switched-mode) power supply circuit to match the instantaneous amplitude (envelope) of the input signal. Inspired by this principle of operation, the presented research investigates a more straightforward approach: feeding the antenna directly with current generated by a highly efficient switched-mode source. The viability of the principle has been verified by simulations and measurement results on a demonstrator in the lower HF frequency range. The results have confirmed the substantial efficiency increase, in the operating frequency range of non-linear circuit.

title: A Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework for Ethical Decision Automation and Regulatory Compliance Management in Organizations
authors: Maha Lakshmi S, Nandini D, Priya R, Hubin Bens L, Thuseenth M, Vimalyabesh A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: With the advent of smart automation, organizational governance systems are being under more pressure to make morally upright and legally correct decisions within dynamic environments. This paper gives a new Trustworthy Data-Driven Governance Framework that incorporates structural causal modeling (SCM), counterfactual reasoning, digital twin simulation, explainable reinforcement learning (XRL), Bayesian trust scoring, and federated auditable ledgers (FAL) in order to achieve transparency, accountability, and regulatory alignment. The model represents the policy effects on virtual simulations, selects the optimal decision paths through XRL, and automatically revises the trust scores of the data sources and service constituents. These experimental findings show a 97.5 prediction accuracy in the Ethics, convergence of the trust score in 6 iterations and 94 % compliance fidelity in the case of simulated governance. Also, the counterfactual engine successfully identified 60 % of ethically suboptimal decisions prior to implementation. The proposed model beats the traditional rule-based compliance engines in terms of ethical foresight, interpretability, and adaptiveness. The article provides a regulatory-conscious, scaled, and explainable governance infrastructure that can be used in areas like finance, healthcare, and government. The future work will introduce and include multi-jurisdictional alignment of regulatory ontology and real-time feedback of human stakeholders to further improve ethical negotiation in conflicting governance situations and assist in hybrid human-AI regulatory symbiosis.

title: Precision Matters: Comparing Deep Models for Visual Marker Detection on Eyewear-Mounted Calibration Objects
authors: Adrian-Pavel Măduța
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of localizing hazard-shaped calibration markers mounted on eyeglass frames, a task relevant to augmented reality, head pose estimation and optical distance calculations. The goal is to accurately detect these markers, which lie in the upper portion of the face, within a rectangular region provided by a face detector. We revisit this problem and ask: how much precision can be reliably extracted, and at what computational cost? Beyond a controlled comparison of heatmap-based segmentation models (HRNet modified for multiscale outputs, MobileNetV3) and object detection with YOLOv8 variants, we introduce two contributions that improve accuracy while retaining real-time performance: (i) a temperature-scaled soft-argmax readout for subpixel localization with calibrated spatial confidence, and (ii) a lightweight Detection → Refinement cascade that couples YOLO proposals with a 7-layer heatmap refiner operating on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64 \times 64$</tex> crops. Experiments are conducted on both synthetic and real datasets spanning diverse capture distances. Our cascade reduces mean pixel error by 17 % over YOLOv8-n while running at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 8 ~ m s}$</tex> per frame (includes additional latency such as image loading, resizing, and patch extraction, inference time alone is 75 ms per frame), and outperforms HRNet by 4.7% AUC@2px at a fraction of its latency. Evaluation includes distance-based error metrics, uncertainty calibration (ECE), and both quantitative and qualitative analyses. Results show a strong correlation between confidence and localization error, enabling practical deployment.

title: Machine Learning-Assisted THz Technology for Nondestructive Testing in the Tobacco Industry_supp1-3685500.docx
authors: Xiaojun Wu
abstract: Abnormal moisture content and impurity contamination are core threats to product quality in the tobacco industry. Rapid, accurate, and non-destructive detection is therefore essential for product optimization. Terahertz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) holds great potential for tobacco detection owing to its non-invasiveness and high penetration capability. However, the effect of laser pulse width on THz radiation characteristics remains to be verified and the challenge of rapid signal analysis remains unsolved. In this work, a free-space THz-TDS system was constructed, which is driven by two compact Ti:sapphire lasers (with pulse widths of 20 fs and 45 fs, respectively) and enables rapid switching of laser sources. Experimental results indicate that the THz wave generated by the 20 fs laser achieves a broader bandwidth (2.5 THz) and a higher center frequency (0.45 THz). Further analysis reveals a strong linear correlation between the THz signal amplitude and the moisture content of tobacco leaves, while distinct waveform differences are observed between tobacco leaves and impurities. By integrating machine learning algorithms, high-precision impurity classification (with an accuracy of 99.9%) and rapid moisture prediction (with a mean absolute error of 0.28%) are realized. The entire signal measurement and analysis process can be completed within 2 seconds. This work provides technical verification for the intelligent upgrading of tobacco detection systems and demonstrates the enormous potential of THz technology in the tobacco industry and even the broader agricultural sector.

title: Scalable and Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Model Checker for Computation Tree Logic
authors: Ghalya Alwhishi, Jamal Bentahar, Amine Andam, Ahmed Elwhishi, Mustapha Hedabou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Formal verification using temporal logics such as computation tree logic (CTL) is essential for validating safety and correctness in complex systems. However, traditional model-checking techniques face severe scalability limitations due to the state explosion problem and their reliance on exhaustive symbolic traversal. Moreover, existing learning-based verification methods often lack formal guarantees and interpretability. These challenges create a pressing need for scalable, learning-based verification methods that preserve verification reliability while improving computational efficiency. This article introduces a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based model checking framework that learns to verify CTL formulas directly through interaction with system models. Unlike traditional symbolic model checkers such as NuSMV, the proposed DRL-CTL checker trained using proximal policy optimization (PPO) interprets CTL semantics over system models represented as Kripke structures without performing symbolic state-space traversal at inference time. Reward functions are designed for individual CTL operators, and fixed-point reasoning is incorporated to handle global temporal properties such as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$AG(\phi)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$EG(\phi)$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves near-constant inference time of approximately 2 ms per formula on an Intel Core i9-13900K CPU (24 cores, 3.0 GHz), 64 GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU (24 GB VRAM), reduces verification time by up to 90% compared with traditional model checkers, and scales to models with more than <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{1192}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> reachable states. The framework also produces witnesses and counterexamples and yields verification outcomes identical to those of symbolic checkers in our experiments. These results highlight the potential of DRL to serve as a scalable, efficient, and explainable alternative to classical CTL model checking.

title: Trajectory Prediction-Based Adaptive Takeover Method for Mobile Robot Teleoperation Systems with Communication Delays
authors: Ruoshan Wang, Pengfei Li, Yun-Sheng Zhao, Yun-Bo Zhao, Yu Kang
abstract: As a typical application of the Internet of Things, mobile robot teleoperation systems play a crucial role in dangerous tasks or special scenarios. The passive takeover mechanism provides effective safety assurance for such systems, relying on a takeover request (TOR) generator to issue warnings and an authority transfer controller to manage control handover. However, variable communication delays can degrade the safety and smoothness of the passive takeover process. To address this issue, we propose a trajectory prediction-based adaptive takeover method that redesigns both the TOR generator and the authority transfer controller. The TOR generator issues early warnings based on the predicted robot trajectory in autonomous control mode. The authority transfer controller uses the control input that tracks the predicted human-intended trajectory as a reconstruction of the delayed human input, and adaptively adjusts the weights between the autonomous input and the reconstructed human input based on the risk indicator and the delay impact level. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through the human-in-the-loop simulation. The results show that our method ensures better safety and smoothness in the takeover process compared to other methods.

title: Bal-DGCN: A Hardware Acceleration Framework for Balanced Computational Efficiency in Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks (DGCNs)
authors: Yingnan Zhao, Ke Wang, Ahmed Louri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks (DGCNs) have emerged as a powerful approach for analyzing dynamic graph-structured data across various applications, such as social networks, recommendation systems, and other domains. Typically, each DGCN layer comprises two distinct modules: a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) module and a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) module with unique data communication and computation patterns.
