By incorporating penalty terms into the loss function, EDPO modifies DPO without violating its principles. Experimental results show that EDPOTrans, using extremely limited monolingual data and less computation than MBR decoding, achieves an average improvement of +2.5 BLEU, +1.4 BLEURT, and +4.4 COMET scores over all baselines in machine translation between Chinese and six low-resource languages.

title: 3DifFusionDet: Diffusion Model for 3D Object Detection with Robust Lidar-Camera Fusion
authors: Xinhao Xiang, Simon Dräger, Jiawei Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: LiDAR-camera fusion has proven effective for 3D object detection by leveraging the complementary strengths of each modality, improving robustness and accuracy. Meanwhile, diffusion models—originally designed for image generation—have shown promising properties such as flexibility, dynamic evaluation, and iterative refinement, and have recently been explored in 2D object detection [1]. However, their potential in 3D object detection remains underexplored. In this work, we propose 3DifFusionDet, a novel framework that formulates 3D object detection as a denoising diffusion process. The model learns to iteratively refine a set of randomly initialized 3D bounding boxes into accurate predictions. Built under a LiDAR-camera fusion pipeline, 3DifFusionDet incorporates a two-branch feature alignment module that employs cross-attention to effectively integrate image and point cloud features. Extensive experiments on KITTI and Waymo Open demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches, with the added benefit of adjustable inference steps to trade off accuracy and speed. Our findings highlight the feasibility and advantages of applying diffusion models to 3D object detection, especially in multi-modal settings.

title: Robust Deepfake Audio Detection via Multi-Level Intermediate Feature Fusion
authors: Jinpeng Zhao, Jian Zhao, Yufei Zhou, Peijia Zheng, Yusong Du
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing deepfake audio detectors, such as the XLSR-Mamba, primarily rely on final-layer features, a paradigm where discriminative details from intermediate layers can be progressively diluted during feature propagation. To alleviate this limitation, we propose a Multi-level Intermediate Feature Fusion (MIFF) module, which explicitly aggregates representations from all Mamba layers. MIFF employs an adaptive recalibration mechanism to dynamically estimate the contribution of each intermediate feature, producing a context-aware weighted fusion. This design preserves discriminative high-level semantics while enriching them with complementary multi-level details, leading to more comprehensive representations. On ASVspoof 2021 DF and In-The-Wild, our system achieves EER of 1.68% and 5.66%, corresponding to relative error reductions of 10.6% and 15.6% over XLSR-Mamba (1.88% and 6.71%), respectively, and demonstrating strong robustness to diverse spoofing attacks.

title: Multisource Localization Using Multimarginal Optimal Transport
authors: Henri Hentilä, Filip Elvander, Visa Koivunen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The problem of localizing multiple non-cooperative signal-emitting sources is studied. Using a set of receivers with known locations, the direction of arrival (DoA) and time difference of arrival (TDoA) of the signals are measured, and these measurements used to perform the localization. Due to the non-cooperative nature of the sources, it is not known which measurement is associated with which source, leading to a joint localization and association problem. This joint problem is solved via a multimarginal version of optimal transport (OT). By combining DoA and TDoA measurements, it is shown that the proposed solution can achieve localization accuracies much higher than when using only one of the two, and close to those achievable when the associations between measurements and sources are known.

title: Reason to Retrieve: Structured Chain-of-Thought for Text-Video Retrieval
authors: Yuhao Chen, Shubin Chen, Qiuming Huang, Guangrun Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Text-Video Retrieval (TVR) faces the challenge of aligning sparse textual queries with rich visual-temporal content. Current methods based on large vision-language models often overlook compositional semantics, while standard datasets lack detailed annotations, limiting generalization. To address this, we propose R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (Reasoning to Retrieve), a framework incorporating structured chain-of-thought reasoning to decompose queries into semantic components (e.g., subject, action, environment) for fine-grained alignment. We also introduce a multi-component scoring mechanism to enhance retrieval accuracy. Furthermore, we construct YouTube CoT 2M (YC2M), the largest reasoning-aware TVR dataset, with 50K videos and 2M structured captions. Experiments demonstrate that R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> consistently improves performance while maintaining efficiency, offering a practical solution for semantic video retrieval.

title: Kalman Filter Based Linear Deformable for Retinal Vessel Segmentation
authors: Zhihao Zhao, Yinzheng Zhao, Junjie Yang, Quanmin Liang, Daniel Zapp, Kai Huang, M.Ali Nasseri
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In the realm of ophthalmic imaging, accurate vascular segmentation is paramount for diagnosing and managing various eye diseases. Contemporary deep learning-based vascular segmentation models rival human accuracy but still face substantial challenges in accurately segmenting minuscule blood vessels in neural network applications. In this paper, we propose a novel network (KaLDeX) for vascular segmentation leveraging a Kalman filter based linear deformable cross attention (LDCA) module integrated within a UNet++ framework. Our approach is based on two key components: Kalman filter (KF) based linear deformable convolution (LD) and cross-attention (CA) modules. The LD module is designed to adaptively adjust the focus on thin vessels that might be overlooked in standard convolution. The CA module improves the global understanding of vascular structures by aggregating the detailed features from the LD module with the high-level features from the UNet++ architecture. Finally, we adopt a topological loss function based on persistent homology to constrain the topological continuity of the segmentation. The proposed method is evaluated on retinal fundus image datasets (DRIVE, CHASE BD1, and STARE) as well as the 3 mm and 6 mm of the OCTA-500 dataset, achieving an average accuracy (ACC) of 97.25%, 97.77%, 97.85%, 98.89%, and 98.21%, respectively.

title: Hierarchical Sparse Vector Transmission for Ultra Reliable and Low Latency Communications
authors: Yanfeng Zhang, Xi’An Fan, Jinkai Zheng, Xiaoye Jing, Weiwei Yang, Xu Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse vector transmission (SVT) is a promising candidate technology for achieving ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC). In this paper, a hierarchical SVT scheme is proposed for multi-user URLLC scenarios. The hierarchical SVT scheme partitions the transmitted bits into common and private parts. The common information is conveyed by the indices of non-zero sections in a sparse vector, while each user’s private information is embedded into non-zero blocks with specific block lengths. At the receiver, the common bits are first recovered from the detected non-zero sections, followed by user-specific private bits decoding based on the corresponding non-zero block indices. Simulation results show the proposed scheme outperforms state-of-the-art SVT schemes in terms of block error rate.

title: CVaR-Aware Network Slicing for Tail Latency Under Tiered Deadlines
authors: Shujia Niu, Qixin Peng, Zhenli He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Real-time distributed computing must satisfy tiered deadlines on shared, heterogeneous infrastructure, where policies that optimize averages often leave tail latency uncontrolled and vulnerable to bursts. We present a conditional value-at-risk (CVaR)-aware network slicing framework that provides end-to-end resource isolation and explicitly optimizes the tail of the delay distribution while enforcing hard-deadline reliability targets. The controller operates in two regimes: when resources are sufficient, it minimizes the CVaR of soft-deadline exceedance and exploits problem monotonicity with a safeguarded hybrid solver that blends adaptive Newton steps with Illinois backstops for global convergence; when resources are scarce, we propose a Priority-Guided Saturation Search (PGSS) that decomposes the Lagrangian conditions into analytically solvable subproblems, allocating additional capacity to delay-sensitive slices without black-box search. Across high-load simulations, our approach compresses extreme tail delays and maintains hard-deadline violation probabilities below target bounds, outperforming CVaR-TD3, PSO-based, Lagrangian-search, and proportional baselines. The framework demonstrates effective tail risk control capability, making it attractive for safety-critical and mission-critical services.

title: Adaptive Task-Incremental Learning For Underwater Acoustic Recognition Based on Mixture-of-Experts Adapter
authors: Yang Zhang, Changjian Wang, Weiguo Chen, Yuan Yuan, Yingzhi Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Underwater acoustic target recognition employs acoustic signals to identify and classify marine objects. In practical applications, the frequent emergence of new tasks makes incremental learning a crucial research direction. Current mainstream methods largely depend on parameter isolation to alleviate catastrophic forgetting. However, these approaches require explicit task identification to activate corresponding sub-networks and often overlook potential correlations among different tasks. To tackle these limitations, we propose an adaptive task-incremental learning framework based on a Mixture-of-Experts Adapter that incorporates autonomous task perception. Our method combines a pre-trained acoustic model with Mixture-of-Experts structure to facilitate cross-task knowledge sharing through task-specific routing. Additionally, we designed a replay-aware task identification module where a lightweight network generates task-aware embeddings and leverages the replay data distribution for discrimination, thereby enabling automatic and adaptive task recognition. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance in terms of average forgetting rate compared to existing methods, while reducing parameter training overhead by more than 90% compared to full-parameter fine-tuning.

title: Radiance Field Rendering with Adaptive Compact Kernel for Novel View Synthesis
authors: Binghao Wu, Haiyang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful explicit representation for novel view synthesis. However, its fixed Gaussian kernels introduce two key limitations: over-smoothed reconstructions due to limited adaptability, and truncation artifacts stemming from the kernel’s long-tailed and unbounded nature. To address these challenges, we present the Adaptive Compact Kernel (ACK), a shape-adaptive and boundary-aware representation that dynamically adjusts its spatial profile to capture both low-frequency structures and fine-grained details, while inherently eliminating truncation artifacts. In addition, we introduce a parameter compression scheme that reorganizes and quantizes primitives into compact PNG-based representations, achieving up to 82% storage reduction and 13% improvement in loading efficiency, with negligible degradation in rendering quality. Extensive experiments on four widely used benchmark datasets, including Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, Deep Blending, and NeRF Synthetic, demonstrate that our method achieves superior efficiency and fidelity over prior state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Aligning Language Models for Lyric-to-Melody Generation with Rule-Based Musical Constraints
authors: Hao Meng, Siyuan Zheng, Shuran Zhou, Qiangqiang Wang, Yang Song
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise in lyric-to-melody generation, but models trained with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) often produce musically implausible melodies with issues like poor rhythm and unsuitable vocal ranges, a phenomenon we term "constraint violation". To address this, we propose a novel alignment framework that instills musical knowledge without human annotation. We define rule-based musical constraints to automatically generate a preference dataset from an SFT model’s outputs. The model is then aligned through a sequential process, first using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on paired preference data, followed by Kahneman-Tversky Optimization (KTO) on unpaired negative samples. Experimental results demonstrate that our aligned model substantially reduces rule violations and outperforms strong baselines in both objective and subjective evaluations, generating melodies with substantially improved musicality and coherence. An interactive demo with audio comparisons is available at https: //arain233.github.io/AligningMelody-demo.

title: Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoding for Irregular Microphone Arrays
authors: Chaoqun Zhuang, Xue Wen, Lin Ma, Lizhong Wang, Liang Wen, Jaehyun Kim, Gangyoul Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Ambisonic encoding from irregular microphone arrays is challenging due to spatial aliasing and limited coverage of the sound field. Existing deep learning approaches typically operate on fixed time–frequency representations (e.g., STFT), which restricts the network’s ability to fully exploit high level spatial correlations. In this work, we propose a Deep Spatial Clue Informed Ambisonic Encoder, a fully end-to-end deep neural network (DNN) that estimates a nonlinear transformation in a learned latent space. Explicit spatial clues (IPD, ILD) are fused with deep spectral features to guide the model toward physically consistent mappings. Unlike prior work, the transformation is applied to latent feature maps instead of shallow spectra, enabling adaptive representation learning and more accurate reconstruction. Experiments on real commercial mobile phone microphone arrays in multi-source reverberant scenes demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms least squares and recent neural ambisonic encoders across spatial similarity, spectral error, and SI-SDR metrics, while requiring fewer parameters than competing neural methods.

title: Bayesian Uncertainty-Aware MRI Reconstruction
authors: Ahmed Karam Eldaly, Matteo Figini, Daniel C. Alexander
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose a novel framework for joint magnetic resonance image reconstruction and uncertainty quantification using under-sampled k-space measurements. The problem is formulated as a Bayesian linear inverse problem, where prior distributions are assigned to the unknown model parameters. Specifically, we assume the target image is sparse in its spatial gradient and impose a total variation prior model. A Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, based on a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler, is then used to sample from the resulting joint posterior distribution of the unknown parameters. Experiments conducted using single- and multi-coil datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed framework over optimisation-based compressed sensing algorithms. Additionally, our framework effectively quantifies uncertainty, showing strong correlation with error maps computed from reconstructed and ground-truth images.

title: Boosting Contextual Adaptive Policy Learning with Foundation Model Guidance
authors: Yuanfei Wang, Hao Dong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Realistic multi-agent environments require agents to cooperate or compete with unfamiliar partners or opponents, which demands efficient exploration and the construction of informative interaction contexts to model and adapt to others. Reinforcement learning methods often struggle to learn such contextual adaptive policies, as environments do not directly reward the long-horizon exploration necessary for successful modeling and adaptation. While recent approaches leverage foundation models to build generalizable and adaptive agents, invoking these models during interaction is both computationally expensive and time-consuming. To address these challenges, we propose FORCAP, a novel framework that performs offline reasoning to generate context functions, from which an expert policy and a reward function are derived to accelerate RL training. FORCAP harnesses the rich knowledge of foundation models while minimizing inference cost. Experiments on both cooperative and competitive multi-agent benchmarks demonstrate that FORCAP significantly improves both training efficiency and performance.

title: A Benchmark Dataset and Baseline Framework for Action Recognition in Power Construction Safety
authors: Shuo Zhang, Xiaoqing Zheng, Weibin Zhang, Yaguang Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Unsafe behaviors of workers are a major cause of accidents in power engineering, while traditional manual inspections struggle to monitor complex, dynamic construction sites. Although video understanding has been widely applied to action recognition, power construction faces challenges such as large target scale variations, complex occlusions, and imbalanced class distributions. To address this, we introduce PCVA, a benchmark dataset for action recognition in power construction safety, and propose MSHCNet, a multi-scale spatio-temporal hierarchical collaborative network with an Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Hierarchical Module (ASHM) as a strong baseline. MSHCNet captures temporal dynamics and adaptively fuses multi-scale features for hierarchical action modeling. Experiments on PCVA show MSHCNet achieves 38.16 mAP, outperforming the previous best method by 7.54%, effectively supporting safety monitoring and accident prevention. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/ys-sy/MSHCNet.

title: Domain-Invariant Mixed-Domain Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation with Clustered Maximum Mean Discrepancy Alignment
authors: Ba-Thinh Lam, Thanh-Huy Nguyen, Hoang-Thien Nguyen, Quang-Khai Bui-Tran, Nguyen Lan Vi Vu, Phat K. Huynh, Ulas Bagci, Min Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning has shown remarkable progress in medical image semantic segmentation, yet its success heavily depends on large-scale expert annotations and consistent data distributions. In practice, annotations are scarce and images are collected from multiple scanners or centers, leading to mixed-domain settings with unknown domain labels and severe domain gaps. Existing semi-supervised or domain adaptation approaches typically assume either a single domain shift or access to explicit domain indices, which rarely hold in real-world deployment. In this paper, we propose a domain-invariant mixed-domain semi-supervised segmentation framework that jointly enhances data diversity and mitigates domain bias. A Copy-Paste Mechanism (CPM) augments the training set by transferring informative regions across domains, while a Cluster Maximum Mean Discrepancy (CMMD) block clusters unlabeled features and aligns them with labeled anchors via an MMD objective, encouraging domain-invariant representations. Integrated within a teacher–student framework, our method achieves robust and precise segmentation even with very few labeled examples and multiple unknown domain discrepancies. Experiments on Fundus and M&Ms benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently surpasses semi-supervised and domain adaptation methods, establishing a potential solution for mixed-domain semi-supervised medical image segmentation.

title: PEAG-Det: A Dual-Stream Phase- and Edge-Aware Framework with Adaptive Gating for Cross-Phase Tumor Detection
authors: Zhiqiang Yang, Qiu Guan, Xueliang Zhang, Chenyan Wu, Xinli Xu, Huafeng Liu, Ying Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Early tumor detection in CT is essential for improving patient outcomes, but current detectors struggle with subtle pathological features and poor use of multi-phase imaging, creating a gap with radiological workflows. This challenge is particularly pronounced in pancreatic cystic neoplasms (PCN), where benign and malignant lesions are easily confused and multi-phase analysis is often required. Motivated by this, we propose PEAG-Det, a dual-stream framework that leverages prior knowledge to learn temporal-phase feature importance through primary and auxiliary branches, integrating three core innovations. First, the Re-parameterized Heterogeneous Edge-Aware (RepHEA) module employs multi-scale axial convolutions and large-kernel attention to enhance sensitivity to tumor boundary gradients and calcification structures. Second, the Phase-Aware Cross-Attention Fusion (PCAF) module enables implicit registration and robust cross-phase interaction to enhance complementary feature integration between non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT. Finally, the Dual-Path Adaptive Gating (DPAG) module adopts a dual-branch gating attention mechanism that adaptively reinforces cross-phase consensus activations while recovering phase-specific cues. Ultimately, PEAG-Det achieves superior performance on both pancreatic CT and brain MRI datasets, surpassing the dual-stream YOLOv12s by 8.7% and 2.2% AP while using less than half the parameters, demonstrating both robust task-level performance and strong cross-organ generalization. The code is available at https://github.com/yang-0201/PEAGDet.

title: SAM-Driven Multi-Scale Gated Network for Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
authors: Wei Wang, Keqi Hou, Xin Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low feature fusion efficiency and inadequate modal representations are two major problems with current approaches, despite the fact that multimodal remote sensing data fusion offers abundant information for accurate semantic segmentation. This study proposes an adaptive multi-modal multi-scale gated network based on SAM— SAM-AM3GNet. This network utilizes the Segment Anything Model(SAM) and markedly enhances the efficacy of multimodal remote sensing image segmentation through the incorporation of three pivotal modules: Multi-Modal Multi-Scale Adapter(M3Adapter), Adaptive Gated Modal Fusion Module (AGMFM), Refined Attention Enhancement Block(RAEB). The mean Intersection over Union(mIoU) for the ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam datasets were 84.66% and 86.49%, respectively. A substantial body of experimental evidence suggests that our proposed SAM-AM3GNet outperforms currently prevalent methods. The implementation of our method is publicly accessible at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SAMSegmentation

title: Parallax-Aware Spatial Transformer: Fusing Physics and Learning for Terahertz Near-Field Localization
authors: Zhi Zeng, Chong Han
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As a key 6G enabler, Terahertz (THz) ultra-massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) introduces a critical localization challenge. The large aperture expands the near-field range, invalidating far-field models and creating a prohibitive search space. This leads to a dilemma that physics-based near-field codebooks require excessive overhead, while generic deep learning models lack physical consistency or interpretability. Challenging the notion that near-field training is necessary, a Parallax-Aware Spatial Transformer (PAST) is proposed to resolve this dilemma by leveraging near-field parallax effects from the lightweight far-field codebook. This introduces a physics-informed attention bias to focus on the most crucial subarray pairs, while maintaining physics consistency. Simulation results demonstrate PAST achieves millimeter-level and 0.06-degree accuracy, defining a new and highly efficient approach for THz near-field localization.

title: Revisiting Backdoor Threat in Federated Instruction Tuning From a Signal Aggregation Perspective
authors: Haodong Zhao, Jinming Hu, Gongshen Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning security research has predominantly focused on backdoor threats from a minority of malicious clients that intentionally corrupt model updates. This paper challenges this paradigm by investigating a more pervasive and insidious threat: backdoor vulnerabilities from low-concentration poisoned data distributed across the datasets of benign clients. This scenario is increasingly common in federated instruction tuning for language models, which often rely on unverified third-party and crowd-sourced data. We analyze two forms of backdoor data through real cases: 1) natural trigger (inherent features as implicit triggers); 2) adversary-injected trigger. To analyze this threat, we model the backdoor implantation process from signal aggregation, proposing the Backdoor Signal-to-Noise Ratio to quantify the dynamics of the distributed backdoor signal. Extensive experiments reveal the severity of this threat: With just less than 10% of training data poisoned and distributed across clients, the attack success rate exceeds 85%, while the primary task performance remains largely intact. Critically, we demonstrate that state-of-the-art backdoor defenses, designed for attacks from malicious clients, are fundamentally ineffective against this threat. Our findings highlight an urgent need for new defense mechanisms tailored to the realities of modern, decentralized data ecosystems.

title: The DFKI-SLT System for ESDD 2026: BiCrossMamba-ST with Attentive SSL Fusion
authors: Yassine El Kheir, Arnab Das, Enes Erdem Erdogan, Feidi Kallel, Tim Polzehl, Sebastian Möller
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Distinguishing authentic from deepfake environmental sounds requires capturing subtle spectro-temporal artifacts across multiple scales. This paper presents our solution for the Environmental Sound Deepfake Detection (ESDD) Challenge 2026. We propose a unified framework that synergizes representations from large-scale Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) models using an attentive layer fusion mechanism to bridge hierarchical features. These representations are processed by a BiCrossMamba-ST backend, a bidirectional selective state space model optimized for temporal consistency. Our ensemble approach demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, securing 2nd place in Track 1 (0.80% EER) and 1st place in Track 2 (0.25% EER).

title: Reinforcement Learning for Optimized Adaptive Sampling
authors: Ryan Pai, John Lipor, Peter Gerstoft
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Level set estimation (LSE)–––finding all points in a domain whose function value lies above or below a known threshold–––is an effective and demonstrated approach to understand the spatial variability of the geoacoustic properties of the ocean floor. The vast scales involved with estimating seabed properties using autonomous underwater vehicles make naïve approaches to spatial sampling expensive or infeasible. Modeling the seabed variation as a Gaussian process (GP) enables an efficient framework that informs adaptive sampling policies. Existing approaches to adaptive sampling utilize heuristics aimed at reducing various forms of uncertainty. We train a reinforcement learning agent using the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) method, learning a policy that considers the entire the sampling trajectory. Instead of using the GP to determine subsequent samples, we use it as observational input for the SAC agent. Results on synthetic GP fields and realistic seabed data show that our approach obtains superior accuracy, uncertainty reduction, and a lower computational cost than existing approaches.

title: From PowerSGD to PowerSGD+: Low-Rank Gradient Compression For Distributed Optimization With Convergence Guarantees
authors: Shengping Xie, Chuyan Chen, Kun Yuan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-rank gradient compression methods, such as PowerSGD, have gained attention in communication-efficient distributed optimization. However, the convergence guarantees of PowerSGD remain unclear, particularly in stochastic settings. In this paper, we show that PowerSGD does not always converge to the optimal solution and provide a clear counter-example to support this finding. To address this, we introduce PowerSGD+, which periodically updates the projection subspace via singular value decomposition, ensuring that it remains aligned with the optimal subspace. We prove that PowerSGD+ converges under standard assumptions and validate its effectiveness through empirical evaluation on large language model tasks.

title: Semantic Relation-Enhanced Clip Adapter for Domain Adaptive Zero-Shot Learning
authors: Jiaao Yu, Mingjie Han, Jinkun Jiang, Junyu Dong, Tao Gong, Man Lan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The high cost of data annotation has spurred research on training deep learning models in data-limited scenarios. Existing paradigms, however, fail to balance cross-domain transfer and cross-category generalization, giving rise to the demand for Domain-Adaptive Zero-Shot Learning (DAZSL). Although vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) have inherent advantages in the DAZSL field, current studies do not fully exploit their potential. Applying CLIP to DAZSL faces two core challenges: inefficient cross-category knowledge transfer due to the lack of semantic relation guidance, and degraded cross-modal alignment during target domain fine-tuning. To address these issues, we propose a Semantic Relation-Enhanced CLIP (SRE-CLIP) Adapter framework, integrating a Semantic Relation Structure Loss and a Cross-Modal Alignment Retention Strategy. As the first CLIP-based DAZSL method, SRE-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on the I2AwA and I2WebV benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches.

title: Channel-Adaptive Robust Aggregation for Over-the-Air Federated Learning in Heterogeneous Networks
authors: Zubaida Fatima, Zubair Shaban, Yusuf Jamal, Nazreen Shah, Ranjitha Prasad, B. N. Bharath
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing demand for privacy-preserving, data-intensive applications such as IoT, augmented reality, and autonomous systems positions Federated Learning (FL) as a key enabler in 6G networks. Over-the-Air FL (OTA-FL) leverages the superposition property of the wireless multiple access channel for efficient aggregation via simultaneous transmissions. Existing methods rely on fixed aggregation schedules and do not jointly address noise, fading, and client heterogeneity. We propose CHARGE-FL (CHannel-Adaptive Robust agGrEgation), a framework that adaptively schedules aggregation based on channel dynamics and application readiness. By combining a tailored optimization strategy with a dual-purpose precoding mechanism, CHARGE-FL mitigates channel distortion and bias from partial updates, achieving superior accuracy, stability, and convergence under realistic wireless conditions. Empirical results under realistic wireless conditions show that CHARGE-FL significantly improves accuracy, stability, and convergence over state-ofthe-art OTA-FL methods, particularly in straggler-prone and noisy scenarios.

title: Wavelet-Aware Anomaly Detection in Multi-Channel User Logs via Deviation Modulation and Resolution-Adaptive Attention
authors: Kaichuan Kong, Dongjie Liu, Xiaobo Jin, Shijie Xu, Guanggang Geng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Insider threat detection is a key challenge in enterprise security, relying on user activity logs that capture rich and complex behavioral patterns. These logs are often multi-channel, non-stationary, and anomalies are rare, making anomaly detection challenging. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework that integrates wavelet-aware modulation, multi-resolution wavelet decomposition, and resolution-adaptive attention for robust anomaly detection. Our approach first applies a deviation-aware modulation scheme to suppress routine behaviors while amplifying anomalous deviations. Next, discrete wavelet transform (DWT) decomposes the log signals into multi-resolution representations, capturing both long-term trends and short-term anomalies. Finally, a learnable attention mechanism dynamically reweights the most discriminative frequency bands for detection. On the CERT r4.2 benchmark, our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines in precision, recall, and F1 score across various time granularities and scenarios.

title: MHAN: A Multi-Module Hybrid Attention Network for Robust Classification of Appendiceal Cancer and Appendicitis
authors: Rengmin Wu, Dan Liang, Jiaqiang Li, Yongtao Zhang, Wenzheng Hu, Hui Zhou, Guangming Li, Xinhua Wei, Tianfu Wang, Baiying Lei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal disease, but appendiceal cancer is a rare malignancy. The similarities between the two diseases often lead to missed diagnosis in clinical practice, resulting in serious consequences. To address this problem, we propose a multi-module hybrid attention network (MHAN) to tackle the challenge of classifying appendiceal cancer and appendicitis in CT images. Our framework comprises four innovative modules. The multi-level feature extraction (MFE) enhances the multi-scale representation of small lesions. The hypergraph-based feature interaction (HFI) captures high-order cross-layer dependencies. The agent attention fusion (AAF) supports cross-layer recursive semantically guided enhancement. Finally, a reinforcement learning-based Dynamic loss optimization (RDLO) improves robustness in imbalanced data problem. We validate the proposed model on both internal and external datasets, and the results demonstrate significant improvements in lesion representation and diagnostic performance.

title: Tass: Task Aligned Subspace Selection for Knowledge Preserving Fine-Tuning
authors: Qi Jing, Peng Qiao, Rongchun Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has become a prominent approach for supervised fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs). Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is among the most widely adopted PEFT methods due to its computational and storage efficiency. However, the low-rank update matrices in LoRA often contain task-irrelevant directions, introducing redundant information that can impair downstream performance and reduce parameter efficiency. To this end, we propose Task Aligned Subspace Selection (TASS), a novel method that bridges pre-trained weights and downstream tasks. Specifically, we compute the principal angles between the pre-training weight matrix W<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> and the low-rank update matrix ΔW to identify the subspace of W<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> that is most relevant to the task. This subspace then guides an adaptive rank reduction of the trained LoRA matrices A and B, effectively removing redundant information while preserving task-relevant knowledge. Experiments demonstrate that TASS consistently outperforms vanilla LoRA and other competitive PEFT methods across diverse tasks, with average gains of +4.76 over LoRA and +1.84 over the best PEFT method.

title: MoRE: Multimodal Relationship Enhancement for Unbiased Scene Graph Generation
authors: Ruiqi Hu, Lida Huang, Bin Jiang, Chao Yang, Jianbo Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) extracts relational triplets 〈subject, predicate, object〉 from images, yet current benchmarks suffer from long-tailed distributions and semantic ambiguity, causing biased predictions toward generic relations (e.g., "on") and neglecting fine-grained ones (e.g., "parked on"). Existing balancing strategies reallocate predicates but largely ignore contextual semantics and underexploit missing relations. We propose Multimodal Relationship Enhancement (MoRE), a framework that improves relation prediction by addressing contextual inconsistency and annotation sparsity. MoRE introduces a Context-relation Bank to dynamically retrieve candidate predicates, and employs Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with context-driven prompts for semantically consistent relation selection. Experiments on VG and GQA demonstrate that MoRE achieves state-of-the-art performance on unbiased metrics, enhancing predicate diversity and prediction balance for SGG.

title: Random Matrix-Driven Graph Representation Learning For Bioacoustic Recognition
authors: Biaohang Yuan, Jiangzhao Wang, YuKai Hao, Ruzhen Chen, Yan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Ecoacoustic monitoring underpins global biodiversity assessment. However, the scarcity of data presents challenges in acoustic signal processing, leading to limited model accuracy due to inadequate training samples. In this work, we propose a Random Matrix-Driven Graph Representation Learning (RM-GRL) framework that utilizes time-frequency hypergraphs and the distance-preserving properties of random matrix theory. By introducing a learnable scaling factor, denoted as α, the framework dynamically adjusts cross-channel weights to effectively capture global and local time-frequency features. Additionally, the inclusion of superedges maintains harmonic structure by connecting time-frequency nodes within the same harmonic component. Our method employs a three-channel Mel spectrogram (log-Mel, delta, delta-delta) and addresses data imbalance by explicitly modeling the harmonic repetition period of the acoustic signal and the temporal irregularity of transient events. The RM-GRL framework is evaluated on the Birdsdata dataset for tasks involving natural sound detection and bullfrog call detection. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance across four groups of imbalanced positive and negative samples and significantly outperforms four selected baselines.

title: Learning Dual Mixture-of-Experts Models for Unified Image Deraining
authors: Tianyi Yang, Mengke Zhang, Tianshuo Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Despite the remarkable progress achieved in image deraining, most existing approaches remain constrained to handling a single type of rain degradation or a specific rain pattern. In practice, however, rain manifests in diverse forms, which severely limits the applicability of current methods under real-world conditions. To overcome this limitation, we first construct a high-quality benchmark named Hybrid-Rain10K, which integrates three representative rain degradation patterns, including rain streaks, raindrops and rain accumulation. Building upon this benchmark, we propose a dual mixture-of-experts (MoE) model for unified image deraining, which adaptively handles various rain degradations and restores clean images. Where the encoder employs a Soft-MoE to assign collaborative weights across all experts, ensuring comprehensive degradation modeling, while the decoder adopts a Hard-MoE with top-k routing guided by high-frequency priors to enhance reconstruction quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our dataset and model.

title: Video Hashing via Transformer and KAN for Retrieval
authors: Likai Yang, Nianqiao Li, Yihua Chen, Zhenjun Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-Supervised Video Hashing (SSVH) is a promising approach for large-scale video retrieval. However, existing methods often neglect fine-grained spatiotemporal details and the local clustering structure of hash codes, limiting retrieval performance. To address this, we propose a novel SSVH method called VHTK based on Transformer and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) architectures. A key contribution is the Wave-Transformer module, which decomposes frame features via the wavelet transform, employs attention mechanisms to highlight critical spatiotemporal regions, and adopts KAN for flexible nonlinear modeling, thereby enhancing video representation. Additionally, a novel triplet loss function is designed to optimize hash code distribution, promoting compact and semantically consistent binary codes. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that VHTK outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in terms of mean Average Precision.

title: Quantitative Analysis of Master-of-Nets Garden Spaces Based on Space Syntax and Research on Resilient Inheritance of Traditional Gardens

abstract: None

title: A Hybrid-Domain Detector for OTFS Signals
authors: Che-Wei Chang, Chun-Hao Yen, Pei-Jin Su, Hsiang-Heng Yeh, Kuei-Chiang Lai
abstract: This paper presents a reduced-complexity iterative detector for zero-padding orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) systems. In each iteration, the detector first cancels the inter-symbol interference (ISI), followed by removing the inter-Doppler interference (IDI) via a hybrid-domain decision-feedback equalizer (DFE). The DFEś feedforward and feedback filters are efficiently designed to operate in the delay-time and delay-Doppler domains, respectively, by exploiting the OTFS signal structures. Computer simulations over the third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) extended vehicular A (EVA) channel with a velocity of 500 km/hr and perfect channel estimation show that, for 16-QAM, the proposed detector outperforms the maximal-ratio combining detector by about 1.18 dB at a bit-error rate of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-4</sup> , at the cost of about 102.1% increase in complexity.

title: A Highly Reliable Drive Circuit for Solid-State High-Voltage Pulse Generators
authors: Yuanhao Liu, Lei Wang, Hongyu Dai, Jun Huang, Liuxia Li, Yi Liu

title: 3D Gaussian Splatting Texture Editing via Single Modified Image
authors: Hanul Baek, Dohae Lee, Kyumin Kim, In-Kwon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful technique for reconstructing high-quality, photorealistic 3D representations of real-world scenes. However, editing 3DGS remains more challenging than mesh-based approaches as it lacks explicit geometry and requires view-consistent updates across Gaussians. Previous approaches have relied primarily on text-driven generative models for 3D Gaussian editing, limiting direct control over specific visual appearances. In this study, we propose a texture editing framework for 3DGS that leverages only a single user-modified image. Our approach maintains coherence across multiple views while accounting for the corresponding lighting adjustments in the edited region. We introduce three key techniques to achieve this: (1) aligned edit propagation, which transfers local edits from the reference view to other viewpoints; (2) mask-based filtering, which restricts modifications to relevant Gaussians and prevents unintended changes; and (3) opacity-based selection, which identifies Gaussians with the most significant visual impact on the edited texture. Through both qualitative and quantitative evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets, we demonstrate that our method achieves more precise and spatially controllable 3DGS editing than existing techniques. We expect these techniques to pave the way for more intuitive 3D Gaussian Splatting editing pipelines and inspire future research.

title: VTBSAE: Provably Secure Verifier-based Password Authenticated Key Exchange for IIoT
authors: Mingping Qi
abstract: Password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) has gained widespread adoption in prominent platforms such as iCloud to provide realistic security assurance. It offers robust security by enabling mutual authentication and strong session key establishment between two parties via a low-entropy password. This paper presents an efficient verifier-based PAKE protocol called VTBSAE (Verifier-based Two-Basis Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) based on the well-known SAE protocol, aiming to provide light, reliable, and scalable security assurances for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). The primary technological innovation lies in the utilization of two distinct bases over the underlying group to implement the Hash-to-Curve (H2C) function employed in SAE, and transform the symmetric SAE protocol into a verifier-based PAKE protocol, thereby enhancing its resilience against server compromise attacks. Moreover, adopting the widely accepted BPR (Bellare-Pointcheval-Rogaway) security model with further considering the perfect forward secrecy, VTBSAE’s security is formally proved under the standard CDH security assumption. This solid foundation provides VTBSAE with certain security advantages over some existing verifier-based PAKE protocols whose security proofs rely on some stronger security assumptions. VTBSAE can work over both the multiplicative and elliptic curve additive groups. Particularly, in the elliptic curve setting, VTBSAE requires no reliance on the H2C function, which is often challenging to implement securely over the commonly used elliptic curve parameters such as the secp256r1. Therefore, VTBSAE demonstrates significant improvements in ease of secure implementation compared to the existing verifierbased PAKE protocols that rely on the H2C function.

title: Prefrontal Hierarchical Control of Corticomuscular Coherence in Stroke
authors: Jasper I. Mark, Rachana Gangwani, Justin Riddle, Jessica M. Cassidy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Stroke recovery involves reorganization of large-scale brain networks, yet most neurophysiological biomarkers assess either motor or cognitive function in isolation. To address this limitation, we introduce phase-modulated corticomuscular coherence (pmCMC), a novel metric that quantifies how prefrontal delta-phase activity modulates beta-band corticomuscular coherence between the primary motor cortex and peripheral muscle. This approach integrates cross-frequency and corticomuscular coupling, offering a dynamic index of cognitive-motor interaction. We applied pmCMC to a cohort of individuals with hemiparetic stroke who performed a precision grip task during high-density EEG and EMG recording at admission and discharge from an inpatient rehabilitation facility. Our findings reveal that pmCMC increases across rehabilitation and positively relates to corticostriatal but not corticospinal tract injury. Additionally, higher pmCMC at admission was positively associated with greater gains in motor coordination, functional independence, and cognitive status. These results position pmCMC as hybrid neurophysiological measure of cognitive-motor integration relevant to post-stroke recovery and encourages further investigation of its prognostic utility, causal mechanisms, and applicability across diverse motor tasks and clinical populations.

title: Modeling and Capacity Analysis of UAV-Assisted Three-tier Heterogeneous Wireless Networks
authors: Su Zhan, Xiaorong Zhu, Xiaohua Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Sudden disasters frequently disrupt terrestrial cellular infrastructure, severely degrading network capacity in affected regions and necessitating the rapid restoration of uplink connectivity for rescue and monitoring operations. To address this challenge, we propose a systematic capacity analysis framework for a three-tier emergency wireless network comprising a device access layer, an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) relay layer, and a backhaul layer. First, within the access layer, heterogeneous ground devices are modeled using a Poisson point process (PPP). We analyze the uplink transmissions under non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA), deriving the coverage probability and ergodic capacity while accounting for imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC). Next, in the relay layer, multiple concurrent flows are scheduled via time-frequency resource allocation across end-to-end UAV links, incorporating practical communication constraints and multihop packet-loss effects. To balance throughput and fairness, we introduce a joint resource allocation scheme that maximizes a novel satisfaction function. In the backhaul layer, the capacity between UAVs and base stations (BSs) is characterized under space-division multiple access (SDMA) with directional beamforming, explicitly modeling the impact of antenna-orientation jitter at both the UAV and BS sides. Finally, the overall end-to-end system capacity is formulated as the bottleneck capacity among the three tiers. Analytical and simulation results validate the accuracy of the proposed theoretical model. Furthermore, comparative evaluations demonstrate that the proposed resource allocation scheme for the UAV relay layer significantly improves allocation fairness among concurrent flows relative to conventional baselines.

title: VTVC: Vignetting Correction Based on Variant Transformer and Spatial Self Attention
authors: Zhuang He, Haoxin Hu, Haowen Zheng, Hai-Miao Hu
abstract: Vignetting correction is an essential part for image signal processor. It is important for imaging systems to get high quality images. Traditional vignetting correction methods are almost based on traditional hand-crafted features, which usually perform poorly in complex scenario. Recently, deep learningbased vignetting correction methods have been widely studied. However, the receptive fields of these methods are relatively small, which is difficult for them to estimate the overall vignetting of the image accurately. In this paper, we proposed a deep learning-based vignetting correction model which includes three major components: variant transformer structure, multiple layer upsampling and spatial self attention. The variant transformer structure is construct by replacing the multi-layer perceptron of traditional transformer with a structure extractor module, so that to improve the receptive fields for vignetting estimation. The multiple layer upsampling and spatial self attention are used to improve the resolution and preserve the image details, thereby improving the accuracy of vignetting correction. Additionally, due to the lack of vignetting correction dataset, we introduced a method to get vignetting images through capturing the real world, and construct the first thousand-level vignetting correction dataset. Besides, we also proposed a data augmentation method based on the optical principle of vignetting. Through training on the large amount of real-world vignetting dataset, the proposed vignetting correction network can be trained accurately. Many experiments have been done on the conducted dataset. Through the comparison with the state-of-the-art algorithms, it has been verified that the proposed vignetting correction network demonstrates the best performance.

title: A Water-Controlled Dielectric Resonator Antenna with Unilateral and Omnidirectional Pattern Reconfigurability
authors: Xuan-Ji Wu, Yu-Tao Liang, Zhe Chen, Tao Yuan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a reconfigurable rectangular dielectric resonator antenna (RDRA) whose radiation patterns can be switched by controlling the liquid water. By loading a high-permittivity material to modify the feeding source, specific resonant modes of the RDRA are excited, thereby achieving reconfigurable radiation patterns. Based on this concept, a 3D-printed microfluidic container is integrated at the bottom of the RDRA. By injecting water into different sections of the container, reconfigurable control of the radiation pattern is realized. To validate the proposed idea, a prototype is fabricated and measured, demonstrating excellent overall performance, including five distinct patterns with an overlapping bandwidth of 20% while maintaining a compact size. The demonstrated performance indicates that the proposed antenna is well-suited for integrated sensing and communication applications.

title: Zr-Based Contacts to n-Type AlN with Improved Performance for Power and High-Temperature Electronics
authors: Zhongyunshen Zhu, Hridibrata Pal, Aditya Dev Varma, Masataka Imura, Hironori Okumura, Tomás Palacios
abstract: This work reports improved ohmic contacts on n-type AlN using a Zr/Al/Mo/Au metal stack with contact resistivities of ~10-3 Ω⋅cm2 at 25°C and ~10-5 Ω⋅cm2 at 500°C, which are over one and two orders of magnitude lower than Ti-based contacts, respectively. Temperature-dependent current-voltage characteristics reveal thermionic-field emission-dominated transport at the Zr/AlN interface, leading to improved conductivity and ohmic behavior above 400°C. Record-low specific on-resistance (Ron,sp) is achieved in AlN Schottky barrier diodes using Zr-based cathode compared to previously reported values at comparable breakdown voltages. The devices reveal 10× enhanced Baliga Figure of Merit at 300°C and 500°C due to significantly lowered Ron,sp < 50 mΩ⋅cm2.

title: Pre-trained Invertible Neural Network Assisted High-Dimensional Bayesian Optimization Framework for Wind Farm Layout Design
authors: Yihuan Zhou, Wenchao Meng, Yuhang Zhao, Jie Zhao, Kai Zhang, Qinmin Yang
abstract: Wake effects in wind farms degrade energy capture and turbine reliability, underscoring the importance of layout optimization. Existing studies either rely on analytical wake models at the expense of accuracy or combine numerical wake models with heuristic algorithms at the expense of efficiency. In contrast, we propose a pre-trained invertible neural network assisted high-dimensional Bayesian optimization framework for wind farm layout design, achieving both high accuracy and high efficiency. Specifically, this study employs a high-fidelity numerical wake model to ensure accurate wake estimation and develops a Bayesian optimization (BO)–based framework to enhance search efficiency. An invertible neural network (INN) is specifically designed and, for the first time, integrated with BO to alleviate the curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional layout spaces. The proposed INN-BO framework is evaluated under five distinct and progressively complex scenarios, including the real offshore data from California. Comparative evaluations against state-of-the-art algorithms demonstrate that INN-BO achieves superior solution quality with reduced convergence time, highlighting its strong generalizability and practical relevance to real-world wind farm design.

title: Leveraging Llms for Functional Clustering of Microservices
authors: Santiago Di Sabatto, Guillermo Rodriguez, Claudia Marcos, Santiago Vidal
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Microservices architectures often suffer from fragmentation and complexity, making system maintenance, scalability, and evolution increasingly difficult. A key challenge in this context is how to effectively group microservices into coherent functional modules. This paper proposes a novel approach for the functional grouping of microservices using Large Language Models (lLMs) and clustering techniques. Our methodology combines semantic and structural analysis of microservices based on their OpenAPI specifications and was validated using real-world microservice datasets. The results demonstrate that integrating semantic and structural similarities leads to more coherent and manageable groupings. Furthermore, leveraging ChatGPT to generate missing endpoint descriptions significantly improved clustering accuracy, achieving a 60 % higher success rate compared to methods without automatic description generation. Our findings suggest that the proposed approach not only enhances the maintainability and scalability of microservice architectures but also streamlines development and deployment workflows, offering a scalable and efficient solution for service organization.

title: When Video Compression Meets Multimodal Large Language Models: A Unified Paradigm for Cross-Modality Video Compression_supp1-3673193.pdf
authors: Shiqi Wang
abstract: Traditional video compression methods perform well at high bitrates but struggle to preserve fine-grained semantic information at low bitrates. Recently, with the blossoming of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), Cross-modal compression techniques offer prospective solutions for improving video compression under low-bitrate conditions. In this paper, we propose a unified Cross-Modality Video Compression (CMVC) framework that integrates multimodal representations and video generative models. The encoder disentangles video into spatial and temporal components, which are mapped to compact cross modal representations using MLLMs. During decoding, different encoding-decoding modes are employed to acquire various video reconstruction qualities, including Text-Text-to-Video (TT2V) for semantic preservation and Image-Text-to-Video (IT2V) for perceptual consistency. Additionally, we elaborate on an efficient frame interpolation model using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to improve the perceptual quality. Experimental results demon strate that TT2V achieves effective semantic reconstruction, while IT2V ensures competitive perceptual consistency. These findings suggest the potential of leveraging multimodal priors to improve video compression, offering promising future research directions.

title: A Novel Technique to Discriminate Interference in the Time-Frequency Plane and Localize Mode Crossing
authors: Sylvain Meignen, Vittoria Bruni, Marcelo A. Colominas
abstract: The localization of interference associated with crossing modes in the time-frequency (TF) plane is of significant interest when the objective is to separate these modes. Prior to localizing mode crossings, it is essential to determine whether the observed interference arises from closely spaced modes or from actual mode crossings. In this paper, we propose a novel technique to discriminate between these different types of interference in the TF plane and to subsequently localize mode crossings. The proposed approach exhibits strong adaptability across a wide range of scenarios and demonstrates robustness to noise, as confirmed by simulation results.

title: Sparsity-Constrained NMF Tokenization for Interpretable Medical Signal Classification
authors: Sridharan Sankaran
abstract: The deployment of deep learning in healthcare is often hindered by the “black-box” nature of standard models. Conventional approaches tokenize physiological spectrograms into arbitrary image patches, severing the link between model inputs and physical signal sources. In this letter, we propose a classification approach that uses sparsity-constrained NMF atoms as interpretable features for transparent medical diagnostics. By utilizing Kullback-Leibler divergence NMF, we decompose spectrograms into physically meaningful frequency atoms. Experimental results on the PhysioNet 2016 dataset ($n=3,541$) demonstrate that our method yields diagnostic heatmaps with high Map Sparsity (0.74, median) and Spectral Concentration (0.65), significantly outperforming Grad-CAM baselines ($p < 0.001$). While incurring a statistically significant accuracy trade-off compared to black-box CNNs ($83.6\%$ vs. $90.3\%$, $p < 0.01$), our approach yields structurally interpretable features that align with physiological events, establishing a verifiable foundation for trustworthy medical diagnostics. To facilitate reproducibility, our code is available publicly.

title: A K-/Ka-Band mmWave Radio-Over-Fiber Transceiver Front-End With Co-Designed SiGe EIC and SiPh PIC for Intrasatellite Links
authors: Torben Onselaere, Gaël Jongbloet, Kieran De Bruyn, Marijn Werbrouck, Reinier Broucke, Jasper Jans, Cedric Bruynsteen, Johan Bauwelinck, Nishant Singh, Guy Torfs
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: To meet the demand for low cost, size, weight, and power (C-SWAP) signal routing in the growing satellite and space markets, we propose and demonstrate an all-silicon fully integrated mmWave radio-over-fiber (RoF) transceiver front-end for frequency bands in the range of 18–36 GHz. The opto-electronic front-end consists of a radio frequency integrated circuit (RFIC) with both transmit and receive paths manufactured in a radiation-tolerant SiGe process, connected to the optical components contained on silicon photonic (SiPh) photonic integrated circuits (PICs). The RX uses a Ge PD, followed by a resonant photoreceiver and a resonant driver on the RFIC. The TX uses a resonant predriver and resonant driver on the RFIC, and an SiPh traveling-wave Mach–Zehnder modulator (TW-MZM) on the PIC. The RX and TX on the RFIC exhibit a total DC power consumption of 212 and 230 mW, respectively. The full-RoF-link is characterized through vector network analyzer (VNA) measurements, and its performance is verified through time-domain experiments at different carrier frequencies, resulting in 1-GBd 128-QAM with 5.2% EVM and 5-GBd 64-QAM with 9.1% EVM on a 22-GHz carrier, and 10-GBd 16-QAM/32-QAM with 11.2% EVM on a 28-GHz carrier.

title: Towards Robust Classification of Non-Manual Signals in Korean Sign Language under Extreme Class Imbalance
authors: Seungjae Lee, Jinha Song, Hyeonjoong Cho
abstract: Non-manual signals, such as facial expressions and eye gaze, play a crucial role in sign language communication, comparable to manual signals. However, previous studies in sign language processing have primarily focused on manual signals and often treat non-manual signals only as auxiliary cues for improving recognition or translation performance. Furthermore, most existing sign language datasets lack explicit annotations for non-manual signals due to the high cost of manual labeling, which limits systematic analysis and independent modeling of these signals. This limitation remains an ongoing challenge in sign language research. To address this gap, we conduct a systematic study on non-manual signal classification using the Korean–Korean Sign Language Parallel Corpus 2022 (Version 1.0), one of the few publicly available datasets that provides structured annotations for non-manual signals. However, the dataset suffers from severe class imbalance among annotated non-manual signal labels, making effective learning difficult—particularly for underrepresented classes. To address this problem, we propose an improved framework that systematically analyzes and classifies non-manual signals using long-tail learning techniques, including loss reweighting, resampling, and focal-based optimization. Experimental results show that the proposed approach improves the Macro-F1 score from 0.6065 to 0.7610 compared with the baseline model, achieving substantial gains for minority classes. We further demonstrate that the model can estimate the temporal occurrence of non-manual signals without explicit frame-level annotations, enabling soft-label generation for unlabeled data. These findings provide a practical direction for reducing annotation costs and supporting scalable analysis of non-manual signals in sign language understanding.

title: Towards a Large Language Model for Guarani-Jopara: Challenges, and Early Outcomes
authors: Margarita Ruiz-Olazar, Rubén Díaz, Diego Díaz, Emilio Díaz, Diego Ihara, Marvin Agüero-Torales
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work presents ongoing research on the development of large language models (LLMs) tailored for GuaraniJopara, a mixed variety of the Guarani language influenced by Spanish and widely spoken in Paraguay. Given the scarcity of digital resources in this language, we constructed a synthetic corpus by translating the Stanford Alpaca dataset into GuaraniJopara using the Google Translate API. The resulting dataset incorporates code-switching phenomena, a core characteristic of Guarani-Jopara. Fine-tuning was performed using HuggingFace's AutoTrain and UnsloothAI's Unsloth on various base models, including Meta LLaMA and Gemma architectures. Model performance was evaluated through both quantitative metrics (SacreBLEU and ROUGE) and qualitative feedback from native speakers. Results indicate that the models capture some linguistic features of Guarani-Jopara, although improvements are needed in naturalness and contextual relevance. This research highlights the feasibility and challenges of applying lLMs to under-resourced, code-switched languages, contributing to the broader goal of inclusive and culturally aware NLP systems.

title: Assessment of Driver Cognitive Load in Autonomous Driving Using Extended Reality Simulation
authors: Seth Hatchett, Connor Viana, Arman Sargolzaei, Saman Sargolzaei
abstract: In this study, an extended reality-based driving simulation combined with electroencephalography (EEG), heart rate variability (HRV), and eye tracking data was used to assess the impact of advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) on cognitive load, which is the proportion of a driver's total cognitive resources needed to complete the driving task. The EEG data, processed using both multitaper spectral estimation (MT) and wavelet transform (WT), showed that AI-assisted steering and fully autonomous driving reduced cognitive load, whereas AIassisted throttle and manual driving did not significantly affect cognitive load relative to the baseline. This was indicated by decreased alpha, theta, and beta for fully autonomous driving and AI-assisted steering trials. HRV data further supported this trend, with the root mean square of successive inter-beat interval differences (RMSSD) increasing during manual and AI-assisted steering trials, indicating reduced cognitive load during these conditions. Eye tracking data also supported the EEG trend, with an increased total absolute change in eye motion speed variance during AI-assisted throttle trials. This exploratory study provides preliminary evidence that different ADAS functions may differentially influence driver cognitive load and engagement.

title: A Software-Defined Testbed for Quantifying Deauthentication Resilience in Modern Wi-Fi Networks
authors: Alex Carbajal, Asma Jodeiri Akbarfam
abstract: Wi-Fi deauthentication attacks remain a practical denial-of-service (DoS) threat by exploiting unprotected management frames to disrupt client connectivity. In this work, we introduce a software-defined testbed to measure Wi-Fi resilience to deauthentication attacks. We experimentally evaluate five wireless security configurations: open networks, WPA1, WPA2 without Protected Management Frames (PMF), WPA2 with PMF, and WPA3. Using controlled experiments, we measure client disconnection rates, packet injection volume, and time-to-disruption under each configuration. Packet-level behavior is analyzed using standard wireless auditing tools. Open networks, WPA1, and WPA2 without PMF proved entirely vulnerable to deauthentication, while no successful attacks were observed for WPA2 with PMF or WPA3 under tested conditions. These findings confirm the effectiveness of management-frame protection and highlight the continued risk posed by legacy or misconfigured wireless deployments.

title: Availability Attacks without an Adversary: Evidence from Enterprise LANs
authors: Rajendra Paudyal, Rajendra Upadhyay, Al Nahian Bin Emran, Lisa Donnan, Duminda Wijesekera
abstract: Denial-of-Service (DoS) conditions in enterprise networks are commonly attributed to malicious actors. However, availability can also be compromised by benign non-malicious insider behavior. This paper presents an empirical study of a production enterprise LAN that demonstrates how routine docking and undocking of user endpoints repeatedly trigger rapid recalculations of the control plane of the Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP) [1]. Although protocol-compliant and nonmalicious, these events introduce transient forwarding disruptions of approximately 2-4 seconds duration that degrade real-time streaming (voice and video) services while remaining largely undetected by conventional security monitoring. We map this phenomenon to the NIST and MITRE insider threat frameworks, characterizing it as an unintentional insider-driven availability breach, and demonstrate that explicit edge-port configuration effectively mitigates the condition without compromising loop prevention.

title: Investigation into Mathematical Modeling of Collision Avoidance Zones Under the Detection and Avoidance Framework
authors: Yuan Zhang, Jialiang Yuan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) technology is experiencing rapid and ever-advancing development, and the potential safety hazards such as collisions and conflicts arising therefrom have become increasingly prominent. Building upon the framework of the DAIDALUS algorithm, this study develops a mathematical model oriented towards horizontal trajectory conflict prevention. The model first employs the Cauchy inequality, in conjunction with the dynamic characteristics of UAVs' noninstantaneous maneuvers, to establish a mathematical description of trajectory conflicts. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed mathematical model exhibits higher accuracy in the computation of collision avoidance intervals and enables reliable collision avoidance functionality.

title: Rainfall Observation - A Comparison of Satellite and Ground-Based Remote Sensing
authors: Soumyasree Guin, Sumon Kumar Mondal, Sohom Roy Chowdhury, Swastika Chakraborty
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Short-term spatiotemporal variability of rainfall over Agartala, a Northeast hill station, has been observed. The study provides an intercomparison of surface rain gauge measurements, ground-based Doppler Weather Radar (DWR) observations at two heights (35 m and 7517 m) and satelliteobserved precipitation on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$22{}^{\text{nd}}$</tex> August, 2022. Near-surface radar <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(35 \mathrm{m})$</tex> measurement is affected by terrain-induced clutter and reflectivity and high-altitude radar (7517 m) measurement consistently suffers due to detection error. GPM captures most of the rainfall event with underestimation. Performance analysis indicates that near-surface radar (35 m) shows higher correlation with the gauge (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P C C} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 5 5 2}$</tex>), while GPM shows the lower RMSE (2.54). Overall, the study highlights the strengths and limitations of each remote observation system, highlights their regional coverage. Satellite and radar products can complement ground-based observations in hilly terrain with suitable adjustment.

title: Aquaculture Net Cage Breakage Detection Based on improved YOLOv8
authors: Jincheng Wan, Lei Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the challenges of insufficient accuracy and real-time performance in detecting defects within aquaculture cage structures, this study proposes an enhanced YOLOv8n-based detection framework through three structural innovations. Firstly, we reconstruct the C2f module by substituting standard Bottleneck layers with Partial-Block units, forming a C2f-Partial architecture that preserves network integrity while strengthening multi-scale feature representation through hierarchical residual connections. Secondly, a Share-Conv Detection Head (SCDH) incorporating Group Normalization Convolution (GN-Conv) is designed to streamline feature aggregation, achieving 39.5% reduction in computational load (3.2 GFLOPs) and 42% model compression (2.45 MB). Thirdly, a novel Focal-SIoU loss function synergistically integrates Focal penalty weights with SIoU geometric constraints, effectively balancing critical sample prioritization and bounding box regression accuracy. Experimental validation demonstrates significant performance gains: precision (92.1%), recall (89.8%), and mAP (91.1%) show absolute increments of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.3 \%, 4.5 \%$</tex>, and 3.5% respectively over baseline YOLOv8n. The optimized model maintains real-time inference capability while outperforming mainstream detectors in speed-accuracy trade-offs, as evidenced by its reduced parameter footprint and computational intensity. These advancements establish a computationally efficient solution for underwater mesh defect identification, particularly suited for edge deployment in resource-constrained aquaculture monitoring systems.

title: Automated Evaluation of Authentication Features of Web Pages from the Low Vision User Perspective
authors: Zeyu He, Suzanna Schmeelk, Jose Florez-Arango, Helen Petrie
abstract: Ensuring secure and accessible authentication is crucial for protecting user data while maintaining inclusivity for individuals with disabilities. However, security mechanisms such as CAPTCHAs, password strength meters, and complex password policies often create barriers that hinder accessibility. This study conducted an automated evaluation of authentication features across 39 widely used websites, assessing CAPTCHA implementation, password strength indicators, and policy transparency using Selenium-based automation. The findings revealed that only 7.7% of analyzed websites implemented CAPTCHA, yet none provided an audio alternative, limiting accessibility for visually impaired users. Password-based authentication remained the dominant mechanism, with 71.8% of websites including password fields; however, only 28.2% utilized password strength meters, and among them, just 23.1% incorporated ARIA attributes necessary for screen reader compatibility. Additionally, password policies were frequently hidden, with only 12.8% of sites displaying complexity requirements before user input. The presence of anti-bot measures on certain websites further restricted automated accessibility evaluations, suggesting that authentication mechanisms dynamically adapt based on user interactions. These findings underscore the need for inclusive security design, ensuring that CAPTCHA alternatives, screen reader-compatible password meters, and transparent password policies are integrated into authentication systems. Future work should explore biometric authentication and adaptive multifactor authentication as potential solutions to enhance both security and accessibility. Expanding automated testing frameworks could also improve large-scale evaluations of authentication barriers, contributing to more inclusive and secure digital environments.

title: EduCore: A Privacy-Aware LLM-Driven Education Framework with Intelligent Multi-Agent Support
authors: Debashis Das, Tenicka Norwood, Pushpita Chatterjee, Uttam Ghosh
abstract: Educational institutions still depend on manual and disconnected systems for administration, teaching, assessment, and student support. This increases faculty workload, slows academic processes, and limits personalized learning. Most existing digital platforms only store content and do not actively assist teachers or students. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) make it possible to build intelligent systems that can support and automate routine academic tasks. Therefore, this paper presents the design and implementation of a privacy-preserving, LLM-driven agentic education framework that integrates smart administration, AI-assisted teaching, personalized learning, and workforce-oriented academic advising into a unified system. The framework uses multiple AI agents, each designed for a specific task, and runs on locally deployed LLMs. A retrieval-based approach is used to ensure responses are accurate, relevant, and secure. By keeping data within the institution, the system protects privacy and reduces operational costs. The proposed system also reduces faculty workload, improves efficiency in academic processes, increases student engagement, and provides better personalized support. Overall, this work shows that agent-based AI can modernize education systems practically and securely to support both educators and students without replacing human decision-making.

title: Enhanced MIT Coffee Can Radar with Integrated Infrared Imaging for Target Detection and Classification for Experiential Learning Platform
authors: Isaac Anokye, Hidalgo Mudonhi, Goodwill Kwenda, Delores Baker
