This enhanced performance is attributed to the polar groups providing controllable polarization ability, increasing dielectric constant, and the cycloaliphatic structure widening the bandgap, suppressing charge transport, and ensuring high breakdown strength. Thus, by finely controlling the molecular chain configuration and aggregation state, the traditional trade-off relationship between dielectric constant and breakdown strength can be broken through, achieving directional design of high-performance insulation materials.

title: A New Method and Dataset for Classroom Teaching Stage Segmentation
authors: Shihao Yang, Nan Zhang, Yue Jiang, Ziyi Zhang, Shuhua Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current integrations of Artificial Intelligence (AI) with classroom teaching primarily focus on individual scenarios or simple tasks, with few studies addressing the segmentation of classroom teaching stages. However, an effective classroom teaching process requires complete teaching stages, which is particularly crucial for pre-service teacher training. This paper is the first study on the issue of teaching stage segmentation. First, we construct a teaching stage segmentation dataset, which includes 1,928 lessons. The classroom teaching is divided into five stages, namely, review, introduction, explanation, summary, and assignment based on the logical structure of the teaching content by educational experts. The dataset includes three modalities: transcribed text, audio, and video. Furthermore, we propose a multimodal classroom teaching stage segmentation method with two key innovations: a general multimodal clustering-separation loss framework and An entropy-based dynamic weighting strategy for multimodal fusion. The experiment verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Soft Graph Transformer for MIMO Detection
authors: Jiadong Hong, Lei Liu, Xinyu Bian, Wenjie Wang, Zhaoyang Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose the Soft Graph Transformer (SGT), a soft-input–soft-output neural architecture designed for MIMO detection. While Maximum Likelihood (ML) detection achieves optimal accuracy, its exponential complexity makes it infeasible in large systems, and conventional message-passing algorithms rely on asymptotic assumptions that often fail in finite dimensions. Recent Transformer-based detectors show strong performance but typically overlook the MIMO factor graph structure and cannot exploit prior soft information. SGT addresses these limitations by combining self-attention, which encodes contextual dependencies within symbol and constraint subgraphs, with graph-aware cross-attention, which performs structured message passing across subgraphs. Its soft-input interface allows the integration of auxiliary priors, producing effective soft outputs while maintaining computational efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that SGT achieves near-ML performance and offers a flexible and interpretable framework for receiver systems that leverage soft priors.

title: β-AVSDNET: A Novel End-To-End Neural Network Architecture For Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
authors: Chang Huai You
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose β-AVSDnet, a novel end-to-end audio-visual speaker diarization (AVSD) system that unifies visual and acoustic processing in a single trainable architecture. A novel ROI-delta visual representation is first constructed to capture both spatial appearance and short-term lip motion. These visual inputs are then processed by a modified LeNet encoder combined with a shared Conformer, which extracts compact visual embeddings. The visual embeddings together with speaker embedding are subsequently passed to a dedicated β-AV embedding subnet. Finally, the resulting embedding representations are integrated with audio features into BLSTMs to perform speaker diarization. Model training is guided by a dual-target strategy. Compared with the multimodal information based speech processing (MISP) baseline, β-AVSDnet reduces parameters by over 50% while improving accuracy: on the MISP far-field development set with single-channel audio, DER decreases from 15.38% to 12.20%, and RTTM-level multi-channel fusion further reduces it to 10.98%.

title: FTIN: Frequency-Time Integration Network for Inertial Odometry
authors: Shanshan Zhang, Qi Zhang, Siyue Wang, Liqin Wu, Tianshui Wen, Ziheng Zhou, Ao Peng, Xuemin Hong, Lingxiang Zheng, Yu Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Inertial odometry (IO) leverages inertial measurement unit (IMU) signals for cost-effective localization. However, high IMU sampling rates introduce substantial redundancy that impedes IO’s ability to attend to salient components, thereby creating an information bottleneck. To address this challenge, we propose a cross-domain IO framework that fuses information from the frequency and time domains. Specifically, we exploit the global context and energy-compaction properties of frequency-domain representations to capture holistic motion patterns and alleviate the bottleneck. To the best of our knowledge, this is among the first attempts to incorporate frequency-domain feature processing into IO. Experimental results on multiple public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed frequency–time-domain fusion strategy.

title: Prototype-Guided Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning for Continual Audio-Visual Sound Separation
authors: Wanrong Ma, Hongyu Wen, Zijian Gao, Qisheng Xu, Kele Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Continual audio-visual sound separation aims to progressively isolate sound sources from mixtures using visual cues, adapting to new categories without degrading performance on previous ones. This task is challenged by catastrophic forgetting, where new learning overwrites prior knowledge, and cross-modal interference, where features from different categories or modalities entangle in the representation space, reducing discriminability. In this paper, we propose Prototype-guided Cross-modal Contrastive Learning (PGCCL) to address these issues. PGCCL builds category-level prototypes as stable anchors to structure the multimodal representation space, enhancing inter-class separability and intra-class consistency, while an Exponential Moving Average mechanism mitigates feature drift. Experiments show that PGCCL achieves superior adaptation to novel categories while preserving earlier performance, providing a robust and scalable solution for continual multimodal learning.

title: C&F-WSVAD: Towards High-Performance Coarse and Fine-Grained Weakly-Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
authors: Yang Wang, Jiaogen Zhou, Jihong Guan, Shuigeng Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Weakly-supervised video anomaly detection (WSVAD) performs frame-level anomaly detection using only video-level labels. However, existing methods mainly rely on visual data, ignoring multimodal cues that could enhance performance — particularly for fine-grained WSVAD (FG-WSVAD), which identifies specific anomaly types, unlike coarse-grained WS-VAD (CG-WSVAD) that only detects anomaly presence. This paper proposes C&F-WSVAD, a novel method leveraging video-text multimodal data and multiscale temporal dependencies to overcome WSVAD limitations. Our approach enhances both coarse-grained (CG) and fine-grained (FG) WSVAD by fusing video-text features and modeling multiscale temporal dependencies. We also propose a pseudo-labeling strategy and a center loss to maximize inter-class separation while minimizing intra-class distance. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance in both FG-WSVAD and FG-WSVAD.

title: Graph Hodge-Laplacian Particle Filtering for Communication-Efficient Distributed Tracking
authors: Dayu Qin, Ercan E. Kuruoglu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We revisit communication-efficient distributed particle filtering through a simplicial lens. Our method, GHLA-DPF, encodes the node log-likelihood signals and compresses them in the L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> Hodge Laplacian basis, exchanging only a small set of spectral coefficients via standard gossip algorithm. This keeps the same communication paradigm as graph Laplacian approaches while explicitly suppressing curl on triangular loops and improving spectral locality for potential-like fields. Experiments on bearings-only tracking with circulation bias show consistently better accuracy–communication trade-offs than GLA-DPF and a selective-gossip algorithm baseline, demonstrating the value of edge-aware compression for distributed tracking.

title: Empirical Analysis of Approximate Message Passing Under non-I.I.D. Measurements with Comparison to State Evolution
authors: Tomoharu Furudoi, Takumi Takahashi, Hideki Ochiai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper investigates a denoiser design that enhances the robustness of approximate message passing (AMP) while retaining high estimation performance. The minimum mean squared error (MMSE)-based Bayesian denoiser, naturally derived from the belief propagation framework, is the most widely used in AMP. While this choice ensures optimal convergence under the ideal i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) measurements in the large-system limit, its aggressiveness increases the sensitivity of AMP to model mismatch. To address this issue, we design a denoiser that adaptively regulates the convergence behavior to improve robustness. We further show that its convergence dynamics can be empirically predicted under non-i.i.d. measurements by heuristically modifying the state evolution equations according to the asymptotic spectrum of measurement matrices.

title: LAKALMANTracker: Robust Learning-Aided Kalman Filtering for Multi-Object Tracking
authors: Jian Song, Wei Mei, Yunfeng Xu, Qiang Fu, Renke Kou, Lina Bu, Yucheng Long
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Kalman filter (KF)-based multi-object tracking methods assume linear object motion; however, they fall short in accurately predicting nonlinear object motion due to model mismatch. To address this limitation, we explore data-driven motion estimators and thus propose Patching-KalmanNet (PKNet), inspired by KalmanNet, which learns the Kalman gain (KG) via deep neural networks. PKNet takes multi-step historical state patches as input and leverages two key components for KG learning: variate-independent patch embedding (capturing heterogeneous motion features across different variates) and attention mechanisms (highlighting critical historical information for feature refinement). With a KF-alike structure, it can integrate directly into existing trackers as a replacement for the KF, forming the Learning-Aided Kalman Tracker (LAKalmanTracker). Experiments on SoccerNet, DanceTrack, and other benchmarks with highly nonlinear object motion show that PKNet significantly outperforms both the KF and existing KalmanNets in boosting tracker performance and robustness.

title: Phase Consistency Enhanced Complex-Valued Neural Network for Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification
authors: Baihe Chen, Jing Xiao, Zeqi Shao, Wenrui Ding, Yufeng Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification (RFFI) is promising for device authentication in IoT, wireless communications, and defense, yet most deep learning methods rely on real-valued networks that underuse phase information. To address this, we propose PCE-CVNN, a complex-valued framework that enforces relative phase consistency to mitigate global phase shifts, leverages complex supervised contrastive learning to transfer discriminative phase features from high-SNR to low-SNR samples, and incorporates a complex confidence decision module to refine classification boundaries. Experiments on 32 ZigBee devices across SNRs from -20 dB to 20 dB show that it achieves 94.19% overall accuracy, maintaining 71.06% at -20 dB, demonstrating the robustness of explicit phase-aware modeling for emitter identification.

title: Transferable Audio Lottery Tickets: Gradient Accumulation for Extreme Sparsity
authors: Hyunjae Kim, Juhan Nam, Kyung Myun Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large-scale neural networks have achieved impressive performance across diverse audio domains, but their growing size raises the need for lightweight alternatives. The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH) offers a compelling direction by revealing sparse subnetworks that can match full network performance, yet its potential across various audio subdomains remains underexplored. In this work, we examine this potential by evaluating sparse subnetworks on diverse audio classification tasks, spanning speech, music, and environmental sound. In particular, we propose a simple modification to the training process that incorporates momentum-like gradient accumulation during subnetwork search. We show that this strategy enables finding extremely sparse subnetworks with less than 1.0% of the initial parameters remaining, while still retaining up to 90% of dense model performance without layer collapse even under severe unstructured pruning. Furthermore, these sub-networks were effectively transferred across different audio subdomains while sustaining their sparsity-robust characteristics.

title: KinGuard: Hierarchical Kinship-aware Fingerprinting to Defend Against Large Language Model Stealing
authors: Zhenhua Xu, Xiaoning Tian, Wenjun Zeng, Wenpeng Xing, Tianliang Lu, Gaolei Li, Chaochao Chen, Meng Han
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Protecting the intellectual property of large language models requires robust ownership verification. Conventional backdoor fingerprinting, however, is flawed by a stealth-robustness paradox: to be robust, these methods force models to memorize fixed responses to high-perplexity triggers, but this targeted overfitting creates detectable statistical artifacts. We resolve this paradox with KinGuard, a framework that embeds a private knowledge corpus built on structured kinship narratives. Instead of memorizing superficial triggers, the model internalizes this knowledge via incremental pre-training, and ownership is verified by probing its conceptual understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate KinGuard’s superior effectiveness, stealth, and resilience against a battery of attacks including fine-tuning, input perturbation, and model merging. Our work establishes knowledge-based embedding as a practical and secure paradigm for model fingerprinting.

title: DPO-Regularized Regression for Age Prediction
authors: Mahsa Zamani, Rita Singh, Bhiksha Raj
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speaker age estimation is a challenging regression task that is typically approached as a pointwise prediction problem, where models are trained to minimize Mean Squared Error (MSE). While effective for reducing absolute error, MSE fails to incorporate the ordinal nature of age. To address this, we propose a hybrid training approach that combines MSE with a pairwise Direct preference optimization (DPO) loss, encouraging the model to prefer bins that are relatively closer to the true age by learning from pairwise comparisons between candidate bins. We conducted experiments on the TIMIT dataset for age prediction from voice and showed that incorporating DPO can lead to improvements in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to the MSE-only baseline.

title: LongSpeech: A Scalable Benchmark for Transcription, Translation and Understanding in Long Speech
authors: Fei Yang, Xuanfan Ni, Renyi Yang, Jiahui Geng, Qing Li, Chenyang Lyu, Yichao Du, Longyue Wang, Weihua Luo, Kaifu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in audio-language models have demonstrated remarkable success on short, segment-level speech tasks. However, real-world applications such as meeting transcription, spoken document understanding, and conversational analysis require robust models capable of processing and reasoning over long-form audio. In this work, we present LongSpeech, a large-scale and scalable benchmark specifically designed to evaluate and advance the capabilities of speech models on long-duration audio. LongSpeech comprises over 100,000 speech segments, each approximately 10 minutes long, with rich annotations for ASR, speech translation, summarization, language detection, speaker counting, content separation, and question answering. We introduce a reproducible pipeline for constructing long-form speech benchmarks from diverse sources, enabling future extensions. Our initial experiments with state-of-the-art models reveal significant performance gaps, with models often specializing in one task at the expense of others and struggling with higher-level reasoning. These findings underscore the challenging nature of our benchmark. Our benchmark will be made publicly available to the research community.

title: Long Chain-Of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization
authors: Xinchen Han, Hossam Afifi, Michel Marot, Xilu Wang, Lu Yin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate unnecessarily verbose Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning that increases computational costs and latency without proportional performance gains. In this paper, we propose Fine-grained Group policy Optimization (FGO), a Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm that refines group responses by subdividing them and assigning appropriate weights based on length and entropy, thereby enabling effective CoT compression. Meanwhile, as an enhanced variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), FGO successfully addresses two major limitations of the GRPO: inefficient data utilization and entropy collapse. We evaluate FGO on multiple reasoning LLMs and benchmarks, including MATH500, AIME24, AMC23, and Minerva. Experimental results show that FGO achieves efficient CoT compression without degrading performance, and simultaneously resolves the key limitations of GRPO.

title: MAG: Multi-Modal Aligned Autoregressive Co-Speech Gesture Generation Without Vector Quantization
authors: Binjie Liu, Lina Liu, Sanyi Zhang, Songen Gu, Yihao Zhi, Tianyi Zhu, Lei Yang, Long Ye
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This work addresses full-body co-speech gesture generation, where prior methods often rely on autoregressive models with vector-quantized tokens, causing information loss and reducing realism. To overcome this, we propose MAG, a multi-modal framework that avoids discrete tokenization and preserves the natural continuity of human motion. MAG consists of two main components: (1) the Motion-Text-Audio-Aligned Variational Autoencoder (MTA-VAE), which leverages pre-trained WavCaps text and audio embeddings to enhance semantic and rhythmic alignment, producing more realistic and contextually appropriate gestures; (2) the Multi-modal Masked Autoregressive Gesture generation model (MMAG), which applies diffusion in continuous motion embedding space to eliminate vector quantization, and incorporates a hybrid granularity audio-text fusion block for strong multi-modal consistency. (3) MAG achieves state-of-the-art performance on two benchmark datasets, generating realistic, diverse, and well-synchronized gestures.

title: MVP4EI: A Multi-View Persistent Spatio-Temporal Feature Learning for EEG Imputation
authors: Guanjian Liu, Huan Liu, Yuhan Shi, Yuzhe Zhang, Yugui Xie, Dalin Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals frequently encounter significant data loss, posing substantial challenges for downstream analysis. While deep learning-based imputation methods have shown promise in handling complex missing patterns, they often overlook the inherent persistence of EEG signals. To address this gap, we propose MVP4EI, a Multi-View Persistent spatio-temporal feature learning framework for EEG Imputation. Our approach introduces a tri-branch learnable codebook mechanism to explicitly capture persistent features across temporal, spatial, and spatio-temporal dimensions, fused via an SE network. These features are then integrated with the corrupted signal through a spatio-temporal attention module to achieve accurate reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MVP4EI significantly outperforms existing methods in both imputation accuracy and downstream task performance. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/XJTU-EEG/MVP4EI.

title: Auxiliary Tasks Benefit Skin Lesion Segmentation
authors: Yuheng Yang, Quanyi Yu, Na Ta, Jing Hong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Skin lesion segmentation is a compelling and fundamental problem in artificial intelligence. The task is inherently challenging due to noise and occlusion that frequently occur in dermoscopic images. Current state-of-the-art methods typically learn pathological structures directly from these corrupted images, leading to unsatisfactory segmentation results. To address this issue, we propose a novel learning framework that incorporates auxiliary tasks into the image modeling network. Specifically, the auxiliary tasks corrupt the input images by adding noise and mask, followed by requiring the network to recover these corrupted images, facilitating more robust boundary features learning and being adaptive to incomplete lesion morphology. Additionally, we mathematically derive an information-theoretic loss function to supervise the auxiliary tasks, ensuring feature consistency and spatial alignment between the recovered and the original images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC2017 and the ISIC2018 datasets.

title: A Light-Weight PRNU-Based Camera-Device Authentication Based on Device-Specific Image Downsampling
authors: Kotaro Naruse, Nagisa Nishimura, Hikari Sasaki, Yoshihiro Midoh, Jun Shiomi, Yuichi Tanaka, Minoru Kuribayashi, Noriyuki Miura
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a light-weight camera-device authentication scheme based on Photo Response Non-Uniformity (PRNU), defined as a unique sensor fingerprint caused by manufacturing imperfections. In the proposed method, a step-wise downsampling with PRNU-based weighted averaging is performed to preserve the device-specific nature of PRNU even in low-resolution images. This light-weight security framework is highly suitable for resource-constrained distributed sensing systems, such as continuous infrastructure monitoring where the network cameras must operate over unprotected open fields under limited energy source and communication bandwidth constraints. In this work, as a proof of concept, the proposed framework was demonstrated in the continuous monitoring system built with > 100 network camera devices. The proposed PRNU-preserving downsampling successfully maintained average F1 scores of > 0.98 even with 1/16 downsampled images. The proposed method significantly saves required energy and is beneficial for scalable physical-attack resilient security, essential for practical use of infrastructure monitoring systems.

title: DPAnet: Dual Pyramid Attention Network for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Qianyang Li, Xingjun Zhang, Shaoxun Wang, Wei Jia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) is hampered by the challenge of modeling complex dependencies that span multiple temporal scales and frequency resolutions. Existing methods, including Transformer and MLP-based models, often struggle to capture these intertwined characteristics in a unified and structured manner. We propose the Dual Pyramid Attention Network (DPANet), a novel architecture that explicitly decouples and concurrently models temporal multi-scale dynamics and spectral multi-resolution periodicities. DPANet constructs two parallel pyramids: a Temporal Pyramid built on progressive downsampling, and a Frequency Pyramid built on band-pass filtering. The core of our model is the Cross-Pyramid Fusion Block, which facilitates deep, interactive information exchange between corresponding pyramid levels via cross-attention. This fusion proceeds in a coarse-to-fine hierarchy, enabling global context to guide local representation learning. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks show that DPANet achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming prior models. Code is available at https://github.com/hit636/DPANet.

title: TrafficGS: Sparse-View Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Roadside Traffic Scene Modeling and Streaming
authors: Renwei Hai, Haolin Zhang, Pengfei Dong, Shitao Chen, Jingmin Xin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent neural rendering techniques, particularly Gaussian Splatting, have achieved remarkable progress in dynamic scene modeling. However, these methods typically rely on dense multi-view inputs, which are impractical for roadside sensor setups. To address this, we propose TrafficGS, a novel method for dynamic traffic scene modeling based on fixed, sparse-view roadside sensors. TrafficGS first generates globally consistent depth maps by fusing LiDAR and camera data through a depth completion network, which also initializes the first frame for Gaussian primitives. We then employ multi-resolution hash encoding to capture the traffic flow across frames and introduce a scene-completion module that repairs missing regions using a ground-plane decoupling mask. Additionally, we construct a dataset specifically designed for evaluating cross-view rendering in roadside scenes. Experiments conducted on this dataset demonstrate that TrafficGS outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving substantial improvements in traffic scene reconstruction and rendering.

title: Ranking The Impact of Contextual Specialization in Neural Speech Enhancement
authors: Peter Leer, Svend Feldt, Zheng-Hua Tan, Jan Østergaard, Jesper Jensen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We systematically investigate neural speech enhancement systems, ranging from very small (~10k parameters) to medium-large (~2-5M parameters), which specialize to acoustic conditions using contextual information such as speaker identity, noise type, speaker gender, spoken language, and SNR. By fine-tuning generalist models on specific data subsets, we find that specializing to a speaker’s identity consistently yields the largest gains in estimated speech intelligibility and quality. In contrast, specializing to SNR, noise type, or gender offers only marginal benefits. Crucially, we show that a small model specialized to both a specific speaker and a specific noise type can match or exceed the performance of a generalist model ten times its size. Further, cross-lingual tests reveal that models specialized to a target language outperform multilingual generalists, suggesting that language is a salient feature for specialization. These findings highlight the potential of small, adaptive models for resource-constrained applications like hearing aids, which specialize on-the-fly to contextual information.

title: WATEMP: Acoustic-Based Non-Contact Water Temperature Measurement System Using Smartphones
authors: Younho Nam, Su Yeon Shim, Young-Joo Suh
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Measuring water temperature is necessary for real-world applications, including proper infant formula concentration, stable aquarium conditions, and food safety. However, existing methods often pose a risk of contamination or require dedicated hardware. To address these limitations, we propose WaTemp, a non-contact water temperature measurement system that utilizes acoustic signals with only a commodity smartphone. Our framework leverages a domain adversarial neural network that learns temperature-relevant yet domain-invariant representations, delivering robust performance across varied containers and volumes. Furthermore, a few-shot learning enables simple calibration to the user’s own containers. Our experiments demonstrate that the system achieves a mean absolute error of 1.55°C within a 25-70°C range.

title: ASRC-SNN: Adaptive Skip Recurrent Connection Spiking Neural Network
authors: Shang Xu, Jiayu Zhang, Ziming Wang, Runhao Jiang, Rui Yan, Huajin Tang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In recent years, Recurrent Spiking Neural Networks (RSNNs) have shown promising potential in long-term temporal modeling. Many studies focus on improving neuron models and also integrate recurrent structures, leveraging their synergistic effects to improve the long-term temporal modeling capabilities of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). However, these studies often place an excessive emphasis on the role of neurons, overlooking the importance of analyzing neurons and recurrent structures as an integrated framework. In this work, we consider neurons and recurrent structures as an integrated system and conduct a systematic analysis of gradient propagation along the temporal dimension, revealing a challenging gradient vanishing problem. To address this issue, we propose the Skip Recurrent Connection (SRC) as a replacement for the vanilla recurrent structure, effectively mitigating the gradient vanishing problem and enhancing long-term temporal modeling performance. Additionally, we propose the Adaptive Skip Recurrent Connection (ASRC), a method that can learn the skip span of skip recurrent connection in each layer of the network. Experiments show that replacing the vanilla recurrent structure in RSNN with SRC significantly improves the model’s performance on temporal benchmark datasets. Moreover, ASRC-SNN outperforms SRC-SNN in terms of temporal modeling capabilities and robustness.

title: First Results on RIS-Enabled Multi-Layer Localization: A Joint Terrestrial and Non-Terrestrial Method
authors: Daniel Egea-Roca, Alda Xhafa, José A. López-Salcedo, Gonzalo Seco-Granados
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Positioning with centimeter-level accuracy and robust coverage in non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions is critical for many applications such as autonomous vehicles and industrial IoT. Terrestrial networks (TNs) provide high accuracy, but are limited by coverage gaps, whereas non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) offer global reach with reduced accuracy in urban environments. This paper presents a hybrid TN-NTN localization framework aided by the Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS) called the Multi-Layer Network (MuLa), which employs a single RIS to enhance integration of terrestrial and satellite downlink signals. The results demonstrate substantial accuracy and robustness improvements, underscoring RIS-enabled integration as a viable approach to meet such stringent localization requirements.

title: Polarization Fingerprint Identification via Cluster-Driven Sub-Classifiers Routing
authors: Xian Li, Jinlong Xu, Dong Wei, Weiqing Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Polarization Fingerprint (PF) enables cost-effective device identification, yet its discriminative capability with a monolithic classifier struggles when facing large-scale device sets. Therefore, we propose a Cluster-driven Sub-classifiers Routing scheme for Polarization Fingerprint Identification (CSR-PFI) to enhance identification performance. CSR-PFI partitions the registered device set into smaller, mutually exclusive, and variable-scale subsets using a clustering model based on PF features. It then routes each incoming PF sample to its dedicated sub-classifier, whose architecture is tailored for the corresponding subset size. On a 5,000 device dataset, CSR-PFI improves identification accuracy from 89.98% to 94.17% at 15 dB SNR, while requiring only 4.37 M additional parameters and reducing computational cost by 22.44M FLOPs. Furthermore, CSR-PFI achieves a substantial reduction in FAR while maintaining the same FRR, thereby significantly enhancing identification security.

title: Class-Aware Permutation-Invariant Signal-to-Distortion Ratio for Semantic Segmentation of Sound Scene with Same-Class Sources
authors: Binh Thien Nguyen, Masahiro Yasuda, Daiki Takeuchi, Daisuke Niizumi, Noboru Harada
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: To advance immersive communication, the Detection and Classification of Acoustic Scenes and Events (DCASE) 2025 Challenge recently introduced Task 4 on Spatial Semantic Segmentation of Sound Scenes (S5). An S5 system takes a multi-channel audio mixture as input and outputs single-channel dry sources along with their corresponding class labels. Although the DCASE 2025 Challenge simplifies the task by constraining class labels in each mixture to be mutually exclusive, real-world mixtures frequently contain multiple sources from the same class. The presence of duplicated labels can significantly degrade the performance of the label-queried source separation (LQSS) model, which is the key component of many existing S5 systems, and can also limit the validity of the official evaluation metric of DCASE 2025 Task 4. To address these issues, we propose a class-aware permutation-invariant loss function that enables the LQSS model to handle queries involving duplicated labels. In addition, we redesign the S5 evaluation metric to eliminate ambiguities caused by these same-class sources. To evaluate the proposed method within the S5 system, we extend the label prediction model to support same-class labels. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and the robustness of the new metric on mixtures both with and without same-class sources.

title: M2FNet: Multi-Level Modality-Fused Network for Robust Fingerprint and Finger Vein Recognition
authors: Wenyang Miao, Xionghan Zhao, Hengyi Ren, Xing Li, Jinting Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal fingerprint recognition combining fingerprint and finger vein is a promising solution for enhancing security and reliability due to their complementary and stable features. However, most existing methods rely on shallow or single-layer fusion, which limits complementarity and is prone to noise. To address this, we propose M2FNet, a hierarchical fusion framework. It first extracts modality-specific features to avoid semantic drift and conflicts, then progressively integrates details, textures, and semantics across spatial and frequency domains, and finally employs an adaptive complementation module to recalibrate features for enhanced discrimination and robustness. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate that M2FNet outperforms existing methods in accuracy and generalization and achieves better accuracy and robustness.

title: Precise Segmentation of Pathological Liver in 3D CT Scans Using Adaptive Shape Guidance from Prior Appearance
authors: Ahmed Semida, Ahmed Sharafeldeen, Hanan M. Amer, Ali Mahmoud, Rasha Karam, Basma A. Elged, Usama Shiha, Ahmad M. Mounir, Fatma Taher, Mohammed Ghazal, Guruprasad A. Giridharan, Sohail Contractor, Ayman El-Baz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel approach for extracting pathological liver regions from 3D computed tomography (CT) images. The proposed method leverages deep learning techniques to precisely segment pathological livers from 3D CT images. This is particularly challenging due to the inhomogeneity and diffused boundaries in the appearance of pathological liver tissue, as well as anatomical complexities such as shape and size variations and the liver’s close proximity to other organs. The proposed approach begins with 3D affine-based registration to roughly maximize the overlap between the subject to be segmented and the created liver atlas. Next, a 3D probabilistic shape map of the liver is generated by adaptively matching the appearance of each voxel with the prior liver atlas. This is achieved by selecting cubic neighbors of varying sizes around the location of each voxel. Finally, the original CT data, along with their corresponding 3D shape maps, are input into the 3D UX-Net. The proposed system is evaluated on 40 patients (20 with normal livers and 20 with pathological livers) using various metrics, including Dice similarity coefficient, overlap coefficient, absolute volume difference, and Hausdorff distance. It achieved scores of 94.66±1.1%, 89.88 ± 1.96%, 3.26± 2.66, and 7.18 ±2.65, respectively, outperforming different deep learning-based segmentation systems.

title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Correlation Meta-Network for Few-Shot Remote Sensing Image Classification
authors: Anurag Kaushish, Ayan Sar, Sampurna Roy, Sudeshna Chakraborty, Prashant Trivedi, Tanupriya Choudhury, Kanav Gupta
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Few-shot learning in remote sensing remains challenging due to three factors: the scarcity of labeled data, substantial domain shifts, and the multi-scale nature of geospatial objects. To address these issues, we introduce Adaptive Multi-Scale Correlation Meta-Network (AMC-MetaNet), a lightweight yet powerful framework with three key innovations: (i) correlation-guided feature pyramids for capturing scale-invariant patterns, (ii) an adaptive channel correlation module (ACCM) for learning dynamic cross-scale relationships, and (iii) correlation-guided meta-learning that leverages correlation patterns instead of conventional prototype averaging. Unlike prior approaches that rely on heavy pre-trained models or transformers, AMC-MetaNet is trained from scratch with only ~ 600K parameters, offering 20× fewer parameters than ResNet-18 while maintaining high efficiency (< 50ms per image inference). AMC-MetaNet achieves up to 86.65% accuracy in 5-way 5-shot classification on various remote sensing datasets, including EuroSAT, NWPU-RESISC45, UC Merced Land Use, and AID. Our results establish AMC-MetaNet as a computationally efficient, scale-aware framework for real-world few-shot remote sensing.

title: Deco3D: Decoupled Semantic and Geometric Learning for Sparse Supervision in 3D Object Detection
authors: Yubo Ai, Xujing Tao, Lifan Wu, Tianzhu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sparse supervision for 3D object detection has gained increasing attention, yet most existing methods rely on bounding-box pseudo-labels to supervise both semantics and geometry, which amplifies errors and noise. We propose Deco3D, a task-decoupled and collaborative learning sparse-supervised 3D detection framework. Deco3D employs coarse pseudo-labels for semantic learning and sparse accurate ground truths for geometric learning. To this end, we introduce the Cluster-Level Label Generation (CLLG) module, which leverages SAM to produce large-scale cluster-level pseudo-labels. We further propose the Cross-Scene Object Mining (CSOM) module, where learnable queries capture intra-modal semantics and activate potential target regions across scenes. Finally, the Collaborative Learning Module (CoLM) module integrates consistency-based label assignment and jointly trains with cluster- and box-level labels. Experiments on KITTI and Waymo demonstrate that Deco3D consistently surpasses prior sparse supervision methods under extremely low annotation cost.

title: G-SFADA: Gradient-Inspired Source-Free Active Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation
authors: Yibin Wen, Defeng Gu, Juepeng Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Domain adaptation addresses the challenge of transferring a pre-trained model from a source domain to an unannotated target domain. However, most works in semantic segmentation assume that source domain data is accessible, which neglects the potential privacy issues associated with the source images. To address this, we propose G-SFADA, a method that combines source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) and active learning (AL). In SFDA, a teacher network generates pseudo-labels for the target domain, while a student network engages in self-supervised learning through the use of gradient-inspired masked images. AL selectively annotates target images based on adaptability, uncertainty, and diversity to improve the target model’s performance. Our experiments show that G-SFADA achieves an improvement of 5.3% to 13.6% compared to other methods on the GTA5 → Cityscapes task.

title: Unit-Based Agent for Semi-Cascaded Full-Duplex Dialogue Systems
authors: Haoyuan Yu, Yuxuan Chen, Minjie Cai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Full-duplex voice interaction is crucial for natural human computer interaction. We present a framework that decomposes complex dialogue into minimal conversational units, enabling the system to process each unit independently and predict when to transit to the next. This framework is instantiated as a semi-cascaded full-duplex dialogue system built around a multimodal large language model, supported by auxiliary modules such as voice activity detection (VAD) and text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis. The resulting system operates in a train-free, plug-and-play manner. Experiments on the HumDial dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, which ranks second among all teams on the test set of the Human-like Spoken Dialogue Systems Challenge[1] (Track 2: Full-Duplex Interaction). Code is available at the GitHub repository https://github.com/yu-haoyuan/fd-badcat.

title: TGME-Net: Text-Guided Multi-Scale Enhancement Network for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Feng Li, Chen Sun, Bing Wang, Jian Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pseudo labels quality is pivotal to the performance of semi-supervised medical image segmentation. Recent studies have explored the use of vision language models (VLM) to improve the quality of pseudo labels by enhancing their ability to describe lesion regions. Nevertheless, existing methods often exhibit limitations such as excessive reliance on textual modalities, performance plateaus, and insufficient integration of multi-grained information. To address these issues, we propose a Text-Guided Multi-scale Enhancement (TGME) network, which enhances pseudo labels through the accumulation of multi-scale cross-modal supervision. Specifically, TGME establishes cross-modal semantic alignments between multi-scale image patches and text tokens, thereby preserving both fine-grained and coarse-grained informative features. Additionally, we introduce a novel learning paradigm named Conflict Feature Supervised Learning (CFSL) to achieve spatial perception of text-described regions within feature. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: From Pretraining to Robustness: Benchmarking SSL Models for Noise-Robust Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Sofia Eleftheriou, Theodoros Giannakopoulos, Themos Stafylakis, Ion Androutsopoulos
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) models are now the dominant architectures for many speech tasks, including speech emotion recognition (SER), achieving state-of-the-art performance on clean benchmarks. Yet their robustness to noise and corruption in realistic environments remains underexplored and often overestimated. This work presents a detailed evaluation of four SSL models on SER with IEMOCAP and MSP-Podcast under diverse corruption types and severity levels, using a standardized benchmark. Our analysis shows substantial performance degradation under corruption, even for the "robust" variant. We also examine data augmentation and leave-one-corruption-out training, demonstrating that while augmentations can recover much of the lost accuracy, their effectiveness varies across corruption families, and generalization to unseen conditions is often limited. The results highlight the need to measure robustness not only in absolute terms but also as the fraction of degradation repaired, and to report augmentation gains separately for seen and unseen corruptions. This study establishes the first comprehensive benchmarking protocol for SER robustness, and further proposes a set of good practices for reproducible evaluation and for guiding the development of robustness-enhancing methods.

title: Revisiting the Connection between mCCA-Genvar and IVA-G: Role of Orthogonality and Deflation
authors: Isabell Lehmann, Ben Gabrielson, Tülay Adali
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Joint matrix decompositions are widely used to extract useful features from multiple datasets. Multiset Canonical Correlation Analysis (mCCA) is one of the oldest and most established methods among those. Prior work claimed that Independent Vector Analysis (IVA), an extension of Independent Component Analysis (ICA) to multiple datasets, generalizes mCCA and that, under the assumption of orthogonality, the objective function of IVA with a Gaussian model (IVA-G) coincides with that of mCCA using the genvar criterion. We revisit this connection and demonstrate that the main difference between these methods is in fact not orthogonality but deflation, which is inherent to most mCCA objective functions, including genvar. To show this, we introduce orthogonal IVA-G (o-IVA-G) and deflationary orthogonal IVA-G (d-o-IVA-G) and compare them with IVA-G and mCCA-genvar in simulations inspired by the functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) subgroup identification problem. Our results suggest that the all-at-once methods can successfully perform Joint Blind Source Separation (JBSS) in more difficult scenarios and are more statistically efficient than deflationary methods.

title: MTS-CR: Contrastive Representation Learning for Real-Time Qos Degradation Detection in Media Cloud Shared Instances
authors: Zhijiao Xiao, Yunyun Qiu, Pengwei Liu, Weipeng Cao, Zhong Ming
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In media cloud environments, shared instances often suffer from unpredictable QoS degradation, leading to impaired user experience in real-time multimedia services such as HD video streaming. Existing methods struggle to model the high-dimensional, nonlinear telemetry dynamics that precede performance drops. To address this, we propose MTS-CR, a novel framework that combines contrastive learning and representation learning for real-time QoS degradation detection. MTS-CR employs a Linear Attention-based Encoder enhanced by Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) to extract discriminative temporal representations from 73 system-level metrics (CPU, memory, I/O, network). A lightweight MLP-based Predictor then forecasts imminent QoS anomalies. Evaluated on real-world cloud telemetry, MTS-CR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both detection accuracy and generalization, offering cloud operators an actionable signal for dynamic resource orchestration — ultimately preserving end-user QoE.

title: OCTIP: Compact Geography-Aware IP embeddings for Nearest-Neighbor IP Signal Retrieval
authors: Hao Feng, Chaokun Wang, Fang Niu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study Nearest-Neighbor IP Signal Retrieval, a problem that retrieves semantically related IP signals directly in the IP address space without relying on precise geographic coordinates, traffic logs, or active measurements. To address this challenge, we propose OCTIP, a compact OCTet-level IP encoder for IPv4, together with IPSPRE, a reproducible framework for geography-preserving evaluation. OCTIP maps four octets to a unit vector using lightweight embeddings and a two-layer MLP, and learns with a ranking objective that aligns distance orderings in the IP space with those induced by a geography-aware text space derived from public IP-to-location records. The text supervision is powered by G2PTL, a geography-graph pretrained encoder, so that coarse attribution strings carry explicit geographic knowledge into training. IPSPRE measures an order-preserving rate, a Spearman rank correlation, a Mapping Consistency Index, and a Shuffle Loss Ratio; fixed sampling ensures robust replicability. On benchmarks built from public IPto-location data, OCTIP yields stronger order preservation and higher geography-oriented correlation than structured bit-level encoders under identical supervision, while remaining architecturally simple and compatible with large-scale approximate nearest-neighbor indices. This approach enables fast nearest-neighbor IP signal retrieval for downstream tasks such as region-aware clustering and attribution.

title: QP-SAM: Query-Based Prompt Generation for Segment Anything Model in Urban Village Identification
authors: Xinyuan Guo, Huijie Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Urban village identification is vital for sustainable development, yet complex morphology and dense structures challenge conventional segmentation methods. While the Segment Anything Model (SAM) demonstrates strong cross-scene segmentation, its heavy reliance on external prompts directly affects reliability and deployment costs. To address this, we propose Query-based Prompt-SAM (QP-SAM), which reformulates prompt generation as a set prediction problem for efficient domain adaptation of SAM. Specifically, with SAM parameters fully frozen, QP-SAM employs a query-based prompt predictor that directly generates sparse and effective bounding box prompts with a few learnable queries, employing the Hungarian algorithm for precise instance matching. Furthermore, with instance- and image-level supervision alongside mask–box consistency constraints, our approach ensures prompt plausibility and segmentation integrity. Experiments on urban village remote sensing datasets show that QP-SAM introduces only minimal additional parameters while achieving higher segmentation accuracy than existing state-of-the-art methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/woshixinyuan/Query-based-Prompt-SAM.

title: FastAV: Efficient Token Pruning for Audio-Visual Large Language Model Inference
authors: Chaeyoung Jung, Youngjoon Jang, Seungwoo Lee, Joon Son Chung
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this work, we present FastAV, the first token pruning framework tailored for audio-visual large language models (AV-LLMs). While token pruning has been actively explored in standard large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (LVLMs), its application to AV-LLMs has received little attention, even though multimodal integration substantially increases their token demands. To address this gap, we introduce a pruning strategy that utilizes attention weights to identify tokens emphasized at different stages and estimates their importance. Building on this analysis, FastAV applies a two-stage pruning strategy: (1) global pruning in intermediate layers to remove broadly less influential tokens, and (2) fine pruning in later layers considering the impact on next token generation. Notably, our method does not rely on full attention maps, which makes it fully compatible with efficient attention mechanisms such as FlashAttention. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FastAV reduces FLOPs by more than 40% on two representative AV-LLMs, while preserving or even improving model performance.

title: CSGAN-VLP: Swin-Transformer Enhanced Gan and Contrastive Alignment for Robust Cross-Scene Passive Visible Light Positioning
authors: Wei Sun, Ziwei Liu, Yimao Sun, Liangyin Chen, Binyu Yan, Yanbing Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Passive visible light positioning (VLP) has attracted significant attention due to its device-free nature and convenience. However, existing methods often struggle to generalize across diverse scenes, where variations in environmental conditions and data distributions hinder practical deployment. To address this challenge, we propose CSGAN-VLP, a robust cross-scene VLP framework that leverages sparse data through generative modeling and feature alignment. Specifically, we employ a Swin-Transformer-based Cycle-GAN to generate realistic cross-scene samples from limited data, effectively capturing distribution shifts while preserving intrinsic spatial correlations. A dilated convolutional autoencoder is then used to extract compact Received Signal Strength representations, while a feature alignment module jointly optimizes generated and original samples to unify domain-specific characteristics. This integration enables the localization network to maintain reliable performance even under sparse cross scenes. Extensive real-world experiments validate the effectiveness of the CSGAN-VLP, achieving an average Root Mean Square Error of 16 cm, which is a fourfold reduction compared to traditional methods.

title: Simple Aggregation is Not Enough: Temporal Knowledge Graph Forecasting Via Decentralized Multi-Chain Reasoning
authors: Rikui Huang, Shengzhe Zhang, Xiaoye Qu, Wei Wei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Forecasting (TKGF) aims to predict future events based on historical events. To achieve interpretable reasoning, explicit multi-hop path methods attract increasing attention. However, existing methods focus on generating a single reasoning path, which may be insufficient to support inference. An intuitive yet marginally beneficial approach is to aggregate information from multiple reasoning chains. We find that an excessive focus on a single high-quality reasoning chain results in a centralized distribution of reasoning chains, hindering multi-chain reasoning. To tackle this problem, we propose TimeFlow, a decentralized multi-chain reasoning TKGF framework. Specifically, we propose a target flow expectation maximization to jointly optimize multiple reasoning chains. Moreover, to generate high-quality reasoning chains, we leverage the temporal context of the query as guidance for the path generator. Experimental results demonstrate that TimeFlow effectively generates a decentralized distribution of reasoning chains and significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models.

title: Unlocking Hidden Potential in Point Cloud Networks with Attention-Guided Grouping-Feature Coordination
authors: Shangzhuo Xie, Qianqian Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Point cloud analysis has evolved with diverse network architectures, while existing works predominantly focus on introducing novel structural designs. However, conventional point-based architectures - processing raw points through sequential sampling, grouping, and feature extraction layers - demonstrate underutilized potential. We notice that substantial performance gains can be unlocked through strategic module integration rather than structural modifications. In this paper, we propose the Grouping-Feature Coordination Module (GF-Core), a lightweight separable component that simultaneously regulates both grouping layer and feature extraction layer to enable more nuanced feature aggregation. Besides, we introduce a self-supervised pretraining strategy specifically tailored for point-based inputs to enhance model robustness in complex point cloud analysis scenarios. On ModelNet40 dataset, our method elevates base-line networks to 94.0% accuracy, matching advanced frameworks’ performance while preserving architectural simplicity. On three variants of the ScanObjectNN dataset, we obtain improvements of 2.96%, 6.34%, and 6.32% respectively.

title: Identifying Common Backbones of Interactions Underlying Food Webs via Non-Deterministic Alignments
authors: Yifan Xu, Carlos Taveras, Lydia Beaudrot, César A. Uribe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Climate change reshapes food webs by altering species distributions and interactions, making it essential to identify structural backbones of interactions that persist across ecosystems. Deterministic alignment methods are computationally slow and restricted to one-to-one correspondences. We introduce a scalable, non-deterministic alignment framework inspired by optimal transport that captures overlapping species roles via many-to-many mappings. Framed via motif-role profiles as a Gromov–Wasserstein transport problem, our method is both efficient and interpretable. We apply the proposed method to a large continental-scale data set of 129 mammal food webs in Sub-Saharan Africa. Pairwise alignments are identified, and we uncover robust backbones with greater connectivity and transitivity than those expected under null models. The proposed approach provides a formal, reproducible tool for forecasting ecosystem reorganization and conservation efforts.

title: Tuning-Free Fidelity-Constrained Decoding for Faithful Legal Reasoning with Open-Domain Large Language Models
authors: Kepu Zhang, Weijie Yu, Jun Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to legal tasks to assist judges and lawyers in decision-making, where outputs must remain faithful to case facts and legal elements to ensure reliable legal interpretations and accurate predictions. Existing approaches—ranging from prompt engineering to task-specific fine-tuning—either demand substantial human effort or lack explicit mechanisms to guarantee such fidelity. To address this issue, we propose Fidelity-Constrained Decoding (FCD), a tuning-free decoding framework that directly constrains generation to preserve strict alignment with both factual details and legal elements. Experiments on three legal datasets with two open-domain LLMs show that FCD consistently improves performance across diverse legal reasoning tasks while avoiding additional training overhead. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/ke-01/FCD.

title: MedMoVE-HRMLLM: A Mixture-of-Vision-Experts MLLM for Fine-Grained High-Resolution Medical Image Understanding
authors: Yujie Bai, Juanjuan Zhao, Yapeng Zhao, Bing Gu, Yan Qiang, Qi Chen, Huilan Zhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The multimodal interpretation of high-resolution medical images is hindered by difficulties in modeling global anatomical and pathological semantics and aligning visual details with textual semantics, exhibiting bottlenecks especially in detecting millimetre-scale lesions and fine textures. We present MedMoVE-HRMLLM, a mixture-of-vision-experts(MoVE) multimodal large language model(MLLM) combining a medical knowledge expert and a high-resolution fine-grained expert. The former enhances the modelling of global organ and pathological concepts by distilling clinical semantic priors and large-scale medical image-text contrastive learning; the latter applies variable-resolution encoding and hierarchical windows to capture minute lesions and complex textures. A text-guided, channel-wise soft routing mechanism enables dynamic fusion of both representations. On multiple medical benchmarks, MedMoVE-HRMLLM (13B) achieves average performance close to 40B-scale models, with strong advantages in high-resolution, detail-sensitive tasks. Accuracy reaches 92.97% on PathVQA and 85.23% on SLAKE. The code will be made publicly available on GitHub.

title: Turn the Black-Box White: Inferring Aggregation Rules in Federated Learning Through Multi-Trigger Geometry-Aware Backdoors
authors: Yang Li, Adams Wai Kin Kong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing federated learning attacks either align with benign updates to stay hidden or assume a fixed server aggregation rule. In black-box settings, where the server configuration is unknown, such attacks often fail due to mismatched assumptions. We show that black-box conditions do not inherently ensure security: the aggregation rule leaves discernible preferences that can be systematically revealed. To this end, we propose Backdoor Cartography, a novel attack framework that infers the server’s aggregation behavior by injecting geometry-aware, multi-trigger backdoors with distinct trigger shapes and target labels into client updates. By observing which backdoors persist after aggregation, the attacker can infer the server’s preference for update norm and direction. With this knowledge, future attacks can be tailored to exploit the revealed preference. Experiments show that Backdoor Cartography reveals aggregation preferences and highlights the need for adaptive defenses.

title: Individual Risky Action Warning of Wearable Sensors on Personalized Health Profiles via LLM
authors: Yiyin Xie, Yuhan Jing, Haifeng Sun, Qi Qi, Bo He, Chengsen Wang, Jingyu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current action recognition methods for health monitoring are often generic, failing to provide personalized warnings and struggling to scale to complex risky behaviors. This paper introduces a two-stage personalized risky action warning system to overcome these limitations. In the first stage, a Large Language Model (LLM) processes user medical histories to generate a tailored set of risky actions and decomposes complex behaviors, significantly enhancing recognition scalability. The LLM then formats this knowledge into structured guidance. In the second stage, an action recognition model, guided by the LLM’s output, utilizes wearable sensor data for a comprehensive risk assessment across action category, intensity, and temporal patterns. This enables precise, real-time warnings. Tested on real-world data for 10 different physical conditions, our system achieves an early warning F1-score of over 90%.

title: AG-Fusion: Adaptive Gated Multimodal Fusion for 3D Object Detection in Complex Scenes
authors: Sixian Liu, Chen Xu, Yiwen Li, Qiang Wang, Donghai Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal camera-LiDAR fusion technology has found extensive application in 3D object detection, demonstrating encouraging performance. However, existing methods exhibit significant performance degradation in challenging scenarios characterized by sensor degradation or environmental disturbances. We propose a novel Adaptive Gated Fusion (AG-Fusion) approach that selectively integrates cross-modal knowledge by identifying reliable patterns for robust detection in complex scenes. Specifically, we first project features from each modality into a unified BEV space and enhance them using a window-based attention mechanism. Subsequently, an adaptive gated fusion module based on cross-modal attention is designed to integrate these features into reliable BEV representations robust to challenging environments. Furthermore, we construct a new dataset named Excavator3D (E3D) focusing on challenging excavator operation scenarios to benchmark performance in complex conditions. Our method not only achieves competitive performance on the standard KITTI dataset with 93.92% accuracy, but also significantly outperforms the baseline by 24.88% on the challenging E3D dataset, demonstrating superior robustness to unreliable modal information in complex industrial scenes.

title: Dual-Branch Spatial-Lighting Network for Photometric Stereo
authors: Xiaoyan Tian, Ye Jin, Zhao Zhang, Peng Liu, Fenglei Ni, Dansong Cheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Photometric stereo recovers surface geometry by analyzing intensity variations under varying lighting conditions, offering high precision in fine-detail reconstruction. However, existing methods face challenges in unconstrained environments: per-pixel models lack global context, whereas all-pixel approaches often lose high-frequency detail. This study addresses the challenge of reconciling global contextual understanding with pixel-wise detail preservation in photometric stereo measurements. A novel dual-branch feature extraction neural architecture augmented by a multisuper-visory gradient propagation scheme is introduced. The proposed framework employs multiscale encoding and decoding mechanisms to integrate global and local representations, enabling end-to-end mapping from the input image sequences to surface normal estimations. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms nearly 20 state-of-the-art methods, highlighting robustness and practical applicability. The code and pretrained model weights are publicly available at https://github.com/zz-ux/ICASSP_2026.

title: GCAF-Net: Geometry-Informed Cross-Attention Fusion Graph Neural Network for Eeg-Based Diagnosis of Alzheimer’s Disease and Frontotemporal Dementia
authors: Shiang Hu, Xiaoliang Sheng, Yifan Hu, Fei Ruan, Juan Hou, Zhao Lv, Pedro A. Valdes-Sosa
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) are frequently misdiagnosed due to overlapping early symptoms. Electroencephalography (EEG) provides a non-invasive, cost-effective modality for diagnosis, yet existing methods often lack global perspective in feature extraction and fusion, while neglecting the complex spatial topology of brain networks. Here, we propose GCAF-Net, a geometry-informed cross-attention fusion graph neural network. Its backbone integrates functional connectivity and spectral features with electrode spatial coordinates and enables the spatial topology to be dynamically updated throughout training. It leverages the Bidirectional Cross-Attention Block (BCAB) and Conditional Local Feature Encoding (CLFE) modules to capture and enhance spatial–functional interactions, thereby generating more discriminative representations. Our method outperforms existing deep learning approaches, achieving 97% accuracy on the ds004504 dataset and 98.69% accuracy on the BrainLat dataset. These results demonstrate that GCAF-Net provides a promising, generalizable framework for EEG-based dementia classification.

title: VP-GNN: A Unified Graph Framework for Variable-Wise and Patch-Wise Modeling of Irregular Clinical Time Series
authors: Yurong He, Boya Zhang, Longfei Liu, Guosheng Cui, Dan Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) play a vital role in clinical risk prediction and disease state assessment. However, EHR data, being inherently irregular multivariate time series, commonly exhibit sparsity, irregular sampling, and heterogeneous variable dependencies, posing significant challenges for accurate modeling. To address these issues, we propose VP-GNN (Variable-wise and Patch-wise Graph Neural Network). VP-GNN first captures asynchronous inter-variable dependencies via selective message-passing in the variable-wise stage, and then models multi-scale temporal patterns through hierarchical Patch-GNN aggregation. We evaluated VP-GNN on two benchmark EHR datasets, PhysioNet 2012 and Sepsis 2019, across tasks such as in-hospital mortality prediction and early sepsis detection. VP-GNN achieves state-of-the-art performance, with AUROC/AUPRC scores of 2.12% / 10.59% on P12 and 4.26% / 4.96% on P19. These results demonstrate the robustness of VP-GNN in sparse and irregular conditions and offer a promising foundation for personalized medicine and intelligent clinical decision support. Our code will be available at https://github.com/bursonz/VP-GNN.

title: Dynamic Adaptive Wavelet State Space Model for Efficient Low-Light Image Enhancement
authors: Yinghui Fang, Xiang Chen, Jinshan Pan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement (LLIE) is a fundamental yet challenging task due to the coexistence of severe noise, low visibility, and loss of structural details. Despite recent progress, existing methods still suffer from inefficient feature representation and inadequate texture restoration. In this paper, we propose DAW-Mamba, a wavelet-guided state space model tailored for LLIE. Specifically, we adopt a multi-scale wavelet-based UNet backbone, where the low-frequency state space block is introduced to effectively model global dependencies in low-frequency components via a dynamic adaptive scanning block (DASB). Unlike manually designed scanning strategies, DASB dynamically learns optimal scanning trajectories, enabling more effective feature aggregation. Furthermore, we propose the high-frequency content-aware block, which leverages structural cues from low-frequency components under dual-attention guidance to restore fine textures in high-frequency regions. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves favorable performance against state-of-the-art approaches, while maintaining high computational efficiency.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Feature Fusion and Multi-Level Domain Adaptation Network for Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition
authors: Chuwen Xie, Rongtao Chen, Zhuoyi Huang, Jiahui Zhang, Lina Qiu, Jiahui Pan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based emotion recognition has emerged as a crucial technique in affective computing, providing an objective measure of emotions. However, inter-subject variability in EEG data distribution causes substantial domain shifts, severely limiting model generalization across subjects. To address this challenge, we propose M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net, a novel network that integrates multi-dimensional feature fusion and multi-level domain adaptation for cross-subject EEG emotion recognition. M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net employs a multi-branch parallel architecture to extract spatial, temporal, and frequency features, which are then adaptively fused via a cross-attention mechanism to form robust, domain-invariant representations. Furthermore, a hierarchical adversarial alignment module is introduced to simultaneously adapt both global marginal and class-aware conditional distributions. For precise conditional alignment, we design a dual-standard pseudo-labeling strategy combining confidence and feature-center proximity to generate reliable supervisory signals. Experiments on SEED and SEED-IV show that M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Net achieves state-of-the-art accuracies of 95.56%±3.81% and 87.38%±7.62%. Source code is available at: https://github.com/xiechuwen1/M-2Net.

title: Self-Supervised Note Tracking and Multi-Pitch Estimation Via Reconstruction-Based Learning
authors: Heng-Hsiu Hu, Li Su
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The limited availability of large, high-quality annotated datasets in music information retrieval (MIR) remains a key challenge for advancing automatic music transcription (AMT). To address this limitation, we introduce a fully self-supervised framework for note-level AMT that integrates multi-pitch estimation (MPE) and onset detection without labeled data. Our method extends the reconstruction/transcription paradigm by incorporating spectral flux and local group delay features. Experiments show consistent gains over prior self-supervised baselines in MPE and promising results in note-level transcription. Although note tracking still trails supervised systems, analysis highlights the role of reconstruction losses in capturing instrument-sensitive pitch/onset information. This work advances self-supervised AMT methods that generalize across diverse musical domains with limited labeled resources.

title: Unsupervised Sentence Stress Detection in L2 Spoken English Via Iterative Adaptation of Whisper ASR Framework
authors: Jhansi Mallela, Abin Roy, Fathima N, Reni K Cherian, Chiranjeevi Yarra
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sentence stress refers to the emphasis placed on specific words within an utterance to convey meaning beyond the literal message. Accurate detection of sentence stress is crucial for spoken language understanding systems, yet existing approaches are limited by the lack of annotated corpora, particularly for non-native speakers. In literature, large pre-trained speech models like Whisper offer a promising foundation for this task but remain untested on non-native data. And their adaptation in low-resource non-native settings is challenging due to the absence of huge annotated data. To address this gap, we propose a novel unsupervised framework that leverages noisy labels derived from energy-based heuristic features to iteratively fine-tune a Whisper-based classification model. We evaluate our approach on low-resource non-native English speech from Italian speakers using F1-score as the metric. Experimental results show that our method performs on par with supervised baselines, achieving absolute improvements of ~10% over the best existing unsupervised models.

title: Direct Preference Optimization For Speech Autoregressive Diffusion Models
authors: Zhijun Liu, Dongya Jia, Xiaoqiang Wang, Chenpeng Du, Shuai Wang, Zhuo Chen, Haizhou Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Autoregressive diffusion models (ARDMs) have recently been applied to speech generation, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in zero-shot text-to-speech. By autoregressively generating continuous speech tokens with next-token diffusion, these models offer a promising alternative to next-token prediction, avoiding the technical complexities associated with discrete speech tokenization. As a relatively new paradigm, research on reinforcement learning (RL)-based fine-tuning of speech ARDMs remains limited. In this paper, we propose Autoregressive Diffusion-Direct Preference Optimization (ARDM-DPO) to advance this research. By fine-tuning the recently proposed zero-shot text-to-speech model DiTAR with DPO, we achieve significant improvements in terms of speech expressiveness and robustness for long texts.

title: GTCL: Graph-Text Contrastive Learning Meets Log Anomaly Detection
authors: Xiao Tan, Jiaxiang Liu, Yujie Wu, Mingkun Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Log analysis is a fundamental technique for diagnosing issues in large-scale modern computing systems. Existing log anomaly detection methods are often unimodal, struggling to effectively integrate both structural information and deep semantic features inherent in log events. To address this issue, we propose a new Graph-Text Contrastive Learning (GTCL) framework tailored for log anomaly detection. GTCL employs a GNN-based graph encoder and an LM-based text encoder to capture complementary features, and unifies them via a graph–text contrastive loss. Across multiple public log benchmarks, GTCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, it remains stable across a wide range of sequence window sizes, suggesting robustness to hyperparameter choices and workload variability typical of real-world logging pipelines.

title: Scaling Multi-Talker ASR with Speaker-Agnostic Activity Streams
authors: Xiluo He, Alexander Polok, Jesús Villalba, Thomas Thebaud, Matthew Maciejewski
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: An increasingly common training paradigm for multi-talker automatic speech recognition (ASR) is to use speaker activity signals to adapt single-speaker ASR models for overlapping speech. Although effective, these systems require running the ASR model once per speaker, resulting in inference costs that scale with the number of speakers and limiting their practicality. In this work, we propose a method that decouples the inference cost of activity-conditioned ASR systems from the number of speakers by converting speaker-specific activity outputs into two speaker-agnostic streams.
