A central challenge is that naïvely merging speaker activities into streams significantly degrades recognition, since pretrained ASR models assume contiguous, single-speaker inputs. To address this, we design new heuristics aimed at preserving conversational continuity and maintaining compatibility with existing systems. We show that our approach is compatible with Diarization-Conditioned Whisper (DiCoW) to greatly reduce runtimes on the AMI and ICSI meeting datasets while retaining competitive performance.

title: FlipCon: Flipped Contrastive Learning for Fine-Grained DOA Representation
authors: Davin Kim, Hyun-Soo Kim, Da-Hee Yang, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In supervised contrastive learning, samples within the same class often exhibit fine-grained variations that must be captured to learn robust representations. However, conventional contrastive losses struggle to preserve these subtle intra-class distinctions, as in direction-of-arrival estimation, where signals may share the same angular label but still differ in their acoustic characteristics. We trace the root cause of this limitation to a structural weakness: as the similarity between positive embeddings increases, the gradient with respect to their angular distance rapidly vanishes. This phenomenon, which we term the double gradient vanishing problem. To address this issue, we propose FlipCon, a principled reformulation of the contrastive loss that inverts the cosine similarity term. This simple modification not only preserves meaningful gradients even for highly aligned positives but also integrates seamlessly into existing frameworks, yielding consistent performance improvements across multiple contrastive learning variants. Furthermore, we extend our formulation with an additive angular margin, originally introduced for classification, and demonstrate additional gains in direction-of-arrival estimation. These results establish FlipCon as an effective and general solution to the limitations of standard contrastive loss.

title: Make Your Move: Make Your 3D Contents by Adapting Multi-View Diffusion Models to External Editing
authors: Weitao Wang, Haoran Xu, Jun Meng, Haoqian Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As 3D generation techniques continue to flourish, the demand for generating personalized content is rapidly rising. Users increasingly seek to apply various editing methods to polish the generated 3D content, aiming to enhance its color, style, and lighting without compromising the underlying geometry. However, most existing editing tools focus on the 2D domain, and directly feeding their results into 3D generative methods (like multi-view diffusions) will introduce information loss, degrading the quality of the final 3D assets. In this paper, we propose a training-free, plug-and-play scheme that aligns edited assets with their original geometry in a single inference run. Central to our approach is a geometry preservation module that guides the edited multi-view generation with original input normal latents. Besides, an injection switcher is proposed to deliberately control the supervision extent of the original normals, ensuring the alignment between the edited color and normal views. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently improves both the multi-view consistency and mesh quality of edited 3D assets, across multiple combinations of multi-view diffusions and editing methods.

title: Magnitude Difference Conditioned All-in-One Image Restoration
authors: Jungwoo Bae, Jitae Shin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent all-in-one image restoration systems often rely on heavy spatial conditioning, inflating parameters and latency, which hinders real-world deployment. We present MDIR, a lightweight framework that conditions a backbone using a compact frequency-domain signature: a 1D Fourier magnitude difference vector with log-spaced radial bins and sinusoidal embeddings. At test time, a small magnitude predictor estimates the clean magnitude directly from degraded inputs for on-the-fly conditioning. This conditioning is injected via adaptive layer normalization, applied strongly in the decoder and lightly in late encoder/skip blocks. The backbone replaces heavy self-attention with efficient spatial propagation and a simplified residual design. Across dehazing, deraining, denoising, deblurring, and low-light enhancement, MDIR delivers substantial latency and memory savings while matching or surpassing heavier schemes, offering a practical solution for real-world deployment. Our codes will be available at: https://github.com/BaeJungWoo/MDIR

title: MSF-Mamba: Multi-Scale Frequency Mamba For Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
authors: Xuesen Ma, Ran Liu, Yan Qiao, Min Hou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recently, a new state-space model (SSM) called Mamba has been introduced, and Mamba shows great potential in balancing forecasting performance and computational efficiency. However, when applied to long sequences in the time domain, Mamba struggles to effectively separate periodic patterns from noise, leading to suboptimal feature extraction efficiency. To this end, we propose MSF-Mamba (Multi-Scale Frequency Mamba), which integrates frequency-domain analysis with multi-scale modeling to enhance feature extraction and overcome the limitations of time-domain approaches. Specifically, MSF-Mamba employs dual-path Fast Fourier Transform to decompose time-series data into the frequency-domain, effectively isolating periodic patterns from noise and capturing cross-variable dependencies. Furthermore, the multi-scale architecture of MSF-Mamba enables simultaneous modeling of both global and local temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on seven real-world datasets demonstrate that our MSF-Mamba model achieves superior performance in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: LightCSeg: Lightweight Crack Segmentation Network With Adaptive Sobel And Local Enhancement
authors: Zilong Zhao, Chenyu Zhang, Zhengming Ding, Feng Guo, Pei Niu, Zhicheng Lin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Real-time crack segmentation is a key technology for infrastructure health monitoring. Existing methods fail to address the challenges of lightweight design and practical applicability. To tackle this challenge, we propose a lightweight real-time crack segmentation network, LightCSeg. First, we designed the Sobel-Guided Adaptive Edge Aware (SAE) module, which fully leverages the edge feature extraction capability of the Sobel operator and the adaptive capacity of convolutional layers. Through a simple yet effective design, it significantly enhances the response intensity of crack textures. Second, we designed the Local Enhanced Multi-Scale Fusion (LEF) module, which employs bilinear interpolation for upsampling while leveraging the advantages of neighborhood attention for local modeling, ensuring high-quality feature fusion. Experiments on benchmark datasets for crack segmentation demonstrate that our method achieves SOTA-comparable performance while significantly reducing both parameters and FLOPs.

title: Secondary Source Placement for Sound Field Control Based on Ising Model
authors: Shihori Kozuka, Shoichi Koyama, Hiroaki Itou, Noriyoshi Kamado
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: A method for optimizing secondary source placement in sound field control is proposed. Sound field control techniques that aim to synthesize the desired spatial sound using multiple loudspeakers (or secondary sources) rely heavily on the placement of sources. Methods for optimizing secondary source placement are typically formulated as combinatorial optimization problems that select the configuration from discrete candidate points on the basis of a certain measure. Whereas the mean-square-error (MSE)-based source placement method has been shown to be effective, a simple greedy algorithm cannot guarantee the near optimality of the solution due to the complexity of its cost function. Furthermore, it also requires significant computation time for sequentially evaluating the cost function. We thus propose a secondary source placement method based on the Ising model. By reformulating the MSE-based cost function as a binary quadratic form, its minimization problem can be efficiently solved by Ising machines. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method yields better solutions, particularly at low frequencies, with significantly less computation time than the greedy algorithm.

title: PG-SE: Predictive Acceleration and Correction for Generative Speech Enhancement
authors: Yikai Huang, Jinjiang Liu, Zijian Lin, Xiang Li, Renjie Yu, Zhiyong Wu, Shiyin Kang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong potential for speech enhancement. However, they remain limited by inferior noise suppression compared to predictive approaches and the high computational burden arising from numerous neural function evaluations (NFEs) in reverse sampling. To overcome these challenges, we propose PG-SE, a novel Predictive-Generative integrated framework for speech enhancement. In this framework, predictive models are introduced at both the pre- and post-stages of diffusion inference to play complementary roles: the pre-stage predictive model provides priors to guide and accelerate score-based conditional diffusion sampling, while the post-stage model refines the generated speech by correcting residual errors, thereby improving both perceived quality and intelligibility of the enhanced speech. Experimental results demonstrate that PG-SE reduces inference NFEs by over 80% while delivering superior performance compared to full-step baselines, enabling low-latency deployment without compromising perceptual quality.

title: KBNet: A Knowledge Bridging Network for Generalizable Deepfake Detection
authors: Chao Liu, Liejun Wang, Zhiqing Guo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generalizable deepfake detection is challenging due to the domain gap between training and real-world data. While CNN-based detectors capture local textures well, they often overfit and generalize poorly. Methods using large pre-trained models, such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), improve cross-domain detection but lack sensitivity to fine-grained artifacts. To overcome these limitations, we propose KBNet, a novel knowledge-bridging framework that dynamically integrates local artifact-sensitive features from a CNN backbone with semantic prior knowledge from CLIP. KBNet consists of two key components: the Bridge Feature Generator (BFG), which uses cross-attention to inject semantic information from CLIP into CNN features at multiple stages. The Cross-Representation Fusion Block (CRFB) aligns and fuses multi-source features into a discriminative representation. Extensive experiments show that KBNet achieves state-of-the-art performance in both intra-dataset and cross-dataset settings, demonstrating strong generalization for real-world deepfake detection.

title: Virtual Consistency for Audio Editing
authors: Matthieu Cervera, Francesco Paissan, Mirco Ravanelli, Cem Subakan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Free-form, text-based audio editing remains a persistent challenge, despite progress in inversion-based neural methods. Current approaches rely on slow inversion procedures, limiting their practicality. We present a virtual-consistency based audio editing system that bypasses inversion by adapting the sampling process of diffusion models. Our pipeline is model-agnostic, requiring no fine-tuning or architectural changes, and achieves substantial speed-ups over recent neural editing baselines. Crucially, it achieves this efficiency without compromising quality, as demonstrated by quantitative benchmarks and a user study involving 16 participants.

title: Gromov-Wasserstein Graph Coarsening
authors: Carlos A. Taveras, Santiago Segarra, César A. Uribe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study graph coarsening in the Gromov-Wasserstein geometry. Specifically, we propose two algorithms that leverage a novel representation of the distortion induced by merging pairs of nodes. The first method, Greedy Pair Coarsening (GPC), iteratively merges pairs of nodes that locally minimize a distortion measure until the desired size is reached. The second method, termed k-means Greedy Pair Coarsening (KGPC), leverages clustering based on pairwise distortion metrics to directly merge node clusters. We provide conditions that guarantee optimal coarsening for our methods and validate their performance across six large-scale datasets and a downstream clustering task. Results show that the proposed methods outperform existing approaches on a wide range of parameters and scenarios.

title: Combining Multi-Order Attention and Multi-Resolution Discriminator for High-Fidelity Neural Vocoder
authors: Yan Shi, Jin Shi, Minchuan Chen, Ziyang Zhuang, Peng Qi, Shaojun Wang, Jing Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generative Adversarial Network (GAN-based) vocoders have gained significant attention for generate high-quality audio with fast inference speed. A typical issue with GAN-based models is phase and artifacts in synthesized speech. In this work, we propose a scalable hierarchical architecture incorporating stacked multi-order spatial-channel attention to enhance feature representation learning. This design enables progressive refinement of acoustic features through attention-guided processing. Moreover, we propose a multi-resolution full-band discriminator, which simultaneously captures local spectral details and global waveform coherence with multiple resolutions, effectively mitigating blurring artifacts that commonly occur in conventional discriminators. Additionally, our discriminator explicitly integrates both the magnitude and complex-domain representations by jointly processing the magnitude, real, and imaginary components. The experimental results show that our proposed method outperforms the baseline models in objective and subjective metrics. Audio samples are available online <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: DynaPredict: Alternating Predictive and Real Iteration for Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning Training
authors: Yiran Liu, Peng Qiao, Rongchun Li, Zhouyu He, Tao Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sample-inefficient in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) impedes scalability and practical deployment. While model-based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) improves efficiency through environmental simulation, it suffers from model bias and high computational costs. Conversely, conventional optimizers adapt poorly to DRL’s non-stationary optimization landscapes. To bridge this gap, we introduce DynaPredict, a novel optimization framework that transposes the concept of environmental simulation from observation space to parameter space. Instead of predicting observations, DynaPredict employs a lightweight predictive component to forecast the trajectory of policy parameter updates, thereby implicitly encoding environmental feedback. Our method alternates between predictive and real update cycles, drastically reducing environment interactions while avoiding model bias. Empirically, DynaPredict achieves consistent performance gains across the Atari benchmark, reducing sample complexity by up to 60% and accelerating wall-clock convergence time, demonstrating benefits orthogonal to specific algorithms or parallel architectures.

title: Photo Shielding: Robust Protection Against AI Manipulation of Images
authors: Amit Chaulwar, Mohammad Haseeb, Vanraj Vala, Ravi Sankar Guntur
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In the era of generative AI, malicious photo manipulation which are shared on social media poses significant risks. Existing methods embed imperceptible noise to protect images but are vulnerable to counterattacks such as image compression and adversarial purification. Their high computational costs also limit practical on-device implementation, a key concern for users prioritizing privacy. To address these issues, we introduce Photo Shielding. Our method employs a novel adversarial perturbation calculation tailored for on-device use, ensuring noise persistence post-compression. It achieves a 5x speedup over prior techniques and demonstrates effectiveness through qualitative and quantitative results, balancing protection and computational efficiency.

title: Sensor Array and Camera Fusion Via Unbalanced Optimal Transport for 3D Source Localization
authors: Ilyes Jaouedi, Gilles Chardon, José Picheral
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: Empowering Transformers Spectrally: Towards Comprehensive Pattern Learning for Image Demoiréing
authors: Sungjun Hong, Seung Woo Kwak, Sangyun Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Digital screen photography frequently suffers from moiré patterns caused by frequency aliasing between camera sensors and display pixels. Removing moiré artifacts is challenging due to their varying color, shape, and scale. Existing methods primarily operate in the spatial domain and thus overlook crucial frequency-domain information inherent in moiré patterns. To address this, we propose Spectrally Empowered Demoiréing Transformers (SEDT), a U-shaped Transformer integrating a Frequency Domain Modulator (FDM) within each Transformer block. The FDM performs spectral filtering through learnable modulation of frequency components, such as channel-wise affine transformations and band-wise frequency masking, enabling effective extraction of moiré-related features across multiple scales and frequency ranges. Evaluations on the TIP-2018 dataset demonstrate that SEDT significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in both PSNR and SSIM, highlighting the potential of frequency-domain enhancements in Transformer architectures for advanced image restoration tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/sylee-skhu/SEDT.

title: Selective Hub Fusion with Modality-Heterogeneous Experts for Multimodal Emotion Recognition
authors: Huan Zhao, Ling Xiong, Kehan Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Humans typically process and integrate multimodal information from vision, audio, text, and other sources to perceive and identify emotions. Existing multimodal models primarily rely on multi-modal data fusion, leveraging complementarity between modalities to enhance classification performance. However, when leveraging this complementarity, the acoustic and visual streams in video often carry dense, fine-grained content with substantial noise. Furthermore, these approaches neglect the differential contributions of each modality to emotion recognition outcomes. To address these issues, we proposes a Selective Hub Fusion with Multimodal Heterogeneous Mixture of Experts (SH-MHMoE) for multimodal emotion recognition, achieving intermodal complementarity. The Selective Hub–Mediated Fusion module imposes path constraints that curb cross-modal propagation of redundant information, thereby mitigating noise. A subsequent multimodal heterogeneous MoE enhances the emotion-specific expressiveness of each unimodal representation. Experimental results on the publicly available multimodal sentiment analysis datasets CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI demonstrate that the SH-MHMoE model outperforms most existing methods.

title: Vector Quantization-Based Watermarking for Autoregressive Generated Images
authors: Qiran Lai, Adrian G. Bors
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: Latent-Space Metrics for Complex-Valued VAE Out-Of-Distribution Detection under Radar Clutter
authors: Y. A. Rouzoumka, E. Terreaux, C. Morisseau, J.-P. Ovarlez, C. Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We investigate complex-valued Variational AutoEncoders (CVAE) for radar Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) detection in complex radar environments. We proposed several detection metrics: the reconstruction error of CVAE (CVAE-MSE), the latent-based scores (Mahalanobis, Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD)), and compared their performance against the classical ANMF-Tyler detector (ANMF-FP). The performance of all these detectors is analyzed on synthetic and experimental radar data, showing the advantages and the weaknesses of each detector.

title: Speaking Clearly: A Simplified Whisper-Based Codec for Low-Bitrate Speech Coding
authors: Xin Zhang, Lin Li, Xiangni Lu, Jianquan Liu, Kong Aik Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Speech codecs serve as bridges between continuous speech signals and large language models, yet face an inherent conflict between acoustic fidelity and semantic preservation. To mitigate this conflict, prevailing methods augment acoustic codecs with complex semantic supervision. We explore the opposite direction: a semantic-first approach that starts from a semantically-capable model and adapts it for high-fidelity acoustic reconstruction. Through empirical analysis, we discover that targeted architectural simplification can unlock the acoustic modeling potential of Whisper, a text-aligned Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) model. Based on this finding, we propose SimWhisper-Codec, a novel codec that balances the semantic and acoustic preservation by leveraging a frozen, simplified Whisper encoder without requiring external supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that SimWhisper-Codec achieves superior performance in both semantic preservation and acoustic quality compared to semantically-supervised codecs such as Mimi Codec and SpeechTokenizer at similar bitrates, validating the effectiveness of our semantic-first approach. Code is available at https://github.com/ZhangXinWhut/SimWhisper-Codec.

title: VChangeCodec: An Ultra Low-Complexity Neural Speech Codec with Built-In Voice Changer for Customized Real-Time Communication
authors: Xusheng Yang, Wei Xiao, Bang Yang, Shidong Shang, Yuexian Zou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present VChangeCodec, an ultra low-complexity neural speech codec with built-in timbre adaptation capabilities designed for customized real-time communication (RTC). Unlike cascaded pipelines that combine neural speech codecs (NSCs) with separate voice conversion (VC) systems, which have high latency, VChangeCodec integrates VC into the NSC itself and can seamlessly switch between original and voice change modes. Specifically, we design the NSC by proposing a fully causal convolutional network with scalar quantization to generate compact tokens. In addition, we introduce a lightweight causal projection network that adapts the tokens with target speaker embeddings for decoding into the converted voice, enabling seamless timbre conversion without the need for an additional VC system. Experiments show that, compared to the state-of-the-art speech codec, VChangeCodec reduces parameters by 96.3%, while maintaining competitive speech quality. More importantly, while the cascaded pipeline suffers from more than 100 ms latency, VChangeCodec achieves integrated timbre customization with only 40 ms, making it well-suited for RTC scenarios.

title: DDSR-Net: Robust Multimodal Sentiment Analysis via Dynamic Modality Reliability Assessment
authors: Jianwen Hou, Enguang Zuo, Chaorui Shi, Kurban Ubul
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) aims to understand human emotions by integrating text, vision, and audio. In real-world scenarios, unaligned data exhibit dynamic and uneven modality quality, posing challenges for robust feature learning and fusion. Existing methods rely on static fusion or feature disentanglement, but generally cannot assess modality reliability at the sample level. To address this, we propose DDSR-Net, a framework for Dynamic-quality guided Disentanglement and Selective Reconstruction Network. DDSR-Net first evaluates modality quality per sample, then disentangles features into shared and specific components, and performs Cross-Modal Synergistic Repair, where reliable modalities enhance low-quality ones. Finally, a dynamic focusing attention mechanism fuses enhanced features adaptively. This evaluate–repair–focus pipeline allows robust handling of noise and asymmetry in unaligned multimodal data. Experiments on MOSI and MOSEI show that DDSR-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness and robustness.

title: AnyRIR: Robust Non-Intrusive Room Impulse Response Estimation in the Wild
authors: Kyung Yun Lee, Nils Meyer-Kahlen, Karolina Prawda, Vesa Välimäki, Sebastian J. Schlecht
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We address the problem of estimating room impulse responses (RIRs) in noisy, uncontrolled environments where non-stationary sounds such as speech or footsteps corrupt conventional deconvolution. We propose AnyRIR, a non-intrusive method that uses music as the excitation signal instead of a dedicated test signal, and formulate RIR estimation as an ℓ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf>-norm regression in the time–frequency domain. Solved efficiently with Iterative Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) and Least-Squares Minimal Residual (LSMR) methods, this approach exploits the sparsity of non-stationary noise to suppress its influence. Experiments on simulated and measured data show that AnyRIR outperforms ℓ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-based and frequency-domain deconvolution, under in-the-wild noisy scenarios and codec mismatch, enabling robust RIR estimation for AR/VR and related applications.

title: Large System Analysis of SURE based Hyper- Parameter Optimizing in Sparse Bayesian Learning
authors: Fangqing Xiao, Jichao Chen, Dirk Slock
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper studies stationary points arising from outputdomain Stein’s unbiased risk estimator (SURE) based hyperparameter optimization in sparse Bayesian learning from a large-system perspective. By analyzing the coordinatewise stationary conditions and leveraging tools from random matrix theory, we show that the high-dimensional problem admits a deterministic decoupling, leading to a distributional fixed-point characterization of stationary solutions. This framework reveals that multiple stationary points may naturally coexist. We further provide a local stability interpretation, clarifying which stationary solutions are observable under coordinatewise optimization. The analysis focuses on stationary-point structure rather than specific algorithms or finite-dimensional performance, and provides a principled theoretical understanding of the stationary landscape of SURE-based hyper-parameter learning in sparse Bayesian learning.

title: Dual-Guided Generative Frame Interpolation
authors: Yiying Wei, Hadi Amirpour, Christian Timmerer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: AdaEvol: Dynamic Adapter Merging for Effective Continual Learning and Knowledge Transfer in Large Language Models
authors: Ran Liu, Min Yu, Jianguo Jiang, Gang Li, Rongsheng Li, Mingqi Liu, Zhen Xu, Weiqing Huang, Ming Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) allows large language models (LLMs) to adapt efficiently, yet existing methods for continual learning often require sequential fine-tuning from scratch and rely on auxiliary routing or selection modules, increasing training complexity. Meanwhile, many open-source LoRA adapters already encode transferable knowledge. To this end, we introduce AdaEvol, a continual learning framework that reuses and evolves LoRAs for efficient adaptation and knowledge transfer. For each new task, AdaEvol rapidly identifies and updates the most compatible LoRA, while at inference it integrates multiple LoRAs without a routing mechanism. Both theoretical analysis and empirical results confirm the effectiveness of AdaEvol. It surpasses training from scratch in single-task adaptation and joint fine-tuning in multi-task continual learning. Moreover, it uniquely achieves positive backward transfer on related tasks, a capability absent in prior state-of-the-art methods.

title: Temporal Context and Architecture: A Benchmark for Naturalistic EEG Decoding
authors: Mehmet Ergezer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We study how model architecture and temporal context interact in naturalistic EEG decoding. Using the HBN movie-watching dataset, we benchmark five architectures, CNN, LSTM, a stabilized Transformer (EEGXF), S4, and S5, on a 4-class task across segment lengths from 8 s to 128 s. Accuracy improves with longer context: at 64 s, S5 reaches 98.7%±0.6 and CNN 98.3% ± 0.3, while S5 uses ∼20 × fewer parameters than CNN. To probe real-world robustness, we evaluate zero-shot cross-frequency shifts, cross-task OOD inputs, and leave-one-subject-out generalization. S5 achieves stronger cross-subject accuracy but makes over-confident errors on OOD tasks; EEGXF is more conservative and stable under frequency shifts, though less calibrated in-distribution. These results reveal a practical efficiency–robustness trade-off: S5 for parameter-efficient peak accuracy; EEGXF when robustness and conservative uncertainty are critical.

title: Fed-Game: Personalized Federated Learning with Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts for Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Yi Li, Han Liu, Mingfeng Fan, Guo Chen, Chaojie Li, Biplab Sikdar
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) on graphs shows promise for distributed time-series forecasting. Yet, existing methods rely on static topologies and struggle with client heterogeneity. We propose Fed-GAME, a framework that models personalized aggregation as message passing over a learnable dynamic implicit graph. The core is a decoupled parameter difference-based update protocol, where clients transmit parameter differences between their fine-tuned private model and a shared global model. On the server, these differences are decomposed into two streams: (1) averaged difference used to updating the global model for consensus (2) the selective difference fed into a novel Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME) aggregator for fine-grained personalization. In this aggregator, shared experts provide scoring signals while personalized gates adaptively weight selective updates to support personalized aggregation. Experiments on two real-world electric vehicle charging datasets demonstrate that Fed-GAME outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL baselines.

title: Reinforced Active Learning for Change Point Detection
authors: Stefano Bertolasi, Diego Carrera, Diego Stucchi, Pasqualina Fragneto, Luigi Amedeo Bianchi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Change Point Detection algorithms often rely on hyperparameters that are difficult to tune and highly sensitive to the specific characteristics of the time series. Active Learning offers a promising solution for tuning these hyperparameters by minimizing annotation costs through selective querying of the most informative samples. While classical active learning methods rely on heuristic strategies and recent data-driven approaches employ Reinforcement Learning to optimize query policies, their use in Change Point Detection remains underexplored. In this paper, we propose ReAL4CPD, a novel reinforcement learning based active learning algorithm designed to efficiently guide the change point detection in time series. Our approach improves the generalization of query strategies across diverse datasets, outperforming existing uncertainty-based active learning methods. Experimental results validate that ReAL4CPD effectively reduces labeling effort while enhancing change point detection accuracy.

title: Contrastive Timbre Representations for Musical Instrument And Synthesizer Retrieval
authors: Gwendal Le Vaillant, Yannick Molle
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficiently retrieving specific instrument timbres from audio mixtures remains a challenge in digital music production. This paper introduces a contrastive learning framework for musical instrument retrieval, enabling direct querying of instrument databases using a single model for both single- and multi-instrument sounds. We propose techniques to generate realistic positive/negative pairs of sounds for virtual musical instruments, such as samplers and synthesizers, addressing limitations in common audio data augmentation methods.The first experiment focuses on instrument retrieval from a dataset of 3,884 instruments, using single-instrument audio as input. Contrastive approaches are competitive with previous works based on classification pre-training. The second experiment considers multi-instrument retrieval with a mixture of instruments as audio input. In this case, the proposed contrastive framework outperforms related works, achieving 84.2% top-1 and 96.4% top-5 accuracies for three-instrument mixtures.

title: Frequency-Aware Dynamic Graph Learning Via Pseudo-Spectral Decomposition for Metro Flow Forecasting
authors: Yanzhe Xie, Tuohang Li, Heng Wang, Yujun Ge, Li Huang, Qiang Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate metro flow forecasting requires modeling highly dynamic and multi-scale correlations across different metro stations. Conventional graph-based approaches typically rely on static or heuristic adjacency structures, which fail to capture the temporal variability and spectral diversity inherent in flow signals. This study proposes FDGP, a Frequency-aware Dynamic Graph learning framework grounded in Pseudo-spectral decomposition. Instead of operating purely in the time domain, FDGP leverages Fourier-based analysis on high-dimensional embeddings to obtain pseudo-frequency representations that reveal latent periodicities and multi-band dependencies. By jointly exploiting magnitude and phase spectra, frequency-domain convolution, and spectral attention, FDGP adaptively generates dynamic adjacency matrices that capture both global periodic trends and local fluctuations. Experiments on two real-world metro flow datasets demonstrate that FDGP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, highlighting the effectiveness of frequency-domain modeling in capturing spatio-temporal dependencies among flow signals.

title: Online Test-Time Adaptation for Shadow Segmentation
authors: Di Wu, Haifeng Xia, Libo Sun, Wenhu Qin, Siyu Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: DNS: Data-Driven Nonlinear Smoother for Complex Model-Free Process
authors: Fredrik Cumlin, Anubhab Ghosh, Saikat Chatterjee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose data-driven nonlinear smoother (DNS) to estimate a hidden state sequence of a complex dynamical process from a noisy, linear measurement sequence. The dynamical process is model-free, that is, we do not have any knowledge of the nonlinear dynamics of the complex process. There is no state-transition model (STM) of the process available. The proposed DNS uses a recurrent architecture that helps to provide a closed-form posterior of the hidden state sequence given the measurement sequence. DNS learns in an unsupervised manner, meaning the training dataset consists of only measurement data and no state data. We demonstrate DNS using simulations for smoothing of several stochastic dynamical processes, including a benchmark Lorenz system. Experimental results show that the DNS is significantly better than a deep Kalman smoother (DKS) and an iterative data-driven nonlinear state estimation (iDANSE) smoother.

title: Boosting Knowledge Distillation Via Local Categories Similarity Scaling
authors: Dingyao Chen, Shanshan Zhang, Keyan Jin, Yuxin Zhang, Long Lan, Xiao Teng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers knowledge from a pre-trained teacher network to a lightweight student model. While most KD methods adjust global category similarities by softening the teacher’s output distribution, they struggle to regulate similarities among local categories. This often leads to overconfident predictions and reduced discriminability for similar classes. To address this, we propose Local Categories Similarity Scaling (LCSKD), a plug-and-play framework that enhances KD by refining local similarity transfer. Specifically, we partition the predicted category probabilities into local groups and distill intra-group relations. A temperature adjustment strategy based on distribution "smoothness" is further introduced to calibrate local similarities effectively. LCSKD introduces negligible computational overhead and is compatible with existing KD methods. Experiments on visual tasks validate its effectiveness.

title: Unigeo: A Unified 3d Indoor Object Detection Framework Integrating Geometry-Aware Learning and Dynamic Channel Gating
authors: Xing Yi, Jinyang Huang, Feng-Qi Cui, Anyang Tong, Ruimin Wang, Liu Liu, Dan Guo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing adoption of robotics and augmented reality in real-world applications has driven considerable research interest in 3D object detection based on point clouds. While previous methods address unified training across multiple datasets, they fail to model geometric relationships in sparse point cloud scenes and ignore the feature distribution in significant areas, which ultimately restricts their performance. To deal with this issue, a unified 3D indoor detection framework, called UniGeo, is proposed. To model geometric relations in scenes, we first propose a geometry-aware learning module that establishes a learnable mapping from spatial relationships to feature weights, which enabes explicit geometric feature enhancement. Then, to further enhance point cloud feature representation, we propose a dynamic channel gating mechanism that leverages learnable channel-wise weighting. This mechanism adaptively optimizes features generated by the sparse 3D U-Net network, significantly enhancing key geometric information. Extensive experiments on six different indoor scene datasets clearly validate the superior performance of our method.

title: Faclip : Learnable Fine-Grained Prompts and Multi-Scale Fusion for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection
authors: Shixin Zhao, Sheng Liu, Yuan Feng, Yiheng Yu, Zhelun Jin, Min Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection (ZSAD) enables detecting anomalies without defective samples and has gained increasing attention, current methods based on Vision-Language Models (VLM) like CLIP demonstrates limited sensitivity to fine-grained anomalies, and its prompt templates exhibit constraints when dealing with specific anomaly types. To address these challenges, we propose a novel ZSAD framework called FACLIP, it eliminates the need for fixed text prompts by incorporating learnable anomaly prompts generated with the help of large language models, and it employs a cross-modal multi-scale attention fusion module to integrate multi-scale local visual features across modalities. A local differences filter module is introduced to integrate local visual discrepancies extracted from the original image into the anomaly map. Furthermore, a global-adapter aligns global visual features with text information. FACLIP achieves advanced performance across multiple datasets and demonstrates superior ZSAD ability compared to other approaches.

title: Scalable Bayesian Fine-Tuning of LLMs for Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization
authors: Haotian Xiang, Heng Zhang, Qin Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As the default choice for surrogate modeling in multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO), Gaussian processes (GPs) struggle with irregular high-dimensional variables and non-stationary spaces. To alleviate these challenges, we adapt the prevailing large language model (LLM) as the surrogate model given its powerful capability in feature extraction based on large-scale pre-training. Specifically, we rely on the Bayesian last layer (BLL) framework with the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) of the LLM as a common feature extractor across objectives, which is amenable to scalable variational (V) training with tractable posterior inference. The resulting LoRA-VBLL-MO surrogate can not only accommodate computation- and storage-efficient recursive Bayesian updates across fine-tuning steps, but can also be coupled with Thompson sampling-based expected hypervolume improvement for acquisition function design. Experimental results on MO molecular optimization benchmarks demonstrate superior performance over baselines.

title: 3D Mesh Steganography Algorithm Based on Non-Additive Distortion Minimization
authors: Shuai Ren, Shaoming Wu, Wenhui Deng, Chen Wu, Mo Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper proposes a 3D mesh steganography algorithm using non-additive distortion minimization (NADM). Conventional additive models treat vertex modifications independently, but this overlooks geometric correlations and nonlinear cumulative effects in 3D meshes. Our approach introduces a non-additive distortion function that explicitly models inter-vertex interactions, capturing how neighboring vertices collaboratively influence embedding quality rather than treating them as isolated modifications. The method also incorporates adaptive bit-plane selection using a stability index for blind extraction. Experiments on multiple datasets show NADM outperforms existing additive methods in imperceptibility and robustness, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of the non-additive modeling approach.

title: Mitigating Domain Shift in Ultrasonic wavefield Pattern Analysis Through Test-Time Training
authors: Jiaxing YE, Takumi Kobayashi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This study addresses the critical challenge of domain shift in physics signal analysis of ultrasonic wavefield imaging data for non-destructive testing using neural networks. In real-world, variable environments, measurement data acquired on-site often exhibit statistical discrepancies from training data, leading to severe degradation in inference performance. To mitigate such domain shift at deployment, we propose an efficient framework based on test-time training (TTT) that enables deployed model to adapt to the input data on-the-fly. The primary contributions are threefold: (1) a benchmarking dataset of annotated real ultrasonic wavefield images has been developed to facilitate rigorous evaluation under diverse domain shift settings, including variations in specimen structures, transducer positions, and defect conditions; (2) an enhanced TTT approach is devised to progressively adapt feature representations of deployed models in a self-supervised manner during inference; (3) comprehensive comparisons and ablation studies validate the proposed framework. This research demonstrate that post-deployment learning could be vital for robust neural network-based signal analysis in real applications. The code and dataset can be accessed online<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: RadarEye: Robust Liquid Level Tracking Using Mmwave Radar in Robotic Pouring
authors: Hongyu Deng, He Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transparent liquid manipulation in robotic pouring remains challenging for perception systems: specular/refraction effects and lighting variability degrade visual cues, undermining reliable level estimation. To address this challenge, we introduce RadarEye, a real-time mmWave radar signal processing pipeline for robust liquid level estimation and tracking during the whole pouring process. RadarEye integrates (i) a high-resolution range–angle beamforming module for liquid level sensing and (ii) a physics-informed mid-pour tracker that suppresses multipath to maintain lock on the liquid surface despite stream-induced clutter and source container reflections. The pipeline delivers sub-millisecond latency. In real-robot water-pouring experiments, RadarEye achieves a 0.35 cm median absolute height error at 0.62 ms per update, substantially outperforming vision and ultrasound baselines.

title: SF-MVD: Sensor Failure-Aware Multi-Modal Vehicle Detection with Lidar-Radar Fusion in Foggy Weather
authors: Jiake Tian, Yi Zou, Dacheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Autonomous driving perception systems face severe challenges under adverse weather, where cameras and LiDAR often degrade or fail completely. Radar sensors are more robust but produce sparse point clouds with unreliable height estimation, limiting the effectiveness of multimodal fusion. To address this, we propose the Sensor Failure-aware Multi-modal Vehicle Detection framework, termed SF-MVD, which for robust vehicle detection under sensor failures. SF-MVD integrates sensor-failure augmentation with a teacher–student collaborative learning paradigm, where both models share identical architectures and incorporate a radar height query module. This module leverages LiDAR data to compensate for radar height deficiencies, enhancing fusion accuracy. The student model is trained on failure-augmented data under teacher consistency supervision, enabling robust knowledge transfer.. Experiments on the Oxford Radar RobotCar dataset show that the teacher model outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving 95.8%, 94.2%, and 81.5% at intersection over union thresholds of 0.5, 0.65, and 0.8. Even under sensor failures, the student model maintains strong performance (90.0%, 88.2%, and 74.6%).

title: SSG-DIT: A Spatial Signal Guided Framework for Controllable Video Generation
authors: Peng Hu, Yu Gu, Liang Luo, Fuji Ren
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Controllable video generation synthesizes content aligning with user conditions like text and images. However, existing models struggle with semantic consistency, as they focus on general text-to-video alignment while overlooking spatial information in prompts, causing semantic-spatial misalignment where objects exist but lack proper positions, scales, and interactions. We propose SSG-DiT (Spatial Signal Guided Diffusion Transformer), an efficient framework for high-fidelity controllable video generation. Our two-stage approach uses Spatial Signal Prompting to generate spatially aware visual prompts via pre-trained CLIP. Combined with original text, these form joint conditions injected into frozen DiT through our lightweight SSG-Adapter. This dual-branch attention design enables the model to harness generative priors while being steered by external spatial signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate SSG-DiT achieves state-of-the-art performance on VBench, particularly excelling in spatial relationship control and consistency, outperforming existing models across multiple key metrics.

title: Diffusion-Based Natural Adversarial Perturbations Towards Segment Anything Model
authors: Haodong Xiao, Wenbo Yu, Hao Fang, Shuoyang Sun, Bin Chen, Xuan Wang, Shu-Tao Xia
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Segment Anything Model (SAM) is widely used in many applications and serves as a foundation model in computer vision. Meanwhile, the security issues of SAM have also attracted widespread attention. Although different adversarial attacks targeting SAM have been proposed, they have not paid sufficient attention to the stealthiness of malicious images crafted by attackers. In this paper, we introduce a diffusion-based approach that generates natural adversarial samples targeting SAM, such that the perturbed images remain imperceptibly natural to human observers while leading to incorrect segmentation. By leveraging gradients derived from the diffusion model’s guidance mechanism, our method generates adversarial samples to fool SAM by perturbing both foreground and background semantics. Additionally, we propose an image high-frequency region selection strategy based on the Sobel operator, which efficiently identifies regions where applying perturbations can minimize the effect on image naturalness, thus achieving an optimal trade-off between adversarial attack effectiveness and visual imperceptibility. Extensive experimental results across diverse models and datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior overall performance in balancing attack efficacy and visual stealth compared to existing approaches.

title: Hashing-Baseline: Rethinking Hashing in the Age of Pretrained Models
authors: Ilyass Moummad, Kawtar Zaher, Lukas Rauch, Alexis Joly
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Information retrieval with compact binary codes, also referred to as hashing, is crucial for scalable fast search applications, yet state-of-the-art hashing methods require expensive, scenario-specific training. In this work, we introduce Hashing-Baseline, a strong training-free hashing method leveraging powerful pre-trained encoders that produce rich embeddings. We revisit classical, training-free hashing techniques—principal component analysis, random orthogonal projection, and threshold binarization—to produce a strong baseline for hashing. Our approach combines these techniques with frozen embeddings from state-of-the-art vision and audio encoders to yield competitive retrieval performance without any additional learning or fine-tuning. To demonstrate the generality and effectiveness of this approach, we evaluate it on standard image retrieval benchmarks as well as a newly introduced benchmark for audio hashing.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: CodecSlime: Temporal Redundancy Compression of Neural Speech Codec via Dynamic Frame Rate
authors: Hankun Wang, Yiwei Guo, Chongtian Shao, Bohan Li, Kai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Current mainstream neural speech codecs are fixed-frame-rate (FFR). However, speech is inherently non-uniform in temporal information density. As a result, many tokens are wasted on steady-state segments like long vowels and silences. To address this mismatch, we present CodecSlime, a plugin-style method for compressing temporal redundancy through supporting dynamic frame rate (DFR) on neural speech codecs. Our method is unsupervised and architecture-agnostic, combining two key innovations, ScheDFR and Melt-and-Cool, for adapting inference and training, respectively. When integrated into a typical VQ-GAN codec backbone and operating at 40 Hz DFR (≈600 bps), the reconstruction WER of CodecSlime is reduced by up to 32% relative to conventional FFR baselines with the same model architecture and similar bitrates, while other metrics are also competitive. CodecSlime also enables flexible trade-offs between reconstruction quality and bitrate: a single model supports inference at multiple frame rates and consistently outperforms FFR models at the corresponding frame rates. Audio samples and more results are available at https://x-lance.github.io/codecslime/.

title: Learning Piezoelectric Hysteresis in In-Ear MEMS Loudspeakers from Acoustic Measurements
authors: Oliviero Massi, Alessandro Ilic Mezza, Riccardo Giampiccolo, Alberto Bernardini
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Piezoelectric MEMS loudspeakers represent a viable technology for in-ear audio applications, offering several key advantages for the audio market geared towards miniaturization. Yet, their nonlinearities, particularly hysteretic effects in electro-mechanical transduction, pose challenges for accurate modeling and distortion compensation. We address this by introducing a hybrid MEMS loudspeaker equivalent circuit model that embeds a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to represent hysteretic transduction as a nonlinear pre-distortion block, driving a Wave Digital Filter (WDF) implementation of a linear equivalent circuit model of the loudspeaker mechanical and acoustic domains. The RNN is trained end-to-end within the differentiable WDF simulation using voltage–pressure measurements. Experiments confirm close agreement with measured responses and highlight the model’s potential for MEMS loudspeaker linearization applications.

title: From Hallucination to Articulation: Language Model-Driven Losses for Ultra Low-Bitrate Neural Speech Coding
authors: Jayeon Yi, Minje Kim
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: "Phoneme Hallucinations (PH)" commonly occur in low-bitrate DNN-based codecs. It is the generative decoder’s attempt to synthesize plausible outputs from excessively compressed tokens missing some semantic information. In this work, we propose language model-driven losses (LM loss) and show they may alleviate PHs better than a semantic distillation (SD) objective in very-low-bitrate settings. The proposed LM losses build upon language models pretrained to associate speech with text. When ground-truth transcripts are unavailable, we propose to modify a popular automatic speech recognition (ASR) model, Whisper, to compare the decoded utterance against the ASR-inferred transcriptions of the input speech. Else, we propose to use the timed-text regularizer (TTR) to compare WavLM representations of the decoded utterance against BERT representations of the ground-truth transcriptions. We test and compare LM losses against an SD objective, using a reference codec whose three-stage training regimen was designed after several popular codecs. Subjective and objective evaluations conclude that LM losses may provide stronger guidance to extract semantic information from self-supervised speech representations, boosting human-perceived semantic adherence while preserving overall output quality. Demo samples, code, and checkpoints are available online<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: IGCNET: Dual-Branch Implicit Feature and Global Context Network for Agricultural Parcel Delineation
authors: Ye Li, Liruizhi Jia, Chao Liu, Bo Kong, Yuan Liu, Shengquan Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The delineation of farmland parcels is fundamental for agricultural monitoring and resource management, yet existing methods often suffer from boundary incompleteness, irregular shapes, and global inconsistency. To address these issues, we propose the Implicit–Global Context Network (IGCNet), which integrates two key branches: one for implicit feature interactions to enhance nonlinear local representations, and another for global context modeling that captures directional long-range dependencies through a state-space mechanism. Experiments on the Netherlands and FHAPD-JS datasets show that IGCNet achieves superior boundary accuracy and structural integrity under diverse farmland conditions. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of the parallel dual-branch design in balancing fine-grained boundary refinement with global consistency. These results demonstrate that IGCNet provides a reliable and adaptable solution for farmland parcel segmentation, supporting applications in crop monitoring, yield estimation, and precision agriculture. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Prodigal-lqg/IGCNET/.

title: FIG: Frequency-Based Integrated Gradients for Robust Feature Attribution
authors: Kun Yang, Zhizhong Niu, Fangmin Xie, Ziyao Tang, Guoqiang Xiao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: LogPTR: Variable-Aware Log Parsing with Pointer Network
authors: Yifan Wu, Bingxu Chai, Siyu Yu, Ying Li, Pinjia He, Wei Jiang, Jianguo Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Due to the sheer size of software logs, developers rely on automated log analysis. Log parsing, which parses semi-structured logs into a structured format, is a prerequisite of automated log analysis. However, existing log parsers are unsatisfactory when applied in practice because they 1) ignore categories of variables, and 2) need labor-intensive model tuning. To address these limitations, we propose LogPTR, a variable-aware log parser that can extract the static and dynamic parts in logs, and further identify categories of variables. The key of LogPTR is formulating log parsing as a text summarization problem and using a pointer mechanism to copy words from the log message and label tokens indicating categories of variables. The experimental results on widely-used benchmark datasets show that LogPTR outperforms state-of-the-art log parsers on both general log parsing that extracts log templates and variable-aware log parsing that further identifies categories of variables.

title: Heterogeneous Spatial Temporal Graph Neural Network for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Yue Zhang, Zhiqiang Li, Zhu Chen, Yimin Qi, Ning Gui
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multivariate time series (MVTS) forecasting requires modeling complex spatial, temporal, and cross-dimensional relations. However, existing methods often focus on homogeneous relations, neglecting heterogeneous interactions. To fill this gap, we propose HSTGNN, a Heterogeneous Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network that represents MVTS as a heterogeneous graph with distinct node types and both homogeneous and heterogeneous edges. A graph structure learning module refines latent dependencies, while a heterogeneous attention layer integrates relation-specific representations. Experiments on real-world datasets show HSTGNN outperforms 11 state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines and captures meaningful dynamics, achieving strong accuracy and interpretability.

title: UA-TTRL: Uncertainty-Aware Test-Time Reinforcement Learning
authors: Zixuan Liu, Siavash Khajavi, Guangkai Jiang, Xinru Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are deployed online, they increasingly must adapt at inference time, where explicit supervision is unavailable and user inputs may deviate substantially from the training distribution. Test-Time Reinforcement Learning (TTRL) addresses this by repeatedly sampling outputs and treating majority vote (MV) as a pseudo-label for policy updates. However, under out-of-distribution (OOD) shift, MV can be unreliable, causing reinforcement of incorrect answers and unstable learning. We propose Uncertainty-Aware TTRL (UA-TTRL), a simple, robust method that quantifies the reliability of MV and uses it to modulate test-time updates. UA-TTRL estimates a lower confidence bound (LCB) on pairwise log-odds between the MV winner and its rivals, converts these statistics into a soft confidence weight, and integrates this weight into PPO as a per-prompt scaling of the loss. We evaluate UA-TTRL on three challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks and observe consistent gains over TTRL baselines that treat MV as a hard label. These results show that uncertainty-aware weighting yields a more faithful proxy for correctness, improves robustness under OOD shift, and reduces reliance on human annotations, enabling more reliable, continual adaptation of LLMs at test time.

title: Residual Vector Quantization for Communication-Efficient Multi-Agent Perception
authors: Dereje Shenkut, B.V.K. Vijaya Kumar
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-agent collaborative perception (CP) improves scene understanding by sharing information across connected agents such as autonomous vehicles, unmanned aerial vehicles, and robots. Communication bandwidth, however, constrains scalability. We present ReVQom, a learned feature codec that preserves spatial identity while compressing intermediate features. ReVQom is an end-to-end method that compresses feature dimensions via a simple bottleneck network followed by multi-stage residual vector quantization (RVQ). This allows only per-pixel code indices to be transmitted, reducing payloads from 8192 bits per pixel (bpp) of uncompressed 32-bit float features to 6-30 bpp per agent with minimal accuracy loss. On DAIR-V2X real-world CP dataset, ReVQom achieves 273× compression at 30 bpp to 1365× compression at 6 bpp. At 18 bpp (455×), ReVQom matches or outperforms raw-feature CP, and at 6-12 bpp it enables ultra-low-bandwidth operation with graceful degradation. ReVQom allows efficient and accurate multi-agent collaborative perception with a step toward practical V2X deployment.

title: Universal Denoising Patterns for Diffusion Image Detection
authors: Yurui Qian, Qi Cai, Yingwei Pan, Ting Yao, Yan Chen, Tao Mei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The proliferation of photorealistic, AI-generated images threatens public trust, creating an urgent need for detectors that can reliably distinguish them from real content. However, existing detectors, typically trained on limited known generators, struggle to generalize to unseen ones. The rapid evolution of diffusion models has intensified this problem, creating a "diffusion fog" that limits practical applicability of existing detectors. To pierce this fog, we posit that the inherent patterns in the denoising process of all diffusion models are key to generalizable detection. To isolate these patterns, we create minimal fake training images by applying a single-step denoising operation to slightly perturb real images, forcing the detector to learn the subtle artifacts of denoising. We then blend these minimal fakes with their real counterparts, erasing superficial cues from the perturbation operation to prevent detector from learning shortcuts. Considering that the fake images closely resemble real ones, we propose a feature separation loss to enhance detector’s discrimination capacity. For rigorous evaluation, we constructed a new benchmark, DiffuGen, comprising 65K synthetic images from 13 modern diffusion models and 5K real images. Empirical results demonstrate that our detector achieves significantly improved generalization, reaching an average accuracy of 99% on DiffuGen.

title: A Graph-Driven Hybrid CNN-GNN Approach for Vessel Classification in Adaptive Optics Images
authors: Abir Aissa, Guillaume Lachaud, Florence Rossant, Hélène Urien, Michel Paques
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Adaptive Optics Ophthalmoscopy (AOO) enables high-resolution retinal imaging for clinical biomarker analysis. While existing methods automatically segment retinal bifurcations and extract vascular biomarkers, they require manual selection of high-quality arterial regions, limiting scalability and efficiency. We propose a fully automated patch-based CNN-GNN framework for vessel classification in AOO images. Our method extracts deep features from image patches, constructs graphs with cost-weighted edges capturing vessel connectivity, then applies Graph Attention Networks (GATs) for classification. This hybrid approach integrates local features with global topological information through graph processing, eliminating manual region selection requirements. We evaluate our pipeline on a dataset of 4,258 vessel patches across four CNN/Transformer backbones (ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B2, DenseNet-121, TinyViT). The backbone+GAT combination consistently outperforms backbone-only baselines, achieving up to 86.5% accuracy with improvements ranging from 1.9% to 4.4% across architectures. Our approach provides a robust, scalable foundation for automated vascular biomarker analysis in AOO imaging, supporting more efficient clinical workflows.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Scale Feature Fusion with Transformer for Time Series Prediction
authors: Jianhong Lin, Zongxia Xie, Yun Wang, Haoyu Xing, Feng Xiong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Real-world time series data typically exhibit multi-scale characteristics, such as short-term local fluctuations and long-term global trends, which are crucial for accurate prediction. However, existing methods often employ simple concatenation, weighted summation, or attention mechanisms to fuse multi-scale feature. They tend to overlook the differences in noise across scales within the input data, as well as the variations in uncertainty within prediction results. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel time series prediction framework—UMFF (Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Scale Feature Fusion with Transformer). UMFF characterizes uncertainty by leveraging input data noise and prediction confidence, and dynamically adjusts fusion weights across multiple scales based on the probability density functions associated with each scale, thereby enhancing the accuracy and stability of predictions. Experimental results demonstrate that UMFF consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance in both point prediction and probabilistic prediction tasks, achieving superior results across multiple benchmark datasets. The code is available at https://github.com/ljhjxt/umff.

title: Cross-View Change Detection via Self-Supervised Contrastive Representation Learning
authors: Ghassen Graja, Olfa Besbes, Takoua Abdellatif
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Change detection (CD) is a fundamental task in remote sensing. Existing CD methods often require precise co-registration, geometric correction, and large annotated datasets, limiting their scalability in heterogeneous or time-critical scenarios. We address cross-view heterogeneous CD between archived satellite imagery and newly acquired UAV data under annotation scarcity. We propose a self-supervised contrastive learning framework that learns robust representations from unlabeled bi-temporal pairs. Our method integrates CutMix-based data augmentation and a cross-attention module within a Swin Transformer backbone to enhance multi-scale, cross-modal feature fusion. Experiments on a challenging cross-view dataset demonstrate that our approach outperforms strong baselines with minimal supervision, highlighting its potential for efficient and reliable real-world change detection.

title: BMDC: A Hybrid Bigbird and Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution Model For Seizure Detection From Scalp EEG
authors: Hao Geng, Jinjie Guo, Guanglong Zhang, Guixia Kang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate seizure detection from electroencephalogram (EEG) remains challenging due to long temporal spans and complex dynamics. EEG signals contain both temporal dependencies and spatial correlations, requiring joint modeling for reliable detection. Existing methods often employ Transformers for spatial representation and LSTMs for temporal dynamics, but suffer from quadratic attention complexity and sequential inefficiency. We propose BMDC, a hybrid model combining BigBird sparse attention with Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution. BigBird captures global and local dependencies with linear complexity, while Multi-Scale Dilated Convolution extracts multi-scale temporal features in parallel. Evaluated on the Temple University Seizure Corpus (TUSZ), BMDC consistently outperforms Transformer–LSTM and other baselines in AUROC, accuracy and Weighted F1-score, while achieving faster inference, demonstrating its scalability and robustness for clinical seizure detection.

title: Enhancing Domain Generation Through Pluggable Style Randomization
authors: Bin Xiao, Jiahao Xu, Mo Yang, Min Wang, Xingpeng Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Domain generalization is an effective approach to mitigating distribution shifts in image data and improving model robustness. However, the limited diversity of styles in the source domain often fails to capture real-world distributions. To address this, we propose Feature Style Randomization (FSR) to sensitivity to style-domain information. FSR introduces noise and integrates a novel style-transfer strategy to enrich the stylistic diversity of training data. To reduce distortions from style augmentation, we design a Style Consistency and Classification (SCC) module that employs dual classifiers to ensure reliable outputs for augmented samples, while introducing a consistency loss to align predictions between augmented and original data. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: On the Role of Extrinsic Value Exchange in Expectation Propagation for Coded MIMO Systems
authors: Fuga Kobayashi, Takumi Takahashi, Hideki Ochiai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper investigates the statistical properties of log-likelihood ratios (LLRs) at the output of an iterative signal detector based on expectation propagation (EP), highlighting the role of moment matching (MM)-based extrinsic value exchange in coded systems. Prior studies have mainly examined convergence behavior in asymptotic regimes; however, EP’s advantage is not always evident in practical finite-size uncoded multi-input multi-output (MIMO) detection, where probabilistic data association (PDA), which lacks extrinsic value exchange, can perform comparably to or even better than EP. In contrast, when channel coding is applied, EP consistently outperforms PDA. To explain this, we analyze the statistical properties of demodulator output LLRs and show that extrinsic value exchange not only ensures inter-iteration independence but also enhances the Gaussianity of LLRs. We quantify this effect using negentropy and demonstrate its benefit in improving the decoder’s error-correcting capability, thereby establishing the superiority of EP in coded systems.

title: Frequency-Modulated Differential Transformer for Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images
authors: Xin Li, Feng Xu, Jue Zhang, Xin Lyu, Fan Liu, Lingqiang Meng, Yiwei Fang, André Kaup
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing images (HRRSIs) is crucial for a wide range of geospatial applications. However, complex textures, ambiguous boundaries, and high background redundancy often degrade model performance. In this paper, we propose FMDFormer, a novel Frequency-Modulated Differential Transformer designed to enhance segmentation precision by integrating spatial differential attention with frequency-domain modulation. Specifically, we introduce the FMDFormer Block, a dual-branch architecture that combines spatial-domain differential attention with frequency-enhanced features derived via Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). This design enables effective suppression of irrelevant activations while emphasizing high-frequency cues such as boundaries and contours. A gated fusion mechanism adaptively integrates both branches to enhance feature diversity and localization capability. Extensive experiments on ISPRS Potsdam and LoveDA datasets demonstrate that FMDFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing existing CNN and transformer-based methods. Comprehensive ablations validate the complementary advantages of the proposed dual-domain attention mechanism.

title: EduGesture: A Dataset of Teachers’ Hand Gestures Toward Pedagogical Intentions
authors: Jiawen Li, Yuqing Chen, Fei Jiang, Aimin Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Teacher gestures are of great importance for nonverbal communication with students during the class. However, existing researches of teachers’ gesture analysis mainly focus on gesture recognition with predefined categories, which are hardly to capture instructional intentions expressed by these gestures. To address such limitation, we present EduGesture, the first dataset of teachers’ hand gestures with explicit instructional intentions description. Specifically, EduGesture comprises 5k+ images curated from award-winning lessons across various subjects and grades. Each image is annotated with a functional label derived from educational research and an expert-refined natural language description that expresses instructional intentions. This dual annotation framework links categorical supervision with teaching semantics, extending gesture recognition from morphology to pedagogical meaning. Then, we build baselines for teacher hand gesture classification and teaching intention understanding for further research on multimodal teaching analysis. Finally, we demonstrate the extra values of EduGesture with two applications, i.e., instructional hands generation and realistic gesture motion generation of virtual teachers.

title: Enhancing Speaker Verification with w2v-BERT 2.0 and Knowledge Distillation Guided Structured Pruning
authors: Ze Li, Ming Cheng, Ming Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large-scale self-supervised Pre-Trained Models (PTMs) have shown significant improvements in the speaker verification (SV) task by providing rich feature representations. In this paper, we utilize w2v-BERT 2.0, a model with approximately 600 million parameters trained on 4.5 million hours of unlabeled data across 143 languages, for the SV task. The MFA structure with Layer Adapter is employed to process the multi-layer feature outputs from the PTM and extract speaker embeddings. Additionally, we incorporate LoRA for efficient fine-tuning. Our model achieves state-of-the-art results with 0.12% and 0.55% EER on the Vox1-O and Vox1-H test sets, respectively. Furthermore, we apply knowledge distillation guided structured pruning, reducing the model size by 80% while achieving only a 0.04% EER degradation. Source code and models are released at https://github.com/ZXHY-82/w2v-BERT-2.0_SV.

title: Penguin: General Vital Sign Reconstruction from PPG with Flow Matching State Space Model
authors: Shuntaro Suzuki, Shuitsu Koyama, Shinnosuke Hirano, Shunya Nagashima
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Photoplethysmography (PPG) plays a crucial role in continuous cardiovascular health monitoring as a non-invasive and cost-effective modality. However, PPG signals are susceptible to motion artifacts and noise, making accurate estimation of vital signs such as arterial blood pressure (ABP) challenging. Existing estimation methods are often restricted to a single-task or environment, limiting their generalizability across diverse PPG decoding scenarios. Moreover, recent general-purpose approaches typically rely on predictions over multi-second intervals, discarding the morphological characteristics of vital signs. To address these challenges, we propose PENGUIN, a generative flow-matching framework that extends deep state space models, enabling fine-grained conditioning on PPG for reconstructing multiple vital signs as continuous waveforms. We evaluate PENGUIN using six real-world PPG datasets across three distinct vital sign reconstruction tasks (electrocardiogram reconstruction, respiratory monitoring, and ABP monitoring). Our method consistently outperformed both task-specific and general-purpose baselines, demonstrating PENGUIN as a general framework for robust vital sign reconstruction from PPG.

title: Isometric Immersion Learning with Riemannian Geometry for Distortion-Free Representation
authors: Zihao Chen, Wenyong Wang, Qinye Zhu, Yu Xiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
