We present a novel MCP-based architecture designed for context-aware configuration, though our current implementation uses simulated MCP responses. The framework includes comprehensive validation, security analysis, and demonstrates the feasibility of natural language-driven infrastructure automation while establishing architectural patterns for future context-aware systems.

title: Identifying Affective Engagement States in Digital Learning Using Synchronized Electroencephalography
authors: Menekse Adar, Serkan Varol, Zack Ridder
abstract: Understanding affective engagement is important in digital learning environments. This study investigates emotional engagement states using electroencephalography (EEG) data collected during online learning activities. EEG signals were recorded from seven undergraduate engineering students while they interacted with four courses that followed different instructional structures. Affective engagement was represented using EEG-derived proxies for valence and arousal, computed from frontal alpha asymmetry and beta–alpha ratios. Temporal patterns, statistical distributions, and spectral band-power maps were analyzed at both participant and course levels. The results show that different course structures are associated with distinct affective engagement patterns. Case-based and chronological courses exhibit higher arousal variability, while structured modular courses show more stable engagement. These findings suggest that instructional design influences learners' affective states and highlight the potential of EEG-based analysis for evaluating digital learning experiences.

title: Building Information Modeling as a Strategic Tool to Implement PV+BESS Microgrids That Enhance Grid Resilience and Sustainability: A Case Study in the World Heritage Island of Fernando De Noronha, Brazil
authors: N/A
abstract: This paper presents an extensive case study on the application of Building Information Modeling (BIM) in the development of the Noronha Verde Project (where “verde” means “green” in Portuguese), a 22.82 MWp photovoltaic complex combined with a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) located on Fernando de Noronha Island, Brazil. The project posed significant challenges due to its remote location, 350 km off the Brazilian coast, its environmental sensitivity as a UNESCO World Heritage site, and complex logistical constraints. The study explores how BIM methodology addresses these challenges in remote renewable energy projects, focusing on risk mitigation, interdisciplinary coordination, environmental impact analysis, and resource optimization. A federated modeling strategy was implemented across three hierarchical levels, supported by cloud-based collaboration through Autodesk Construction Cloud, which served as the Common Data Environment (CDE). 3D coordination tools, automatic clash detection, and more accurate quantity estimates helped identify issues earlier and improved the predictability of the project. Early identification of environmental conflicts—such as flood—prone areas detected during the basic design phase-helped prevent costly delays. The methodology also enabled effective coordination among geographically dispersed teams and ensured precise material specifications, which are critical in logistically constrained environments. Despite these benefits, the study identified challenges such as resistance to methodological changes, uneven BIM expertise among stakeholders, software interoperability issues, and the need for accurate baseline data. Nevertheless, results confirm BIM's strong viability and substantial advantages in remote and environmentally sensitive contexts. The paper concludes that BIM should be highly recommended for renewable energy projects in isolated areas, particularly those with complex environmental constraints and distributed teams. Furthermore, the research highlights the promising potential of integrating BIM with Digital Twin technologies to extend benefits beyond design and construction into operation and maintenance of remote energy infrastructure.

title: Greedy-Cover and Greedy-Demand: Two Enhanced Algorithms for the Maximum Happy Set Problem
authors: Sini Song, Weidong Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Within social network analysis, the Maximum Happy Set (MaxHS) problem—first introduced in 2021 and proven NP-hard—aims to select a fixed-size vertex subset that maximizes the number of “happy” vertices: those whose closed neighborhoods are entirely contained within the chosen subset. Though relevant to network optimization tasks, research on MaxHS remains limited; existing greedy methods, while simple, prioritize immediate gains and often produce suboptimal solutions by overlooking vertices' potential future contributions. Leveraging the well-established efficiency of greedy algorithms in combinatorial optimization, this paper refines the state-of-the-art Greedy-Pick framework with two enhanced approaches. First, Greedy-Cover introduces a cover-degree metric to resolve ties among vertices with identical minimum need-degree, optimizing resource utilization in selection. Second, Greedy-Demand employs a dynamic demand-degree function to quantify each vertex's potential to boost future happy vertex counts, enabling more forward-looking choices. Extensive experiments on 21 real-world social network datasets show that both algorithms outperform Greedy-Pick: Greedy-Cover improves the number of happy vertices by 4.7% on average, while Greedy-Demand achieves a 19.7% average gain—validating their ability to elevate solution quality over conventional greedy strategies.

title: Development of a Calculator to Estimate the Potential for Generating Electricity from Biogas Produced from Municipal Solid Waste
authors: Gabriela González Troche, Daniel Ríos Festner
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Biogas is a renewable energy source that can harness organic waste to produce electricity, supporting the circular economy and environmental sustainability. The objective of this work was to develop a calculation tool to estimate the generation of electricity from biogas produced from Municipal Solid Waste. The methodology was based on the identification of key variables and constants for the calculation, structuring and design of the algorithm, programmed in HTML, CSS and JavaScript; with simple human-computer interaction to minimize data entry errors. As a result, the designed web calculator was obtained, and for its application a case study was carried out in the Polytechnic Faculty, quantifying its residues by Simple Random Sampling and quartering. The values obtained for the Faculty were 189 kg of usable waste per day that produces a daily potential of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$23.88 \text{m3}$</tex> of biogas and 42.76 kWh of electricity, values obtained through the calculator. Unlike other calculation tools that require biochemical parameters of the substrate - information that is often not available in the preliminary stages of project management - as input, this tool uses standard values from the literature. It was concluded that the calculator represents an innovation for decision-making by offering a preliminary estimate that guides the decision-making of project managers.

title: Engineering Analysis and Quantitative Research on the Platform-Based Evolution of Enterprise Communication Systems
authors: Guangbin Gao, Runda Gao, Runhang Gao, Hongchen Zhou
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This study, based on long-term engineering practice, conducted a systematic analysis of the evolution process of the enterprise communication system from the application layer to the platform layer. By integrating the operation and maintenance records of multiple communication systems from 2014 to 2024, a quantitative study was carried out from dimensions such as function module reusability, operational complexity, and service capacity. The statistical results show that as the degree of system platformization increases, the reusability of core functional modules has risen from approximately 30% to over 65%. The annual maintenance manpower input has decreased by approximately 35% to 40%. The research results indicate that the platform-based architecture has significant advantages in supporting the long-term evolution and multi-service coordination of enterprise communication systems.

title: A Systematic Study of Autonomous Driving Perception: Models, Co-operation and Constraints
authors: Vijay Saravana Jaishanker
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: As the research on self-driving systems and autonomous vehicles shift from rule-based autonomy to AIdriven autonomy, machine-learning (ML) inference is used increasingly to support perception, prediction and planning with strict timing and safety constraints. Though the accuracy and complexity of the detection/ fusion models have increased rapidly, the commercialization and robustness of these models are limited by where the inference happens. Factors like latency, reliability, energy use, safety rating and communication overhead decide whether the system is commercially deployable. This paper focuses on the inference and deployment of autonomous systems among three regimes: (i) on-vehicle inference, (ii) co-operative edge inference which uses other vehicles and surrounding smart infrastructure to enhance the AV's worldview, and (iii) cloud/ hybrid inference which offloads heavy computation to a remote server. The trade-offs in safety, communication overhead and robustness under operational stress (sensor failure, network overload, heating issues) are studied. The bottlenecks and performance gains introduced by each method are analyzed and open problems and future focus areas for safety-critical autonomous systems are outlined.

title: Keystone with Linux PREEMPT_RT: Real-Time Enclaves on RISC-V?
authors: Oscar Van Slijpe, Jean-Michel Dricot, Jan Tobias Mühlberg
publish: Proceedings of the 9th Workshop on System Software for Trusted Execution
abstract: None

title: Energy-Aware Images Via Pixel Value Reduction: The Impact of Compression on Attenuation Maps
authors: Emmanuel Sampaio, Franck Aumont, Olivier Le Meur, Thomas Maugey
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video consumption accounts for a significant share of global energy use, with end devices responsible for most of it. On end devices, display technology plays an important role in energy consumption. Interestingly, OLED technology allows power to be adapted via pixel-intensity manipulation. In this context, Pixel Value Reduction (PVR) has shown promising results for lowering display power by generating attenuation maps that adapt image luminance. However, the use of this technology in streaming services has not been fully studied. In this work, we analyze the effect of attenuation-map compression on perceptual quality, bitrate overhead, and end-device energy consumption. Using a pixel-value-reduction model, we generate attenuation maps for target power-reduction levels (10%, 20%, and 40%) and encode them with the HEVC video codec at various quantization-parameter (QP) values (i.e., codec QP). Experiments on 4K content with real OLED power measurements show that compressed attenuation maps maintain high fidelity to the originals, achieving different levels of power reduction with negligible quality loss. Moreover, the results indicate that proper alignment between content and map quantization parameters is critical for reducing bitrate overhead. These findings highlight the feasibility of transmitting compressed attenuation maps to minimize display’s energy consumption.

title: Dual-Decoder Network with Topology Prompting and Edge Refinement for Vascular Segmentation in X-Ray Angiography
authors: Meng Cui, Nan Jiang, Tiansong Li, Zhiming Zhu, Hongbo He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vascular segmentation can accurately extract vascular structures, provide a key basis for the diagnosis and treatment of vascular diseases. However, most existing methods emphasize pixel-level accuracy at the expense of vascular topological connectivity. Maintaining the connectivity of vascular structures is crucial for reliable clinical diagnostics. To address this limitation, we propose a novel dual-decoder architecture, named TER-Net, which incorporates a dedicated topological decoder to explicitly learn the topological properties of vascular networks. The structural priors captured by this decoder are embedded as prompting cues into a semantic decoder, thereby guiding the model toward more precise pixel-wise segmentation while ensuring structural continuity. Extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed approach not only surpasses existing methods but also exhibits consistently superior performance. The code has been made publicly available on https://github.com/TiansongLi/TER-Net.

title: Handling Heterogeneous Features: Modeling Continuous-Discrete Feature Interaction for Time Series Anomaly Detection Via Conditional Diffusion
authors: Anyi Zhang, Peng Xu, Chai Kiat Yeo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Time series anomaly detection is critical for modern systems. Recent diffusion-based models capture complex temporal distributions but usually assume homogeneous features, overlooking the heterogeneity of real data where continuous sensor values and discrete system states interact. We propose CDDiff, a Conditional Diffusion framework for heterogeneous anomaly detection, integrating two designs: a dual-path diffusion process where an autoregressive path reconstructs continuous features and a conditional path which dynamically integrates the discrete features at each reverse step. An anomaly scoring mechanism based on the reconstruction error complements CDDiff’s ability to amplify the subtle anomalies that might be missed by other methods. Experiments on multiple multivariate datasets show that CDDiff consistently surpasses State-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in modeling continuous–discrete interactions and handling real-world complexity.

title: A Dynamic Dual-Backbone Model for Adaptive Vehicle-Pedestrian Detection
authors: Wenbo Liu, Haoran Liu, Chunyu Zhao, Tao Deng, Fei Yan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: As a cornerstone of decision-making in intelligent transportation systems, vehicle-pedestrian detection requires both accuracy and efficiency, yet static models struggle to balance them. Therefore, we present DVPNet, a dynamic dual-backbone detection framework that adapts computation to scene complexity. The model integrates an Efficient Spatial Perception Attention Module (ESPAM) to enhance feature perception, and employs a differentiable neural architecture search–based selector for difficulty-aware path routing. Experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that DVPNet achieves superior robustness and accuracy while reducing redundant overhead, offering a practical solution for real-time vehicle-pedestrian detection.

title: Frequency-Aware Contrastive Learning and Spectral Disentanglement for Unsupervised Image Deraining
authors: Tao Jiang, Zikang Chen, Xiaowan Hu, Wang Zhang, Huaqiu Li, Haoqian Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We reformulate unsupervised image deraining as a signal separation task, addressing the practical challenge of acquiring paired training data. Existing methods often neglect the intrinsic physical prior of the rain signal: its directional high-frequency energy in the Fourier amplitude spectrum. Based on this insight, our method is driven by two core innovations. First, a novel frequency amplitude loss acts as an unsupervised spectral separation constraint, penalizing any spectral overlap to ensure the recovered background is verifiably free of rain’s unique signature. Second, a dual-domain contrastive learning strategy trains a rain streak extractor that accurately models the joint spatio-spectral priors of rain streaks and learns their consistent characteristics. This robust modeling allows our framework to surpass all unsupervised benchmarks and demonstrate superior generalization over state-of-the-art supervised methods on real-world datasets, validating the superiority of our signal prior-based approach. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Windrain7/DDCL.

title: DSTCS: Dual-Student Teacher Framework with Segment Anything Model For Semi-Supervised Pubic Symphysis-Fetal Head Segmentation
authors: Yalin Luo, Shun Long, Huijin Wang, Jieyun Bai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Segmentation of pubic symphysis and fetal head (PSFH) is a critical procedure in intrapartum monitoring, essential for evaluating labor progression and identifying potential delivery complications. However, achieving accurate segmentation remains a significant challenge due to class imbalance, ambiguous boundaries, and noise interference in ultrasound images, compounded by a scarcity of high-quality annotated data. Current research on PSFH segmentation pre-dominantly relies on CNN and Transformer architectures, leaving the potential of more powerful models underexplored. We propose a Dual-Student and Teacher combining CNN and SAM (DSTCS) framework, which integrates the powerful Segment Anything Model (SAM) into a dual student-teacher architecture. A cooperative learning mechanism between CNN and SAM branches significantly improves segmentation accuracy. The proposed scheme also incorporates a specialized data augmentation strategy optimized for boundary processing and a novel loss function. Extensive experiments on the MICCAI 2023 and 2024 PSFH segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that our method exhibits superior robustness and significantly outperforms existing techniques, providing a reliable segmentation tool for clinical practice.

title: RISC-V Microarchitecture Information Leakage Attack via Transient Execution
authors: Jiashun Wang, Rundi Zhai, Yishuo Wang, Chaoyue Liang, Baojiang Cui
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The increasing threat of microarchitectural transient attacks in recent years has presented a significant challenge to processor security. Immediate measures are essential to address the microarchitectural security vulnerabilities in RISC-V processors. This paper systematically evaluates seven commercially available RISC-V processors. The proposed attack framework comprehensively analyzes cache security and timing side-channel attacks across various microarchitectures. Additionally, it introduces three transient execution attack primitives targeting the RISC-V microarchitecture, including a previously undiscovered sub-variant. We implement proof-of-concept attacks on seven distinct microarchitectures and validate their feasibility and effectiveness. Experimental results show that unprivileged attackers can illicitly access sensitive data on real-world RISC-V processors using timing side-channel attacks and transient execution attack primitives. Furthermore, the attack demonstrates a high degree of accuracy, successfully compromising seven microarchitectures with a success rate of approximately 96% and a 30% improvement in efficiency. We reported two CVE vulnerabilities, both of which were confirmed by the developers, highlighting the practicality of the security analysis.

title: TF-MAMBANET: A Temporal and Frequency Fused Bidirectional Mamba Architecture for PPG Foundation Model
authors: Zhanpeng Bao, Yannick Benezeth, Fan Yang, Yonglin Zhang, Haibin Wang, Chao Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Photoplethysmography (PPG) is the most widely used non-invasive physiological signal monitoring technology, which has been extensively applied in clinical health management and wearable devices. However, most existing deep learning models based on PPG are designed for specific tasks and lack cross-scenario generalization capability. This paper proposes TF-MambaNet, a foundation model for PPG signals. The model is pre-trained on 50,000 hours of real-world PPG data collected from 100 users in real-world scenarios. It adopts a lightweight bidirectional Mamba architecture that integrates temporal and frequency domain information, combined with a novel multi-scale hybrid loss function. This design enables the model to capture global trends, local patterns, and amplitude dynamics of blood volume pulse (BVP) signals, and can be easily used for multiple downstream tasks. TF-MambaNet outperforms existing models in 9 of 15 tasks across 8 public datasets, while maintaining an extremely small parameter size, making it directly applicable as a feature extractor for personalized health monitoring.

title: Unlocking The Potential of Social Media Preference for Annotation-Efficient Large Language Model Alignment
authors: Wenbin Zhang, Wanxiang Che
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Preference learning is essential for aligning the generative outputs of Large Language Models (LLMs) with human values, which often relies on costly preference annotation. To mitigate this expense, annotating preference with user feedback from social media platforms presents a promising alternative. However, existing Social Media Preference (SMP) datasets show a significant performance gap compared to benchmark preference datasets. To bridge this gap, we introduce FIR, an analytical framework that dissects SMP dataset into three core components: Feedback, Instruction, and Response pairs. The FIR analysis yields a set of platform-agnostic principles that establish a systematic methodology to guide the construction of SMP datasets: 1) Using verified marks as feedback, 2) A dual-criteria instruction filtering, and 3) Optimizing response pairs. Guided by FIR, we find that carefully designed SMP dataset yields performance that rivals state-of-the-art preference datasets, successfully unlocking the potential of social media data as an annotation-efficient resource for high-quality LLM alignment.

title: Non-Uniform Haze Removal for Remote Sensing Image Based on Wavelet-Domain Haze-Aware Complementary Learning
authors: Yanmeng Liu, Libao Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Non-uniform haze removal in remote sensing image (RSI) remains highly challenging due to the difficulty of modeling spatially varying haze distributions and preserving fine-grained textures. To address these issues, we propose a wavelet-domain haze-aware complementary learning (WHCL) pipeline, which operates primarily in the wavelet domain for more effective, locally adaptive restoration. First, we design a novel haze density analyzer that fuses dark channel, contrast, and color shift cues into haze density maps, providing reliable local conditioning for subsequent restoration. Based on this, we propose a haze-aware complementary modulation framework composed of two cooperating modulators: a global structure modulator (GSM) that adaptively recalibrates low-frequency representations to suppress large-scale atmospheric scattering, and a texture detail modulator (TDM) that selectively amplifies structurally salient high-frequency responses to recover fine-grained textures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WHCL achieves superior performance in both quantitative metrics and visual quality.

title: Solving Poisson Inverse Problems with Diffusion Models via the Plug-and-Play Scheme
authors: Yang Zheng, Wen Li, Zhaoqiang Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Poisson inverse problems (IPs) commonly arise in photon-limited imaging applications, where the number of detected photons is extremely small and the noise is most accurately modeled by a Poisson distribution. In this work, we leverage diffusion models (DMs) to address Poisson IPs. We first propose a new DM-based method for Poisson IPs and formulate the corresponding optimization problem. Furthermore, we decompose the optimization problem in our proposed framework into two subproblems, which enables more efficient inference. Extensive experiments on multiple image datasets and across different Poisson noise intensities demonstrate that our methods outperform existing DM-based approaches in most cases.

title: KLGate: Leveraging LLM Explanations via KL-Guided Gating for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
authors: Jie Xiong, Ran Zhang, Jun Wang, Yang Zhang, Li Yu, Jigang He
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) must recognize ironic intent that often arises from semantic mismatch between image and text. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can verbalize such mismatches as conflict explanations, offering useful high-level cues, but their outputs are prone to noise and hallucination. To reconcile these strengths and weaknesses, we propose KLGate. It leverages LLM-generated conflict explanations and employs a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence–based gate to align them with the original modalities, preserving reliable signals while suppressing noise. The gated features are fused with visual and textual representations to generate the final prediction. KLGate sets a new state of the art on standard benchmarks, achieving a binary F1 of 90.35 with 1.42 points gain over the best baseline.

title: FED-PISA: Federated Voice Cloning Via Personalized Identity-Style Adaptation
authors: Qi Wang, Shituo Ma, Guoxin Yu, Hanyang Peng, Yue Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Voice cloning for Text-to-Speech (TTS) aims to generate expressive and personalized speech from text using limited data from a target speaker. Federated Learning (FL) offers a collaborative and privacy-preserving framework for this task, but existing approaches suffer from high communication costs and tend to suppress stylistic heterogeneity, resulting in insufficient personalization. To address these issues, we propose Fed-PISA, which stands for Federated Personalized Identity-Style Adaptation. To minimize communication costs, Fed-PISA introduces a disentangled Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) mechanism: the speaker’s timbre is retained locally through a private ID-LoRA, while only a lightweight style-LoRA is transmitted to the server, thereby minimizing parameter exchange. To harness heterogeneity, our aggregation method, inspired by collaborative filtering, is introduced to create custom models for each client by learning from stylistically similar peers. Experiments show that Fed-PISA improves style expressivity, naturalness, and speaker similarity, outperforming standard federated baselines with minimal communication costs.

title: Negative-Aware Routing Network with Adversarial Knowledge Injection for Efficient LLM Adaptation
authors: Xingchen Li, Qibo Qiu, Chao Sun, Yilin Zhang, Yuchao Yan, Li Yu, Jian Jiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has been widely used in Large Language Model (LLM) adaptation for its ability to scale model capacity with limited computation. However, conventional routing strategies assign only non-negative weights to experts, which may overlook the adversarial effects of conflicting expert knowledge. We argue that negative routing scores can indicate such conflicts and should be preserved to model complex token–expert interactions. Our empirical results further show that retaining negative signs of routing weights can improve model performance. On this basis, we propose a novel framework, NOVA, consisting of two key components: a Negative-Aware Routing Network (NARN), which embeds tokens and experts into a shared knowledge space and leverages cosine similarity to capture both positive and negative influences; and an Adversarial Knowledge Injection (AKI) module, which synthesizes adversarial token signals to explicitly guide the model in distinguishing expert contributions. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks verify the effectiveness of our method.

title: Rail-Level Power Profiling of Embedded Video Playback
authors: Sayeh Janani, Muhammad Amin Qureshi, Christian Herglotz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This work presents an experimental study of power consumption during video playback on an embedded platform with ARM Cortex-A53 processors. We analyze how workloads based on the H.264 and H.265 video coding standards affect power usage across different internal power rails. We perform detailed rail-level monitoring during decoding. The results show that the power of the platform is primarily driven by resolution, with only a marginal difference between H.264 and H.265 decoding. At the subsystem level, the SoC and platform domains dominate overall consumption, while ARM activity grows with resolution, and CPU/HDMI rails remain negligible.

title: GEIA: Generative Enhancement Inversion Attack Targeting Machine Unlearning
authors: Yaoxuan Ma, Xiaoyang Xu, Juan Wang, Wenzhe Yi, Yaxin Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Machine unlearning aims to empower models to selectively expunge designated data upon request, thereby safeguarding individual privacy. However, recent studies have indicated that unlearning methods may not fully mitigate privacy risks, as disparities between original and unlearned models might inadvertently expose excised data details. Nonetheless, existing attack methods frequently fail to capture the pixel-level details of unlearned samples, resulting in feature loss and excessive pixelation. In this paper, we introduce a novel attack framework targeting the feature recovery of unlearned samples, named Generative Enhancement Inversion Attack (GEIA). Unlike prior works, GEIA employs a two-stage strategy for high-fidelity feature recovery. The first stage uses latent semantic modeling to extract intrinsic patterns, while the second integrates a novel hierarchical architecture for multi-scale semantic detail reconstruction, effectively recovering local textures and global structures. Extensive experiments confirm GEIA’s superiority over the state-of-the-art methods, exposing vulnerabilities in machine unlearning.

title: TLD-PGD: Two-Stage Low Frequency Degradation Adversarial Attack in Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Ziyu You, Yaqi Liu, Song Xiao, Weize Li, Yifan Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Enhancing the transferability of adversarial attacks is a critical challenge in Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification. This is difficult for HSI, as its complex localized features cause models to learn distinct representations, limiting attack generalization. Traditional frequency-domain attacks, often global, fail to disrupt these crucial local features. This paper proposes the Two-Stage Low-Frequency Degradation Projected Gradient Descent (TLD-PGD) framework to address this. TLD-PGD employs a novel Low-Frequency Degradation (LFD) loss to disrupt localized low-frequency patterns that are more likely to be model-invariant. It also integrates a Wavelet-based Low-Frequency Gradient Smoothing (WLGS) technique to refine the perturbation direction and suppress gradient noise, thereby improving transferability. Experiments on two HSI datasets validate that our method generates resilient adversarial examples with superior transferability across diverse target architectures.

title: Brain-Score Meets Representational Similarity Analysis: a Methodological Convergence in Model-Brain Alignment
authors: Zhejun Zhang, Wenqing Zhou, Chengyi Zhao, Liang Dong, Lin Zhang, Lei Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Brain-Score (BS) and Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) are two mainstream approaches for evaluating model-brain alignment, based on predictive mapping and geometric similarity paradigms, respectively. Despite widespread adoption, their intrinsic consistency remains unvalidated. Using the DERCo EEG dataset, we systematically examined correspondence between BS and RSA across 47 Large Language Models (LLMs) spanning 0.5B–14B parameters. Our results reveal remarkable convergence: both metrics exhibit consistent inverted U-shaped distributions peaking at intermediate layers, with strong positive correlations within individual models (46/47 significant, mean r = 0.725) and robust aggregate correlation across 1,496 observations (r = 0.403, p < 0.001). These findings were further validated on the Podcast ECoG dataset. Our architecture-agnostic, cross-modality findings demonstrate that predictive mapping and geometric similarity capture highly overlapping aspects of model-brain alignment, establishing empirical foundations for unified NeuroAI frameworks. Code is available at https: //github.com/Mochizuki-BUPT/Brain-Score-Meets-RSA.

title: SODA: A Unified Framework for Joint Estimation of Speaker Orientation and Direction of Arrival
authors: Dongchan Kim, Jonguk Yoo, Kangseok Choi, Chang Woo Han, Hoon-Young Cho, Jong Hwan Ko
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Next-generation voice user interfaces must jointly infer a speaker’s orientation (direction of voice, DOV) and direction of arrival (DOA), yet practical deployment of such joint estimators has been hindered by large microphone arrays and the lack of unified datasets. We propose SODA, a unified framework for few-channel (2 or 4) settings that addresses these barriers. SODA repurposes an existing DOV dataset to generate DOA labels, employs a multi-task architecture, and, through stepwise lightweight design, selects a practical complexity–performance balance to support deployment on resource-constrained devices. Experiments show that SODA with 2 or 4 channels surpasses the 16-channel baseline on distance and orientation metrics, demonstrating the feasibility of high-precision spatial audio sensing with few microphones.

title: Single-DMRS Based CFO Estimation for Low Latency Cellular Communications
authors: Ahmed Abdelkader, Waqar Anwar, Zhibin Yu, Xiaofeng Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The emerging cellular communication systems require both high throughput and low latency, which can be realized by high-order modulation schemes and reliable carrier-frequency offset (CFO) estimation with a low pilot overhead. In the fifth generation (5G) new radio (NR) system, single front-loaded demodulation reference symbols (DMRS) have been introduced for low-latency channel estimation in ultra reliable low latency communications (URLLC) scenarios. However, due to the multi-access interference (MAI) within a single DMRS, the CFO estimation still has to rely on other paired reference symbols, such as the tracking reference symbols (TRS). This paper proposes a novel method which enables CFO estimation by using a single DMRS. The method leverages the structure of the block interleaved frequency division multiple access (B-IFDMA) to perform joint MAI separation and CFO estimation. Simulations show that the proposed method can approach the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) and can sufficiently avoid (DL) error vector magnitude (EVM) degradation for high-order modulation based low-latency communications.

title: Deep Unfolded Superiorized POCS for Robust Joint Transmission under Phase Misalignment
authors: Maximilian Klemm, Jochen Fink, Ezer Melzer, Doron Ezri, Sławomir Stańczak
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Joint transmission (JT) is a promising technology to increase the spectral efficiency in next-generation Wi-Fi systems by serving users coherently via multiple access points (APs) in the downlink. However, in practical systems, phase misalignment between APs can degrade the performance of JT schemes. The recently proposed robust joint transmission (ZI-RJT) scheme addresses this problem through closed-form precoders that are restricted to a certain subspace. Since the ZI-RJT precoders are derived assuming a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), we propose a new precoding strategy which takes into account the noise variance. We propose an iterative algorithm based on superiorization and projections onto convex sets (POCS) which aims at maximizing the spectral efficiency. Moreover, we propose a deep-unfolded variant (DU-S-POCS) of this algorithm, which achieves superior performance compared to ZI-RJT with just one iteration.

title: ParaEdit: Unifying Parallel Transport and Generative Flows for High-Fidelity Image Editing
authors: Yuan Zhou, Jinyoung Wen, Yuyang Hong, Kun Ding, Yan Zhang, Guangwen Yang, Shiming Xiang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing inversion-based image editing methods often rely on a two-stage process of inverting an image to a noisy latent and then regenerating the image, a detour which introduces semantic artifacts and degrades fine-grained details. We present ParaEdit, a training-free, model-agnostic framework that reimagines editing through differential geometry. ParaEdit avoids explicit inversion by constructing a direct editing trajectory on the latent manifold between source and target states, guided by parallel transport. The trajectory follows a minimal-energy principle, analogous to a Linear Quadratic Regulator, via incremental corrections to the underlying vector fields. Experiments show ParaEdit preserves fine details, and demonstrates its superior sharpness and semantic consistency over state-of-the-art methods, underscoring the efficacy of a geometric approach for high-fidelity editing.

title: Personal Sound Zones with Flexible Bright Zone Control
authors: Wenye Zhu, Jun Tang, Xiaofei Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Personal sound zone (PSZ) reproduction system, which attempts to create distinct virtual acoustic scenes for different listeners at their respective positions within the same spatial area using one loudspeaker array, is a fundamental technology in the application of virtual reality. For practical applications, the reconstruction targets must be measured on the same fixed receiver array used to record the local room impulse responses (RIRs) from the loudspeaker array to the control points in each PSZ, which makes the system inconvenient and costly for real-world use. In this paper, a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) designed for PSZ reproduction with flexible control microphone grid and alternative reproduction target is presented, utilizing the virtual target scene as inputs and the PSZ prefilters as output. Experimental results of the proposed method are compared with the traditional method, demonstrating that the proposed method is able to handle varied reproduction targets on flexible control point grid using only one training session. Furthermore, the proposed method also demonstrates the capability to learn global spatial information from sparse sampling points distributed in PSZs.

title: Resolution-Progressive Diffusion Model for Pansharpening
authors: Shaowu Wu, Keyan Jin, Lihui Chen, Lihua Jian, Xingyu Liu, Liwei Jing, Xiaoguang Niu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models have gained widespread adoption in pansharpening tasks due to their superior generative capabilities and robust performance. However, existing diffusion model-based pansharpening methods primarily focus on conditional denoising. These methods overlook the fundamental objective of pansharpening: the explicit enhancement of spatial resolution, which results in excessive sampling steps and reduced efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose a Resolution-Progressive Diffusion Model (RPDiff) that reduces the sampling steps of diffusion processes and inherently aligns with the pansharpening task by progressively reconstructing images at increasing resolutions. Our model employs Implicit Neural Representation (INR) to enable continuous resolution progression and incorporates a multi-branch fusion architecture. The model first employs three encoders to extract spectral information, spatial information, and noise observation features from source images, respectively. Subsequently, the extracted features are processed through implicit neural representations to construct feature maps at the next timestep resolution. Finally, a decoding fusion module integrates spectral and spatial information to produce the fused high-resolution multispectral image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed model achieves superior performance in both visual quality and quantitative metrics compared to state-of-the-art approaches. Project page at: https://github.com/ShaowuCh/RPDiff

title: Image-Pixel Realignment for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation via Self-Training
authors: Anyi Yang, Qibo Liu, Yawen Fan, Quan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) seeks to assign pixel-level labels to arbitrary categories, including unseen ones, but faces the challenge of transferring image-level semantics from vision-language models to fine-grained dense prediction. We introduce STSeg, a novel framework that integrates pixel-level semantic alignment with adaptive self-training. A dual embedding aggregation module fuses global semantics from CLIP with spatially precise features from a frozen SAM model via cross-attention, yielding high-quality pixel representations. On top of this, a self-learning module refines SAM-guided pseudo-labels through iterative optimization, enabling the model to continuously update its knowledge. This mechanism allows STSeg not only to generalize effectively to novel categories but also to dynamically adapt during inference without additional supervision. Extensive evaluations on multiple OVSS benchmarks validate the effectiveness of STSeg, demonstrating its competitive accuracy, strong generalization, thereby highlighting its potential for real-world open-world applications.

title: Automated Dysphagia Screening Using Noninvasive Neck Acoustic Sensing
authors: Jade Chng, Rong Xing, Yunfei Luo, Kristen Linnemeyer-Risser, Tauhidur Rahman, Andrew Yousef, Philip A Weissbrod
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pharyngeal health plays a vital role in essential human functions such as breathing, swallowing, and vocalization. Early detection of swallowing abnormalities, also known as dysphagia, is crucial for timely intervention. However, current diagnostic methods often rely on radiographic imaging or invasive procedures. In this study, we propose an automated framework for detecting dysphagia using portable and noninvasive acoustic sensing coupled with applied machine learning. By capturing subtle acoustic signals from the neck during swallowing tasks, we aim to identify patterns associated with abnormal physiological conditions. Our approach achieves promising test-time abnormality detection performance, with an AUC-ROC of 0.904 under 5 independent train–test splits. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using noninvasive acoustic sensing as a practical and scalable tool for pharyngeal health monitoring.

title: IBMCT: Breaking The Cost Barrier in Industrial Internet of Things Via High-Fidelity Virtual Sensing
authors: Qingzheng Hu, Chong Zhang, Qiuyan He, Wenyang Xiao, Hangyu Xiong, Ningrong Lai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is essential for developing smart cities, yet its large-scale deployment is constrained by the high cost of sensor hardware. To overcome this limitation, we propose IBMCT, a deep learning model designed to generate high-fidelity virtual sensor data from sparse physical sensor networks through high-precision, time-series-based spatial interpolation. To achieve this goal, we propose Inception Bidirectional Mamba (IB-Mamba) block, which integrates the Mamba with a multi-scale convolutional module, effectively capturing spatiotemporal dependencies at various scales and complex temporal patterns. Additionally, we propose the Trend-Infused Coordinate Attention (TICA) mechanism, which reallocates spatiotemporal weights and emphasizes temporal trends, thereby improving spatial interpolation accuracy and enhancing robustness against noise. Evaluated using real-world data from public datasets, our model offers a cost-effective pathway to advance IIoT development and its scalable application in urban infrastructure.

title: MAPEX: A Multi-Agent Pipeline for Keyphrase Extraction Using LLMs
authors: Liting Zhang, Shiwan Zhao, Aobo Kong, Qicheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Keyphrase extraction is a fundamental task in natural language processing. However, existing unsupervised prompt-based methods with Large Language Models (LLMs) typically rely on simplistic prompt designs, which limit their robustness across document lengths and hinder the full exploitation of LLMs’ reasoning and generation capabilities. To address these challenges, we propose MAPEX, the first framework that introduces multi-agent collaboration into keyphrase extraction. MAPEX coordinates an LLM-based Extractor and an Enhancer, leveraging modules for role-based prompting, topic guidance, knowledge augmentation, and post-processing. A dual-path strategy enables dynamic adaptation to document length: short texts are processed via knowledge-driven extraction, while long texts benefit from topic-guided extraction. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets across three different LLMs demonstrate its strong generalization and universality, outperforming the state-of-the-art (SOTA) unsupervised method by 2.44% and standard LLM baselines by 4.01% in F1@5 on average. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/NKU-LITI/MAPEX.

title: Toward Faithful Explanations in Acoustic Anomaly Detection
authors: Maab Elrashid, Anthony Deschênes, Cem Subakan, Mirco Ravanelli, Rémi Georges, Michael Morin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Interpretability is essential for user trust in real-world anomaly detection applications. However, deep learning models, despite their strong performance, often lack transparency. In this work, we study the interpretability of autoencoder-based models for audio anomaly detection, by comparing a standard autoencoder (AE) with a mask autoencoder (MAE) in terms of detection performance and interpretability. We applied several attribution methods, including error maps, saliency maps, SmoothGrad, Integrated Gradients, GradSHAP, and Grad-CAM. Although MAE shows a slightly lower detection, it consistently provides more faithful and temporally precise explanations, suggesting a better alignment with true anomalies. To assess the relevance of the regions highlighted by the explanation method, we propose a perturbation-based faithfulness metric that replaces them with their reconstructions to simulate normal input. Our findings, based on experiments in a real industrial scenario, highlight the importance of incorporating interpretability into anomaly detection pipelines and show that masked training improves explanation quality without compromising performance.

title: Towards Language-Independent Face-Voice Association with Multimodal Foundation Models
authors: Aref Farhadipour, Teodora Vukovic, Volker Dellwo
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper describes the UZH-CL system submitted to the FAME2026 Challenge. The challenge focuses on cross-modal verification under unique multilingual conditions, specifically unseen and unheard languages. Our approach investigates two distinct architectures, consisting of a baseline dual-encoder system trained from scratch using contrastive and orthogonal projection losses, and a foundation model approach leveraging ImageBind with LoRA. To address the data scarcity and language constraints of the challenge, we curated an external Arabic dataset from VoxBlink. Our best-performing system, ImageBind-LoRA, demonstrates remarkable cross-lingual generalization: despite being fine-tuned exclusively on Arabic audio, it achieved an EER of 24.73% on the evaluation set (English and German), securing 2nd place in the competition.

title: MIDI-LLaMA: An Instruction-Following Multimodal LLM for Symbolic Music Understanding
authors: Meng Yang, Jon McCormack, Maria Teresa Llano, Wanchao Su, Chao Lei
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLM) for audio music have demonstrated strong capabilities in music understanding, yet symbolic music, a fundamental representation of musical structure, remains unexplored. In this work, we introduce MIDI-LLaMA, the first instruction-following MLLM for symbolic music understanding. Our approach aligns the MIDI encoder MusicBERT and Llama-3-8B via a two-stage pipeline comprising feature alignment and instruction tuning. To support training, we design a scalable annotation pipeline that annotates GiantMIDI-Piano with fine-grained metadata, resulting in a MIDI–text dataset. Compared with the baseline trained on converting MIDI into ABC notation under the same instruction-tuning procedure, MIDI-LLaMA substantially outperforms in captioning and semantic alignment in question answering. Human evaluation further confirms the advantages of MIDI-LLaMA in music understanding, emotion recognition, creativity, and overall preference. These findings demonstrate that incorporating symbolic music into large language models enhances their capacity for musical understanding.

title: FedAMF: Federated Alignment with Multi-Part Feature for Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
authors: Bhairi Neelima, Nirmala Murali, Deepak Mishra
abstract: Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification (VI-ReID) faces challenges due to the large domain gap between RGB - IR images of numerous sensors and the privacy issues associated with centralizing surveillance data. To handle these two issues, we propose FedAMF (Federated Alignment with Multi-part Feature) that integrates a part-based feature learning module to enhance local representations with detailed body-region features, hence improving resistance to inter-class similarity, pose changes, and illumination changes. Key to our model is a collaborative alignment mechanism through which clients obtain globally aggregated class prototypes that represent feature vectors for each identity which are later used to direct local feature learning. By matching local features with shared prototypes, FedAMF effectively mitigates cross-modality inconsistencies and inter-client feature variances without needing the raw data exchange. This prototype-based, multi-objective training approach guarantees that the acquired representations are both discriminative and modality-invariant. Furthermore, we incorporate Differential Privacy (DP) to formally measure the security of the model, and our privacy leakage analysis shows a low bounded privacy loss of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon=1.23$</tex>. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that FedAMF attains competitive VI-ReID performance while maintaining stringent privacy standards, providing a viable and scalable solution for real-world applications.

title: An Efficient Channel Estimation Structure for 6G IRS-Assisted Mimo Via DFT-Noma with Enhanced Uplink Performance
authors: S. Seethalakshmi, Mahendran R, M Soniya, Surendran R, K S. Balamurugan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Computing, Intelligent Communication and Smart Electrical Systems (ICSES)
abstract: This article develops a novel channel estimation scheme for the IRS-aided 6G MIMO uplink communications. Different from the current IRS-aided NOMA architectures, this model includes an enhanced downlink DFT-based multiuser approach, in addition to the optimised IRS beamforming and the reducedcorrelation channel modelling. For the sake of fair comparison, the system parameters are typical and updated as follows: 12 W transmit power, 128 IRS elements, 120 GHz carrier frequency, 8 dB antenna gain, reflection coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.95$</tex>, and optimised RRC pulse with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta=0.3$</tex> in order to have strong signal reception performance improvements. Simulation results show significant performance improvement over the baseline method, i.e., received power gain exceeding 50 % for any user pair, SINR increase of 17 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2 ~ d B}$</tex> and correlation reduction by more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 5 \%}$</tex>. It confirms that the introduced enhanced-DFT IRSMIMO uplink model substantially enhances channel estimation accuracy, spectral efficiency and multiuser robustness, thereby making it a promising candidate for 6 G communication systems.

title: A Lightweight and Accurate Defect-Detection Framework for Battery Cells Based on EfficientNet-YOLOv9
authors: Yixiang Liu, Jinlong Wu, Bing Leng, Haihua Guo, Yibin Li
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Applications (ICARA)
abstract: In this study, a lightweight and high-accuracy visual inspection framework is developed for distinguishing defective and normal prismatic lithium-ion battery cells under non-standardized recycling conditions. An EfficientNet-YOLOv9 variant is constructed by replacing the baseline CSP backbone with a compound-scaled feature extractor. Experimental results show that the proposed EfficientNet-YOLOv9 achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text {1. 1 1 M}$</tex> parameters and 6.4 GFLOPs, representing a 46.6% reduction in parameters and a 24.7% reduction in computation compared with YOLOv9-t(2.08M, 8.5 GFLOPs). And the EfficientNet-YOLOv9 model achieves strong detection results, with Precision reaching 0.946, Recall reaching 0.960, mAP50 reaching 0.986, and mAP50-95 reaching 0.915. These results indicate that the proposed framework maintains high discriminative capability while substantially reducing model size and computational cost. Overall, EfficientNet-YOLOv9 provides an effective, lightweight, and computation-efficient solution for large-scale battery-cell inspection and offers strong potential for deployment in automated recycling systems.

title: Chairs and Program Committees: SYNASC 2025
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 27th International Symposium on Symbolic and Numeric Algorithms for Scientific Computing (SYNASC)

title: AI-Based Offline Speech Recognition for Kazakh, Russian and English Languages
authors: Nursultan Nyssanov, Zuleikha Syzdykova, Kuandyk Niyazaliyev, Ibraheem Shayea
publish: 2025 IEEE/ACIS 29th International Conference on Software Engineering, Artificial Intelligence, Networking and Parallel/Distributed Computing (SNPD)
abstract: This paper presents a novel fully autonomous multilingual audio transcription system tailored to Kazakh, Russian, and English. The proposed solution integrates a language detection module based on SpeechBrain with a transcription engine using Vosk, and employs FFmpeg for robust audio preprocessing. The system automatically detects the language from the initial 10 seconds of an audio stream, selects the corresponding acoustic model, and produces an accurate text transcription. Experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real audio data indicate that our approach achieves competitive performance in terms of accuracy (with word error rates ranging from 5 to 10% under optimal conditions) and processing speed, while operating entirely on local resources without dependency on cloud services. These features make it particularly suitable for applications in digital forensics and other domains that require secure real-time transcription capabilities.

title: CFAI-Net: Cross-Stage Frequency-Aware Interaction Network For Fine-Grained Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Yang Liu, Ge Jiao, Guangcheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Edge blurring and structural detail loss remain major obstacles in medical image segmentation. Although Vision Mamba models long-range dependencies efficiently, they insufficiently capture high-frequency information, leading to degraded boundary reconstruction. We propose CFAI-Net (Cross-Stage Frequency-Aware Interaction Network), which integrates two key modules for cross-stage frequency modeling. The Cross-stage Frequency Interaction Module (CFIM) adaptively decouples and enhances high- and low-frequency components during feature fusion, while the Wavelet Frequency Refinement Block (WFRB) performs frequency-band decomposition and multi-scale aggregation for precise boundary reconstruction. Experiments on Synapse and ACDC datasets show that CFAI-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving average Dice by up to 1.35%, validating its effectiveness in detail preservation and segmentation accuracy. Code is available at: https://github.com/ipika9/CFAI-NET.

title: Learning Class Similarities for Enhanced Image Out-Of-Distribution Detection
authors: Dimitrios Spanos, Nikolaos Passalis, Anastasios Tefas
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OoD) detection is critical for deploying deep learning models in safety-sensitive signal applications such as image recognition. Conventional classification pipelines, which rely on one-hot labels and cross-entropy loss, often suffer from feature collapse, where semantically diverse or ambiguous inputs are mapped to indistinguishable representations. This limits the reliability of uncertainty estimation and hampers OoD detection. In this work, we address these limitations by introducing a framework that explicitly models class similarities during training. An auxiliary neural network is employed to refine labels dynamically, enabling the feature space to better capture semantic relationships and mitigating the risk of collapse. Our approach utilizes soft labels that arise by the inherent similarities of the classes identified by the neural network and integrates seamlessly into existing pipelines. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves OoD detection performance as well as calibration error metrics.

title: Change-Aware Temporal Alignment on Heterogeneous Graph Snapshots for Insider Threat Detection
authors: Min Yang, Yingjie Zhou, Di Wu, Guansong Pang, Ce Zhu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Accurate insider threat detection (ITD) is crucial for uncovering potential security risks in organizations, such as data leaks and privilege abuse. However, the covert nature of insider threats poses significant challenges to detecting anomalies from massive heterogeneous log data. We propose a user-level ITD approach based on temporally-aligned heterogeneous graph snapshots, which can effectively model the evolution of users’ interaction patterns in a continuous semantic space. The method employs network logs to construct heterogeneous graph snapshots, which characterize entities’ complex interactions based on specially designed meta-paths. Compared with a single oversized graph structure, our graph construction avoids high storage and computational overhead. The temporal dynamics of entities’ interactions are also effectively captured by a change-aware smoothing regularizer through enforcing the semantic alignment of representations of similar behaviors across adjacent time steps. Experiments on two datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach under limited anomaly annotations.

title: Conditional Random Fields for Interactive Refinement of Histopathological Predictions
authors: Tiffanie Godelaine, Maxime Zanella, Karim El Khoury, Saïd Mahmoudi, Benoît Macq, Christophe De Vleeschouwer
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Assisting pathologists in the analysis of histopathological images has high clinical value, as it supports cancer detection and staging. In this context, histology foundation models have recently emerged. Among them, Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide strong yet imperfect zero-shot predictions. We propose to refine these predictions by adapting Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to histopathological applications, requiring no additional model training. We present HistoCRF, a CRF-based framework, with a novel definition of the pairwise potential that promotes label diversity and leverages expert annotations. We consider three experiments: without annotations, with expert annotations, and with iterative human-in-the-loop annotations that progressively correct misclassified patches. Experiments on five patch-level classification datasets covering different organs and diseases demonstrate average accuracy gains of 16.0% without annotations and 27.5% with only 100 annotations, compared to zero-shot predictions. Moreover, integrating a human in the loop reaches a further gain of 32.6% with the same number of annotations. The code is available on https://github.com/tgodelaine/HistoCRF.

title: TURTLEBENCH: Evaluating Top Language Models via Real-World Yes/No Puzzles
authors: Qingchen Yu, Shichao Song, Ke Fang, Yunfeng Shi, Zifan Zheng, Hanyu Wang, Simin Niu, Zhiyu Li, Yanfang Chen, Xiaoqing Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing benchmarks for Large Language Models (LLMs) are often static, knowledge-dependent, or costly, hindering the reliable evaluation of reasoning in dynamic user interactions. To address this, we propose TurtleBench, a novel benchmark derived from real user guesses in an online "Turtle Soup Puzzle" game. This approach yields a dynamic dataset that isolates logical reasoning from background knowledge, mitigating risks of data contamination and model cheating. TurtleBench contains 1,532 annotated user guesses, on which we evaluated nine leading LLMs. Our results show that OpenAI's o1 series models lag behind other top competitors. We hypothesize this stems from limitations in their latent reasoning, such as simplistic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) processes and noise from longer reasoning paths. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/mazzzystar/TurtleBench.

title: Empowering the Tail: Adaptive Semantic Neighborhood Enhancement for Long-Tail Reasoning in Temporal Knowledge Graphs
authors: Yi Yang, Bin Chen, Zhangtao Cheng, Kaiju Li, Jia Chen, Ting Zhong, Fan Zhou
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning (TKGR) plays a vital role in modeling dynamically evolving real-world facts. However, its performance is severely hindered by the long-tail distribution of entities, which induces strong predictive bias against infrequent (tail) entities and significantly undermines the effectiveness of existing methods. To address this challenge, we propose ALSNE, a lightweight and model-agnostic plug-in for adaptive long-tail semantic neighborhood enhancement, designed to alleviate the long-tail issue in TKGR. ALSNE first retrieves semantically similar head entities from the recently interacted neighborhood of each tail entity to provide rich transferable contextual knowledge. This informative knowledge from head entities is then adaptively integrated into the tail entity, facilitating the effective transfer of rich evolutionary patterns that enhance the tail entities’ representation. Extensive experiments conducted on three real-world TKG datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ALSNE.

title: Robust Kalman Filter for Additive Gaussian-Student’s T Distribution
authors: Gurui Jin, Pengcheng Hao, Zhenyu Liu, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Ercan E. Kuruoğlu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing robust Kalman filters (RKFs) fail to capture hybrid noise structures, where Gaussian background noises coexist with impulsive heavy-tailed components. In this paper, we propose RKF-AGST, a novel variational Bayesian framework formulated by the additive Gaussian-Student's t (AGST) distribution. Unlike single heavy-tailed distributions such as Gaussian or Student's t (ST) used in conventional RKFs, AGST explicitly decomposes noises into Gaussian and Student's t components, thereby enabling more accurate modeling of hybrid noise environments. A maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimator is incorporated within the variational framework to handle the non-conjugate inference for the scale parameter. Our method is validated in simulated target tracking scenarios. Experiments show that RKF-AGST achieves lower RMSE (position) compared to both the reference filters.

title: RIR-Former: Coordinate-Guided Transformer for Continuous Reconstruction of Room Impulse Responses
authors: Shaoheng Xu, Chunyi Sun, Jihui Aimee Zhang, Prasanga Samarasinghe, Thushara Abhayapala
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Room impulse responses (RIRs) are essential for many acoustic signal processing tasks, yet measuring them densely across space is often impractical. In this work, we propose RIR-Former, a grid-free, one-step feed-forward model for RIR reconstruction. By introducing a sinusoidal encoding module into a transformer backbone, our method effectively incorporates microphone position information, enabling interpolation at arbitrary array locations. Furthermore, a segmented multi-branch decoder is designed to separately handle early reflections and late reverberation, improving reconstruction across the entire RIR. Experiments on diverse simulated acoustic environments demonstrate that RIR-Former consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of normalized mean square error (NMSE) and cosine distance (CD), under varying missing rates and array configurations. These results highlight the potential of our approach for practical deployment and motivate future work on scaling from randomly spaced linear arrays to complex array geometries, dynamic acoustic scenes, and real-world environments.

title: Test-Time Adaptation for Speech Enhancement via Mask Polarization
authors: Tobias Raichle, Erfan Amini, Bin Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Adapting speech enhancement (SE) models to unseen environments is crucial for practical deployments, yet test-time adaptation (TTA) for SE remains largely under-explored due to a lack of understanding of how SE models degrade under domain shifts. We observe that mask-based SE models lose confidence under domain shifts, with predicted masks becoming flattened and losing decisive speech preservation and noise suppression. Based on this insight, we propose mask polarization (MPol), a lightweight TTA method that restores mask bimodality through distribution comparison using the Wasserstein distance. MPol requires no additional parameters beyond the trained model, making it suitable for resource-constrained edge deployments. Experimental results across diverse domain shifts and architectures demonstrate that MPol achieves very consistent gains that are competitive with significantly more complex approaches.

title: C-Conformer: Channel-Augmented Conformer for Sound Event Localization and Detection
authors: Changjiang He, Siyao Cheng, Jiahua Bao, Jie Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Three-dimensional sound event localization and detection (SELD) jointly performs event classification, direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, and source distance estimation, enabling comprehensive acoustic scene understanding. Recently, the ResNet–Conformer architectures have shown strong performance on SELD. However, the Conformer is primarily designed for single-channel audio recognition and models only temporal dependencies, overlooking inter-channel features for multi-channel spatial perception. To address this, we propose the Channel-augmented Conformer (C-Conformer), which extends the Conformer by explicitly modeling both temporal and inter-channel relationships. C-Conformer preserves temporal self-attention (TSA) and introduces a lightweight channel self-attention (CSA) module using depthwise convolutions to maintain temporal order. The TSA and CSA outputs are fused via learnable weights. To mitigate the distribution gap between synthetic and real data, we further design a Dual-of-Experts (DOE) CSA mechanism, enhancing cross-domain robustness. Extensive experiments show that ResNet–C-Conformer (RCC) consistently outperforms most existing models, achieving up to 8.4% F-score improvement. With DOE CSA, RCC effectively leverages both synthetic and real datasets, surpassing our previous system, which ranked second in the DCASE2025 SELD Challenge.

title: A Unified Multi-Encoder Framework for Music Lyric Intelligibility Assessment: Bridging Invasive and Non-Invasive Paradigms
authors: Jing Yang, Shenghao Liao, Shuqing Zhang, Yongyi Deng, Pan Li, Zerui Han, Gongping Huang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Lyric intelligibility significantly impacts music engagement and comprehension, especially for hearing-impaired individuals. Existing speech intelligibility metrics perform poorly in musical contexts due to differences in prosody, timing, and spectral dynamics. We propose a unified multi-encoder framework that supports both invasive and non-invasive modeling for robust lyric intelligibility prediction. By capturing complementary local acoustic-semantic alignments, global musical semantics, and ASR-derived textual cues, our method achieves superior performance over baselines on the ICASSP 2026 Cadenza dataset, with the best model achieving 26.61 RMSE, a 9.2% improvement over the baseline, demonstrating its flexibility and robustness.

title: AGI-CLIP: Multi-Modal LLM Knowledge Transfer For Ai-Generated Image Quality Assessment
authors: Chi Zhang, Xiangfei Sheng, Zhichao Yang, Pengfei Chen, Leida Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of generative models, AI-generated image quality assessment (AGIQA) has become increasingly critical. Existing AGIQA methods primarily rely on CLIP, which suffers from significant domain gaps between CLIP’s natural image training data and AI-generated images, along with insufficient task-specific knowledge. To address these limitations, we propose AGI-CLIP, which incorporates quality-aware knowledge from multi-modal large language models (MLLMs). Specifically, we first design a multi-MLLM judge strategy that employs ensemble and verification mechanisms to generate reliable multi-attribute quality comments. Then we develop Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) multi-attribute adapters to map the initial CLIP into feature spaces that capture representations of quality attributes. Extensive experiments on multiple AGIQA benchmarks demonstrate that AGI-CLIP significantly outperforms existing methods. Data and code will be publicly available https://github.com/1carrot1/AGI-CLIP.

title: Trustworthy and Privacy-Preserving Perceptual Hashing with Zero-Knowledge Proofs for Client-Side Content Scanning
authors: Dehong Meng, Zhangshuang Guan, Peng Zhao, Hongbing Cheng, Zhiguo Wan
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In client-server applications such as copyright protection and content moderation, learning-based perceptual hashing compresses images into compact binary codes whose Hamming distances approximate perceptual similarity. Clients then transmit these codes to servers for comparison. However, this approach faces dual challenges: algorithmically, how to effectively balance robustness and discriminability while mitigating bit imbalance issues; protocol-wise, transmitting these hashes compromises client privacy through content inference and cross-platform user tracking. To address these challenges, we propose a trustworthy privacy-preserving framework that integrates deep hashing with zero-knowledge proofs. The framework comprises: (1) A robust deep hashing module that generates discriminative binary codes by optimizing a composite objective function composed of the Angular Triplet and quantization losses, while using a multi-scale strategy to correct bit imbalance. (2) A privacy-preserving similarity comparison protocol based on Sumcheck and Logarithmic Lookup, which enables clients to locally prove batch Hamming distance relationships against public dataset entries without disclosing their hash values. We conducted comprehensive evaluations to demonstrate the practicality and efficiency of our design compared to existing schemes. Source code is available at https://github.com/mengdehong/zkph.

title: Distribution-Aware Mobility-Assisted Decentralized Federated Learning
authors: Md Farhamdur Reza, Reza Jahani, Richeng Jin, Huaiyu Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) eliminates reliance on a central server by enabling peer-to-peer model aggregation, but suffers from limited connectivity and non-IID data. In many applications, some clients are mobile, yet the role of mobility in DFL remains underexplored. This work shows that introducing even a small fraction of mobile clients, moving randomly, significantly improves information flow and accuracy. To further enhance performance, we propose structured distribution-aware mobility strategies, where mobile clients navigate the network using knowledge of data distributions and static client locations. These strategies mitigate the impact of data heterogeneity and accelerate convergence. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of induced mobility in DFL and demonstrate the superiority of our proposed mobility patterns over random movement.

title: Event-Aided Semantic Scene Completion
authors: Shangwei Guo, Hao Shi, Song Wang, Xiaoting Yin, Kailun Yang, Kaiwei Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Recent advances in Semantic Scene Completion (SSC) for autonomous driving underscore the limitations of RGB-based approaches, which struggle under motion blur, poor lighting, and adverse weather. Event cameras, offering high dynamic range and low latency, address these challenges by providing asynchronous data that complements RGB inputs. We present DSEC-SSC, the first real-world benchmark specifically designed for event-aided SSC, which includes a novel 4D labeling pipeline for generating dense, visibility-aware labels that adapt dynamically to object motion. Our proposed RGB-Event fusion framework, EvSSC, introduces an Event-aided Lifting Module (ELM) that effectively bridges 2D RGB-Event features to 3D space, enhancing view transformation across SSC models. Evaluations on DSEC-SSC and simulated SemanticKITTI-E show that EvSSC is adaptable to both transformer-based and LSS-based SSC architectures, achieving up to a 52.5% relative improvement in mIoU under degraded conditions on the corrupted SemanticKITTI-C dataset. The established datasets and our codebase will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Pandapan01/EvSSC.

title: Joint Compression and Direction-of-Arrival Estimation in Distributed Sensor Networks
authors: Amir Weiss
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The growing prevalence of large-scale sensor networks calls for direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation methods that operate under stringent communication constraints. In this work, we show that DOA can be reliably inferred when each remote sensor conveys only extremum indices of its observations, requiring just a few bits. Using these indices, the fusion center computes a steered-response-power-like criterion, whose maximizer is a consistent DOA estimator. Perhaps surprisingly, simulations reveal that our method approaches the accuracy of a theoretical rate-distortion-based benchmark while requiring dramatically fewer bits than conventional compression followed by classical DOA methods.

title: MatchGaussian: Adaptive Densification with Matching Priors for Generalizable Gaussian Splatting
authors: Zhiyan Wang, Zhanke Wang, Jiahao Wu, Guanhua Wu, Runling Liu, Ronggang Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
