To address these limitations, we propose MedCoRef, a semi-supervised classification framework that improves accuracy under limited annotation and mitigates error propagation by integrating a weak–strong augmentation co-training mechanism to enrich data diversity, contrastive-guided loss integration to steer model updates toward reliable representations, and validation-guided parameter optimization to stabilize training against residual label noise. We evaluate MedCoRef on multiple public medical image datasets and demonstrate that with either 1% or 10% of the dataset labeled it achieves a roughly 4% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods. These results highlight MedCoRef’s effectiveness and robustness in resource constrained medical imaging scenarios.

title: Evaluating Pretrained Speech Embedding Systems for Dysarthria Detection Across Heterogenous Datasets
authors: Lovisa Wihlborg, Jemima Goodall, David Wheatley, Jacob J. Webber, Johnny Tam, Christine Weaver, Suvankar Pal, Siddharthan Chandran, Sohan Seth, Oliver Watts, Cassia Valentini-Botinhao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present a comprehensive evaluation of pretrained speech embedding systems for the detection of dysarthric speech using existing accessible data. Dysarthric speech datasets are often small and can suffer from recording biases as well as data imbalance. To address these we selected a range of datasets covering related conditions and adopt the use of several cross-validations runs to estimate the chance level. To certify that results are above chance, we compare the distribution of scores across these runs against the distribution of scores of a carefully crafted null hypothesis. In this manner, we evaluate 17 publicly available speech embedding systems across 6 different datasets, reporting the cross-validation performance on each. We also report cross-dataset results derived when training with one particular dataset and testing with another. We observed that within-dataset results vary considerably depending on the dataset, regardless of the embedding used, raising questions about which datasets should be used for benchmarking. We found that cross-dataset accuracy is, as expected, lower than within-dataset, highlighting challenges in the generalization of the systems. These findings have important implications for the clinical validity of systems trained and tested on the same dataset.

title: Feature Identification for Hierarchical Contrastive Learning
authors: Julius Ott, Nastassia Vysotskaya, Huawei Sun, Lorenzo Servadei, Robert Wille
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Hierarchical classification is a crucial task in many applications, where objects are organized into multiple levels of categories. However, conventional classification approaches often neglect the relationships between classes at different hierarchy levels, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose a novel hierarchical contrastive learning method that leverages a Gaussian Mixture Model and an attention mechanism to capture hierarchy-specific features. Our approach explicitly models the interclass relationships and imbalanced class distribution at higher hierarchy levels, enabling fine-grained clustering across all hierarchy levels. On the competitive CIFAR 100 and ModelNet40 datasets, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in linear evaluation, outperforming existing hierarchical contrastive learning methods by 2%. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in learning hierarchical representations and highlight its potential for applications in computer vision and beyond.

title: Object-Aware Restoration Diffusion: Progressive and Interactive Framework for Blind Compressed Image Restoration
authors: Lijie Yang, Juan Song, Mingtao Feng, Hang Zhong
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: JPEG compression is widely adopted for image storage and transmission, yet aggressive compression introduces artifacts such as blocking and texture loss, degrading visual quality and hindering downstream tasks. Blind Compressed Image Restoration (CIR) aims to mitigate compression artifacts caused by unknown quality factors. Existing CIR methods typically treat the image holistically, ignoring object-specific texture and compression sensitivity. To address this, we introduce Object-aware Restoration Diffusion (ORD), a progressive and interactive framework for blind compressed image restoration that incorporates semantic object control into a multi-stage diffusion pipeline. ORD first employs an initial artifact removal module (e.g., FBCNN) to remove compression artifacts without hallucination. It then extracts object semantics using vision-language models and progressively refines object-wise details via Object Restoration with Attention Guidance (ORAG), an IRControlNet-based module that integrates semantic masks and textual prompts. Additionally, an interactive click-to-restore mode enables user-driven, selective restoration for efficient enhancement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that proposed ORD achieves state-of-the-art performance in both perceptual quality and semantic fidelity under aggressive compression. The code is available at https://github.com/yang-xidian/ORD.

title: G2LST: Global to Local Stackelberg Decision Model for Computation Offloading Under Mobile Internet of Things
authors: Jiaxuan Yu, Yifu Li, Yijie Wang, Zhigang Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The exponential growth of mobile Internet of Things (IoT) and the surging demand for low-latency streaming applications pose significant challenges for resource-limited edge devices. Edge task offloading has been proposed as a promising paradigm to distribute computationally intensive tasks to nearby edge servers in urban Internet of Things scenarios. Previous studies on edge task offloading have primarily focused on multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). However, the limited computational power of edge devices constrains their ability to capture global dependencies in complex multi-agent environments. The Global to Local Stackelberg decision model (G2LST) is designed to enable edge terminals to adaptively extract key global dependencies while leveraging local observations. In the MARL driven edge task offloading environment, G2LST operates through a hierarchical infrastructure to facilitate superior task offloading strategies. Extensive experiments on real world datasets demonstrate that G2LST achieves the highest task completion ratio while maximizing computational resource utilization in complex IoT environments.
