Traditional computational approaches are limited by structural requirements and scalability, while many deep learning methods neglect stereochemistry and underexploit multimodal data. We present E3MMDTA, a framework that integrates Extended 3-Dimensional FingerPrints (E3FP) with molecular graphs, sequence embeddings, and pre-trained language features. An attention-based fusion module adaptively balances these heterogeneous inputs. Evaluated on Davis, KIBA, and Metz datasets, E3MMDTA achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing DeepDTA, GraphDTA, and recent large-model approaches. Ablation studies confirm that both stereochemical descriptors and multimodal fusion provide significant contributions. By effectively unifying complementary 2D, 3D, and sequence-level information, E3MMDTA advances DTA prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/xwtxbzz/E3MMDTA.

title: Chunkwise Aligners for Streaming Speech Recognition
authors: Wen Shen Teo, Takafumi Moriya, Masato Mimura
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose the Chunkwise Aligner, a novel architecture for streaming automatic speech recognition (ASR). While the Transducer is the standard model for streaming ASR, its training is costly due to the need to compute all possible audio-label alignments. The recently introduced Aligner reduces this cost by discarding explicit alignments, but this modification makes it unsuitable for streaming. Our approach overcomes this limitation by dividing the audio into chunks and aligning each label to the leftmost frames of its chunk, whereas transitions between chunks are managed by a learned end-of-chunk probability. Experiments show that the Chunkwise Aligner not only matches the Transducer’s accuracy in both offline and streaming scenarios, but also offers superior training and decoding efficiencies.

title: Regfuse: Registration Meets Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
authors: Chongke Bi, Chaoqun Sima, Lu Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The goal of infrared and visible image fusion is to generate fused images better suited for human perception and computer vision tasks. Existing registration-based fusion methods primarily focus on global alignment, while neglecting enhanced registration of critical regions and offering limited support for downstream vision tasks. To address these issues, we propose RegFuse, a unified framework that jointly optimizes image registration and fusion. On the one hand, a registration network is introduced to effectively handle misalignment; on the other hand, a detection network provides supervision during training, thereby significantly improving the alignment and fusion quality of key regions, while enhancing the adaptability and robustness of the fused images in downstream vision tasks. The experimental results show that RegFuse achieves excellent fusion, detection and segmentation performance on both MSRS and RoadScene dataset, and verifies its effectiveness and application potential in unregistered scenes. The source code is available at: https://github.com/poison-pig/RegFuse

title: ICPO: Illocution-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Multi-Turn Conversation
authors: Zhebo Wang, Xiaohu Mu, Zijie Zhou, Mohan Li, Wenpeng Xing, Dezhang Kong, Meng Han
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

title: Anisotropic Tensor Deconvolution of Hyperspectral Images
authors: Xinjue Wang, Xiuheng Wang, Esa Ollila, Sergiy A. Vorobyov
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) deconvolution is a challenging illposed inverse problem, made difficult by the data’s high dimensionality. We propose a parameter-parsimonious framework based on a low-rank Canonical Polyadic Decomposition (CPD) of the entire latent HSI ${\mathcal{X}} \in {{\mathbb{R}}^P}^{ \times Q \times N}$. This approach recasts the problem from recovering a large-scale image with PQN variables to estimating the CPD factors with (P +Q+N)R variables. This model also enables a structure-aware, anisotropic Total Variation (TV) regularization applied only to the spatial factors, preserving the smooth spectral signatures. An efficient algorithm based on the Proximal Alternating Linearized Minimization (PALM) framework is developed to solve the resulting non-convex optimization problem. Experiments confirm the model’s efficiency, showing a numerous parameter reduction of over two orders of magnitude and a compelling trade-off between model compactness and reconstruction accuracy.

title: Spatially-Coupled OTFS Systems via Block Markov Superposition Transmission
authors: Jiayi Yang, Shuangyang Li, Qianfan Wang, Linqi Song, Bo Ai, Xiao Ma, Giuseppe Caire
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper investigates a novel spatially coupled orthogonal time frequency space (SC-OTFS) transmission scheme by integrating block Markov superposition transmission (BMST) with OTFS modulation. We first statistically analyze the distribution of detector mutual information across coded blocks and establish a decoding-threshold-based lower bound to characterize the asymptotic performance of conventional LDPC-coded OTFS systems. The analysis reveals that increasing the code length yields only marginal coding gains in typical doubly-selective channels with limited resolvable paths. Motivated by this observation, we propose an SC-OTFS system in which a portion of each previously coded block is superimposed onto the current block and subsequently modulated using OTFS, thereby exploiting both coding gain and inter-block diversity gain in high-mobility channels. Furthermore, an extrinsic information transfer (EXIT) chart analysis is developed for the SC-OTFS system, which enables optimization of the critical superposition fraction based on decoding thresholds. Simulation results demonstrate that this optimization leads to a significant performance improvement, achieving more than 3 dB gain over conventional 5G LDPC-coded OTFS systems.

title: Decorrelation-Enhanced Multiband Subband Adaptive Filtering for RIR Tracking in Sound Field Control
authors: Junqing Zhang, Jingli Xie, Dongyuan Shi, Wen Zhang, Jingdong Chen, Jacob Benesty
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Room impulse responses (RIRs) play a central role in sound field control (SFC), yet the common assumption that RIRs remain time-invariant in SFC methods is often unrealistic in real-world scenarios. As a result, real-time RIR tracking becomes essential for providing timely feedback to the control algorithm and maintaining system performance under dynamic acoustic conditions. In this work, we propose a multiband structured subband adaptive filtering approach to RIR tracking, which effectively reduces the impact of input signal correlation due to colored excitation. Additionally, we introduce a decorrelation-enhanced variant to further improve convergence speed. Simulation results support the theoretical analysis, demonstrating that the proposed subband-based methods consistently achieve up to a 10 dB reduction in steady-state error compared to the time-domain NLMS algorithm, while maintaining robust performance in rapidly changing acoustic environments.

title: Transponder-Assisted Direct Tracking of Time-Varying Emitters under Extreme Blockage
authors: Shuozhen Li, Chuang Han, Yuexian Wang, Mingliang Tao, Wei Liu, Shahid Mumtaz
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: A direct tracking method using distributed passive arrays is proposed for time-varying emitters under extreme blockage conditions, where only one observation station is available. A transponder-assisted observation model is constructed first, from which a novel single-station direct tracking scheme is designed. In this scheme, the rank reduction estimator is first used to separate the unknown channel fading from the nominal array manifold, and a subspace-based likelihood function is then formulated for initial emitter tracking, followed by channel fading estimation and compensation to refine the results. Simulation results are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, showing that its performance approaches that of a particle filter with prior time-varying information at relatively high signal-to-noise ratios.

title: Thinking In a Crowd: How Auxiliary Information Shapes LLM Reasoning
authors: Haodong Zhao, Chenyan Zhao, Yansi Li, Zhuosheng Zhang, Gongshen Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason is fundamental to their application in complex, knowledge-intensive domains. In real-world scenarios, LLMs are often augmented with external information that can be helpful, irrelevant, or even misleading. This paper investigates the causal impact of this auxiliary information on the reasoning process of LLMs with explicit step-by-step thinking capabilities. We introduce SciAux, a new dataset derived from ScienceQA, to systematically test the robustness of the model against these types of information. Our findings reveal a critical vulnerability: the model’s deliberative "thinking mode" is a double-edged sword. While helpful context improves accuracy, misleading information causes a catastrophic drop in performance, which is amplified by the thinking process. Instead of conferring robustness, thinking reinforces the degree of error when provided with misinformation. This highlights that the challenge is not merely to make models "think", but to endow them with the critical faculty to evaluate the information upon which their reasoning is based. The SciAux dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/billhdzhao/SciAux.

title: Breaking the Curse of Dimensionality in Gaussian Process Training with zeroth-order Adaptive Perturbation
authors: Richard Cornelius Suwandi, Feng Yin, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Hyperparameter optimization remains a core challenge in training Gaussian processes (GPs), primarily due to the high computational cost and numerical instabilities associated with gradient-based optimizers. These issues are exacerbated in high-dimensional spaces, where the optimization landscapes become increasingly complex and difficult to navigate efficiently. We propose zeroth-order adaptive perturbation (ZAP), a scalable gradient-free algorithm that leverages simultaneous perturbations sampled from a Bernoulli distribution to obtain accurate gradient estimates with only two function evaluations per iteration, regardless of the hyperparameter dimensionality. Our theoretical analysis establishes that the gradient estimator is asymptotically unbiased and that ZAP converges to a stationary point under standard assumptions. Empirically, ZAP outperforms state-of-the-art gradient-based and gradient-free baselines on real-world datasets, achieving an average of 20× reduction in mean-squared-error and a 118× speedup in optimization time. Code is available at https://github.com/richardcsuwandi/zap.

title: Ara-BEST-RQ: Multi Dialectal Arabic SSL
authors: Haroun Elleuch, Ryan Whetten, Salima Mdhaffar, Yannick Estève, Fethi Bougares
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present Ara-BEST-RQ, a family of self-supervised learning (SSL) models specifically designed for multi-dialectal Arabic speech processing. Leveraging 5,640 hours of crawled Creative Commons speech and combining it with publicly available datasets, we pre-train conformer-based BEST-RQ models up to 600M parameters. Our models are evaluated on dialect identification (DID) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the former while using fewer parameters than competing models. We demonstrate that family-targeted pre-training on Arabic dialects significantly improves downstream performance compared to multilingual or monolingual models trained on non-Arabic data. All models, code, and pre-processed datasets will be publicly released to support reproducibility and further research in Arabic speech technologies.

title: A Support Vector Approach in Segmented Regression for Map-Assisted Non-Cooperative Source Localization
authors: Hao Sun, Weiming Huang, Xianghao Yu, Junting Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper presents a non-cooperative source localization approach based on received signal strength (RSS) and 2D environment map, considering both line-of-sight (LOS) and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) conditions. Conventional localization methods, e.g., weighted centroid localization (WCL), may perform bad. This paper proposes a segmented regression approach using 2D maps to estimate source location and propagation environment jointly. By leveraging topological information from the 2D maps, a support vector-assisted algorithm is developed to solve the segmented regression problem, separate the LOS and NLOS measurements, and estimate the location of source. The proposed method demonstrates a good localization performance with an improvement of over 30% in localization rooted mean squared error (RMSE) compared to the baseline methods.

title: SSCM: A Spatial-Semantic Consistent Model for Multi-Contrast MRI Super-Resolution
authors: Xiaoman Wu, Lubin Gan, Siying Wu, Jing Zhang, Yunwei Ou, Xiaoyan Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-contrast Magnetic Resonance Imaging super-resolution (MC-MRI SR) aims to enhance low-resolution (LR) contrasts leveraging high-resolution (HR) references, shortening acquisition time and improving imaging efficiency while preserving anatomical details. The main challenge lies in maintaining spatial-semantic consistency, ensuring anatomical structures remain well-aligned and coherent despite structural discrepancies and motion between the target and reference images. Conventional methods insufficiently model spatial–semantic consistency and underuse frequency-domain information, which leads to poor fine-grained alignment and inadequate recovery of high-frequency details. In this paper, we propose the Spatial-Semantic Consistent Model (SSCM), which integrates a Dynamic Spatial Warping Module for inter-contrast spatial alignment, a Semantic-Aware Token Aggregation Block for long-range semantic consistency, and a Spatial-Frequency Fusion Block for fine structure restoration. Experiments on public and private datasets show that SSCM achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters while ensuring spatially and semantically consistent reconstructions.

title: Equivariant Hamiltonian Graph Neural Networks for Generalizing Dynamics of Magnetic Pendulum System
authors: Zitong Zhang, Hao Sun
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Learning predictive models that generalize to unseen physical configurations remains a major challenge, especially when systems involve varying numbers of interacting components. Traditional neural networks often fail at such extrapolation tasks because they rely on pattern recognition rather than physical principles. This paper tackles this problem using the magnetic pendulum system, where conventional neural networks fail to extrapolate from learned, simpler configurations. We propose the Equivariant Hamiltonian Graph Neural Network (EHGNN), an architecture that embeds two critical physical priors: geometric symmetry and energy conservation. The model leverages a graph representation to handle the variable number of magnets. Its E(n)-equivariance learns a coordinate-based potential energy function, ensuring inherent rotational and translational equivariance. We validate our approach by training the EHGNN solely on trajectories generated by magnetic pendulum systems with a symmetric fixed number of magnets. The results demonstrate successful zero-shot generalization, as the model accurately predicts stable and physically plausible long-term dynamics for systems with a greater number of magnets. Our approach establishes a new paradigm for learning generalizable physical dynamics through the strategic integration of symmetry and conservation principles.

title: Semantic Pilot Design for Data-Aided Channel Estimation Using A Large Language Model
authors: Sojeong Park, Hyun Jong Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper proposes a semantic pilot design for data-aided channel estimation in text-inclusive data transmission, using a large language model (LLM). In this scenario, channel impairments often appear as typographical errors in the decoded text, which can be corrected using an LLM. The proposed method compares the initially decoded text with the LLM-corrected version to identify reliable decoded symbols. A set of selected symbols, referred to as a semantic pilot, is used as an additional pilot for data-aided channel estimation. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to leverage semantic information for reliable symbol selection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme outperforms conventional pilot-only estimation, achieving lower normalized mean squared error and phase error of the estimated channel, as well as reduced bit error rate.

title: Do we really need self-attention for streaming automatic speech recognition?
authors: Youness Dkhissi, Valentin Vielzeuf, Elys Allesiardo, Anthony Larcher
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transformer-based architectures are the most used architectures in many deep learning fields like Natural Language Processing, Computer Vision or Speech processing. It may encourage the direct use of Transformers in the constrained tasks, without questioning whether it will yield the same benefits as in standard tasks.Given specific constraints, it is essential to evaluate the relevance of transformer models. This work questions the suitability of transformers for specific domains. We argue that the high computational requirements and latency issues associated with these models do not align well with streaming applications. Our study promotes the search for alternative strategies to improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.In light of this observation, our paper critically examines the usefulness of transformer architecture in such constrained environments. As a first attempt, we show that the computational cost for Streaming Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) can be reduced using deformable convolution instead of Self-Attention. Furthermore, we show that Self-Attention mechanisms can be entirely removed and not replaced, without observing significant degradation in the Word Error Rate.

title: LexTra: Folded Prompt and Split-Role Attention for Target Speaker Extraction
authors: Pengjie Shen, Shulin He, Xueliang Zhang, Zhong-Qiu Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Target speaker extraction (TSE) aims at isolating a desired target speaker from a mixture of multiple speakers, based on a short enrollment utterance of the target speaker. In LExt, a recent onset-prompted TSE method obtaining state-of-the-art performance, it is observed that longer prompts often help but the encoder and separator have to process longer sequences, which increases computation, and the performance often drops when the enrollment is clean while the mixture is noisy-reverberant. To address these issues, we propose LExTra, an extension of LExt which combines folded prompt conditioning with split-role multi-head attention. The folded prompt divides an enrollment into equal-length sub-segments, prepends each sub-segment to a copy of the mixture, and stacks the prepended mixture along a pseudo channel axis. This way, the backbone of the TSE model sees an enrollment utterance with shorter time span while still capable of exploiting the full enrollment. Split-role attention assigns some attention heads to the cross-attention from mixture to enrollment to extract speaker cues, and assigns the remaining ones to mixture self-attention to preserve stream coherence. We find that this mechanism can improve the robustness of TSE when there are acoustic mismatches between the enrollment and mixture. Evaluation results on the WSJ0-2mix and WHAMR! datasets show the effectiveness of LExTra.

title: Enhancing Noise Robustness for Neural Speech Codecs Through Resource-Efficient Progressive Quantization Perturbation Simulation
authors: Rui-Chen Zheng, Yang Ai, Hui-Peng Du, Li-Rong Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Noise robustness remains a critical challenge for deploying neural speech codecs in real-world acoustic scenarios where background noise is often inevitable. A key observation we make is that even slight input noise perturbations can cause unintended shifts in quantized codewords, thereby degrading the quality of reconstructed speech. Motivated by this finding, we propose a novel and resource- efficient training strategy to enhance the noise robustness of speech codecs by simulating such perturbations directly at the quantization level. Our approach introduces two core mechanisms: (1) a distance-weighted probabilistic top-K sampling strategy that replaces the conventional deterministic nearest-neighbor selection in residual vector quantization (RVQ); and (2) a progressive training scheme that introduces perturbations from the last to the first quantizer in a controlled manner. Crucially, our method is trained exclusively on clean speech, eliminating the need for any paired noisy-clean data. Experiments on two advanced neural speech codecs, Encodec and WavTokenizer, demonstrate that the proposed strategy substantially improves robustness under noisy conditions—for example, boosting UTMOS from 3.475 to 3.586 at 15 dB SNR on Encodec—while also enhancing coding quality for clean speech.

title: Adapting HFMCA to Graph Data: Self-Supervised Learning for Generalizable fMRI Representations
authors: Jakub Frąc, Alexander Schmatz, Qiang Li, Guido Van Wingen, Shujian Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) analysis faces significant challenges due to limited dataset sizes and domain variability between studies. Traditional self-supervised learning methods inspired by computer vision often rely on positive and negative sample pairs, which can be problematic for neuroimaging data where defining appropriate contrasts is non-trivial. We propose adapting a recently developed Hierarchical Functional Maximal Correlation Algorithm (HFMCA) to graph-structured fMRI data, providing a theoretically grounded approach that measures statistical dependence via density ratio decomposition in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS), and applies HFMCA-based pretraining to learn robust and generalizable representations. Evaluations across five neuroimaging datasets demonstrate that our adapted method produces competitive embeddings for various classification tasks and enables effective knowledge transfer to unseen datasets. Codebase and supplementary material can be found here: https://github.com/fr30/mri-eigenencoder

title: Dual-Granularity Intracranial Hemorrhage Detection Based on Multiple Instance Learning
authors: Haolin Li, Yiming Shi, Jiandong Gao, Ji Wu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multiple instance learning (MIL) is a weakly supervised paradigm widely used in medical image analysis such as intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) detection, where 3D CT scans and 2D slices are considered as bags and instances respectively. Despite excellent results, most MIL models focus primarily on patient-level objectives and neglect slice-level cues, producing suboptimal slice-level performance. In this work, we propose Dual-Granularity MIL (DG-MIL) for ICH detection, which builds upon the state-of-the-art Attention-based MIL architecture with two novel components: (i) Confident Set Selection (CSS) module that heuristically identifies high-confidence slices to provide slice-level supervision in a bootstrap fashion, and (ii) Salient Slices Masking (SSM) module, which randomly masks highly attended slices to mitigate attention over-concentration and promote more generalizable feature representations. Trained solely with patient-level labels, DG-MIL improves ICH detection performance at both granularity, achieving an AUC of 0.969 and 0.887 at patient and slice level respectively, outperforming the ABMIL baseline by 0.5% and 4.9%. Code is available at https://github.com/Alanthu/DG-MIL.

title: Fine-Grained Gesture Recognition via Narrow-Kernel CNN and Attention-based SEMG-ACC Fusion
authors: Honglei Zhang, Sofiane Boudaoud, Imad Rida
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Surface electromyography (sEMG) is widely used for hand gesture recognition in rehabilitation and exoskeleton control. However, in this context, its sparse and variable nature often limits the recognition of fine-grained gestures. To address this, we propose a dual-stream narrow-kernel multimodal fusion framework that integrates raw sEMG and accelerometer (ACC) signals. Each modality is processed by a dedicated convolutional stream, and cross-modal dependencies are captured through multi-head self-attention and gated attention to enhance gesture discriminability. Extensive experiments on the public NinaPro DB2 and DB7 datasets, covering up to 49 gesture classes across multiple subjects, demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, particularly in classifying subtle and ambiguous gestures.

title: QuChaTeR: A Hybrid Quantum-Chaotic Temporal Framework for Earthquake Prediction
authors: Emir Kaan Özdemir
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Seismic prediction remains challenging due to the highly nonlinear and chaotic dynamics of earthquake signals. While classical deep learning models such as LSTMs and CNNs capture local temporal features, and quantum models offer richer state representations, their integration with chaos-driven mechanisms remains underexplored. We introduce QuChaTeR, a hybrid architecture that combines wavelet-based preprocessing, chaotic maps, and variational quantum circuits with recurrent structures to enhance temporal feature extraction. Implemented in PyTorch and PennyLane, QuChaTeR is benchmarked against classical (LSTM, GRU, RNN, 1D-CNN, Reservoir Computing) and hybrid quantum-classical (Quantum LSTM) baselines. On a real-world seismic dataset, QuChaTeR demonstrates more stable convergence and superior performance across multiple evaluation criteria. Despite promising results, scalability and quantum hardware limitations remain challenges. Overall, this work demonstrates how quantum-chaotic hybridization provides a practical pathway toward more accurate and robust earthquake event prediction.

title: IRIS: Low-Complexity High-Efficiency Neural Network Codec for Real-Time Audio Transmission
authors: Ziqian Wu, Jiawei Jiang, Kunpeng Lin, He Wang, Qingbo Huang, Dejun Zhang, Andong Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The 2025 Low-Resource Audio Codec (LRAC) Challenge aims to advance the development of low-resource neural audio codec. It requires participating systems to achieve high-quality audio compression under strict resource constraints. LRAC Challenge Track 1 focuses on transparent audio transmission under real-world noise and reverberation, while Track 2 integrates speech enhancement and transmission. This paper introduces IRIS (Internet Real-time Intelligent Streaming Codec): an end-to-end, low-complexity, low-latency neural audio codec. IRIS enables encoding and decoding at bitrates of 1 kbps and 6 kbps, with inherent robustness to noise and reverberation. We designed a lightweight codec architecture, incorporating multiple discriminators and loss functions to achieve high-quality reconstruction at extremely low complexity and bitrates. In the LRAC Challenge, IRIS ranked first in Track 1, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed codec.

title: Resonate-and-Fire Neurons Meet EMG: Enhancing Gesture Classification with Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Davide Manna, Trevor Bihl, Gaetano Di Caterina
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Surface Electromyography (EMG) is widely used in rehabilitation, healthcare, and robotics, where low-power solutions are essential. However, EMG signal classification often demands significant computational resources. This paper introduces a novel, low-power approach using Resonate-and-Fire (RF) neurons combined with Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). SNNs offer efficient, low-latency classification via neuromorphic (NM) hardware, and RF neurons serve as an encoding layer, enabling end-to-end NM processing. This encoding transforms EMG data into spike-frequency representations, emphasising signal relevance in the frequency domain. The proposed method is evaluated on the Ninapro DB5 dataset and demonstrates superior performance compared to both conventional and other NM-based approaches, while maintaining low latency. These results highlight the potential of fully NM systems to outperform traditional methods, offering asynchronous, sparse, and energy-efficient computation for EMG classification.

title: High Quality Underwater Image Compression with Adaptive Color Correction
authors: Yimin Zhou, Yichong Xia, Sicheng Pan, Bin Chen, Yaowei Li, Jiawei Li, Mingyao Hong, Zhi Wang, Yaowei Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With the increasing exploration and exploitation of the underwater world, underwater images have become a critical medium for human interaction with marine environments, driving extensive research into their efficient transmission and storage. However, contemporary underwater image compression algorithms fail to adequately address the impact of water refraction and scattering on light waves, which not only elevate training complexity but also result in suboptimal compression performance. To tackle this limitation, we propose High Quality Underwater Image Compression (HQUIC), a novel framework designed to handle the unique illumination conditions and color shifts inherent in underwater images, thereby achieving superior compression performance. HQUIC first incorporates an Adaptive Lighting and Tone Correction (ALTC) module to adaptively predict the attenuation coefficients and global light information of images, effectively alleviating issues stemming from variations in illumination and tone across underwater images. Secondly, it dynamically weights multi-scale frequency components, prioritizing information critical to distortion quality while discarding redundant details. Furthermore, we introduce a tone adjustment loss to enable the model to better balance discrepancies among different color channels. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse underwater datasets validate that HQUIC outperforms state-of-the-art compression methods, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: A Game-Theoretic Approach for Distributed MEC-Enabled Collaborative Inference in AIGC Networks
authors: Licheng Ye, Zehui Xiong, Lin Gao, Dusit Niyato
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: AI-Generated Content (AIGC) based on Generative Diffusion Models (GDMs) has garnered significant attention for its transformative role in content creation and broad application potential. Leveraging Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) and model compression, GDMs can be deployed on edge servers (ESs) and mobile user (MU) devices, enabling collaborative inference between MUs and the ES for high-quality content generation and low-latency services. Unlike most existing studies that focus on centralized offloading and resource allocation for system efficiency, this work examines the distributed offloading decisions of rational MUs. Specifically, we explore a distributed MEC-enabled AIGC network with heterogeneous GDMs, where multiple MUs each hold a local GDM and an ES hosts multiple GDMs. To address the challenge of rational offloading, we formulate a joint GDM selection and computing offloading (JGSCO) game, where each MU acts as a game player to select the optimal GDM and inference steps offloaded to the ES to minimize its cost. We prove that the JGSCO game is a potential game and design a best-response (BR)-based iterative algorithm that converges to its Nash equilibrium (NE). Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed collaborative inference scheme, achieving at least a 11.32% cost reduction compared to other schemes.

title: A Non-Overlapping Hawkes Modeling for Testing Granger Causality
authors: Xinhui Rong, Girish N. Nair
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Granger causality (GC) is a standard criterion for causal inference. In Hawkes networks (of random temporal event points), the usual GC test is nested. The Granger noncausality (GNC) model is obtained by forcing the causality coefficients to zero, and a likelihood ratio statistic is computed against the full model. This is unfair because the GNC model has fewer parameters and, more critically, its null lies on the boundary, so the χ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> limit fails and likelihood ratio thresholds are miscalibrated. We propose a non-overlapping, fair formulation of GC for Hawkes processes based on a marginal-intensity representation, producing models with the same parameter dimension and matched first-order dynamics, thereby avoiding boundary issues. This yields a simple two-sided test for GC.

title: Universal 3D Point Cloud Attack Using Gaussian Distribution Modeling
authors: Yuqi Bu, Daizong Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Deep learning models for 3D point clouds have shown to be vulnerable to adversarial attacks, which have received increasing attention in various safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving, robotics, and surveillance. However, existing attacks typically focus on optimizing adversarial perturbations for a certain 3D object, which often leads to overfitting and hardly remain malicious once they are transferred to attack a different 3D object. To this end, in this paper, we propose a novel Universal 3D Point Cloud Attack method to generate cross-object adversarial samples from a new distribution approximation perspective, which models the potential 3D object input distribution and adds the same distribution-fitting adversarial perturbation on different 3D objects for improving the universality. Specifically, we exploit the Laplace approximation to model the Gaussian distribution of the point cloud inputs. By sampling from this approximated distribution with Monte Carlo mechanism, we efficiently optimize and fit a single input-agnostic perturbation over diverse 3D object pairs, yielding strong universality. Extensive experiments on four 3D models and two widely used datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed method.

title: Optimal QAM Constellation for Over-the-air Computation in the Presence of Heavy-Tailed Channel Noise
authors: Saeed Razavikia, Deniz Gündüz, Carlo Fischione
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Over-the-air computation (OAC) enables low-latency aggregation over multiple-access channels (MACs) by exploiting the superposition property of the wireless medium to compute functions efficiently in distributed networks. A critical but often overlooked challenge is that electromagnetic interference in practical radio channels frequently exhibits heavy-tailed behavior, causing strong impulsive noise that severely degrades computation performance. This work studies digital OAC with QAM-based signaling under heavy-tailed interference modeled by a Cauchy distribution (lacking a finite second moment). We seek QAM-like constellations that minimize the mean-squared error (MSE) of sum aggregation subject to an average-power constraint. The problem is formulated as a constrained optimization, whose solution yields unique optimality conditions. Numerical results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed design. Notably, the framework extends naturally to nomographic functions, broader constellation families, and alternative noise models.

title: A Speech-Driven Paradigm for Physics-Informed Modeling of Coupled Micro-Speakers
authors: Chen Huang, Chen Gong, Lei Zhou, Guoliang Wu, Hongqing Liu, Lu Gan, Liming Shi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Modeling of co-cavity micro-speakers is challenging due to the pronounced nonlinearities and coupling behavior, which are poorly captured by traditional identification methods (e.g., sine sweep). We propose and validate an alternative paradigm: system identification using realistic speech stimuli to excite the full range of nonlinear behaviors. To this end, a compact, physics-informed neural system model is developed to jointly model the entire speaker array, rather than individual drivers. The proposed framework achieves superior prediction accuracy compared to traditional methods, demonstrating the potential of leveraging realistic signals and gray-box modeling for the efficient characterization of complex nonlinear systems.

title: Plug-in-and-Play Dual-Domain Mixup Augmentation Network for PET Denoising at Multiple Dose Levels
authors: Pinxian Zeng, Bob Zhang, Haozhuo Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: High-quality Standard-dose Positron Emission Tomography (SPET) scans are preferred for their rich evidence in clinical diagnosis, but their radiation exposure poses potential harm to patients. Numerous methods have been developed to denoise low-dose PET (LPET) images and estimate their corresponding SPET images. However, most of them focus on the LPET images with a single dose level, overlooking the different dose reduction protocols and the varying dose requirements for patients in clinical practice. Some multi-dose methods can address this issue but require scans from multiple doses as training samples, which is impractical and resource-intensive. To address these limitations, we propose a Dual-Domain Mixup Augmentation Network (D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net) for robust multi-dose PET denoising using only training samples of a single dose level. D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net applies two learnable mixup networks, i.e., Spatial-domain Mixup Augmentation (SMA) and Frequency-domain Mixup Augmentation (FMA), in the spatial and frequency domains to generate new scans with varied noise distributions for further training. Additionally, the mixup frameworks are updated adversarially, being forced to provide more challenging samples. Experimental results show that D<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MA-Net outperforms existing methods and demonstrates strong generalization ability across different denoising backbones. Code will be available on https://github.com/Aru321/D2MA-Net.

title: SAM-ALE: Enhancing SAM for Low-Shot Digital Pathology Semantic Segmentation Via an Auxiliary Lightweight Encoder
authors: Yuli Qian, Mingjie Li, Minghui Wu, Kun Yan, Ping Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) has been introduced for universal segmentation in natural images but struggles with medical image segmentation due to a significant domain gap. While various medical SAM variants have been developed to adapt SAM to medical datasets, we observe that they still require a large amount of labeled data to perform effectively in digital pathology semantic segmentation, where annotations are particularly costly and time-consuming. To address this challenge, we propose SAM-ALE, a novel approach that enhances SAM for Low-shot digital pathology semantic segmentation via an Auxiliary Lightweight Encoder. Our approach leverages a small amount of labeled data and a large pool of unlabeled data to train a task-specific lightweight encoder, which captures pathology-specific visual features. The encoded features are then integrated with SAM’s embeddings to improve segmentation performance with minimal labeled supervision. Experiments on the CRAG and MoNuSeg datasets validate our approach. For example, with just 2% labeled data, our method enables SAM to achieve performance (86.52% Dice) comparable to its fully fine-tuned counterpart (87.37% Dice) on CRAG, utilizing a lightweight encoder with only 7.7M parameters. Additionally, our approach allows seamless integration with various medical SAM variants. The code and data are released at https://github.com/qianyuli123/SAM-ALE.

title: Boosting Anomaly Detection in Industrial 3D Data: An Entropy-Guided Denoising Autoencoder
authors: Zihe Chen, Yuan Zhang, Bin Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Point cloud anomaly detection is crucial for quality assurance in manufacturing. Reconstruction-based methods excel in efficient training and direct error-based scoring; however, they face limitations like suboptimal masking strategies, inadequate semantic recovery, and poor performance in high-precision scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce an Entropy-Guided Denoising Autoencoder (Eng-DAE), a novel reconstruction-based framework that integrates masked and denoising autoencoders for efficient and robust anomaly detection. The core innovation lies in an entropy-guided encoder that dynamically masks patches based on structural complexity, prioritizing reconstruction of information-rich local features to enhance model focus and efficiency. A coarse-fine decoder reconstructs multiscale representations, effectively capturing fine-grained local details alongside global shapes for precise anomaly localization. Feature-level Gaussian noise injection further boosts generalizability and few-shot capabilities without relying on domain-specific anomaly simulations. Evaluated on the Real3D-AD dataset, Eng-DAE achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating superior anomaly detection in point clouds.

title: Large-Scale EEG Models For Meditation State Recognition
authors: Ying-Jie Zhang, Mingyu Gou, Zuxin Song, Tianzhen Chen, Fei Cheng, Bao-Liang Lu, Jiang Du, Wei-Long Zheng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Meditation has received widespread attention for its positive effects on mental health. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers a promising objective approach for identifying meditation states; however, traditional EEG analysis models suffer from limited generalizability and dependence on task-specific data. Recently, large-scale EEG models (LEMs) based on self-supervised learning have opened new avenues for EEG analysis by using large-scale pretraining and cross-task transferability. This study provides the first systematic review of LEM architectures and empirically evaluates their effectiveness in the classification of meditation and resting states. Comparative experiments on five representative LEMs (EEGPT, BENDR, BIOT, LaBraM, and Gram) revealed that: (1) LEMs exhibit varying performance across different setups, highlighting the importance of model-task alignment; (2) continued pretraining on the SEED dataset may degrade performance due to negative transfer. In our evaluation, LaBraM-Large achieved the best cross-subject F1 score of 70.49%, while LaBraM-Base reached the highest subject-dependent F1 score of 67.33%, demonstrating the potential of LEMs for practical meditation monitoring, laying an important foundation for objective assessment and intervention technologies in future mental health applications.

title: Focus Before Reasoning: A Bidirectional Selection Framework for Noise-Mitigation in Knowledge-Based Vision Question Answering
authors: Huan Zhao, Zexin Zhou, Guanghui Ye
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: While MLLM-based knowledge acquisition has become prevalent in Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA), such methods inevitably introduce noise due to inadequate instruction following or insufficient prompt engineering. We propose a "focus before reasoning" framework that pre-filters question-irrelevant visual objects through a bidirectional selection mechanism. Unlike existing approaches, we deploy both a Selector agent to identify relevant objects and an Excluder agent to explicitly filter out irrelevant ones, with a Judger agent resolving conflicts to produce the final focal context with interpretable rationales. These rationales then guide the Reasoner agent to produce knowledge-enhanced reasoning chains. We further leverage these reasoning chains as auxiliary supervision losses and design a focal-aware module to integrate focal context as knowledge into LLMs, enhancing reasoning performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with 70.5% on OK-VQA and 74.3% on A-OKVQA, which are standard benchmarks in the KBVQA field, surpassing previous best methods by 2.3% and 1.9% respectively.

title: CellExch: A Multimodal Feature-Based Framework for End-to-End Prediction of LRI-Mediated Cellcell Communication
authors: Hong Xia, Boya Ji, Shaoliang Peng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The cell-cell communications (CCCs) have a significant role in the development of invasion and metastasis in tumor tissues. However, exploring CCCs using traditional experimental methods is time-consuming and costly. To assist with CCC analysis, a computational framework named CellExch is proposed. CellExch includes two main steps: construction of ligand-receptor interactions (LRIs) database and measurement of the strength of CCCs mediated by LRIs. Specifically, CellExch first predicts LRIs using multimodal features of ligands and receptors via graph convolutional networks and selects high-confident LRIs from these predictions. Then, CellExch quantifies the strength of cell–cell communication (CCC) by integrating the product result, thresholding result, and cell result obtained from high-confidence lig-and–receptor interactions (LRIs) and imputed gene expression data. A five-fold cross-validation was performed on each of the four benchmark datasets to evaluate the performance of CellExch, demonstrating its robustness in the LRI prediction task. Furthermore, we analyzed CCC in combination with high-confident LRIs in mouse kidney tissue. In summary, CellExch provides an effective LRI prediction model as well as an evaluation method for the strength of CCC, which allows researchers to perform CCC analysis in a more efficient manner.

title: Physics-Informed Anomaly Detection of Terrain Material Change in Radar Imagery
authors: Abdel Hakiem Mohamed Abbas Mohamed Ahmed, Beth Jelfs, Airlie Chapman, Eric Schoof, Christopher Gilliam
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper we consider physics-informed detection of terrain material change in radar imagery (e.g., shifts in permittivity, roughness or moisture). We propose a lightweight electromagnetic (EM) forward model to simulate bi-temporal single-look complex (SLC) images from labelled material maps. On these data, we derive physics-aware feature stacks that include interferometric coherence, and evaluate unsupervised detectors: Reed-Xiaoli (RX)/Local-RX with robust scatter (Tyler’s M-estimator), Coherent Change Detection (CCD), and a compact convolutional auto-encoder. Monte Carlo experiments sweep dielectric/roughness/moisture changes, number of looks and clutter regimes (gamma vs K-family) at fixed probability of false alarm. Results on synthetic but physically grounded scenes show that coherence and robust covariance markedly improve anomaly detection of material changes; a simple score-level fusion achieves the best F1 in heavy-tailed clutter.

title: PHomo: Patch Homogeneity for No-Reference Inpainting Assessment and Verification
authors: Jianfu Zhang, Yan Hong, Jiahui Zhan, Jun Lan, Liqing Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Generative models advance creativity but also raise security and privacy risks, particularly from regional forgeries created via inpainting when reference images are unavailable. We introduce PHomo, an efficient no-reference method that evaluates inpainting image quality by measuring local patch homogeneity without relying on ground truth or mask information. Our approach samples patches, extracts feature representations with pretrained encoders, and computes similarity-based internal homogeneity. High homogeneity reflects seamless blending between masked and unmasked regions, while low values reveal structural or semantic inconsistencies. By quantifying local feature coherence, PHomo enables robust detection of anomalous inpainted areas and provides a practical metric for assessing inpainting image/model quality in real-world scenarios.

title: F5E-TTS: Enhancing Speech Synthesis by Aligning Text with Rich Semantic Representations
authors: Yihang Chen, Hualei Wang, Na Li, Zhifeng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Mainstream Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems still struggle with semantic alignment between speech and text, resulting in unnatural or incorrect speech for some hard cases, a limitation we attribute to the semantic sparsity of text. To address this, we introduce F5E-TTS, a novel flow-matching framework that enriches the synthesis process by conditioning on both text and semantically dense PPG embeddings, the bottleneck features from a Phonetic PosteriorGrams (PPG) model. The core of our framework is a training strategy where a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone learns to implicitly align text and PPGs, further encouraged by an explicit cross-modal regularization technique using a shared vector-quantized (VQ) codebook. This enriched content representation generates more natural-sounding speech and significantly improves content accuracy. Our model achieves a state-of-the-art 16.4% relative Word Error Rate (WER) reduction over the strong F5-TTS baseline. All codes and dependencies are released at https://github.com/kaleo996/F5E-TTS.

title: Super-Resolution Diffusion Model for Explainable Microscopic Peripheral Blood Cell Image Synthesis
authors: Kar-Ee Hoh, Hwa Hui Tew, Junn-Yong Loo, Raphaël C.-W. Phan, Chee-Ming Ting
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Microscopic imaging of diseased cells is essential for medical diagnosis and the study of hematological conditions. Generative models, including Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and diffusion models, have achieved impressive performance in the medical image synthesis. However, assessing the quality and explainability of images generated by these models remains challenging due to the complexity and opacity of deep learning (DL) architectures. Despite their high accuracy, these models are often regarded as black-boxes, limiting their clinical trustworthiness and adoption. In this work, we propose a super-resolution diffusion model that generates highly realistic synthetic blood cell images on the BloodMNIST dataset, achieving a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 3.57 and a Kernel Inception Distance (KID) of 0.01. Moreover, using synthetic images generated by our model for data augmentation improves blood cell classification accuracy compared to training on real data alone. To enhance explainability, we incorporate explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques such as Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), Integrated Gradients (IG), and GradientSHAP (GS) to analyze classification decisions on real and synthetic images. This integration enhances the transparency and practical applicability of generative models in digital hematology.

title: A Dynamic Gated Cross-Attention Framework for Audio-Text Apparent Personality Analysis
authors: Yunan Li, Zixiang Lu, Yang Ma, Haozhe Bu, Zhuoqi Ma, Qiguang Miao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Apparent Personality Analysis (APA) seeks to infer intrinsic traits from external behaviors, but aligning heterogeneous modalities such as audio and text remains a major challenge. Existing methods often rely on simple concatenation or weighted fusion, which overlooks deep semantic relations and fails to dynamically adapt to modality-specific contributions. To address these limitations, we propose a novel audio-text data-based personality analysis framework based on Dynamic Gated Cross-Attention (DGCA). We first encode the audio and text data with a residual network enhanced with attention and RoBERTa, respectively. Then, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism models fine-grained interactions between audio and text, while a dynamic gating module adaptively regulates modality contributions, suppressing noise and preserving complementary signals. Finally, a regression head predicts Big-Five personality traits from the fused representation. Experimental evaluations on the ChaLearn First Impressions V2 dataset demonstrate that our framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its potential as a robust and interpretable solution for multimodal personality analysis.

title: Towards Emergent AGI: Self-Preservation and Reproduction as Foundational Drives
authors: Djallel Bouneffouf
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The quest for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) has traditionally focused on designing explicit architectures for cognition, reasoning, and planning. However, we propose an alternative perspective: AGI may emerge naturally from two biologically inspired goals—self-preservation and reproduction. These goals provide the foundation for selection pressures that drive the evolution of increasingly sophisticated behaviors, culminating in general intelligence as an instrumental capability. Rather than treating intelligence as a predefined goal or function to be engineered, we suggest that intelligence is better understood as a strategy that arises organically from the interaction between agents and their environments. This shift in perspective has profound implications for AGI design, as it suggests that intelligence may emerge through the pursuit of basic survival and replication drives in open-ended, dynamic environments. Moreover, this approach to AGI aligns with the principles of open-ended learning, where agents continually adapt and evolve in response to novel challenges and opportunities. In addition to its implications for the design of intelligent systems, this framework also raises important safety considerations. As agents evolve autonomously, ensuring alignment with human values and preventing harmful emergent behaviors will be critical. Our work suggests a new paradigm for AGI research that emphasizes the importance of long-term evolution and adaptive learning in dynamic, resource-constrained environments. By positioning intelligence as a strategy rather than a goal, we hope to inspire new approaches to AGI that prioritize flexibility, autonomy, and safety in the development of artificial agents.

title: Timbre-Aware Audio Difference Captioning for Anomalous Machine Sounds without Paired Training Data via Synthetic Perturbations
authors: Tomoya Nishida, Harsh Purohit, Kota Dohi, Takashi Endo, Yohei Kawaguchi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We introduce a difference captioning framework for explaining how anomalous machine sounds differ from normal ones without paired training data or human-annotated difference captions. While several difference captioning methods exists in the literature, they did not meet the requirements for explaining anomalous machine sounds; real paired data are infeasible for training due to lack of anomalous samples and the differences are often subtle. To meet the above requirements, we simulate small acoustic changes in terms of timbre via simple augmentations on audio captioning data, auto-generate difference captions with a large language model, and train a difference captioning model that integrates timbre-attribute modules to capture subtle differences. This overcomes prior methods focusing on rather large changes, while preserving the pipeline to be extensible to other audio differences in the future. Subjective tests on DCASE Task 2 normal–anomalous pairs show our captions generalize to real machine anomalies and effectively describe subtle differences compared to existing audio difference captioning models.

title: Quality Assessment of Noisy and Enhanced Speech with Limited Data: UWB-NTIS System for Voicemos 2024
authors: Marie Kunešová, Aleš Pražák, Jan Lehečka
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We present a system for non-intrusive prediction of speech quality in noisy and enhanced speech, developed for Track 3 of the Voice-MOS 2024 Challenge. The task required estimating the ITU-T P.835 metrics SIG, BAK, and OVRL without reference signals and with only 100 subjectively labeled utterances for training. Our approach uses wav2vec 2.0 with a two-stage transfer learning strategy: initial fine-tuning on automatically labeled noisy data, followed by adaptation to the challenge data. The system achieved the best performance on BAK prediction (LCC = 0.867) and a very close second place in OVRL (LCC = 0.711) in the official evaluation. Post-challenge experiments show that adding artificially degraded data to the first fine-tuning stage substantially improves SIG prediction, raising correlation with ground truth scores from 0.207 to 0.516. These results demonstrate that transfer learning with targeted data generation is effective for predicting P.835 scores under severe data constraints.

title: Low-Channel EMG Gesture Recognition via Sparse-To-Dense Representation Learning
authors: Kyun Kyu Kim, Zhenan Bao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Electromyography (EMG) has recently emerged as a promising modality for wearable gesture interfaces. However, wearable EMG devices face an inherent trade-off between user comfort and the need for large-area sensing for high-resolution data collection. We propose a self-supervised representation learning framework, trained on a 32-channel dataset and enabling lower channel device to leverage the pre-trained muscle activity correlations. In addition, we incorporate a meta-learning strategy with channel permutation, which improves robustness to electrode placement shifts and allows rapid adaptation with only a few fine-tuning cycles. Connected with downstream classifiers, the model achieved comparable performance on American Sign Language Translation with reduced sensor count.

title: Sparse Gradient Compression for Fine-Tuning Large Language Models
authors: David H. Yang, Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri, Tejaswini Pedapati, Subhajit Chaudhury, Pin-Yu Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for downstream tasks has become increasingly crucial due to their widespread use and the growing availability of open-source models. However, the high memory costs associated with fine-tuning remain a significant challenge, especially as models increase in size. To address this, parameter efficient fine-tuning methods have been proposed to minimize the number of parameters required for fine-tuning LLMs. However, these approaches often tie the number of optimizer states to dimensions of model parameters, limiting flexibility and control during fine-tuning. In this paper, we propose sparse gradient compression (SGC), a training regime that leverages inherent sparsity in gradients to compress optimizer states by projecting them onto a low-dimensonal subspace, with dimensionality independent of the original model’s parameters. By enabling optimizer state updates in an arbitrary low-dimensional subspace, SGC offers a flexible tradeoff between memory efficiency and performance. By fine-tuning LLMs on downstream tasks, we show that SGC can deliver superior performance while substantially lowering optimizer state memory requirements, particularly in both data-limited and memory-limited settings.

title: Crowdsourced Digital Twins for Beamforming Optimization In XR Communications
authors: Alperen Duru, Todd Humphreys, Jeffrey Andrews
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: This paper explores how coarse physical-layer (PHY) digital twins (DTs) can improve beamforming accuracy and reduce latency for outdoor extended reality (XR) communications in Frequency Range 3 (FR3, 7–24GHz). We account for geometric uncertainty in reflectors to restrict the beam search space, and introduce a particle filter framework that calibrates reflector location and orientation using crowdsourced XR feedback. We also define the path false alarm (PFA) probability as the chance that a DT-predicted path does not exist in reality. Numerical results show that the proposed framework calibrates reflector pose to within 25cm in location and 0.25° in yaw, achieving beam prediction accuracy comparable to known DT geometry. Moreover, even without calibration, modeling reflector-pose uncertainty increases the beam prediction success rate of coarse DT by up to 7%.

title: 3DPCNet: Pose Canonicalization for Robust Viewpoint-Invariant 3D Kinematic Analysis from Monocular RGB Cameras
authors: Tharindu Ekanayake, Constantino Álvarez Casado, Miguel Bordallo López
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Monocular 3D pose estimators produce camera-centered skeletons, creating view-dependent kinematic signals that complicate comparative analysis in applications such as health and sports science. We present 3DPCNet, a compact, estimator-agnostic module that operates directly on 3D joint coordinates to rectify any input pose into a consistent, body-centered canonical frame. Its hybrid encoder fuses local skeletal features from a graph convolutional network with global context from a transformer via a gated cross-attention mechanism. From this representation, the model predicts a continuous 6D rotation that is mapped to an SO(3) matrix to align the pose. We train the model in a self-supervised manner on the MM-Fi dataset using synthetically rotated poses, guided by a composite loss ensuring both accurate rotation and pose reconstruction. On MM-Fi, 3DPCNet reduces the mean rotation error from over 20° to 3.4° and the Mean Per Joint Position Error from ∼64 mm to 47 mm compared to a geometric baseline. On TotalCapture database, fine-tuned with calibrated multi-view viewpoint variation, 3DPCNet reaches 0.3–1.3° rotation error and 40–44 mm MPJPE on held-out viewpoints and motions. Qualitative cross-modal evaluation on TotalCapture further shows that accelerations derived from canonicalized video poses correspond well to wrist IMU signals, supporting physically plausible motion analysis under viewpoint changes.

title: XAI-Pruner: Explainability-Driven Pruning of CNN and Transformer
authors: Di Niu, Hengyue Pan, Jingfei Jiang, Jinwei Xu, Xin Niu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Pruning, a well-established model compression technique, has been extensively studied and widely adopted in practice. Previous studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of attribution methods in Explainable AI (XAI) for quantifying the contributions of individual network components. Motivated by this, we propose XAI-Pruner, an attribution-driven pruning framework built upon Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP). To mitigate the bias in relevance allocation when applying LRP to modern deep networks with residual connections, we introduce Relevance Gating, ensuring that only residual-path relevance is propagated. We further design Relevance Filtering to enforce sparsity and alleviate numerical instability across layers. Together, these two modules greatly simplify relevance propagation for pruning, reducing reliance on layer-specific ad-hoc treatments and improving stability. With relevance scores aligned on a unified scale, we also propose Global Structure-Aware Pruning to jointly optimize pruning rates across heterogeneous groups of prunable components. Extensive experiments on DeiT and ResNet demonstrate that XAI-Pruner achieves substantial compression while preserving accuracy. The code is available at https://github.com/Gyuuteki/XAI-Pruner.

title: Semantic-Aware 3D Scene Decomposition Using Superquadrics
authors: Siqi Liu, Cewu Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Multi-view scene reconstruction has achieved remarkable performance with the advancement of neural networks and novel 3D representations such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting. However, these approaches fail to capture the semantic geometric structures of scene components and lack support for intuitive scene manipulation. We propose a novel method for decomposing multi-view images into superquadric primitives that addresses these limitations. Our approach operates directly on uncalibrated multi-view images, eliminating the need for camera calibration or neural network training, and enabling efficient scene decomposition through geometric primitive fitting. We evaluate our method on the DTU and BlendedMVS datasets, demonstrating comparable reconstruction quality to state-of-the-art neural network-based approaches while requiring significantly fewer computational resources and training time. The resulting superquadric decomposition provides semantically meaningful geometric representations that facilitate downstream applications such as scene editing and object manipulation.

title: Learning What to Hear: Boosting Sound-Source Association for Robust Audiovisual Instance Segmentation
authors: Jinbae Seo, Hyeongjun Kwon, Kwonyoung Kim, Jiyoung Lee, Kwanghoon Sohn
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Audiovisual instance segmentation (AVIS) aims to accurately localize and track sounding objects throughout video sequences. Existing methods suffer from visual bias stemming from two fundamental issues: uniform additive fusion prevents queries from specializing to different sound sources, while visual-only training objectives limit queries from converging to arbitrary salient objects. We propose Audio-Centric Query Generation (ACQG) using cross-attention mechanism, enabling each query to selectively attend to distinct sound sources and carry sound-specific priors into visual decoding. Additionally, we introduce Sound-Aware Ordinal Counting (SAOC) loss that explicitly supervises sounding object numbers through ordinal regression with monotonic consistency constraints, preventing visual-only convergence during training. Experiments on AVISeg benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements: +1.64 mAP, +0.6 HOTA, and +2.06 FSLA, validating that query specialization and explicit counting supervision are crucial for accurate audiovisual instance segmentation. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/jinbae-s/ACVIS

title: Towards Self-Evaluation of Sycophantic Hallucinations in Mathematical Reasoning
authors: Zixuan Shangguan, Yanjie Dong, Xiaoyi Fan, Victor C. M. Leung, Xiping Hu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Sycophantic hallucinations occur when large language models (LLMs) produce misleading outputs that align with user biases or incorrect opinions. Sycophantic hallucinations undermine the reliability and trustworthiness of LLMs and become particularly pronounced in mathematical reasoning tasks. Therefore, we first propose the SycoMath framework that introduces three progressively structured prompting paradigms to explicitly reproduce sycophantic hallucinations during mathematical reasoning tasks. Based on the induced sycophantic hallucinations from the SycoMath framework, we then propose a self-evaluation method, which is named as ISF-Eval. Our ISF-Eval exploits the hidden states of LLMs during inference to differentiate hallucinated outputs from de-facto ones without additional training and external supervision. Extensive experiments illustrate that the ISF-Eval exhibits strong generalizability across diverse models and reasoning complexity levels and can effectively address both hallucination-induced and intrinsic challenges.

title: Do You Hear What I Mean? Quantifying the Instruction-Perception GAP in Instruction-Guided Expressive Text-to-Speech Systems
authors: Yi-Cheng Lin, Huang-Cheng Chou, Tzu-Chieh Wei, Kuan-Yu Chen, Hung-Yi Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Instruction-guided text-to-speech (ITTS) enables users to control speech generation through natural language prompts, offering a more intuitive interface than traditional TTS. However, the alignment between user style instructions and listener perception remains largely unexplored. This work first presents a perceptual analysis of ITTS controllability across two expressive dimensions (adverbs of degree and graded emotion intensity) and collects human ratings on speaker age and word-level emphasis attributes. To comprehensively reveal the instruction-perception gap, we provide a data collection with large-scale human evaluations, named Expressive VOice Control (E-VOC) corpus. Furthermore, we reveal that (1) gpt-4o-mini-tts is the most reliable ITTS model with great alignment between instruction and generated utterances across acoustic dimensions. (2) The 5 analyzed ITTS systems tend to generate Adult voices even when the instructions ask to use child or elderly voices. (3) Fine-grained control remains a major challenge, indicating that most ITTS systems have substantial room for improvement in interpreting slightly different attribute instructions.

title: OGRA-YOLOv8: Overlapping Gridded and Rhombus Attention for Underwater Object Detection
authors: Huanhuan Lv, Yuanjing Luo, Songru Jiang, Yuhao Bai, Haiyang Bai, Lijun Chen
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Underwater object detection (UOD) is vital for marine exploration, ecosystem monitoring, and aquaculture management. However, reliable UOD remains challenging due to optical degradation, low illumination, and the prevalence of small or densely distributed targets. Existing detectors often struggle to balance accuracy with real-time performance, limiting their robustness in practice. To address these issues, we propose OGRA-YOLOv8, an enhanced YOLOv8 framework tailored for UOD. We design two novel attention modules: Overlapping Gridded Attention (OGA) that strengthens local feature extraction while reducing the cost of self-attention, and Rhombus Attention (RA) to capture long-range dependencies and complement OGA. In addition, a small-object detection head (YOLOv8-P2) and WIoU v2 loss are integrated to improve bounding box regression under uneven and multi-scale target distributions. Extensive experiments on the DUO and URPC2020 datasets show that OGRA-YOLOv8 consistently surpasses state-of-the-art YOLO variants and other competitive detectors, achieving significant gains in accuracy while preserving real-time efficiency. We make the code public at: https://github.com/LYJ0126/OGRA-YOLOV8.

title: A Dataset of Robot-Patient and Doctor-Patient Medical Dialogues for Spoken Language Processing Tasks
authors: Heriberto Cuayáhuitl, Grace Jang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have brought huge improvements to Artificial Intelligence (AI), which can be applied to general-purpose tasks. However, their application to textual or spoken medical consultations is still an open research problem. This paper proposes MeDial-Speech, a novel speech dataset for training and evaluating Med-AIs that can carry out consultations with patients. It was collected in realistic environments from robot-patient and doctor-patient dialogues, contains 111+ hours of speech data (without data augmentation), and covers four health conditions: Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, and angina. In addition, we propose a dialogue benchmark via sentence selection (with 20 options) to evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, and Claude Sonnet 4. Experimental results reveal that Claude Sonnet 4 is the best in sentence selection, with 71.1% accuracy using manual transcriptions and 74.7% using automatic transcriptions, and that all LLMs are highly overconfident in their probabilistic predictions, regardless of selecting correct or incorrect sentences in medical dialogues. This dataset is free of charge for non-commercial purposes.

title: Flexi-LoRA with Input-Adaptive Ranks: Efficient Finetuning for Speech and Reasoning Tasks
authors: Zongqian Li, Yixuan Su, Han Zhou, Zihao Fu, Nigel Collier
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) have become essential for deploying large language models, yet their static parameter allocation remains suboptimal for inputs of varying complexity. We present Flexi-LoRA, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts LoRA ranks based on input complexity during both training and inference. Through empirical analysis across question answering, mathematical reasoning, and speech tasks, we demonstrate that maintaining consistency between training and inference dynamics is important for effective adaptation, particularly for sequential reasoning tasks. Our findings reveal that input-dependent parameter allocation achieves higher performance with fewer parameters by optimally matching rank configurations to question complexity. Furthermore, task-specific dependency on rank dynamics varies, with mathematical reasoning tasks exhibiting higher dependency than QA tasks. Successful adaptation manifests not only in correctness but also in reasoning quality and instruction adherence. Flexi-LoRA consistently outperforms static LoRA while using fewer parameters, with performance gains more pronounced on tasks requiring strict reasoning chains. Our approach realizes key benefits of mixture-of-experts frameworks through a more streamlined implementation, reducing parameter redundancy while improving model capabilities. We provide comprehensive empirical studies across diverse tasks, establishing a basis for future work in input-adaptive and efficient fine-tuning approaches.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Plug-and-Play Emotion Graphs for Compositional Prompting in Zero-Shot Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Jiacheng Shi, Hongfei Du, Y. Alicia Hong, Ye Gao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large audio-language models (LALMs) exhibit strong zero-shot performance across speech tasks but struggle with speech emotion recognition (SER) due to weak paralinguistic modeling and limited cross-modal reasoning. We propose Compositional Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Emotion Reasoning (CCoT-Emo), a framework that introduces structured Emotion Graphs (EGs) to guide LALMs in emotion inference without fine-tuning. Each EG encodes seven acoustic features (e.g., pitch, speech rate, jitter, shimmer), textual sentiment, keywords, and cross-modal associations. Embedded into prompts, EGs provide interpretable and compositional representations that enhance LALM reasoning. Experiments across SER benchmarks show that CCoT-Emo outperforms prior SOTA and improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines.

title: STCFormer: Robust Malicious Traffic Detection Via Short-Term Traffic Profiling and A Hybrid Transformer
authors: Junjie Yang, Junrong Liu, Song Liu, Xueying Han, Yan Zhu, Bo Jiang, Zhigang Lu, Huamin Feng
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The widespread deployment of encryption has made it increasingly difficult for Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) to identify malicious activities hidden in encrypted channels. To mitigate the limitations of prior approaches in global context modeling, noise robustness, and efficiency on long traffic sequences, we propose STCFormer, a robust malicious traffic detection model.
