The key idea is a Short-Term Traffic Grid Matrix (TGM) that encodes packet-byte content and short-term temporal ordering into an image-like representation, preserving both semantic and spatiotemporal cues. Built on this representation, STCFormer adopts a hybrid Transformer that combines the global modeling capability of the Swin Transformer with the local feature refinement efficiency of inverted residual blocks. Extensive Experiments on three public benchmarks show that STCFormer consistently outperforms strong baselines. Moreover, under random noise injection up to 30%, STCFormer maintains high detection accuracy, indicating strong robustness and generalization.

title: Online Register For Dual-Mode Self-Supervised Speech Models: Mitigating the Lack of Future Context
authors: Keita Goto, Takashi Maekaku, Jin Sakuma, Jinchuan Tian, Yusuke Shinohara, Shinji Watanabe
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised speech models (S3Ms) have become a key foundation for modern speech processing. However, models pre-trained under offline conditions often suffer from degraded performance in online scenarios due to the absence of future context. To address this challenge, we propose online registers, learnable tokens appended to each chunk in online mode. These tokens act as virtual place-holders for unseen future frames, enabling the model to compensate for missing context without introducing additional latency. Furthermore, we introduce a future prediction loss that explicitly guides the registers to capture predictive cues, thereby enriching their ability to retain future information. Experiments on LibriSpeech, and out-of-domain benchmarks demonstrate that online registers consistently reduce the performance gap between offline and online modes, achieving a 3.4% relative improvement on LibriSpeech with 160 ms chunks, especially in low-latency settings.

title: Tackling Data Heterogeneity in Parameter-Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models
authors: Jun Liu, Yunming Liao, Hongli Xu, Yang Xu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs). To reduce costs, FedLoRA integrates low-rank adaptation (LoRA) into FL, exchanging only a small subset of parameters. However, FedLoRA still suffers from heavy local computation and slow convergence under data heterogeneity. To this end, we propose HierFedLoRA, a hierarchical FedLoRA framework that jointly addresses these challenges. HierFedLoRA partitions devices into near-IID groups and adapts their intra-group aggregation frequency to mitigate non-IID effects. Meanwhile, it dynamically assigns diverse fine-tuning depths, i.e., the number of continuous layers tuned from the output, to reduce resource consumption. By jointly optimizing aggregation frequency and fine-tuning depth, HierFedLoRA balances efficiency and convergence. Experiments on a physical testbed with 80 commercial devices show that HierFedLoRA accelerates training by at least 1.97× and improves accuracy by 1.6%–4.1% compared to strong baselines.

title: Graph-Guided Contrastive Learning for Incomplete Multi-View Clustering with Consistent Global Graph
authors: Zhepeng Wang, Zhenghao Zhang, Tianyu Zong, Feng Chen, Jun Xie
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely used in Incomplete Multi-View Clustering (IMVC) for their exceptional graph learning and attribute aggregation capabilities. However, most GNN-based IMVC methods construct adjacency graphs directly from raw data, making them susceptible to view-specific noise which degrades views fusion quality. To overcome this, we propose Consistent Global Graph Learning (CGGL), designed to learn a purer, more consistent global adjacency graph by filtering out view-specific noise. Furthermore, we introduce Graph-guided Cross-view Contrastive Learning (GraphCC) to promote more compact and discriminative clustering structures in the fused features. We integrate CGGL and GraphCC into a unified framework, termed Graph-Guided Contrastive Learning for Incomplete Multi-view Clustering with Consistent Global Graph (GCIMCG). Extensive experiments on four mainstream datasets demonstrate GCIMCG’s superior performance against seven state-of-the-art methods.

title: Supervised Makeup Transfer with a Curated Dataset: Decoupling Identity and Makeup Features for Enhanced Transformation
authors: Qihe Pan, Yiming Wu, Xing Zhao, Liang Xie, Guodao Sun, Ronghua Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Diffusion models have recently shown strong progress in generative tasks, offering a more stable alternative to GAN-based approaches for makeup transfer. Existing methods often suffer from limited datasets, poor disentanglement between identity and makeup features, and weak controllability. To address these issues, we make three contributions. First, we construct a curated high-quality dataset using a train–generate–filter–retrain strategy that combines synthetic, realistic, and filtered samples to improve diversity and fidelity. Second, we design a diffusion-based framework that disentangles identity and makeup features, ensuring facial structure and skin tone are preserved while applying accurate and diverse cosmetic styles. Third, we propose a text-guided mechanism that allows fine-grained and region-specific control, enabling users to modify eyes, lips, or face makeup with natural language prompts. Experiments on benchmarks and real-world scenarios demonstrate improvements in fidelity, identity preservation, and flexibility. Examples of our dataset can be found at: https://makeup-adapter.github.io/.

title: Active Jammer Localization via Acquisition-Aware Path Planning
authors: Luis González-Gudiño, Mariona Jaramillo-Civill, Pau Closas, Tales Imbiriba
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose an active jammer localization framework that combines Bayesian optimization with acquisition-aware path planning. Unlike passive crowdsourced methods, our approach adaptively guides a mobile agent to collect high-utility Received Signal Strength measurements while accounting for urban obstacles and mobility constraints. For this, we modified the A* algorithm, A-UCB*, by incorporating acquisition values into trajectory costs, leading to high-acquisition planned paths. Simulations on realistic urban scenarios show that the proposed method achieves accurate localization with fewer measurements compared to uninformed baselines, demonstrating consistent performance under different environments.

title: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Video Colorization and Color Propagation Methods
authors: Sergey Muravlev, Sergey Lavrushkin, David Chikovani, Dmitriy Vatolin
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Video colorization and color propagation transform grayscale footage into a realistic color version. The field lacks a standardized evaluation framework to compare algorithms under diverse conditions. In this paper, we present the first comprehensive benchmark for video colorization, providing a rigorous comparison of 13 exemplar-based and fully automatic colorization methods. We introduce new datasets consisting of 153 diverse real-world scenes with annotated subjective quality scores. We also propose a novel evaluation metric, the Video Colorization Quality Index (VCQI), which is trained on human ratings and outperforms existing objective metrics in predicting perceived video colorization quality. Extensive experiments on this benchmark highlight the relative strengths and weaknesses of current colorization methods.

title: Bayesian Channel Estimation with Diffusion Probabilistic Priors
authors: Cheng Jin, Qitan Shi, Yuantao Gu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: We propose a generalized maximum a posteriori (MAP) channel estimation framework that exploits diffusion probabilistic priors for downlink massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. The channel estimation problem is reformulated as a generalized MAP inference task, where we derive the probability density of the implicit channel prior encoded by the diffusion model and obtain a simplified gradient expression of its log-prior. This leads to an efficient stochastic gradient ascent algorithm, termed Diffusion Prior Bayesian Stochastic Optimization (DP-BSO). Numerical experiments show that DP-BSO consistently outperforms classical baselines, with particularly significant gains under limited computational resources.

title: Generative Spatiotemporal Modeling for Uncertainty Quantification in High-Dimensional Physical Systems
authors: Fangzhou Liu, Chang Liu, Yuqi Li, Zhijiang Wang, Chuanguang Yang, Libo Huang, Zhulin An
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Forecasting high-dimensional chaotic systems, such as weather and turbulence, is fundamentally limited by uncertainty. Traditional deterministic models fail to capture this, collapsing to blurry, physically implausible mean-state predictions and offering no measure of confidence. We introduce Prism, a generative spatiotemporal framework that directly addresses this by learning the probability distribution of future states. Instead of regressing a single outcome, Prism, built on a conditional diffusion model, generates a diverse ensemble of high-fidelity, physically consistent future trajectories. This ensemble enables principled uncertainty quantification and improves the capture of low-probability, high-impact events. On standard benchmarks like WeatherBench and 2D turbulence, Prism significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on probabilistic metrics such as the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Our work establishes generative modeling as a powerful paradigm for building trustworthy AI tools for scientific discovery.

title: Contextual Relationship Feature-Enhanced Steganalysis for Social Texts
authors: Kaibo Huang, Xiaohan Zhang, Wansheng Wu, Yukun Wei, Zhongliang Yang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Steganalysis techniques for social media are used primarily to detect potential steganographic content within social networks, contributing to cybersecurity efforts. Most existing research focuses on analyzing individual media features, neglecting the social connectivity aspects that are crucial for identifying steganographic texts in fragmented social media environments. To address this gap, we propose a feature-enhanced linguistic steganalysis model that integrates improved node features, optimized message passing, and robust feature fusion. This model enables more accurate detection of steganographic texts even in complex social media scenarios. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art steganalysis techniques in both accuracy and efficiency.

title: A Modified Yolo with Dual-Branch Attention for High-Accuracy Detection of Corn Seedlings in UAV Images
authors: Haiqing Gou, Di Su, Jianjun Du, Xiaoze Jia, Yanyan Su, Yiyang Li, Shengze Yang, Shenglian Lu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: To address low detection accuracy and missed small targets of corn seedlings under complex seed-field conditions, we propose MS-YOLO, an improved model built upon YOLOv11. The model performance is enhanced through the integration of the PFC3k2 and DFC2PSA modules, together with an additional small-object detection head. Experimental results on the MS906 maize seedling dataset show that MS-YOLO achieves a 0.7 percentage-point improvement in mAP@50 compared with YOLOv11s. On the VisDrone2019 small object dataset, MS-YOLO outperforms YOLOv11s and YOLOv12s by 3.6 and 2.8 percentage points in mAP@50, respectively, demonstrating its effectiveness for small-object detection in UAV imagery.

title: When Large Vision-Language Models Meet Person Re-Identification
authors: Qizao Wang, Bin Li, Xiangyang Xue
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that incorporate visual models and large language models have achieved impressive results across cross-modal understanding and reasoning tasks. In recent years, person re-identification (ReID) has also started to explore cross-modal semantics to improve the accuracy of identity recognition. However, effectively utilizing LVLMs for ReID remains an open challenge. While LVLMs operate under a generative paradigm by predicting the next output word, ReID requires the extraction of discriminative identity features to match pedestrians across cameras. In this paper, we propose LVLM-ReID, a novel framework that harnesses the strengths of LVLMs to promote ReID. Specifically, we employ instructions to guide the LVLM in generating one semantic token that encapsulates key appearance semantics from the person image. This token is further refined through our Semantic-Guided Interaction (SGI) module, establishing a reciprocal interaction between the semantic token and visual tokens. Ultimately, the reinforced semantic token serves as the representation of pedestrian identity. Our framework integrates the semantic understanding and generation capabilities of LVLM into end-to-end ReID training, allowing LVLM to capture rich semantic cues during both training and inference. LVLM-ReID achieves competitive results on multiple benchmarks without additional image-text annotations, demonstrating the potential of LVLM-generated semantics to advance person ReID.

title: FANSR: Frequency Adaptive Network for Efficient Image Super-Resolution
authors: Qinhong Yang, Dongdong Chen, Qi Chu, Tao Gong, Qiankun Liu, Zhentao Tan, Huamin Feng, Nenghai Yu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Transformer models have advanced single image super-resolution (SISR), but most emphasize enlarging the receptive field while underusing high-frequency cues across spatial and channel dimensions. To mitigate this self-attention(SA)’s low-pass bias, We propose FANSR (Frequency Adaptive Network for Efficient Image Super Resolution), a lightweight framework that considering receptive-field expansion with explicit high-frequency modeling. FANSR introduces two frequency-based token mixers that transform features into frequency representations along spatial and channel axes, efficiently isolating high-frequency components and injecting them as sparse priors into the subsequent Self-Attention block. We further introduce a Frequency Enhancement Loss (FE-Loss) that adaptively prioritizes high-frequency regions, enabled by the model’s explicit distinction of low- and high-frequency content. Extensive experiments show that FANSR achieves a superior trade-off between image quality and latency compared to state-of-the-art lightweight methods. Code and pretrained models will be released.

title: Retrieval Augmented Pretrained Transformer for Competing Risks Survival in Statistical Signal Processing
authors: Yingzi Wang, Jiaxin Li, Xiaoming Wang, Chaobo Zhang, Yiming Li, Xiaojun Liang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: High frequency limit order books (LOBs) stream is execution cancellation event sequences that form a structured time series signal critical to algorithmic execution, transaction cost analysis, and risk control. This work contributes to the exploration of pre-trained transformer-based model in algorithmic trading and execution risk management. However, building a pre-trained model for transaction data is non-trivial due to the censoring structure, regime dependent order flows, and severe class imbalance under mutually exclusive outcomes. In this paper, we propose TABR-CSH, a retrieval enhanced pre-trained model for prediction of mutually exclusive execution and cancellation probabilities in LOBs. We integrate the historical retrieval block to provide context from similar past events and the cause specific hazards heads to estimate time varying execution and cancellation hazards. We evaluate TABR with a real word tick-level order execution and cancellation dataset and demonstrated state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance AUROC over 0.713 in prediction accuracy.

title: Are Modern Speech Enhancement Systems Vulnerable to Adversarial Attacks?
authors: Rostislav Makarov, Lea Schönherr, Timo Gerkmann
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Machine learning approaches for speech enhancement are becoming increasingly expressive, enabling ever more powerful modifications of input signals. In this paper, we demonstrate that this expressiveness introduces a vulnerability: advanced speech enhancement models can be susceptible to adversarial attacks. Specifically, we show that adversarial noise, carefully crafted and psychoacoustically masked by the original input, can be injected such that the enhanced speech output conveys an entirely different semantic meaning. We experimentally verify that contemporary predictive speech enhancement models can indeed be manipulated in this way. Furthermore, we highlight that diffusion models with stochastic samplers exhibit inherent robustness to such adversarial attacks by design.

title: DocLayout: Elevating the Role of Complex Layout Understanding in Document Visual Question Answering
authors: Jinxu Zhang, Yongqi Yu, Qiyuan Fan, Yuqi Wang, Haoxuan Zhu, Haofeng Wang, Yu Zhang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in document visual question answering (DVQA). However, complex document layouts still present significant challenges for these models. This work proposes DocLayout, a comprehensive multimodal benchmark comprising 1,305 question–answer pairs derived from 328 complex, multi-source document images. An empirical evaluation of 12 LVLMs shows that these challenges persist in complex layout understanding. To address this limitation, we introduce the Layout-aware Reasoning and Document Understanding (LRDU) framework, which combines semantic document segmentation and multi-agent collaboration. Experimental results demonstrate that LRDU significantly improves performance and highlight the critical need for enhanced visual reasoning capabilities in LVLMs for complex document understanding tasks.

title: Sparse Bayesian Learning with Simple and Interpretable DNNs Exploiting Data-Driven Priors
authors: Hitesh D. Khunti, Bhaskar D. Rao
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: In this paper, we present a low complexity deep neural network, built using algorithmic unrolling, for hyper-parameter estimation in the sparse Bayesian learning methodology. The resulting sparse recovery algorithm is shown to be better or comparable to existing algorithms with many additional virtues, notable of which are the ability to learn priors from the data and the trained network being interpretable. The architecture is motivated by looking at the structure in the maximum a posteriori estimation framework and abstracting the mapping in the Expectation-Maximization algorithm. A key feature of the proposed deep neural network architecture is its simplicity in that for each hyper-parameter, for each iteration, the network has a single input and a single output. The network configuration is flexible: it can share weights across iterations and hyper-parameters, or be tailored per iteration and variable, depending on the prior assumptions. Numerical results are presented that show the benefits of the data driven approach over conventional modeling in terms of their superior performance in support recovery as well as learning priors without any user assistance.

title: Training-Free Framework For Defending Unsafe Image Synthesis Attack
authors: Junha Park, Jaehui Hwang, Ian Ryu, Hyungkeun Park, Jiyoon Kim, Jong-Seok Lee
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: With advances in generative models, image generation has shown significant improvements. This raises concerns about potential abuse of generation models, such as creation of explicit or violent images, commonly referred to as Not Safe For Work (NSFW) content. To address this, the Stable Diffusion model includes safety checkers to censor initial text prompts and output images from the model. However, these safety checkers have vulnerabilities against adversarial attacks, allowing them to generate NSFW images. In this paper, we find that these adversarial attacks are not robust against small changes in embeddings of text prompts. Based on this, we propose a training-free defense method using randomized probing of prompt embeddings for text-to-image generation (T2I) to detect adversarial attacks generating NSFW images. We demonstrate the superiority of our method in terms of performance and applicability.

title: Three Seconds is Sufficient: A Multi-Pronged Framework for Model-Based Speaker Adaptation in ASR Under Data-Scarce Conditions
authors: Jiajun Deng, Guinan Li, Chunyat Wu, Tristan Tsoi, Huimeng Wang, Tao Zhong, Zhaoqing Li, Chengxi Deng, Youjun Chen, Shujie Hu, Xunying Liu, Simon Lui
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: The practical application of model-based adaptation for end-to-end ASR is severely hindered by speaker-level data sparsity. To address this critical limitation, this paper presents a holistic, multi-pronged framework that simultaneously targets three key areas: 1) model efficiency using a low-rank subspace factorization with probabilistic adaptive training to reduce data requirements; 2) data augmentation through novel speaker retrieval and guided synthesis methods; and 3) parameter stability by adopting a Bayesian estimation and gradient stabilization for uncertainty modeling. Experiments on the 300-hour Switchboard and a 5,000-hour in-house dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently delivers robust performance gains across all data conditions, achieving absolute WER reductions of up to 1.6% (Switchboard) and 4.3% (in-house) over the standard model adaptation with only three seconds of speech from the target speaker.

title: Can Synthetic Images Serve as Effective and Efficient Class Prototypes?
authors: Dianxing Shi, Dingjie Fu, Yuqiao Liu, Jun Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong performance in zero-shot image classification tasks. However, existing methods, including Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), all rely on annotated text-to-image pairs for aligning visual and textual modalities. This dependency introduces substantial cost and accuracy requirement in preparing high-quality datasets. At the same time, processing data from two modes also requires dual-tower encoders for most models, which also hinders their lightweight. To address these limitations, we introduce a "Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training via Large-Language-Model-based Generation (LGCLIP)" framework. LGCLIP leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate class-specific prompts that guide a diffusion model in synthesizing reference images. Afterwards these generated images serve as visual prototypes, and the visual features of real images are extracted and compared with the visual features of these prototypes to achieve comparative prediction. By optimizing prompt generation through the LLM and employing only a visual encoder, LGCLIP remains lightweight and efficient. Crucially, our framework requires only class labels as input during whole experimental procedure, eliminating the need for manually annotated image-text pairs and extra pre-processing. Experimental results validate the feasibility and efficiency of LGCLIP, demonstrating great performance in zero-shot classification tasks and establishing a novel paradigm for classification. Codes at Project Link.

title: Lend a Hand: Semi Training-Free Cued Speech Recognition via MLLM-Driven Hand Modeling for Barrier-Free Communication
authors: Guanjie Huang, Danny H.K. Tsang, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Li Liu
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Cued Speech (CS) is an innovative visual communication system that integrates lip reading with hand coding, designed to enhance effective communication for individuals with hearing impairments. Automatic CS Recognition (ACSR) refers to the AI-driven process of automatically recognizing hand gestures and lip movements in CS, converting them into text. Previous work was hindered by the scarcity of data, which prevented the full training of the hand-lip modeling and fusion modules, resulting in unsatisfactory performance that significantly limited ACSR’s effectiveness. To address this issue, we have innovatively explored the capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in recognizing hand codings in CS and propose a new Semi Training-Free paradigm for ACSR, named STF-ACSR. More precisely, we first design the CS Prompt Module (CSPM), which enables zero-shot recognition of hand movements through a training-free keyframe filtering mechanism and customized prompt engineering with MLLMs. Second, we introduce the Minimalist Fusion Module (MFM) to seamlessly integrate the recognition results into the lip reading model, effectively achieving superior recognition results. Extensive experiments have demonstrated that STF-ACSR significantly outperforms previous methods on both normal-hearing and hearing-impaired data. Implementations and more experimental results are available at: https://github.com/DennisHgj/STF_ACSR.

title: Towards Data Drift Monitoring for Speech Deepfake Detection in the Context of MLOps
authors: Xin Wang, Wanying Ge, Junichi Yamagishi
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: When being delivered in applications or services on the cloud, static speech deepfake detectors that are not updated will become vulnerable to newly created speech deepfake attacks. From the perspective of machine learning operations (MLOps), this paper tries to answer whether we can monitor new and unseen speech deepfake data that drifts away from a seen reference data set. We further ask, if drift is detected, whether we can fine-tune the detector using similarly drifted data, reduce the drift, and improve the detection performance. On a toy dataset and the large-scale MLAAD dataset, we show that the drift caused by new text-to-speech (TTS) attacks can be monitored using distances between the distributions of the new data and reference data. Furthermore, we demonstrate that fine-tuning the detector using data generated by the new TTS deepfakes can reduce the drift and the detection error rates.

title: Seaf: Semantic-Aware Frame Selection for Long-form Video Understanding
authors: Mengyu Yang, Ye Tian, Lanshan Zhang, Deli Yu, å Peizhuang Cong, Wendong Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: While recent advances in multimodal large language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in image-language reasoning tasks, their performance is often hampered when applied to long videos. Traditional frame selection methods mainly rely on uniform sampling, which is inherently inflexible. In this work, we propose Seaf, a semantic-aware frame sampling framework designed specifically for long-form video understanding via MLLMs. Unlike traditional uniform sampling methods, Seaf adaptively selects key frames that are relevant to the question from the entire video sequence. Notably, our method is fully training-free and parameter-free, making it highly compatible with any existing MLLM architecture as a plug-and-play module. Extensive experiments on multiple MLLMs demonstrate the effectiveness of our Seaf in achieving effective and scalable comprehension of long-form videos.

title: Knowledge Distillation via Generative Reconstruction Pathways for End-to-End Automatic Speech Recognition
authors: Sang-Heon Jeong, Dong-Hyun Kim, Joon-Hyuk Chang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: End-to-end automatic speech recognition (ASR) has achieved remarkable progress, with encoder-only models based on the connectionist temporal classification (CTC) objective being widely adopted for their efficiency and simplicity. However, the large model size of state-of-the-art systems remains a major obstacle for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge distillation (KD) provides a promising solution to compress ASR models while preserving accuracy, but most existing approaches rely on point-wise feature alignment losses, which bias the student toward reproducing local correspondences of teacher features. In this work, we propose a generative reconstruction pathway knowledge distillation (GRP-KD) framework that leverages flow matching and diffusion processes to achieve richer knowledge transfer. By treating teacher features as empirical distributions, GRP-KD formulates distillation as a generative reconstruction task that drives the student to approximate the distributional structure of teacher representations. Experiments on the LibriSpeech and GigaSpeech datasets demonstrate that GRP-KD consistently outperforms feature alignment–based KD while retaining a compact parameter footprint.

title: Scale-Aware Self-Supervised Learning for Segmentation of Small and Sparse Structures
authors: Jorge Quesada, Ghassan AlRegib
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful strategy for representation learning under limited annotation regimes, yet its effectiveness remains highly sensitive to many factors, especially the nature of the target task. In segmentation, existing pipelines are typically tuned to large, homogeneous regions, but their performance drops when objects are small, sparse, or locally irregular. In this work, we propose a scale-aware SSL adaptation that integrates small-window cropping into the augmentation pipeline, zooming in on fine-scale structures during pretraining. We evaluate this approach across two domains with markedly different data modalities: seismic imaging, where the goal is to segment sparse faults, and neuroimaging, where the task is to delineate small cellular structures. In both settings, our method yields consistent improvements over standard and state-of-the-art baselines under label constraints, improving accuracy by up to 13% for fault segmentation and 5% for cell delineation. In contrast, large-scale features such as seismic facies or tissue regions see little benefit, underscoring that the value of SSL depends critically on the scale of the target objects. Our findings highlight the need to align SSL design with object size and sparsity, offering a general principle for buil ding more effective representation learning pipelines across scientific imaging domains.

title: Low-Bandwidth High-Fidelity Speech Transmission with Generative Latent Joint Source-Channel Coding
authors: Guangkuan Li, Shengshi Yao, Sixian Wang, Zhenyu Liu, Kai Niu, Jincheng Dai
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Existing speech joint source-channel coding (JSCC) methods show advantages in robustness, however, when it goes to extreme-low bandwidth, its perceptual quality drops sharply. This poses severe challenges for high-fidelity speech transmission over bandwidth-limited weak networks. To tackle this, we propose a new Generative Latent JSCC (GL-JSCC) framework. It performs JSCC in the generative latent space learned by a residual vector quantization generative adversarial network (RVQ-GAN), instead of in the source space. Our generative latent code exhibits higher sparsity and better alignment with human perception, and generative modeling in the latent space provides an additional prior for better quality in low bandwidth. Moreover, a three-stage progressive training strategy is given to ensure effective model alignment and training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GL-JSCC achieves robust, high-quality speech transmission across a range of low SNR and bandwidth settings. Our scheme achieves the same perceptual quality while saving up to 60% of the bandwidth compared to Opus + 5G LDPC transmission.

title: Expressive Voice Conversion with Controllable Emotional Intensity
authors: Nannan Teng, Ying Hu, Zhijian Ou, Sheng Li
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Expressive voice conversion (VC) aims to transfer the speaker identity and emotion from the target speech to the source speech. In this paper, we propose an expressive voice conversion model with controllable emotional intensity named CEIVC, which includes a specific attribute augmentation (SAA) training strategy for perturbing the speaker and emotional attributes of source speech, and an emotional disentanglement and intensity control (EDIC) module to control the emotional intensity of converted speech. Moreover, we leverage perturbation adaptive instance normalization (PbAdaIN) to further boost the synthesis quality. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed CEI-VC outperforms five compared models and is capable of controlling the emotional intensity of converted speech. Demos and codes are available at https://tengnn.github.io/ExpressiveVC/.

title: Accelerating Vehicular Federated Learning via Convergence-Aware Hierarchical Scheduling
authors: Jiasheng Zhu, Xinrui Gao, Yaolin Wang, Haoran Yang, Shangpeng Wang
publish: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
abstract: Efficient client scheduling is vital for federated learning in integrated space-air-ground vehicular networks. Existing methods often neglect dynamic communication links and learning convergence metrics. We propose a Hierarchical Decoupling Scheduling (HDS) framework that aligns network decisions with long-term learning objectives. This approach splits the complex joint scheduling problem into a stable matching game for client association and a convex subproblem for resource allocation. Our convergence-aware method manages data heterogeneity and prevents client starvation. Simulations show our framework outperforms baselines in convergence speed and final model accuracy, achieving over 80% accuracy on highly non-IID data while maintaining near-linear scalability.

title: Commerce User Review Sentiment Analysis Model Based on BERT and Transformer
authors: Tuli Chen
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Digital Media Technology and Interaction Design
abstract: None

title: Intelligent Design Research on Ceramic Patterns Based on Generative Adversarial Networks
authors: Weijun Wu
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Digital Media Technology and Interaction Design
abstract: None

title: Large-Scale Energy Storage Systems: Comparative Analysis between BESS and HSS
authors: Amaro Krob, Carlos Frederico, Miguel Udaeta, Viviane Nascimento, Vanessa Massara, Andrea Collin
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative analysis of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and Hydrogen Storage Systems (HSS) for large-scale energy storage in photovoltaic applications. A multicriteria evaluation based on the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) was employed to assess key performance indicators, including energy efficiency, environmental impact, cost, safety, and recyclability. The results indicate that BESS demonstrate superior efficiency and operational flexibility, while HSS offer advantages in longduration storage, environmental sustainability, and system lifespan. The findings highlight the complementary nature of both technologies and the strategic potential of Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS) that integrate their strengths to overcome individual limitations. The study also discusses technological and regulatory challenges, smart grid integration, and emerging innovations, concluding that HESS represents a resilient and scalable pathway toward future sustainable energy systems.
